topics.title: Preliminary Work: Testing and Troubleshooting of Power Diodes, Power Transistors, MOSFETs, IGBTs, Op-Amps, and MOSFET Drivers Using Multimeters and Datasheets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL507 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Preliminary Work: Testing and Troubleshooting of Power Diodes, Power Transistors, MOSFETs, IGBTs, Op-Amps, and MOSFET Drivers Using Multimeters and Datasheets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Preliminary Work in Power Electronics Lab

**Preliminary work** in a Power Electronics Laboratory refers to the systematic pre-experimental verification of semiconductor devices, integrated circuits, and driver modules using basic test instruments (chiefly the **Digital Multimeter - DMM**) and the manufacturer's **datasheet parameters**. It is the *first line of defense* against circuit failure, miswiring, and inaccurate measurements during subsequent experiments such as chopper drives, inverter testing, and SMPS evaluation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis (PCEOL507):**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus for PCEOL507 explicitly requires students to:
> 1. *Identify and test* power semiconductor switches before circuit integration.
> 2. *Cross-verify* multimeter readings against datasheet specifications.
> 3. *Diagnose* common faults (open, short, leakage, gate-oxide rupture) using structured troubleshooting methodology.
> 4. *Document* observations in a lab record aligned with **Course Outcomes CO1–CO3** (Hardware Identification, Circuit Realization, and Testing & Measurement).

---

## 1.2 Component-Wise Definitions

### 1.2.1 Power Diode
A **power diode** is a two-terminal, unidirectional, uncontrolled power semiconductor switch that conducts current only when forward-biased above its **cut-in voltage** ($V_F \approx 0.7\text{ V}$ for silicon, $1.0\text{–}1.2\text{ V}$ for fast/Schottky types). It is the simplest building block of every rectifier, freewheeling path, and snubber circuit.

> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a power diode as a **one-way hydraulic check-valve** in a water pipe. Water (current) can flow only in the arrow-marked direction; reverse flow is physically blocked. If the valve is *stuck open* (shorted diode) or *permanently sealed* (open diode), the entire plumbing system fails.

### 1.2.2 Power BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor)
A **power BJT** is a three-terminal, **current-controlled** device with terminals **Base (B), Collector (C), and Emitter (E)**. A small base current $I_B$ controls a much larger collector current $I_C$ through the relation $I_C = h_{FE} \cdot I_B$.

> **Intuitive Analogy:** A BJT behaves like a **faucet (base) controlling a main water pump (collector)**. Turning the small faucet handle slightly causes a huge water flow from the pump. If the faucet is broken (open base) or jammed (shorted B-E), the pump either never starts or never stops.

### 1.2.3 Power MOSFET (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor)
A **power MOSFET** is a three-terminal, **voltage-controlled** majority-carrier device with terminals **Gate (G), Drain (D), and Source (S)**. Conduction is initiated by applying $V_{GS} \geq V_{GS(th)}$ (typically $2\text{–}4\text{ V}$), causing the channel to invert and conduct.

> **Intuitive Analogy:** A MOSFET is like a **garden hose with an electronic pinch-valve**: the valve opens when you apply voltage to the gate wire (no current needed to *hold* it open — only a tiny pulse to *change* state). The hose's "stiffness" when open is the on-state resistance $R_{DS(on)}$.

### 1.2.4 IGBT (Insulated-Gate Bipolar Transistor)
An **IGBT** combines the **high input impedance (MOSFET gate) with low on-state voltage drop (BJT collector)**. It has terminals **Gate (G), Collector (C), and Emitter (E)**. It is the workhorse of medium-to-high power converters ($1\text{ kW} \text{ to } 1\text{ MW}$).

> **Intuitive Analogy:** An IGBT is a **hybrid car** — the *electrical gate input* is from the MOSFET world (effortless, voltage-driven), but the *power-handling muscle* comes from the BJT world (low drop, high current). It gives you the *fuel efficiency* of an EV (MOSFET) with the *towing capacity* of a truck (BJT).

### 1.2.5 Op-Amp (Operational Amplifier)
An **operational amplifier** is a high-gain ($A_{OL} \approx 10^5 \text{ to } 10^6$) DC-coupled differential amplifier IC with two inputs (**Inverting: $V^-$**, **Non-inverting: $V^+$**) and one output ($V_{out}$). In power electronics, it serves in **feedback loops, comparators, current sensing, and PWM generation**.

> **Intuitive Analogy:** An op-amp is a **precise see-saw balance**: the output tilts one way or the other by an enormous force, by even the tiniest difference in weight between the two pans ($V^+ - V^-$). In closed-loop, external feedback "restrains" it to behave as a precise amplifier, integrator, or filter.

### 1.2.6 MOSFET / IGBT Gate Driver
A **gate driver** is a buffer/level-shifter IC that translates a low-power logic signal (e.g., $3.3\text{ V}$ from a microcontroller) into a high-current, properly-rated gate drive signal (e.g., $+15\text{ V} / -8\text{ V}$) capable of rapidly charging/discharging the device's input capacitance $C_{iss}$. Examples: **TLP250, IR2110, HCPL-3120, UCC27714**.

> **Intuitive Analogy:** A gate driver is a **professional translator in a UN-style conference**. The microcontroller speaks softly in *logic-level English* (3.3 V), and the IGBT/MOSFET only understands *high-power shouting* ($\pm15$ V, several amps peak). The driver instantly translates and amplifies — without the driver, the microcontroller's whisper is inaudible to the power device.

---

## 1.3 The Digital Multimeter (DMM) — The Student's First Tool

A **Digital Multimeter** is a multi-function electronic measuring instrument that combines:
- **Voltmeter** (DC and AC voltage)
- **Ammeter** (DC and AC current)
- **Ohmmeter** (resistance)
- **Continuity Tester** (beep on low resistance)
- **Diode Tester** (forward voltage drop measurement)
- **Capacitance Meter** (in modern DMMs)

For preliminary work, the **diode test mode** and **resistance mode** are used most.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical DMM Rule for Power Electronics:**
> Never connect the DMM (especially in diode/continuity mode, which sources $2\text{–}5\text{ mA}$) to a **powered circuit**. Always isolate the device by desoldering one leg or ensuring all energy-storage capacitors are fully discharged. The DMM is for *static, unpowered* testing only.

---

## 1.4 Datasheet — The Engineer's "Truth Document"

A **datasheet** is a manufacturer-issued document specifying electrical, thermal, and mechanical limits of a component. Key sections used in preliminary work:

| Section | What It Tells You |
|---|---|
| **Absolute Maximum Ratings** | Hard limits — exceeding them destroys the device |
| **Electrical Characteristics** | Guaranteed performance (with min/typ/max) |
| **Typical Performance Curves** | How parameters vary with $I$, $V$, $T$ |
| **Pin Configuration / Package** | Physical identification |
| **Thermal Characteristics** | $R_{\theta JC}$, $R_{\theta JA}$ for heatsink design |
| **Test Conditions** | The exact $V_{GS}$, $I_D$, $T_J$ at which values are measured |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "verify" a device, your answer must show: *(a) datasheet value, (b) measured value, (c) tolerance band, (d) conclusion (PASS / FAIL / borderline)*. This is exactly what the KTU external examiner expects in the lab record's preliminary work page.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Principle — Why Multimeter Testing Works

Every semiconductor junction is fundamentally a **PN junction**. The multimeter's diode-test mode injects a small known current (typically $I_{test} = 1\text{–}5\text{ mA}$) and measures the resulting forward voltage drop. This works because:

$$\text{Forward-biased PN junction} \implies \text{measured } V_F \text{ is } 0.3\text{–}0.9\text{ V (silicon)}$$

$$\text{Reverse-biased PN junction} \implies \text{measured } V_F = \text{OL (Over-Limit / open)}$$

A junction is **healthy** if it shows approximately the right forward drop and is reverse-blocking. A junction is **shorted** if it shows $\sim 0\text{ V}$ in both directions, and **open** if it shows OL in both directions.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Testing Logic for Each Device

### 2.2.1 Power Diode Test Procedure

**Component to test:** e.g., MUR460, BYW29, 1N5408

1. Set DMM to **diode mode** (symbol: ▶|).
2. Connect **red (+) lead to Anode**, **black (–) lead to Cathode** (stripe-marked end).
3. **Expected reading (forward):** $V_F \approx 0.5\text{–}1.2\text{ V}$ (silicon rectifier: $0.9\text{–}1.2\text{ V}$; Schottky: $0.3\text{–}0.5\text{ V}$).
4. **Reverse the leads.** Expected reading: **OL (over-range)**.
5. **Fault diagnosis:**

| Reading | Diagnosis |
|---|---|
| $0.5\text{–}1.2\text{ V}$ forward, OL reverse | ✅ **Healthy** |
| $0.000\text{ V}$ both directions | ❌ **Shorted** |
| OL both directions | ❌ **Open (blown)** |
| Forward drop > datasheet max | ⚠️ **Degraded** (high leakage / overheating) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power diodes are *non-replaceable fuses* in many ways:** When they fail in a real converter, 90 % of the time they fail **short-circuited** (silicon melts and forms a metallic bridge), causing catastrophic supply-side faults. This is why *preliminary diode testing saves the entire lab setup*.

---

### 2.2.2 Power BJT (NPN) Test Procedure

A BJT contains **two PN junctions**: B-E and B-C.

1. Set DMM to **diode mode**.
2. For an **NPN transistor**:

| Test | Red Lead (+) | Black Lead (–) | Expected Reading |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Base | Emitter | $0.6\text{–}0.7\text{ V}$ (B-E forward) |
| 2 | Emitter | Base | OL (B-E reverse) |
| 3 | Base | Collector | $0.6\text{–}0.7\text{ V}$ (B-C forward) |
| 4 | Collector | Base | OL (B-C reverse) |
| 5 | Collector | Emitter | OL ($V_{CEO}$ blocking, typically high) |
| 6 | Emitter | Collector | OL |

For **PNP**, swap red/black roles in tests 1–4.

> **Healthy BJT Rule:** *Both junctions behave like diodes (one direction conducts, other blocks), and C-E blocks in both polarizations.*

---

### 2.2.3 Power MOSFET Test Procedure

A power MOSFET's **gate is insulated** (no DC path to source). All measurements use the DMM's internal test source.

1. **Discharge the gate first:** Briefly short G to S with a finger or wire (MOSFETs retain static charge!).
2. Set DMM to **diode mode**.

| Test | Red Lead (+) | Black Lead (–) | Expected Reading |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gate | Source | OL (or momentary then OL) — measures $V_{GS}$ capacitance charging |
| 2 | Source | Gate | OL |
| 3 | **Drain** | **Source** | OL (body-diode reverse) **or** **$0.4\text{–}0.9\text{ V}$** depending on device |
| 4 | **Source** | **Drain** | $0.4\text{–}0.9\text{ V}$ — **the intrinsic body diode forward drop** |

3. **Dynamic "turn-on" test** (the definitive MOSFET check):
   - Place **red on Drain, black on Source**.
   - Touch the **Gate momentarily with the red lead** (charges $C_{iss}$ → MOSFET turns ON).
   - Reading should drop from OL to a **low value** (typically $0.01\text{–}0.5\text{ V}$ for power MOSFETs, corresponding to $I \cdot R_{DS(on)}$).
   - **Remove the gate connection** — the reading should *stay low* (MOSFET latches ON via $C_{iss}$).
   - **Short G to S** to discharge — reading should return to OL.

> [!WARNING]
> This dynamic test is **the single most powerful MOSFET test possible with a DMM**. It verifies the gate insulation, channel formation, and $R_{DS(on)}$ in one go. A device that "turns on" only while the gate is touched but snaps back to OL immediately is **leaky / partially failed** (gate oxide damage).

---

### 2.2.4 IGBT Test Procedure

An IGBT behaves *exactly like a MOSFET on the input side* and *like a BJT on the output side*, with one extra diode (the anti-parallel body diode, present in many modules).

| Test | Red Lead (+) | Black Lead (–) | Expected Reading |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gate | Emitter | OL |
| 2 | Emitter | Gate | OL |
| 3 | Collector | Emitter | OL |
| 4 | Emitter | Collector | Body diode drop, $0.5\text{–}1.5\text{ V}$ (if anti-parallel diode present) |
| 5 | **Dynamic turn-on:** Red on C, touch Gate with Red lead | Emitter | Reading should drop (IGBT turns ON, $V_{CE(sat)}$ develops) |
| 6 | Short G to E, retry | — | Reading returns to OL |

> **Note:** Some IGBTs (e.g., discrete IRG4BC30) have *no co-packaged diode* — Test 4 will show OL, and that is normal. Always check the datasheet's "Internal Schematic" first.

---

### 2.2.5 Op-Amp Test Procedure

A standard op-amp (e.g., LM741, TL081, LM358) has **8 pins** in DIP/SOIC packages. Pinout (e.g., LM741, DIP-8):

| Pin | Function |
|---|---|
| 1 | Offset Null |
| 2 | Inverting Input ($V^-$) |
| 3 | Non-inverting Input ($V^+$) |
| 4 | $V^-$ supply (–15 V typical) |
| 5 | Offset Null |
| 6 | Output |
| 7 | $V^+$ supply (+15 V typical) |
| 8 | NC |

**Preliminary static checks (DMM, ohms-mode):**
- Pin 8 to Pin 4: should be **OL** (no internal connection).
- Pin 1 to Pin 5: typically $1\text{ k}\Omega$ to $30\text{ k}\Omega$ (offset null resistor — refer to datasheet).
- Pin 4 to Pin 7: should be **OL** (no direct supply short — supplies are isolated through the IC).

> **Powered sanity test (after circuit is built):**
> - Apply $\pm 15\text{ V}$, ground both inputs.
> - Output should sit at $\approx 0\text{ V}$ ($\pm$ a few mV of $V_{OS}$ — input offset voltage).
> - Tie $V^+ = 0$, $V^- = +1\text{ V}$ → output should saturate near $-V_{EE}$ (e.g., $-13\text{ V}$).
> - Reverse inputs → output should saturate near $+V_{CC}$.
> - If output is **stuck at one rail regardless of input**, the op-amp is **damaged**.

---

### 2.2.6 MOSFET / IGBT Gate Driver Test Procedure

Driver ICs (e.g., **TLP250**, **IR2110**) typically have 8 or 14 pins.

**Key pins to identify via datasheet:**
- $V_{CC}$ (logic supply, +5 V or +3.3 V)
- $V_{BS}$ / $V_{B}$ (bootstrap / high-side supply, +15 V)
- $V_{SS}$ / COM (ground reference)
- HIN, LIN (logic inputs, high-side and low-side)
- HO, LO (gate drive outputs)
- $V_{S}$ (high-side floating supply return)

**Preliminary tests:**
1. **Pin-continuity check** (ohms mode): No two adjacent power pins should show low resistance (e.g., $V_{CC}$ to GND should be OL — anything below $10\text{ }\Omega$ suggests a destroyed internal regulator).
2. **Powered functional test:**
   - Apply $V_{CC} = +5\text{ V}$ (logic), $V_{B} = +15\text{ V}$ (drive).
   - Apply 5 V square wave at HIN (1 kHz, 50 % duty).
   - Observe HO with oscilloscope → expect 0–15 V square wave in phase with HIN.
   - Apply signal to LIN → expect 0–15 V at LO.
   - **Propagation delay** $t_{PLH}$ and $t_{PHL}$ should match datasheet (typically $100\text{–}500\text{ ns}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why driver testing is critical:** A shorted driver is the *most common* cause of exploded MOSFETs/IGBTs in student labs. A short between HO and $V_S$ (or between LO and COM) connects the full DC bus directly to the gate, instantly destroying the gate oxide of the power device.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Parameter Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Instruction:** All `|` (pipe) symbols below are rendered as `\vert` to comply with markdown table safety rules.

| # | Parameter / Formula | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward voltage drop | $V_F$ | $0.7\text{–}1.2\text{ V}$ (Si) | Diode test |
| 2 | Reverse leakage current | $I_R$ | $\mu\text{A to mA}$ range | Datasheet verification |
| 3 | DC current gain | $h_{FE} = I_C / I_B$ | $10\text{–}100$ (power BJT) | BJT functional check |
| 4 | Gate threshold voltage | $V_{GS(th)}$ | $2.0\text{–}4.5\text{ V}$ | MOSFET turn-on test |
| 5 | On-state resistance | $R_{DS(on)}$ | $10\text{ m}\Omega\text{–}1\text{ }\Omega$ | MOSFET conduction loss |
| 6 | Continuous drain current | $I_D$ | $10\text{–}200\text{ A}$ | Current rating |
| 7 | Breakdown voltage | $V_{(BR)DSS}$ / $V_{CEO}$ | $30\text{–}1200\text{ V}$ | Voltage rating |
| 8 | Collector-emitter saturation | $V_{CE(sat)}$ | $1.5\text{–}3.5\text{ V}$ (IGBT) | IGBT conduction loss |
| 9 | Input capacitance | $C_{iss}$ | $1\text{–}10\text{ nF}$ | Driver sizing |
| 10 | Gate charge | $Q_g$ | $20\text{–}200\text{ nC}$ | Drive energy, switching loss |
| 11 | Open-loop gain | $A_{OL}$ | $10^5\text{–}10^6$ V/V | Op-amp DC performance |
| 12 | Input offset voltage | $V_{OS}$ | $0.1\text{–}5\text{ mV}$ | Op-amp DC error |
| 13 | Slew rate | $SR$ | $0.3\text{–}20\text{ V/}\mu\text{s}$ | Op-amp AC limit |
| 14 | Propagation delay (driver) | $t_{pd}$ | $100\text{–}500\text{ ns}$ | Driver timing |
| 15 | Power dissipation | $P_D = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C + V_F \cdot I_F$ | Watts | Thermal check |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This preliminary work is *not* academic ritual — it directly enables:

1. **Field service & repair** — A field engineer uses the exact same DMM-based tests to diagnose a blown VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) in an industrial plant. The preliminary work is, in effect, **vocational training for power-electronics maintenance**.
2. **Pre-shipping QC (Quality Control)** — Manufacturers like Siemens, ABB, and Schneider test every IGBT module with automated versions of these exact tests before it leaves the factory.
3. **Reverse engineering & counterfeit detection** — Counterfeit semiconductors (a multi-billion dollar problem) are routinely caught because their dynamic turn-on behavior, leakage, or $R_{DS(on)}$ deviates from datasheet norms.
4. **Failure analysis in research labs** — The first step in *any* failure analysis is "did the device fail open, short, or parametrically?" — these DMM tests answer that in seconds.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Calculations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why a Healthy Power Diode Shows $V_F \approx 0.7\text{ V}$ Under DMM Diode Test

The DMM's diode-test mode approximates the **Schockley diode equation** at a fixed test current $I_{test}$ (typically $1\text{ mA}$ or $2\text{ mA}$, model-dependent).

$$I_D = I_S \left( e^{V_D / (n V_T)} - 1 \right)$$

At room temperature ($T = 300\text{ K}$), the thermal voltage is:

$$V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 25.85 \text{ mV}$$

For a power silicon diode, typical parameters are:
- Saturation current $I_S \approx 10^{-9}\text{ A}$ to $10^{-6}\text{ A}$ (power diodes: $10^{-9}\text{ A}$)
- Ideality factor $n \approx 1.5\text{–}2.0$ (power rectifiers skew higher due to recombination in the drift region)

Solving for $V_D$ at $I_D = I_{test} = 2\text{ mA}$ and $I_S = 10^{-9}\text{ A}$, $n = 1.8$:

$$V_D = n V_T \cdot \ln\!\left( \frac{I_{test}}{I_S} + 1 \right)$$

$$V_D = (1.8)(0.02585) \cdot \ln\!\left( \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-9}} + 1 \right)$$

$$V_D = 0.04653 \cdot \ln(2 \times 10^{6})$$

$$V_D = 0.04653 \cdot \ln(2,000,000)$$

$$V_D = 0.04653 \cdot 14.508$$

$$\boxed{V_D \approx 0.675 \text{ V}}$$

This is the **expected DMM diode-test reading** for a healthy silicon power diode. A reading within $\pm 0.1\text{ V}$ of this value confirms a good junction. This is the **mathematical foundation** of the preliminary diode test.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: MOSFET Dynamic Turn-On Threshold Verification

A power MOSFET turns ON when $V_{GS}$ exceeds $V_{GS(th)}$. The DMM's diode-mode voltage is *just enough* to charge $C_{iss}$ (typically $1\text{–}10\text{ nF}$) past this threshold.

The DMM's internal source voltage in diode mode is typically $V_{src} \approx 2.5\text{–}3.0\text{ V}$ (open-circuit). When connected to G-S:

$$Q_{gate} = C_{iss} \cdot V_{GS(th)}$$

For an IRF540N (a common KTU-lab MOSFET): $C_{iss} \approx 1.96\text{ nF}$, $V_{GS(th)} \approx 3.5\text{ V}$ (max).

$$Q_{gate} = 1.96 \times 10^{-9} \times 3.5 = 6.86 \text{ nC}$$

The DMM's internal current limit in diode mode is about $1\text{–}5\text{ mA}$. Charging time constant:

$$\tau = \frac{C_{iss} \cdot V_{src}}{I_{limit}} = \frac{(1.96 \times 10^{-9})(2.7)}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 2.65 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$$

So the MOSFET turns on within **a few microseconds** of touching the gate — easily observable on the DMM as the drain-source reading drops from OL to a low value. This derivation justifies *why* the "touch the gate" test works reliably.

---

## 3.3 Calculation: Estimating $R_{DS(on)}$ from a DMM Reading

When the MOSFET is fully ON, the DMM in diode mode sources $I_{test} \approx 2\text{ mA}$ from D to S. The displayed voltage $V_{meas}$ is the IR-drop across the channel:

$$V_{meas} = I_{test} \cdot R_{DS(on)}$$

Solving for $R_{DS(on)}$:

$$R_{DS(on)} = \frac{V_{meas}}{I_{test}}$$

**Worked example:**
Suppose for an IRF540N, $V_{meas} = 0.054\text{ V}$ at $I_{test} = 2\text{ mA}$.

$$R_{DS(on)} = \frac{0.054}{0.002} = 0.027 \text{ }\Omega = 27 \text{ m}\Omega$$

Datasheet specifies $R_{DS(on)} = 44\text{ m}\Omega$ at $V_{GS} = 10\text{ V}$ and $I_D = 22\text{ A}$ — but at *much lower* $V_{GS} \approx 3\text{ V}$ (what the DMM provides), $R_{DS(on)}$ can be *higher*. The lower-than-datasheet reading here is acceptable because the device is in *partial enhancement*. KTU students are expected to record both values and note the discrepancy with reasoning.

---

## 3.4 Calculation: IGBT $V_{CE(sat)}$ Verification

For an IGBT (e.g., IRG4BC30K), the DMM shows $V_{CE}$ when ON:

$$V_{meas} \approx V_{CE(sat)} \text{ at } I_{test} = 2 \text{ mA}$$

Datasheet: $V_{CE(sat)} = 2.1\text{ V}$ at $I_C = 16\text{ A}$, $V_{GE} = 15\text{ V}$. At $I_{test} = 2\text{ mA}$, $V_{CE(sat)}$ is much lower because IGBTs have an offset voltage $\approx 0.7\text{ V}$ (the BJT base-emitter-like threshold) plus a small slope resistance. Expect a reading of $\approx 0.7\text{–}1.2\text{ V}$.

---

## 3.5 Op-Amp Offset Voltage Estimation

The output of an op-amp with both inputs grounded is:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot V_{OS}$$

For LM741: $A_{OL} \approx 200{,}000$, datasheet $V_{OS} = 2\text{ mV}$ (typical).

$$V_{out} = 200{,}000 \times 0.002 = 400 \text{ V}$$

But the output cannot exceed $V_{CC}$, so it **saturates near $+V_{CC} - 2\text{ V} \approx +13\text{ V}$**. This is the correct expected behavior in a powered test. A reading of $0\text{ V}$ would mean $V_{OS}$ is uncommonly low or the device is not powered. A reading at $-13\text{ V}$ would mean $V_{OS}$ is negative — still healthy.

---

## 3.6 Driver Propagation Delay Verification

Driver datasheets (e.g., TLP250) specify $t_{PLH}$ and $t_{PHL}$ at test conditions $V_{CC} = 30\text{ V}$, $I_{O} = 0.1\text{ A}$. Expected:

$$t_{PLH} = 0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{s (typ)}, \quad t_{PHL} = 0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{s (typ)}$$

Student measures with oscilloscope: $t_{PLH,meas} = 0.55\text{ }\mu\text{s}$, $t_{PHL,meas} = 0.48\text{ }\mu\text{s}$. Both within $\pm 20\%$ of datasheet → PASS.

---

## 3.7 Comprehensive Preliminary Work Observation Table (Analytical Matrix)

> **This is the master table students must fill in the lab record. KTU examiners explicitly check this format.**

| S.No. | Device | Test Configuration | DMM Mode | Expected (Datasheet) | Measured | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Diode 1N5408 | A-K, forward | Diode | $0.9\text{–}1.2\text{ V}$ | $0.94\text{ V}$ | ✅ PASS |
| 2 | Diode 1N5408 | K-A, reverse | Diode | OL | OL | ✅ PASS |
| 3 | BJT SL100 (NPN) | B-E, forward | Diode | $0.6\text{–}0.7\text{ V}$ | $0.685\text{ V}$ | ✅ PASS |
| 4 | BJT SL100 (NPN) | B-E, reverse | Diode | OL | OL | ✅ PASS |
| 5 | BJT SL100 (NPN) | C-E, both polarities | Diode | OL both | OL both | ✅ PASS |
| 6 | MOSFET IRF540N | G-S, both | Diode | OL | OL | ✅ PASS |
| 7 | MOSFET IRF540N | S-D (body diode) | Diode | $0.5\text{–}0.9\text{ V}$ | $0.72\text{ V}$ | ✅ PASS |
| 8 | MOSFET IRF540N | Touch G with red lead | Diode | Drops to $0.02\text{–}0.5\text{ V}$ | $0.054\text{ V}$ | ✅ PASS |
| 9 | IGBT IRG4BC30K | C-E reverse | Diode | OL | OL | ✅ PASS |
| 10 | IGBT IRG4BC30K | Touch G with red lead | Diode | Drops to $0.5\text{–}1.5\text{ V}$ | $0.93\text{ V}$ | ✅ PASS |
| 11 | Op-Amp LM741 | Pin 7 to Pin 4 (supply isolation) | Ohms | OL | OL | ✅ PASS |
| 12 | Op-Amp LM741 | Pin 1 to Pin 5 (offset null) | Ohms | $\sim 1\text{ k}\Omega$ | $1.05\text{ k}\Omega$ | ✅ PASS |
| 13 | Op-Amp LM741 | Powered test: $V^+ = V^- = 0$ | DC V | $\pm 50\text{ mV}$ | $+12\text{ mV}$ | ✅ PASS |
| 14 | Driver TLP250 | Pin $V_{CC}$ to GND | Ohms | OL | OL | ✅ PASS |
| 15 | Driver TLP250 | Powered, 1 kHz at HIN → HO | Oscilloscope | $0\text{–}15\text{ V}$ square | $0.8\text{–}14.6\text{ V}$ | ✅ PASS |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid Block Diagrams)

## 4.1 Master Preliminary-Work Testing Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Lab Kit Unpacked] --> nodeB[Inventory Check\nList all components]
    nodeB --> nodeC[For Each Component:\nIdentify by marking\nand datasheet lookup]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Powered or\nUnpowered Test?}
    nodeD -- Unpowered --> nodeE[Set DMM to\nDiode Mode]
    nodeD -- Powered --> nodeF[Apply rails as per\ndatasheet, use scope]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Measure V_F forward]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Reading within\ndatasheet band?}
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeI[Mark HEALTHY\nRecord in log]
    nodeH -- No --> nodeJ[Diagnose:\nOpen / Short / Leaky]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Quarantine and\nreplace component]
    nodeI --> nodeL{All components\ntested?}
    nodeL -- No --> nodeC
    nodeL -- Yes --> nodeM[Proceed to circuit\nassembly]
    nodeF --> nodeN[Functional test:\ninputs, outputs, timing]
    nodeN --> nodeO{Performance\nmatches datasheet?}
    nodeO -- Yes --> nodeI
    nodeO -- No --> nodeK
```

## 4.2 Device-Specific Test Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Power Diode] --> nodeA1[DMM Diode Test:\nForward V_F check]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA2[Reverse OL check]
    nodeB[Power BJT] --> nodeB1[Base-Emitter diode]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2[Base-Collector diode]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3[Collector-Emitter OL check]
    nodeC[Power MOSFET] --> nodeC1[Gate insulation check]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2[Body diode check]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC3[Dynamic turn-on\nvia gate touch]
    nodeD[IGBT] --> nodeD1[Gate insulation check]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD2[Anti-parallel diode]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD3[Dynamic turn-on\ncheck]
    nodeE[Op-Amp] --> nodeE1[Pin continuity]
    nodeE1 --> nodeE2[Powered:\nInput grounding test]
    nodeE2 --> nodeE3[Powered:\nSlew rate / bandwidth]
    nodeF[Gate Driver] --> nodeF1[Pin supply isolation]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF2[Powered:\nLogic-in to drive-out]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF3[Propagation delay\nmeasurement]
```

## 4.3 MOSFET Dynamic Test — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[DMM in Diode Mode\nRed on Drain, Black on Source] --> nodeB[Initial reading: OL]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Touch Gate\nwith Red lead\nCharges C_iss]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Channel forms\nV_DS drops low]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Remove gate contact\nV_DS stays low\nC_iss holds charge]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Short Gate to Source\nC_iss discharges]
    nodeF --> nodeG[V_DS returns to OL\nMOSFET is OFF]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Conclusion:\nGate oxide OK\nChannel forms OK\nR_DS on within range]
```

## 4.4 Power Electronics Lab Preliminary-Work Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Microcontroller\n3.3 V PWM] --> nodeB[Gate Driver\nTLP250]
    nodeB --> nodeC[MOSFET / IGBT\nGate receives 0-15 V]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Power stage\nD, S / C, E]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Load\nMotor / Lamp / R]
    nodeF[Op-Amp\nCurrent sense] --> nodeB
    nodeF --> nodeG[Feedback to MCU\nADC input]
    nodeH[DMM Pre-test\nAll blocks] --> nodeA
    nodeH --> nodeB
    nodeH --> nodeC
    nodeH --> nodeF
```

## 4.5 Fault-Diagnosis Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Device under test] --> nodeB{Forward drop}
    nodeB -- Normal 0.5-1.2 V --> nodeC{Reverse\nreading?}
    nodeB -- Zero volt --> nodeD[SHORT diagnosis\nReplace device]
    nodeB -- Over-Limit --> nodeE[OPEN diagnosis\nReplace device]
    nodeC -- Over-Limit --> nodeF[HEALTHY:\nForward and reverse\nasymmetric]
    nodeC -- Same as forward --> nodeG[LEAKY device\nR_parallel very low\nMarginal - replace]
    nodeC -- Zero volt --> nodeD
    nodeF --> nodeH[Record and\nproceed]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part A consists of short, direct questions testing recall and basic understanding. Answer length: 3–5 lines.

---

### **Question 1 (3 marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]*
**Q:** List the three tests you would perform on a power MOSFET using only a digital multimeter (DMM) to confirm it is functional. What readings do you expect?

**Model Answer:**

The three DMM-based tests on a power MOSFET (DMM set to diode mode) are:

1. **Gate-to-Source insulation test:** Connect red and black leads to G and S in both polarities. **Expected: OL** in both directions, because the gate is oxide-isolated and has no DC conduction path.

2. **Body-diode test:** Red lead to **Source**, black lead to **Drain**. **Expected: $0.4\text{–}0.9\text{ V}$** — the intrinsic body diode's forward drop.

3. **Dynamic turn-on test:** With red on **Drain** and black on **Source** (reading = OL), briefly touch the **Gate with the red lead**. The reading should **drop to a low value** ($0.02\text{–}0.5\text{ V}$), confirming channel formation. A healthy MOSFET holds this low reading even after the gate lead is removed (gate capacitance latches it ON) until G–S is shorted.

If all three tests pass, the MOSFET is functional.

---

### **Question 2 (3 marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]*
**Q:** Why is it essential to check the datasheet of a power diode before testing it with a multimeter? What datasheet parameters are most relevant for preliminary multimeter-based testing?

**Model Answer:**

Datasheet checking is essential because multimeters operate at very low test currents ($1\text{–}5\text{ mA}$), which represent only a *small fraction* of the device's rated current. The measured forward voltage $V_F$ at the DMM's test current is always *less* than the datasheet value rated at full load. Therefore, the student must know the *expected band* of $V_F$ at low current to avoid falsely flagging a healthy device as degraded.

The most relevant datasheet parameters for DMM-based preliminary testing are:

- **$V_F$ at the test current** — used to set the pass/fail window. For 1N5408: $V_F = 0.9\text{ V}$ (typ) at $1\text{ A}$; at $2\text{ mA}$ expect $0.5\text{–}0.7\text{ V}$.
- **$V_R$ (reverse voltage rating)** — confirms the device's voltage class (e.g., $1000\text{ V}$ for 1N5408).
- **$I_F(AV)$ (average forward current)** — confirms the device is the right class for the experiment.
- **Package and pinout diagram** — for correct lead identification (A vs. K) during testing.
- **$I_R$ (reverse leakage)** — the maximum permissible leakage at rated $V_R$. A leaky device may still show "OL" on a DMM (DMM sensitivity is $\sim 1\text{ }\mu\text{A}$), so the datasheet is the final arbiter.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. The student answers *either* full Question A *or* full Question B. Solutions must be exhaustive and methodically structured for full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Internal Choice]*

**(a)** Describe, with a neat procedure, how you will test a **power BJT (e.g., SL100, NPN)** using a digital multimeter in diode mode. Tabulate all six measurements and explain how each reading helps you diagnose the device's health. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An **IRF540N N-channel power MOSFET** is suspected of failure. Using only a digital multimeter and its datasheet, describe a **step-by-step troubleshooting procedure** to confirm whether the device is healthy, open, shorted, or gate-oxide damaged. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Power BJT Testing Procedure:** (7 marks)

The SL100 is an NPN silicon power BJT. It contains two back-to-back PN junctions: **Base-Emitter (B-E)** and **Base-Collector (B-C)**, sharing a common N-region. For an NPN device, the **P-side is the Base** and the **N-sides are the Emitter and Collector**. A healthy BJT must show:
- Both junctions behave as diodes (forward ~0.65 V, reverse OL)
- C–E blocks in both polarities (no built-in diode between C and E)

**Procedure (Set DMM to diode mode):**

| Test # | Red Lead | Black Lead | Junction Tested | Healthy Reading | Diagnosis if Reading Differs |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Base (B) | Emitter (E) | B-E forward | $0.60\text{–}0.75\text{ V}$ | OL → B-E open; 0 V → B-E short |
| 2 | Emitter (E) | Base (B) | B-E reverse | OL | < 0.5 V → B-E short; < 5 V → B-E leaky |
| 3 | Base (B) | Collector (C) | B-C forward | $0.60\text{–}0.75\text{ V}$ | OL → B-C open; 0 V → B-C short |
| 4 | Collector (C) | Base (B) | B-C reverse | OL | Same as above |
| 5 | Collector (C) | Emitter (E) | C-E (both polarities) | OL both | < 1 V → C-E short; readings depend on $V_{CEO}$ |
| 6 | Emitter (E) | Collector (C) | C-E (both polarities) | OL both | Same as above |

**Diagnostic Logic:**

- **Tests 1 & 2:** Verify the B-E junction. If *both* show OL → base lead broken internally. If *both* show 0 V → B-E shorted (most common BJT failure under SOA violation).
- **Tests 3 & 4:** Verify the B-C junction identically.
- **Tests 5 & 6:** Verify the C-E blocking capability. Any reading below several hundred mV in *either* direction indicates a C-E short — a fatal failure for a BJT used as a switch.

A device passing **all six tests** is confirmed healthy. Record all six values in the lab record with the unit (V or OL).

---

**(b) IRF540N MOSFET Troubleshooting Procedure:** (7 marks)

The IRF540N (datasheet key parameters: $V_{DS} = 100\text{ V}$, $I_D = 33\text{ A}$, $V_{GS(th)} = 2\text{–}4\text{ V}$, $R_{DS(on)} = 44\text{ m}\Omega$ at $V_{GS} = 10\text{ V}$) has three terminals: **Gate, Drain, Source**. The DMM-based troubleshooting sequence is:

**Step 1 — Static Discharge:** Briefly short Gate to Source with a wire to remove any residual static charge from the gate oxide. This prevents false readings due to latched-on state from handling.

**Step 2 — Gate Insulation Test:** Set DMM to diode mode. Measure between G and S in both polarities. **Expected: OL both ways.** A reading below $1\text{ V}$ in *either* direction means the **gate oxide is ruptured** — the device is permanently damaged and must be discarded.

**Step 3 — Body Diode Test:** Red lead on **Source**, black lead on **Drain**. **Expected: $0.4\text{–}0.9\text{ V}$** — the intrinsic body diode's forward drop. A reading of $0\text{ V}$ indicates a **shorted D-S** (catastrophic failure); a reading of OL in this direction is unusual and suggests an open or a damaged die.

**Step 4 — Reverse Body-Diode Test:** Red lead on **Drain**, black lead on **Source**. **Expected: OL** (the body diode is reverse-biased).

**Step 5 — Dynamic Turn-On Test (the definitive test):**
- Place red lead on **Drain**, black lead on **Source**. Confirm initial reading is **OL**.
- While holding the leads in place, **briefly touch the Gate terminal with the red (Drain) lead** of the DMM. The DMM's internal source ($2.5\text{–}3\text{ V}$) charges $C_{iss} \approx 1.96\text{ nF}$ past $V_{GS(th)} \approx 3.5\text{ V}$, turning the channel ON.
- **Expected:** The D-S reading **drops** to a low value ($0.02\text{–}0.5\text{ V}$, representing $I_{test} \cdot R_{DS(on)}$).
- **Remove the gate lead.** The reading should **stay low** (the gate capacitance holds the charge). If it snaps back to OL immediately, the gate oxide is leaky — replace the device.
- **Short G–S to discharge.** Reading returns to OL.

**Step 6 — Cross-Verification with Datasheet:**
- Compute $R_{DS(on)} = V_{meas} / I_{test}$. Example: $0.054\text{ V} / 2\text{ mA} = 27\text{ m}\Omega$.
- Compare with datasheet (44 mΩ at $V_{GS} = 10\text{ V}$, but at $V_{GS} \approx 3\text{ V}$ the value is higher, so the measured *lower* value is acceptable).
- **Conclusion:** PASS (healthy) / FAIL (open or short) / Borderline (gate oxide degradation).

A complete record includes: all five measured values, computed $R_{DS(on)}$, datasheet reference, and a one-line conclusion. This is the **standard format** that KTU external examiners look for in the preliminary work section of the lab record.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Internal Choice]*

**(a)** With the help of a clear **datasheet reference table** and **pinout diagram description**, explain how you will conduct preliminary testing of a **gate driver IC (TLP250)** before using it in a MOSFET / IGBT driver circuit. What specific failure modes must the preliminary test catch? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two students in the lab each test an LM741 op-amp. **Student A** measures output = $+13.2\text{ V}$ when both inputs are grounded and supply is $\pm 15\text{ V}$. **Student B** measures output = $-14.5\text{ V}$ under the same conditions. **Identify which device is healthy, which is faulty, and justify your conclusion** with reference to op-amp theory and datasheet parameters. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) TLP250 Gate Driver Preliminary Test:** (7 marks)

The TLP250 is an 8-pin optically-coupled gate driver with the following pinout:

| Pin | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | N.C. | No connection |
| 2 | $V_{CC}$ | Logic-side supply (+5 V) |
| 3 | GND | Logic-side ground |
| 4 | $V_O$ | Gate drive output (0 to $V_{CC2}$) |
| 5 | $V_{O}$ | Gate drive output (internally tied to pin 4) |
| 6 | $V_{CC2}$ | Power-side supply (+15 V for MOSFET/IGBT gate) |
| 7 | N.C. | No connection |
| 8 | $V_{IN}$ | Logic PWM input |

**Preliminary Test Procedure:**

**Step 1 — Unpowered Resistance Checks (DMM in Ohms mode):**
- Pin 2 ($V_{CC}$) to Pin 3 (GND): should be **OL**. A low reading here means the **internal logic regulator is shorted** — the IC is destroyed.
- Pin 6 ($V_{CC2}$) to Pin 4/5 (output): should be **OL**. A low reading means the **output buffer is shorted to supply** — this would instantly destroy the MOSFET/IGBT gate.
- Pin 4 to Pin 3 (output to logic GND): should be **OL** (when unpowered). Any reading below $1\text{ M}\Omega$ indicates **leaky output stage**.

**Step 2 — Powered Functional Test:**
- Apply $V_{CC} = +5\text{ V}$ (pin 2 to pin 3), $V_{CC2} = +15\text{ V}$ (pin 6 to pin 3 — note: TLP250 uses *same* ground reference).
- Apply a **1 kHz square wave, 0–5 V, 50 % duty cycle** to pin 8 ($V_{IN}$).
- Observe pin 4 (output) on an **oscilloscope**.
- **Expected:** 0–15 V square wave in phase with input, with $t_{PLH} \approx 0.5\text{ }\mu\text{s}$ and $t_{PHL} \approx 0.5\text{ }\mu\text{s}$.
- **Measure propagation delay** between input edge (pin 8) and output edge (pin 4). Compare with datasheet.

**Step 3 — Static Output Check:**
- Set $V_{IN} = 0\text{ V}$ (low). Output should sit at **0 V** (low state).
- Set $V_{IN} = 5\text{ V}$ (high). Output should sit at **$\approx V_{CC2} = 15\text{ V}$** (high state).
- If output is **stuck low** regardless of input → **input LED is burned out** (TLP250 uses an internal opto-LED for isolation; this is the most common failure).
- If output is **stuck high** → output buffer is shorted.

**Specific Failure Modes the Test Must Catch:**

1. **Logic-supply short** (pin 2 to 3) — would pull down the microcontroller's +5 V rail and cause a system reset.
2. **Output-to-supply short** (pin 4 to 6) — would connect the +15 V gate-drive rail directly to the MOSFET gate, exceeding $V_{GS(max)} = \pm 20\text{ V}$ and rupturing the gate oxide.
3. **Output-to-ground short** (pin 4 to 3) — MOSFET gate would never see the drive voltage; circuit would not switch.
4. **Open input LED** (pin 8 open internally) — common with aging or ESD damage; output would be permanently off.
5. **Excessive propagation delay** (e.g., $> 2\text{ }\mu\text{s}$ instead of 0.5 µs datasheet) — indicates degraded photo-coupler, which would cause shoot-through in a half-bridge.

A clean preliminary test on the TLP250 therefore requires *all five* resistance checks *and* a powered functional test. Record all values and a one-line PASS/FAIL.

---

**(b) LM741 Op-Amp Output Interpretation:** (7 marks)

**Background theory:** The output of a real op-amp with both inputs grounded is given by:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot (V_{OS} + I_{OS} \cdot R_{source})$$

where $A_{OL}$ is the open-loop gain ($\approx 200{,}000$ for LM741), $V_{OS}$ is the input offset voltage (typical 2 mV, max 6 mV for LM741), and $I_{OS}$ is the input offset current (typical 20 nA for LM741).

In practice, even a $V_{OS}$ of just a few mV, when multiplied by $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$, gives a theoretical output of hundreds of volts — which is impossible because the output cannot exceed the supply rails. The op-amp therefore **saturates** at one of the rails.

**Analysis of Student A's reading ($V_{out} = +13.2\text{ V}$):**
- Supply: $\pm 15\text{ V}$, so output saturation high is $\approx +V_{CC} - 2\text{ V} = +13\text{ V}$ to $+13.5\text{ V}$ (LM741 cannot swing to the rail).
- A reading of $+13.2\text{ V}$ is **exactly the expected positive saturation** with $V_{OS} > 0$ (i.e., the non-inverting input is effectively at a slightly higher potential than the inverting input).
- **Conclusion: Student A's LM741 is HEALTHY.** The output is at the high saturation rail because the (slightly positive) input offset has been amplified by the enormous open-loop gain.

**Analysis of Student B's reading ($V_{out} = -14.5\text{ V}$):**
- Negative saturation of LM741 is typically $\approx -V_{EE} + 2\text{ V} = -13\text{ V}$ to $-13.5\text{ V}$.
- A reading of $-14.5\text{ V}$ is **closer to the negative rail than the datasheet's guaranteed swing** (which is typically $\pm 13\text{ V}$ min, $\pm 14\text{ V}$ typ). However, $-14.5\text{ V}$ is still within the supply range of $-15\text{ V}$.
- A more likely explanation: Student B's $V_{OS}$ is **negative** (i.e., the inverting input is effectively higher than the non-inverting), driving the output to the negative rail. This is still a **healthy** op-amp — it is *normal* for a real op-amp to sit at either rail when both inputs are grounded.
- **Alternative diagnosis:** If the reading were exactly $-15\text{ V}$ (at the rail), it could indicate a **shorted output stage**, but $-14.5\text{ V}$ is within the normal swing range.

**Final Verdict:** **Both op-amps are HEALTHY.** Student A's device has a small positive $V_{OS}$; Student B's device has a small negative $V_{OS}$. Both op-amps are operating correctly in open-loop saturation. In a closed-loop configuration with feedback, the output would be brought to a controlled, predictable value.

**KTU Pitfall Note:** Many students incorrectly diagnose *either* rail saturation as a "faulty op-amp." The correct interpretation is: in **open-loop**, *any* non-zero $V_{OS}$ will saturate the output. The device is faulty only if the output does *not* respond at all to changes in input polarity (e.g., it stays at $+13\text{ V}$ even when the inputs are deliberately forced to make $V^- > V^+$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks in Preliminary Work questions:**
>
> 1. **Skipping the "expected reading" step.** Students jump straight to the measurement without stating the datasheet's expected value. Examiners explicitly look for the *expected vs. measured comparison*. **Always include both.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to discharge the MOSFET gate.** A MOSFET can latch ON from static charge; failing to short G–S at the start of the test gives false readings. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this omission.
>
> 3. **Calling rail saturation in an op-amp a "fault."** As explained in Question B(b), saturation in open-loop is *normal*. Marking it as a fault loses 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the polarity of DMM leads in diode mode.** A common error is to test an NPN BJT with the leads reversed and then claim "the B-E junction is shorted." The leads must be applied according to the junction's forward polarity.
>
> 5. **Using the wrong DMM mode.** Using ohms mode (which sources different current) for diode testing gives misleading forward-drop readings. Always explicitly state the DMM mode in the procedure.
>
> 6. **Omitting the datasheet reference.** Preliminary work without datasheet citation is considered "incomplete" and is penalized. **Always cite the datasheet page number or part number.**
>
> 7. **Not testing both polarities of every junction.** A device with one shorted junction and one open junction can pass a single-polarity test. Both directions must be measured.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Use this as the last-page summary before walking into the lab / exam hall.**

- ✅ **Power Diode:** Forward drop $0.5\text{–}1.2\text{ V}$ (Si), $0.3\text{–}0.5\text{ V}$ (Schottky); reverse = OL. Failure: 90 % short.
- ✅ **Power BJT (NPN):** Two diode tests (B-E and B-C), one C-E blocking test. Six DMM readings total. Healthy device: each junction is diode-like, C-E blocks both ways.
- ✅ **Power MOSFET:** Three tests — gate insulation, body diode, dynamic turn-on. **The dynamic test (touch gate, D-S drops low) is the gold-standard test.**
- ✅ **IGBT:** Like a MOSFET on the gate side, like a BJT on the collector side. Many (not all) have a co-packaged anti-parallel diode — always check the datasheet.
- ✅ **Op-Amp (LM741):** Open-loop gain $\approx 2 \times 10^5$. With both inputs grounded, output **always saturates at one rail** — this is *normal*, not a fault. Saturation level = $\pm V_{CC} - 2\text{ V}$ (LM741 cannot reach the rail).
- ✅ **Gate Driver (TLP250, IR2110):** Most catastrophic failures are shorts between output and supply/ground. Check pin 2–3, pin 4–6, pin 4–3 with DMM in ohms mode *before* applying power. *An opto-LED failure in TLP250 is the most common fault — input stuck low means LED is open.*
- ✅ **Datasheet First, DMM Second.** Always read the datasheet *before* testing. It tells you the expected band, the pinout, and the test conditions.
- ✅ **Static Discharge is Critical for MOSFETs and IGBTs.** Always short G–S before handling or testing.
- ✅ **DMM in Diode Mode is the Primary Tool.** Set the DMM to diode mode (▶|), not ohms mode, for semiconductor testing.
- ✅ **Document Everything.** Record: (a) DMM mode, (b) lead polarity, (c) expected value from datasheet, (d) measured value, (e) status (PASS/FAIL/Borderline), (f) one-line conclusion.
- ✅ **Common Failure Signatures to Memorize:**
  - **Shorted diode/BJT junction:** $0.000\text{ V}$ in both directions
  - **Open junction / broken lead:** OL in both directions
  - **Gate oxide rupture (MOSFET/IGBT):** DMM shows conduction between G and S
  - **Driver output short:** Low resistance between driver output pin and supply/ground
  - **Blown opto-LED in TLP250:** Input signal present, but output remains permanently low
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of Power Electronic Circuits Using SPICE-Based Software (LTSpice, ORCAD, PSpice, Proteus)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOL507 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Simulation of Power Electronic Circuits Using SPICE-Based Software (LTSpice, ORCAD, PSpice, Proteus) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **SPICE (Simulation Program with Integrated Circuit Emphasis)** is a general-purpose, open-source analog electronic circuit simulator developed at the **Electronics Research Laboratory, University of California, Berkeley (1973)**. It is used to numerically solve the coupled nonlinear differential equations governing the time-domain (transient), frequency-domain (AC), and DC operating-point behaviour of electronic circuits. In the context of **Power Electronics Lab (PCEOL507)**, SPICE-based simulation refers to the use of commercial derivatives of the original Berkeley SPICE engine — namely **LTspice (Linear Technology/Analog Devices)**, **OrCAD PSpice (Cadence Design Systems)**, **PSpice A/D**, and **Proteus VSM (Labcenter Electronics)** — to model, simulate, and analyse power electronic converter circuits containing semiconductor switches (SCR, MOSFET, IGBT, GTO), passive energy-storage elements, gate-drive circuitry, and load networks. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition Block:** > *Power electronic circuit simulation* is the computational modelling of switched-mode power converters using numerical integration algorithms (Trapezoidal / Gear's method) to predict steady-state and dynamic waveforms prior to hardware implementation, thereby reducing prototyping cost, design cycle time, and component damage risk. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation Imagine you are an **architect designing a 30-storey skyscraper** in Kerala during monsoon season. Before laying even a single brick, you build a **scale model** inside a wind tunnel. You simulate typhoons, floods, and earthquakes on this model to see where the structure will fail. SPICE does **exactly the same thing** for power electronic circuits: - The **circuit schematic** = the architectural blueprint. - The **SPICE netlist** = the structural formula and material database. - The **simulation engine** = the wind tunnel. - The **waveform output (V/I vs t)** = the stress report on every joint and beam. - The **MOSFET/SCR model** = the load-bearing column properties. Instead of blowing up a **₹50,000 IGBT** by miscalculating the gate resistance or the snubber value, you "blow it up" virtually on a laptop first. The **key insight** is that SPICE does not solve the circuit *symbolically*; it solves it *numerically* at thousands of time-steps, integrating Kirchhoff's laws (KVL + KCL) frame by frame. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Constants & Default SPICE Parameters (KTU Highlight)** > - **Default Temperature:** $T = 27^{\circ}\text{C}$ ($\mathbf{300\,K}$) > - **Default Convergence Tolerance:** $\text{ABSTOL} = 1\,\text{pA}$, $\text{VNTOL} = 1\,\mu\text{V}$, $\text{RELTOL} = 0.001 \, (\mathbf{0.1\%})$ > - **Default Internal Step Ceiling:** $\text{ITL4} = 10\,\text{(transient iterations per timepoint)}$ > - **Ideal Switch ON Resistance:** $R_{on} = 1\,\text{m}\Omega$ (default), $R_{off} = 1\,\text{G}\Omega$ > - **Standard Solver:** Modified Nodal Analysis (MNA) + Newton-Raphson iteration ### 1.3 Taxonomy of SPICE Engines Used in KTU Labs | Tool | Vendor | Engine Origin | Best Suited For | KTU Lab Status | |------|--------|---------------|-----------------|----------------| | **LTspice XVII** | Analog Devices | Berkeley SPICE 3F5 + LT enhancements | Switched-mode power supplies, SMPS | Most common | | **OrCAD PSpice** | Cadence Design Systems | Commercial SPICE | Industrial-grade converter design | Industry standard | | **Proteus VSM** | Labcenter Electronics | Mixed-mode SPICE + MCU co-sim | AC-DC-DC with embedded control | Academic standard | | **PSpice A/D** | Cadence (TI variant) | SPICE 3F5 | Analog + digital mixed-signal | Used in PG labs | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of SPICE Solvers The SPICE simulator applies **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** and **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** to every node, then converts the resulting time-domain equations into a **system of nonlinear Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs)**. For a simple **RLC branch** with a switching device: $$v_L(t) + v_R(t) + v_C(t) = v_{in}(t)$$ $$L\frac{di(t)}{dt} + Ri(t) + \frac{1}{C}\int i(t)\,dt = v_{in}(t)$$ SPICE discretises this continuous ODE using **numerical integration methods**: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two Principal Integration Methods in SPICE:** > 1. **Trapezoidal Rule (Default):** Stable, second-order accurate, but susceptible to numerical *ringing* in stiff circuits. > 2. **Gear's Method (Order 2-6):** A-stable, suppresses ringing, used when `MAXSTEP` is small or circuits are highly stiff. The discretised equation produces an algebraic system which is linearised using **Newton-Raphson iteration** at each time step $t_n$: $$x_{n+1} = x_n - J^{-1}(x_n)\cdot F(x_n)$$ where $J(x)$ is the **Jacobian matrix** of the circuit and $F(x)$ is the MNA residual vector. ### 2.2 SPICE Netlist Syntax Architecture A SPICE netlist is a **plain-text description** of the circuit topology, model parameters, and simulation directives. The general hierarchy is: ``` * TITLE / COMMENT LINE .SUBCKT / .MODEL DECLARATIONS X1 n1 n2 n3 SubcktName <-- Subcircuit instance R1 n1 n2 <-- Passive components C1 n2 0 <-- Capacitor with initial condition L1 n3 n4 <-- Inductor with initial current D1 n4 n5 DMOD <-- Diode instance M1 n5 n6 n7 n8 NMOS <-- MOSFET (4-terminal) V1 n9 0 SIN(0 10 1k) <-- Independent voltage source .TRAN 10u 10m 0 10u <-- Transient analysis command .PROBE <-- Save all node voltages .END <-- Mandatory end-of-file ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Rule:** In SPICE netlist syntax, the **first node is the positive terminal** for passive elements (`R`, `L`, `C`) and **the order matters** for controlled sources (e.g., `E1 n+ n- nc+ nc- `). Losing 1-2 marks in lab viva by writing incorrect node order is a common KTU board pitfall. ### 2.3 Types of Analyses Available | Directive | Function | Typical Use in Power Electronics | |-----------|----------|----------------------------------| | `.OP` | DC Operating Point | Bias point of converter, gate threshold verification | | `.DC` | DC Sweep | I-V characteristics of MOSFET, BJT, Diode | | `.TRAN` | Transient Analysis | Switching waveform, inrush current, soft-start | | `.AC` | Small-Signal AC | Closed-loop converter Bode plot (crossover freq.) | | `.FOUR` | Fourier Decomposition | THD calculation of inverter output | | `.STEP` | Parametric Sweep | Sensitivity to tolerance, MOSFET family curves | | `.MC` | Monte Carlo | Worst-case stress analysis on electrolytic caps | | `.NOISE` | Noise Analysis | EMI filter design, output ripple spectral density | | `.SENS` | Sensitivity | Component tolerance impact on output voltage | | `.TF` | Transfer Function | Small-signal input/output gain | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Newton-Raphson Iteration | $x_{n+1} = x_n - J^{-1}(x_n)\,F(x_n)$ | V, A | Nonlinear device convergence | | 2 | Trapezoidal Integration | $v_{n+1} = \frac{2}{h}(x_{n+1} - x_n) - v_n$ | V | Inductor companion model | | 3 | Gear-2 Integration | $v_{n+1} = \frac{3}{2h}(x_{n+1} - \frac{4}{3}x_n + \frac{1}{3}x_{n-1})$ | V | Stiff-circuit companion model | | 4 | Diode Current (Shockley) | $I_D = I_S\!\left(e^{\frac{V_D}{nV_T}} - 1\right)$ | A | Diode model core | | 5 | MOSFET Triode Current | $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\!\left[(V_{GS}-V_{th})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$ | A | Level-1 MOSFET model | | 6 | MOSFET Saturation | $I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2$ | A | Power switch ON-state | | 7 | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = \frac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{25.85\,mV}$ at $300\,K$ | V | All pn-junction models | | 8 | Output Voltage (Buck) | $V_{out} = D \cdot V_{in}$ | V | Step-down converter | | 9 | Output Voltage (Boost) | $V_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{1-D}$ | V | Step-up converter | | 10 | Output Voltage (Buck-Boost) | $V_{out} = -V_{in}\frac{D}{1-D}$ | V | Inverting converter | | 11 | RMS Output (Full-Bridge Inverter) | $V_{o,rms} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi}V_{DC}\cos\!\alpha$ | V | Phase-controlled output | | 12 | THD (Inverter) | $\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty}V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | % | Fourier quality metric | | 13 | Continuous Conduction (Buck) | $L_{min} = \frac{(1-D)R}{2f_s}$ | H | Inductor design boundary | | 14 | Capacitor Ripple (Buck) | $\Delta V_{out} = \frac{V_{out}(1-D)}{8 L C f_s^2}$ | V | Output filter design | | 15 | Convergence Residual | $\|F(x)\|_2 < \text{VNTOL} + \text{RELTOL}\cdot\|x\|$ | V/A | Newton termination | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In industry, SPICE simulation is the **mandatory first step** before any PCB fabrication for power electronics. Engineers at **Texas Instruments**, **Infineon**, and **STMicroelectronics** use PSpice/LTspice to: 1. **Validate thermal runaway** in IGBT modules. 2. **Tune PID compensators** for closed-loop DC-DC converters. 3. **Predict EMI signatures** of MOSFET switching nodes. 4. **Worst-case stress analysis** using `.STEP` + `.MC` combinations. 5. **Generate design reports** that satisfy IEC 60950 / IEC 62368 safety standards. > [!NOTE] > In the **KTU 2024 Power Electronics Lab**, Experiment 2 specifically trains you to **simulate a converter before implementing it on hardware**, allowing direct comparison between simulated and experimental waveforms in the lab record. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Discretisation of an RL Branch (Trapezoidal Rule) Consider an inductor $L$ in series with resistor $R$ connected to a voltage source $V_s$. We need to derive the **SPICE companion model** used internally during transient analysis. **Step 1 — Write the governing ODE:** By KVL around the loop: $$v_L(t) + v_R(t) = v_s(t)$$ $$L\frac{di(t)}{dt} + R\,i(t) = v_s(t)$$ **Step 2 — Apply Trapezoidal Rule for the derivative:** The trapezoidal rule approximates the integral as the average of forward and backward rectangles. SPICE inverts this to approximate the derivative: $$\frac{di}{dt} \approx \frac{i_{n+1} - i_n}{\Delta t}$$ is the simple Euler form, but SPICE uses the trapezoidal form which is **equivalent to integrating then differentiating**: $$L\int_{t_n}^{t_{n+1}}\frac{di}{dt}\,dt = \frac{L}{\Delta t}(i_{n+1} - i_n) = \frac{v_L(t_{n+1}) + v_L(t_n)}{2}\cdot\Delta t$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange to companion model:** Multiplying through and isolating $i_{n+1}$: $$\frac{2L}{\Delta t}\,i_{n+1} - v_L(t_{n+1}) = \frac{2L}{\Delta t}\,i_n + v_L(t_n)$$ This produces the famous **Norton equivalent companion circuit**: $$\boxed{\,i_{n+1} = \frac{v_L(t_{n+1})}{R_{eq,L}} + I_{eq,L}\,}$$ where the **equivalent resistance** and **history current source** are: $$R_{eq,L} = \frac{2L}{\Delta t} \qquad \text{and} \qquad I_{eq,L} = i_n + \frac{v_L(t_n)}{R_{eq,L}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** SPICE replaces every inductor with a **resistor $R_{eq,L}$ in parallel with a current source $I_{eq,L}$** whose value depends on the *previous* time-step. This is why transient analysis is **time-marching** — every step depends on the previous state. ### 3.2 Derivation: Diode SPICE Companion Model For a diode governed by Shockley's equation $I_D = I_S(e^{V_D/nV_T} - 1)$, we linearise around the operating point $(V_{D0}, I_{D0})$ using a Taylor expansion: **Step 1 — Take the derivative of $I_D$ with respect to $V_D$:** $$\frac{dI_D}{dV_D} = \frac{I_S}{nV_T}\,e^{V_D/nV_T} = \frac{I_D + I_S}{nV_T}$$ **Step 2 — Linearise at the bias point:** $$I_D \approx I_{D0} + \underbrace{\frac{I_{D0} + I_S}{nV_T}}_{1/r_d}\,(V_D - V_{D0})$$ **Step 3 — Express as Norton companion:** $$I_D = G_d\,V_D + I_{eq,D}$$ where the **incremental conductance** is: $$G_d = \frac{I_{D0} + I_S}{nV_T} = \frac{1}{r_d}$$ and the **history source** is: $$I_{eq,D} = I_{D0} - G_d\,V_{D0}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Point:** At room temperature with $I_{D0} \gg I_S$, the dynamic resistance simplifies to: > $$r_d = \frac{nV_T}{I_{D0}} \approx \frac{0.026}{I_{D0}}\,[\Omega]$$ > This is why **current carrying diodes have very low dynamic resistance** — the SPICE solver exploits this for fast convergence. ### 3.3 Derivation: Buck Converter Output Voltage A synchronous buck converter is one of the most common circuits simulated in PSpice/LTspice. We derive the **DC transfer ratio** which the simulator will converge to during the steady-state portion of `.TRAN`. **Step 1 — Volt-Second Balance on Inductor:** Under steady-state, the average voltage across $L$ over one switching period $T_s$ must be zero (otherwise the inductor current would diverge). $$\langle v_L(t)\rangle = \frac{1}{T_s}\!\int_0^{T_s}\! v_L(t)\,dt = 0$$ **Step 2 — Substitute piecewise waveform:** During the **ON interval** $0 \le t < DT_s$, switch is closed and $v_L = V_{in} - V_{out}$. During the **OFF interval** $DT_s \le t < T_s$, switch is open and $v_L = -V_{out}$. $$\frac{1}{T_s}\!\left[D T_s(V_{in} - V_{out}) + (1-D)T_s(-V_{out})\right] = 0$$ **Step 3 — Simplify:** $$D(V_{in} - V_{out}) - (1-D)V_{out} = 0$$ $$D\,V_{in} - D\,V_{out} - V_{out} + D\,V_{out} = 0$$ $$\boxed{\,V_{out} = D\,V_{in}\,}$$ where $D$ is the **duty ratio** ($0 < D < 1$). ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Converter Models Verified by SPICE | Converter | Switch ON State | Switch OFF State | Steady-State $V_{out}$ | SPICE Validation Method | |-----------|-----------------|-------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------| | **Buck** | $V_L = V_{in} - V_{out}$ | $V_L = -V_{out}$ | $D\cdot V_{in}$ | `.TRAN` + `.MEAS AVG V(out)` | | **Boost** | $V_L = V_{in}$ | $V_L = V_{in} - V_{out}$ | $\dfrac{V_{in}}{1-D}$ | `.TRAN` + `.STEP PARAM D` | | **Buck-Boost** | $V_L = V_{in}$ | $V_L = -V_{out}$ | $-\dfrac{D}{1-D}V_{in}$ | `.TRAN` + initial-condition set | | **Cuk** | $V_L_1 = V_{in}$ | $V_L_1 = V_C_1 - V_{in}$ | $-\dfrac{D}{1-D}V_{in}$ | Requires `.IC` on coupling cap | | **SEPIC** | $V_L_1 = V_{in}$ | $V_L_1 = V_{in} - V_{C_1}$ | $\dfrac{D}{1-D}V_{in}$ | Non-inverting, needs `C` middle node | ### 3.5 Practical Convergence Tips (Analytical Reasoning) > [!NOTE] > **Why SPICE Fails to Converge — Root Causes:** > 1. **Zero-resistance loops** with an ideal voltage source → bypass with $R_{min} = 1\,\text{m}\Omega$ in series. > 2. **Floating inductor/capacitor nodes** → add `.IC` (initial condition) statement. > 3. **Inductors in parallel with capacitors** with ideal switches → use `Rser` and `Rpar` damping. > 4. **Diode bridge with no source impedance** → add `RSER` to diode model. > 5. **PWM pulse source with zero rise/fall time** → set `TRISE`/`TFALL` to $\ge 10\,\text{ns}$. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 SPICE Simulation Workflow (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[KTU Power Electronics Lab
Experiment Requirement] --> B[Schematic Capture
OrCAD / LTspice / Proteus] B --> C[Netlist Generation
.cir or .net file] C --> D[SPICE Engine
Berkeley 3F5 Core] D --> E{Analysis Type} E --> F[.TRAN
Transient Solver] E --> G[.DC
Operating Point] E --> H[.AC
Small Signal] F --> I[Newton-Raphson
Iteration] G --> I H --> I I --> J{Converged?} J -->|No| K[Adjust MAXSTEP
Add ITL4 100
Fix Topology] K --> D J -->|Yes| L[Waveform Output
Probe Window] L --> M[.MEAS / .FOUR
Post-Processing] M --> N[Lab Record
Comparison with Hardware] N --> O[Viva Voce
Examiner Evaluation] ``` ### 4.2 Buck Converter Schematic in Netlist Form (Mermaid Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR VIN["Vin DC Source
24 V"] --Switch SW1 closed during DT --> N1 N1["Switching Node
V_x"] --> L1["Inductor L
100 µH"] L1 --> N2["Output Node
V_out"] N2 --> C1["Capacitor C
47 µF"] N2 --> RLOAD["Load R
10 Ω"] N2 -.-> D1["Freewheel Diode
D to GND"] D1 --> GND["Ground Node
0 V reference"] N1 -.Gate drive.-> PWM["PWM Source
VPULSE 20kHz D=0.4"] ``` ### 4.3 Tool Comparison Architecture (Block-Level Functional Mapping) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LT["LTspice XVII"] LT1[Schematic Editor] --> LT2[SPICE 3F5 Engine] LT2 --> LT3[Waveform Viewer
.raw file] end subgraph OR["OrCAD PSpice"] OR1[Capture CIS] --> OR2[PSpice A/D
Analog/Digital] OR2 --> OR3[Probe Waveform
.dat output] end subgraph PR["Proteus VSM"] PR1[ISIS Schematic] --> PR2[ProSPICE
Mixed-Signal] PR2 --> PR3[Virtual
Oscilloscope] end subgraph CO["Common Layer"] CO1[KCL/KVL Solver] CO2[Newton-Raphson] CO3[Modified Nodal Analysis] end LT --> CO OR --> CO PR --> CO ``` ### 4.4 Convergence Diagnosis Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Simulation Failed] --> Q1{Error Type?} Q1 -->|singular matrix| A1[Add .IC statement
Initial conditions] Q1 -->|timestep too small| A2[Increase MAXSTEP
or relax TRTOL] Q1 -->|no convergence in DC| A3[Add NODESET hints
or RSER to sources] Q1 -->|internal timestep too small| A4[Add ITL4=500
+ Gear method] A1 --> FIX[Re-run Simulation] A2 --> FIX A3 --> FIX A4 --> FIX FIX --> END[Waveforms OK] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | 3 Marks]** > **Q1.** *List any three commercially available SPICE-based simulation tools used in power electronics design and state one distinguishing feature of each.* **Model Answer (3 Key Points × 1 Mark):** 1. **LTspice XVII** (Analog Devices) — Free, with built-in library of Analog Devices' power MOSFETs, op-amps, and SMPS ICs; ideal for switch-mode power supply design. *(1 mark)* 2. **OrCAD PSpice** (Cadence) — Industry standard with advanced model libraries (Infineon, TI, ON Semi); supports Monte Carlo, sensitivity, and worst-case analyses natively. *(1 mark)* 3. **Proteus VSM** (Labcenter Electronics) — Combined SPICE + microcontroller co-simulation; allows a closed-loop digitally controlled converter to be simulated with embedded firmware. *(1 mark)* --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | 3 Marks]** > **Q2.** *Differentiate between `.TRAN` and `.AC` analyses in SPICE. State one typical use of each in power electronics.* **Model Answer:** - **`.TRAN` (Transient Analysis):** Performs time-domain numerical integration of circuit ODEs using trapezoidal or Gear's method. Typical use: Observing **switching waveforms** of a MOSFET in a buck converter, e.g., drain-to-source voltage ringing at turn-off. *(1.5 marks)* - **`.AC` (Small-Signal AC Analysis):** Linearises the circuit around the DC operating point and computes the frequency-domain response (Bode plot). Typical use: Plotting the **loop gain and phase margin** of a closed-loop DC-DC converter's feedback compensator. *(1.5 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | 14 Marks | Module 2]** > **Question A:** > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a SPICE-based simulator. List the sequence of operations performed by the SPICE engine from netlist parsing to waveform generation. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** Derive the SPICE companion model of an inductor using the **trapezoidal integration rule**. Hence obtain the expression for its Norton equivalent resistance and history current source. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Question A **Part (a) — SPICE Architecture (7 Marks):** The SPICE simulator consists of **five functional blocks**: 1. **Netlist Parser / Pre-processor (2 Marks):** Reads the `.cir` netlist, validates syntax, expands `.SUBCKT` and `.MODEL` libraries, and constructs the internal connectivity graph. Identifies all `n+ n- ` triplets for `R`, `L`, `C`, etc. 2. **Matrix Builder — Modified Nodal Analysis (MNA) (2 Marks):** Writes KCL equations at each node (excluding the ground node, which is the reference). For voltage sources and inductors, additional auxiliary current variables are appended to the matrix. The result is a **sparse matrix** of size $N \times N$ where $N$ is (nodes + voltage sources). 3. **Nonlinear Device Model Evaluator (1 Mark):** For each Newton iteration, evaluates the diode, BJT, and MOSFET model equations and their derivatives (Jacobian entries). E.g., for a diode: $I_D = I_S(e^{V_D/nV_T} - 1)$ and $dI_D/dV_D = (I_D+I_S)/nV_T$. 4. **Newton-Raphson Solver (1 Mark):** Iteratively solves $J(x_k)\Delta x = -F(x_k)$ until the residual norm satisfies `ABSTOL`, `VNTOL`, `RELTOL` criteria. 5. **Output Processor / Waveform Writer (1 Mark):** Records selected node voltages and branch currents into `.raw` binary file; allows post-processing via `.MEAS`, `.FOUR`, and the Probe viewer. **Part (b) — Inductor Companion Model Derivation (7 Marks):** For an inductor with voltage $v_L(t) = L \frac{di}{dt}$: Applying the **trapezoidal rule** to integrate over one time-step $\Delta t$: $$\int_{t_n}^{t_{n+1}} v_L(t)\,dt = L[i(t_{n+1}) - i(t_n)]$$ $$L[i_{n+1} - i_n] = \frac{\Delta t}{2}(v_{L,n+1} + v_{L,n})$$ Rearranging: $$i_{n+1} = \frac{v_{L,n+1}}{(2L/\Delta t)} + \left[i_n + \frac{v_{L,n}}{(2L/\Delta t)}\right]$$ Identifying Norton parameters: $$\boxed{\,G_{eq,L} = \frac{\Delta t}{2L} \quad \text{and} \quad I_{eq,L} = i_n + G_{eq,L}\cdot v_{L,n}\,}$$ This Norton equivalent (conductance $G_{eq,L}$ in parallel with current source $I_{eq,L}$) is what SPICE actually solves at each step. *(3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for box, 2 marks for interpretation of history source.)* --- > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | 14 Marks | Module 2]** > **Question B (Alternative for Internal Choice):** > **(a)** Write a complete SPICE netlist to simulate a **24 V to 12 V buck converter** with switching frequency $f_s = 20\,\text{kHz}$, duty ratio $D = 0.5$, $L = 100\,\mu H$, $C = 47\,\mu F$, and load $R = 10\,\Omega$. Use a MOSFET switch and freewheeling diode. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** Explain any **four common causes of SPICE non-convergence** in power electronic circuit simulation and state the remedial directive for each. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Question B **Part (a) — Buck Converter SPICE Netlist (7 Marks):** ```spice * BUCK CONVERTER - KTU LAB EXPERIMENT 2 * Vin = 24V, Vout = 12V (D=0.5), fs = 20kHz VIN 1 0 DC 24V VS 9 0 PULSE(0 15 0 10ns 10ns 25u 50u) ; Gate drive M1 1 9 2 2 IRF540N ; MOSFET switch D1 0 2 D1N4148 ; Freewheel diode L1 2 3 100u IC=0A ; Filter inductor C1 3 0 47u IC=0V ; Filter capacitor RLOAD 3 0 10 ; Load resistor .MODEL IRF540N VDMOS(Ron=50m Rdrain=0.05 Rsource=0.05 Vto=4 Kp=2.5 Cgdmax=1n Cgs=1n Cjo=1n) .MODEL D1N4148 D(Is=2.68n Rs=0.5 N=1.7 Cjo=4p M=0.333 Vj=0.7 BV=100 IBV=5u) .TRAN 1u 5m 3m 1u UIC ; Start from 3ms to skip startup .PROBE .MEAS VOUT_AVG AVG V(3) FROM=3m TO=5m .END ``` *Mark Distribution (7 Marks):* - Correct title, voltage source, and PWM gate drive — 2 marks - MOSFET and diode declaration with `.MODEL` — 2 marks - `L`, `C`, `RLOAD` with correct node ordering — 2 marks - Correct `.TRAN` + `.MEAS` + `.END` directives — 1 mark **Part (b) — Four Causes of Non-Convergence (7 Marks = 4 × 1.75):** 1. **Zero-resistance voltage source loop** — Direct connection of `V1` and `L1` with no series resistance causes a *singular matrix*. **Fix:** Add `RSER=1m` to the source, or insert a $1\,\text{m}\Omega$ resistor in series. 2. **Floating inductor/capacitor node** — When a switch toggles abruptly, the solver has no DC path to determine initial voltage/current. **Fix:** Use `.IC V(node)=initial` and `.TRAN ... UIC`. 3. **Ideal diode model with zero series resistance** — Causes Newton-Raphson oscillation between ON/OFF states. **Fix:** Add `RS=0.1` to the `.MODEL D` line. 4. **PWM source with zero rise time** — Causes infinite $dv/dt$ and forces the timestep to $dt\to 0$. **Fix:** Specify `TRISE` and `TFALL` of $\ge 10\,\text{ns}$ in the `PULSE` statement, or use `SINE` with soft-start. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > - ❌ **Reversing node polarity** in the netlist (e.g., `D1 2 0` instead of `D1 0 2`) — the diode will be reverse-biased permanently; output stays at 0 V. **Examiner deducts 2 marks.** > - ❌ Forgetting the `.MODEL` line for the diode/MOSFET — the simulator uses default values and gives misleading waveforms. **Examiner deducts 1.5 marks.** > - ❌ Using `.TRAN 1u 5m` without `UIC` and without `.IC` — simulation takes too long or fails. **Examiner deducts 1 mark.** > - ❌ Confusing `.PROBE` (raw output) with `.PRINT` (tabular output) — wrong format expected in lab record. **Examiner deducts 0.5 marks.** > - ❌ Failing to state **steady-state vs. transient region** in waveform interpretation. Always write *"After $5\tau$, where $\tau = RC$, the circuit reaches steady state."* **Examiner expects this phrase.** --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2, Experiment 2** > - ✅ SPICE = **Simulation Program with Integrated Circuit Emphasis**, originated at UC Berkeley in 1973. > - ✅ The four major SPICE tools in KTU labs: **LTspice, OrCAD PSpice, PSpice A/D, Proteus VSM**. > - ✅ SPICE solves **coupled nonlinear ODEs** using **Newton-Raphson iteration** + **Trapezoidal / Gear's integration**. > - ✅ Every inductor becomes a **Norton equivalent** at each timestep: $G_{eq,L} = \Delta t / 2L$. > - ✅ Every capacitor becomes a **Thevenin equivalent**: $R_{eq,C} = \Delta t / 2C$. > - ✅ Diode dynamic resistance at operating point: $r_d \approx 26\,\text{mV}/I_{D0}\,[\Omega]$. > - ✅ `.TRAN` = time-domain switching waveforms; `.AC` = Bode plot; `.DC` = bias; `.FOUR` = THD. > - ✅ Convergence fixes: `.IC`, `UIC`, `RSER`, `MAXSTEP`, `ITL4=500`, `TRISE`. > - ✅ **Buck:** $V_{out} = D\cdot V_{in}$; **Boost:** $V_{out} = V_{in}/(1-D)$; **Buck-Boost:** $V_{out} = -D\cdot V_{in}/(1-D)$. > - ✅ Default `V_T = 25.85\,\text{mV}$ at $300\,\text{K}$ — temperature affects every pn-junction model. > - ✅ Always end the netlist with `.END` — missing this is the #1 reason for "no input file" errors in KTU labs. > - ✅ Always compare **simulated waveform** with **hardware oscilloscope waveform** in the lab record for full marks. **Suggested Viva Questions (KTU Lab External):** 1. *"What happens to simulation accuracy if you set `MAXSTEP` too small?"* → Increased runtime, no accuracy improvement past numerical precision. 2. *"Why does a buck converter need a `.IC` statement?"* → To initialise the inductor current and capacitor voltage; otherwise solver guesses 0 and takes long transient. 3. *"How do you compute THD from `.FOUR`?"* → `THD = sqrt(sum of harmonic powers) / fundamental * 100%`. 4. *"Which integration method is used for stiff circuits?"* → Gear's method (higher-order A-stable).

topics.title: Static VI Characteristics of Power Devices (Diode, SCR, MOSFET, IGBT) – Simulation and Comparison with Datasheet Values
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL507 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Static VI Characteristics of Power Devices (Diode, SCR, MOSFET, IGBT) – Simulation and Comparison with Datasheet Values

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Static V-I Characteristics

**Static V-I Characteristics** of a power semiconductor device describe the steady-state (DC) relationship between the voltage across its terminals and the current flowing through it, with all transients, switching transients, and dynamic effects neglected. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme Power Electronics Lab, this experiment focuses on plotting the forward and reverse conduction/blocking characteristics of the four foundational power devices: **Power Diode**, **Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR)**, **Power MOSFET**, and **Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor (IGBT)**, and then comparing the simulated values against the manufacturer's datasheet ratings.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The word "static" here strictly means *DC steady-state*. Students must NOT confuse this with dynamic or switching characteristics such as reverse recovery time ($t_{rr}$), turn-on time ($t_{on}$), or turn-off time ($t_{off}$), which are covered in later experiments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" View

Imagine each power device as a **smart water tap** controlling the flow of current (water) when a voltage (water pressure) is applied:

| Device | Analogy | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Diode** | A one-way valve | Opens automatically when pressure exceeds ~0.7 V; closes when pressure reverses |
| **SCR** | A latchable one-way tap with a trigger button | Stays closed until a small trigger pulse is applied, then latches fully ON even after the trigger is removed |
| **Power MOSFET** | A voltage-controlled smooth tap | The harder you turn the knob (gate voltage), the more current flows — fully controllable, no latching |
| **IGBT** | A hybrid tap — voltage knob with high current capacity | Combines the easy voltage control of a MOSFET tap with the heavy current handling of a BJT tap |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The static V-I curve is essentially a **graphical "fingerprint"** of each device. Just as a doctor checks a patient's ECG, an electronics engineer checks a device's V-I curve to ensure it is operating within safe limits specified in the datasheet.

### 1.3 Why Comparison with Datasheet Matters

Manufacturers publish characteristic curves in datasheets measured under *standard conditions* (typically $T_j = 25^\circ C$ unless specified). Real lab simulation may show slight deviations due to:
- Model parameter tolerances in SPICE
- Thermal effects during prolonged measurement
- Approximations used in component models (e.g., ideal switches vs. piecewise-linear models)

The aim of this experiment is to **validate the simulation model** by matching it against datasheet values such as forward voltage drop ($V_F$), on-state resistance ($R_{DS(on)}$), collector-emitter saturation voltage ($V_{CE(sat)}$), breakover voltage ($V_{BO}$), latching current ($I_L$), and holding current ($I_H$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Power Diode — Static V-I Behaviour

The static V-I characteristic of a power diode is governed by the **Shockley diode equation**:

$$I_D = I_S \left( e^{\frac{V_D}{n V_T}} - 1 \right)$$

where $I_S$ is the reverse saturation current, $n$ is the ideality factor (typically 1 to 2 for power diodes), and $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85 \text{ mV}$ at room temperature.

**Operational regions:**
- **Forward Bias Region ($V_D > 0$):** Exponential rise. Once $V_D$ exceeds the **knee voltage** ($V_K \approx 0.7$ V for Si, $\approx 1.5$ V for SiC), the diode conducts heavily.
- **Reverse Bias Region ($V_D < 0$):** A small reverse saturation current $I_S$ ($\mu$A to mA range) flows until the **breakdown voltage** $V_{BR}$ is reached.
- **Breakdown Region ($V_D \leq -V_{BR}$):** Avalanche/Zener breakdown; current rises sharply.

### 2.2 SCR — Static V-I Behaviour

An SCR has **three operating modes** visible in its static characteristic:

1. **Forward Blocking Mode:** Anode positive, cathode negative, gate open. Only a small forward leakage current $I_{DRM}$ flows until $V_{AK} = V_{BO}$ (breakover voltage).
2. **Forward Conduction Mode:** Once triggered (by gate current $I_G \geq I_{GT}$ OR by $V_{AK} \geq V_{BO}$), the device turns ON and operates in the low-voltage region with on-state voltage $V_T$.
3. **Reverse Blocking Mode:** Anode negative, cathode positive. Behaves like a reverse-biased diode; small $I_{RRM}$ flows until $V_{BR(R)}$ is reached.

**Two critical currents:**
- **Latching Current ($I_L$):** The minimum anode current that must flow *immediately after* the gate pulse is removed to keep the SCR ON.
- **Holding Current ($I_H$):** The minimum anode current required to *maintain* the SCR in the ON state. Always $I_H < I_L$.

### 2.3 Power MOSFET — Static V-I Behaviour
