The power MOSFET is a **voltage-controlled majority carrier device**. Its output characteristic is plotted as $I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$ for various values of $V_{GS}$.

**Three regions of operation:**

$$I_D = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } V_{GS} < V_{th} \quad \text{(Cutoff)} \\ \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right] & \text{if } V_{GS} > V_{th},\ V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{th} \quad \text{(Triode/Ohmic)} \\ \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS}) & \text{if } V_{GS} > V_{th},\ V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th} \quad \text{(Saturation)} \end{cases}$$

For power electronics switching applications, the MOSFET is operated in the **Ohmic (Triode) region** where it acts as a resistance $R_{DS(on)}$.

### 2.4 IGBT — Static V-I Behaviour

The IGBT exhibits an **output characteristic similar to a BJT** but is controlled by a **MOS gate**. Its key static parameter is the **collector-emitter saturation voltage $V_{CE(sat)}$**.

The on-state voltage drop in the saturation region is modelled as:

$$V_{CE(sat)} = V_{BE(on)} + I_C \cdot R_{channel}$$

where $V_{BE(on)}$ is the internal MOSFET-BJT interface drop and $R_{channel}$ is the conducting channel resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The IGBT is operated in the **saturation region** for switching applications (ON state) and in the **cutoff region** for the OFF state. Unlike MOSFET, it does NOT enter active region during normal operation.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Device | Key Static Parameters | Governing Equation | Typical Value Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power Diode** | $V_F$, $I_R$, $V_{BR}$ | $I_D = I_S \left( e^{V_D / (n V_T)} - 1 \right)$ | $V_F = 0.7 - 3.3$ V |
| **SCR** | $V_{BO}$, $I_L$, $I_H$, $I_{GT}$, $V_T$ | Triggering: $I_G \geq I_{GT}$ and $I_A \geq I_L$ | $I_L \approx 10 - 100$ mA, $V_{BO} \approx 200 - 6500$ V |
| **Power MOSFET** | $R_{DS(on)}$, $V_{th}$, $V_{DS(max)}$ | Ohmic: $V_{DS} = I_D \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ | $R_{DS(on)} \approx 1 \text{ m}\Omega - 10 \ \Omega$ |
| **IGBT** | $V_{CE(sat)}$, $V_{GE(th)}$, $I_C$ | Saturation: $V_{CE(sat)} = V_{BE(on)} + I_C R_{channel}$ | $V_{CE(sat)} = 1.5 - 3.0$ V |

> [!NOTE]
> In all of the above, always remember the thermal derating rule: parameter values from the datasheet are quoted at $T_j = 25^\circ C$ unless explicitly stated. As junction temperature rises, $V_F$ (diode), $R_{DS(on)}$ (MOSFET), and $V_{CE(sat)}$ (IGBT) **decrease**, while leakage currents **increase**.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding static V-I characteristics is essential for:
- **Thermal Design:** Calculating conduction losses $P_{cond} = V \cdot I$ or $I^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$.
- **Device Selection:** Choosing between MOSFET (low voltage, high frequency) and IGBT (medium-high voltage, low frequency) for inverter/converter design.
- **Protection Circuit Design:** Setting snubber ratings based on breakdown voltages.
- **Reliability Prediction:** Verifying that operating points stay within the **SOA (Safe Operating Area)** of the device.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Shockley Diode Equation (Static V-I)

Starting from the semiconductor physics of the pn-junction depletion region, applying the **Boltzmann statistics** to the carriers crossing the junction:

**Step 1:** The total current density $J$ across the depletion region is the sum of hole diffusion current and electron diffusion current:

$$J = J_p + J_n$$

**Step 2:** Under equilibrium ($V = 0$), the net current is zero. When a forward bias $V$ is applied, the barrier potential reduces from $V_{bi}$ to $V_{bi} - V$, and the carrier injection at the edges increases exponentially.

**Step 3:** Integrating the minority carrier diffusion equations at the depletion edges gives:

$$\begin{aligned}
J_p &= \frac{q D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} \left( e^{V / V_T} - 1 \right) \\
J_n &= \frac{q D_n n_{p0}}{L_n} \left( e^{V / V_T} - 1 \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Adding both components and multiplying by the cross-sectional area $A$:

$$I_D = I_S \left( e^{V_D / (n V_T)} - 1 \right)$$

where the saturation current is:

$$I_S = qA \left( \frac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \frac{D_n n_{p0}}{L_n} \right)$$

**Step 5:** At room temperature ($T = 300$ K), the thermal voltage evaluates to:

$$V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 25.85 \text{ mV}$$

This explains why a forward voltage of about **0.7 V** is sufficient for a silicon diode — it represents roughly $\frac{0.7}{0.02585} \approx 27 V_T$, producing an enormous exponential current.

### 3.2 Derivation of SCR Latching Condition

The SCR latches when the anode current $I_A$ exceeds a critical value that maintains the internal regenerative feedback between the two transistor analogues (npn + pnp).

**Step 1:** The two-transistor model of the SCR gives common-base current gains $\alpha_1$ (npn) and $\alpha_2$ (pnp). The anode current satisfies:

$$I_A = \frac{\alpha_2 I_G + I_{CBO1} + I_{CBO2}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

**Step 2:** The condition for turn-on (positive denominator instability) is:

$$\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$$

**Step 3:** Since $\alpha$ is a function of emitter (anode) current, define $I_L$ as the anode current at which $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$. Then:

$$I_A \geq I_L \Rightarrow \text{SCR latches ON}$$

**Step 4:** Once the gate pulse is removed ($I_G = 0$), the condition becomes:

$$\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1 \Rightarrow I_A \geq I_H < I_L$$

Thus mathematically, $I_H < I_L$, confirming the experimental observation.

### 3.3 Derivation of MOSFET On-State Resistance (Static)

For a power MOSFET in the **ohmic (triode) region**, the channel behaves like a voltage-controlled resistor.

**Step 1:** Start from the long-channel equation:

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$$

**Step 2:** For small $V_{DS}$ (the regime of static ON-state operation), neglect the $V_{DS}^2$ term:

$$I_D \approx \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th}) V_{DS}$$

**Step 3:** Define the on-state resistance as the small-signal ratio:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{DS(on)} &= \left. \frac{V_{DS}}{I_D} \right\vert_{V_{GS} = \text{const}} \\
&= \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Since $V_{GS} \gg V_{th}$ during ON state, $R_{DS(on)}$ becomes small — typically a few milliohms for high-current power MOSFETs.

### 3.4 Derivation of IGBT Saturation Voltage

The IGBT consists of a **MOSFET driving a BJT** internally. In saturation, both devices are ON.

**Step 1:** The collector current flows through the MOSFET channel first, then through the BJT's base-emitter junction:

$$I_C = I_{MOSFET} = \frac{V_{GE} - V_{GE(th)}}{R_{channel}}$$

**Step 2:** The total collector-emitter voltage is the sum of two drops:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{CE(sat)} &= V_{channel} + V_{BE(ON)} \\
&= I_C \cdot R_{channel} + V_{BE(ON)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Unlike a MOSFET (where $V_{DS(sat)} \to 0$ as $R_{DS(on)} \to 0$), the IGBT always has a minimum drop of approximately $V_{BE(ON)} \approx 0.7$ V — this is the *price* paid for its high current capability.

### 3.5 Comparison Matrix — All Four Devices at a Glance

| Parameter | Power Diode | SCR | Power MOSFET | IGBT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Control** | Uncontrolled | Current-triggered ($I_G$) | Voltage-triggered ($V_{GS}$) | Voltage-triggered ($V_{GE}$) |
| **Turn-off** | Natural commutation only | Forced or natural | Gate-controlled | Gate-controlled |
| **Static on-state drop** | $V_F \approx 0.7 - 1.5$ V | $V_T \approx 1 - 2$ V | $I^2 R_{DS(on)}$ | $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1.5 - 3$ V |
| **Conduction loss** | Lowest | Moderate | Low at low V | Low at high V |
| **Bidirectional blocking?** | No (only reverse blocking) | Yes (forward + reverse) | No (body diode only) | No (no reverse blocking) |
| **Typical application** | Rectifier, freewheeling | AC-DC converter, cyclo | SMPS, low-V inverter | Medium-V inverter, motor drive |
| **Symbol in datasheet** | $V_F$, $I_R$ | $V_{DRM}$, $V_{RRM}$, $I_{GT}$ | $R_{DS(on)}$, $V_{th}$ | $V_{CE(sat)}$, $V_{GE(th)}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Simulation & Datasheet Comparison Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete experimental workflow** for obtaining static V-I characteristics and validating them against datasheet values.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Target Device] --> B{Choose Device Type}
    B --> D1[Diode e.g. 1N4007]
    B --> D2[SCR e.g. TYN612]
    B --> D3[MOSFET e.g. IRF540N]
    B --> D4[IGBT e.g. IRG4BC40W]
    D1 --> E1[Build Test Circuit in LTspice or PSIM]
    D2 --> E2[SCR Test Circuit with Gate Pulse Source]
    D3 --> E3[MOSFET Test Circuit with Variable VGS]
    D4 --> E4[IGBT Test Circuit with Variable VGE]
    E1 --> F[Apply DC Sweep Analysis]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Plot Forward and Reverse Characteristics]
    G --> H[Extract Key Parameters from Curve]
    H --> I1[Diode: VF, IR, VBR]
    H --> I2[SCR: VBO, IL, IH, IGT]
    H --> I3[MOSFET: RDS on, Vth]
    H --> I4[IGBT: VCE sat, VGE th]
    I1 --> J[Compare with Manufacturer Datasheet]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    J --> K{Match within 5 to 10 percent?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Model Validated: Record Results]
    K -->|No| M[Adjust Model Parameters and Re simulate]
    M --> F
    L --> N[End of Experiment]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an IGBT Static Test Bench

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        VCC[Variable DC Source VCC 0 to 15 V]
        VGE[Variable Gate Drive VGE 0 to 20 V]
    end
    subgraph DEVICE[Device Under Test DUT]
        IGBT[IGBT Module]
    end
    subgraph MEASURE[Measurement Stage]
        VM[Voltmeter VCE]
        IM[Ammeter IC]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage]
        OSC[Oscilloscope or Plotter]
        DATA[Datasheet Reference Table]
    end
    VCC --> IGBT
    VGE --> IGBT
    IGBT --> VM
    IGBT --> IM
    VM --> OSC
    IM --> OSC
    OSC --> DATA
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Characteristic Curve Identification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Begin Curve Analysis] --> Q{Identify the Quadrant}
    Q -->|Q1 Forward| R[Examine Knee and Slope]
    Q -->|Q3 Reverse| S[Examine Leakage and Breakdown]
    R --> R1{Linear or Exponential?}
    R1 -->|Exponential| R2[Diode or IGBT VCE sat region]
    R1 -->|Linear| R3[MOSFET Ohmic region]
    S --> S1{Sharp avalanche?}
    S1 -->|Yes| S2[Identify VBR or VBO]
    S1 -->|No| S3[Reverse blocking region of SCR or MOSFET body diode]
    R2 --> T[Record Key Parameter]
    R3 --> T
    S2 --> T
    S3 --> T
    T --> U[Cross Check with Datasheet Page Reference]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers above use alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `R1`, `S2`, `DUT`) to comply with the KTU-Premier-Engine V10 reserved-keyword avoidance rule. Labels contain only clean uppercase text without bold or italics markers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**
Define *latching current* and *holding current* of an SCR. Why is $I_L$ always greater than $I_H$?

**Model Answer:**
- **Latching current ($I_L$):** It is the minimum anode current that must flow through the SCR at the instant the gate pulse is removed, in order to ensure the device remains in the ON state.
- **Holding current ($I_H$):** It is the minimum anode current required to *maintain* the SCR in the ON state once it is already conducting.
- **Reason $I_L > I_H$:** During turn-on, the regenerative feedback between the internal npn and pnp transistor analogues has not yet fully built up, so a larger current is needed to sustain the condition $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$. Once the SCR is fully ON, the alphas are well-saturated, so the device can be sustained by a smaller current $I_H$. Mathematically, $I_H$ is measured on the *falling* part of the anode current when the SCR finally drops out of conduction, whereas $I_L$ is measured at the *rising* triggering point.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**
What is meant by $R_{DS(on)}$ of a power MOSFET? Why is this parameter critical for switching converter design?

**Model Answer:**
- **$R_{DS(on)}$** is the static drain-to-source on-state resistance of the MOSFET when the gate is fully driven ($V_{GS} \gg V_{th}$) and the device is operating in the ohmic (triode) region.
- **Importance:** The conduction loss in a power MOSFET is given by $P_{cond} = I_D^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$. A lower $R_{DS(on)}$ means lower conduction loss, higher efficiency, and less heat dissipation. For example, the IRF540N has $R_{DS(on)} = 44 \text{ m}\Omega$, which determines its suitability for switch-mode power supplies and motor drives.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer **ONE** of the two alternatives below. Each alternative carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A — Alternative 1 (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat labelled circuit diagram and characteristic curves, explain the procedure for obtaining the **static forward V-I characteristics of an SCR** using a simulation tool (LTspice/PSIM). List the key parameters you would extract from the curve and compare each with datasheet values of a typical SCR (e.g., TYN612 or BT151).

**Model Solution:**

1. **Circuit Setup:**
   - Place an SCR model (e.g., `2N6394` or `TYN612`) in series with a variable DC voltage source $V_s$ (range 0–600 V) and a resistive load $R_L$ (e.g., 1 k$\Omega$).
   - Connect a gate pulse source between Gate and Cathode (typical $I_G = 10–50$ mA pulse of width 50 $\mu$s).
   - Insert a voltmeter across anode–cathode and an ammeter in the anode line.

2. **Simulation Commands:**
   - Perform a **DC sweep** of $V_s$ from 0 V to $V_{DRM}$ in steps of 10 V.
   - For each sweep point, apply the gate pulse and record $(V_{AK}, I_A)$.

3. **Curve Plotting:**
   - Plot $I_A$ on the Y-axis (linear scale, mA to A) versus $V_{AK}$ on the X-axis.
   - Mark the three regions: forward blocking, forward conduction, and reverse blocking.

4. **Key Parameters Extracted:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Simulation Value (TYN612 example) | Datasheet Value | % Deviation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Breakover voltage | $V_{BO}$ | ~605 V | 600 V | ~0.8% |
| On-state voltage | $V_T$ | 1.6 V | 1.6 V max | < 1% |
| Latching current | $I_L$ | 30 mA | 40 mA max | ~25% (model dependent) |
| Holding current | $I_H$ | 8 mA | 15 mA max | ~46% (model dep.) |

5. **Conclusion:** A reasonable match (within 10%) for $V_{BO}$ and $V_T$ validates the simulation. Larger deviations in $I_L$ and $I_H$ are expected because SPICE models often use simplified alpha curves.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the static V-I characteristics of a **power MOSFET and an IGBT**. Mention typical datasheet values for IRF540N (MOSFET) and IRG4BC40W (IGBT) and explain why IGBTs are preferred for high-voltage (> 600 V) applications while MOSFETs dominate below 200 V.

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | IRF540N (MOSFET) | IRG4BC40W (IGBT) |
|---|---|---|
| Rated voltage | $V_{DS} = 100$ V | $V_{CE} = 600$ V |
| Static ON-state loss mechanism | $I^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ | $V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C$ |
| Typical ON-state value | $R_{DS(on)} = 44 \text{ m}\Omega$ | $V_{CE(sat)} = 2.0$ V |
| Conduction loss at 10 A | $10^2 \cdot 0.044 = 4.4$ W | $2.0 \cdot 10 = 20$ W |
| Conduction loss at 50 A | $50^2 \cdot 0.044 = 110$ W (excessive) | $2.0 \cdot 50 = 100$ W (acceptable) |

**Why MOSFETs lose at high voltage:**
For high-voltage MOSFETs, the **epitaxial drift region** must be thick and lightly doped to support the high $V_{DS}$. This dramatically increases $R_{DS(on)}$, making conduction losses prohibitive. As a rough rule of thumb:

$$R_{DS(on)} \propto V_{DS(rated)}^{2.5}$$

**Why IGBTs win at high voltage:**
The IGBT uses **conductivity modulation** — minority carriers flood the drift region, reducing its effective resistance by orders of magnitude. The on-state drop becomes a near-constant $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1.5–3$ V, independent of rated voltage. Hence IGBTs are dominant in traction drives, UPS systems, and HVDC converters above 600 V.

---

#### **Question B — Alternative 2 (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the static V-I characteristic of a **power diode**. Label the forward conduction region, reverse blocking region, and breakdown region. Explain how you would extract the **forward voltage drop $V_F$** and **reverse leakage current $I_R$** from a simulated curve and verify against the 1N4007 datasheet.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Circuit:** Series combination of the 1N4007 SPICE model, a variable DC source, a 10 $\Omega$ current-sense resistor, and an ammeter.
2. **DC Sweep:** Forward sweep from 0 to 1.5 V; reverse sweep from 0 to –1000 V.
3. **From Curve:**
   - **Forward voltage drop $V_F$:** Locate the point where $I_F = 1$ A (the datasheet test condition). Read $V_F$ from the X-axis. Typical datasheet: $V_F = 1.0$ V at 1 A.
   - **Reverse leakage current $I_R$:** At $V_R = 1000$ V (maximum rated), read the Y-axis value. Datasheet: $I_R = 5 \ \mu$A typical, 50 $\mu$A max at 25°C.

4. **Verification Table:**

| Parameter | Simulation | Datasheet (1N4007) | Tolerance Check |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_F$ at 1 A | 0.93 V | 1.0 V max | ✓ within limit |
| $I_R$ at 1000 V | 8 $\mu$A | 50 $\mu$A max | ✓ well within |

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a circuit block diagram, describe the **complete experimental procedure** to obtain the static V-I characteristic of an **IGBT** in simulation. List the datasheet parameters to be compared and tabulate the result.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Circuit Description:**
   - Variable DC source $V_{CC}$ (0–600 V) connected to the collector.
   - Variable gate drive $V_{GE}$ (0–20 V) connected between gate and emitter.
   - Load resistor $R_C$ (e.g., 10 $\Omega$, high-wattage) between emitter and ground.
   - Voltmeter across C–E; ammeter in collector line.

2. **Procedure:**
   - Fix $V_{GE}$ at 15 V (well above $V_{GE(th)} = 5.5$ V).
   - Sweep $V_{CC}$ from 0 to 20 V (in saturation region).
   - Record $(V_{CE}, I_C)$ pairs.
   - Repeat for $V_{GE} = 10$ V and $V_{GE} = 12$ V to obtain a family of curves.

3. **Extracted Parameters (IRG4BC40W):**

| Parameter | Simulation | Datasheet | Match |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{GE(th)}$ | 5.4 V | 4.5 – 6.0 V | ✓ |
| $V_{CE(sat)}$ at 20 A | 2.05 V | 2.0 V typ, 2.5 V max | ✓ |
| Collector cutoff current $I_{CES}$ at 600 V | 1.8 mA | 2.0 mA max | ✓ |

4. **Conclusion:** The simulation matches the IRG4BC40W datasheet within acceptable engineering tolerance, validating the IGBT SPICE model.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing static vs. dynamic:** Writing $t_{rr}$, $t_{on}$, $t_{off}$ in static V-I answers costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Writing "$V_F = 1$" without "Volts" is treated as incomplete.
> 3. **Missing the body diode of MOSFET:** When drawing the third quadrant, students often forget the intrinsic body diode, leading to a half-mark deduction.
> 4. **No reference conditions:** Stating "1N4007 has $V_F = 1$ V" is incomplete; always specify "at $I_F = 1$ A and $T_j = 25^\circ$C".
> 5. **Mixing SCR and TRIAC symbols:** The SCR is unidirectional; drawing reverse conduction in quadrant III is an error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Power Diode:** Uncontrolled, one-way conduction. Static curve follows the Shockley equation. Datasheet key values: $V_F$, $I_R$, $V_{BR}$.
- **SCR:** Latchable, current-triggered. Three modes: forward blocking, conduction, reverse blocking. Critical currents $I_L > I_H$.
- **Power MOSFET:** Voltage-controlled, majority carrier, no tail current. Used in **ohmic region** for switching. Conduction loss $= I_D^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$.
- **IGBT:** Voltage-controlled, minority carrier (conductivity modulated). Output characteristic resembles a BJT. Always operates in **saturation region** when ON.
- **MOSFET vs. IGBT Rule of Thumb:** MOSFET for $< 200$ V and high switching frequency; IGBT for $> 600$ V and moderate frequency.
- **Datasheet Comparison Discipline:** Always quote values with three conditions — the parameter name, the test condition (current/voltage), and the temperature ($T_j$).
- **Simulation Tool Selection:** LTspice (free, ideal for MOSFET and diode) and PSIM (preferred for power electronics including IGBT and SCR).
- **Accuracy Expectation:** Datasheet-matching within $\pm 10\%$ is considered excellent; within $\pm 25\%$ is acceptable for educational simulations.
- **Static ≠ Dynamic:** Reverse recovery, turn-on/off times, and switching losses belong to a *different* experiment — never mix them in static V-I questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Frequency Diode: Measurement of Power Loss and Reverse Recovery Time, Comparison with Theoretical Estimates and Schottky Diode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL507 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** High Frequency Diode: Measurement of Power Loss and Reverse Recovery Time, Comparison with Theoretical Estimates and Schottky Diode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **High Frequency Diode** is a fast-recovery semiconductor device specifically engineered to operate efficiently in switching power electronic circuits where the switching frequency typically ranges from **1 kHz to several hundred kHz**. In the KTU 2024 PCEOL507 syllabus, the experiment aims to practically evaluate the dynamic and static characteristics of such diodes by:

1. **Measuring the Power Loss** ($P_{loss}$) — which includes both conduction loss and switching loss.
2. **Measuring the Reverse Recovery Time** ($t_{rr}$) — the transient duration required for the diode to switch from ON-state to OFF-state.
3. **Comparing practical results with theoretical estimates** derived from manufacturer datasheet parameters.
4. **Comparing the high-frequency PN-junction diode with a Schottky diode** to highlight the fundamental trade-off between switching speed and forward voltage drop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The diode under test (DUT) is generally a **fast-recovery epitaxial diode (FRED)** such as **MUR460, BYV27, or UF4007**, which is contrasted against a **Schottky diode** (e.g., **MBR2045 or 1N5819**) used in low-voltage, ultra-high-frequency applications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **busy one-way automatic toll-booth gate** on a highway:

- When a car (current) approaches from the correct side, the gate opens instantly — this is the **forward conduction** state.
- However, when a car tries to enter from the wrong side, the gate does not slam shut immediately. First, it pushes the last car that was halfway through back to the correct side, and **only after that car is fully cleared** does the gate lock. The duration of this "pushing back" process is the **Reverse Recovery Time ($t_{rr}$)**.
- The **energy wasted** in pushing that car back, multiplied by the number of times the gate operates per second, is the **Reverse Recovery Loss ($P_{sw}$)**.
- A **Schottky diode**, in this analogy, is a toll-booth with **no car halfway through** — it is a single-piece metal gate that snaps shut almost instantaneously. Hence, its reverse recovery time is **negligible (in nanoseconds)**, but the gate itself is slightly heavier to lift (higher forward drop for low-voltage types).

> [!NOTE]
> **Reverse Recovery Time ($t_{rr}$)** is the most critical figure-of-merit for a high-frequency diode. A smaller $t_{rr}$ directly translates to lower switching losses and higher allowable switching frequency $f_s$.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in the Experiment

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reverse Recovery Time | $t_{rr}$ | 25 – 500 | ns |
| Forward Voltage Drop | $V_F$ | 0.7 – 1.5 | V |
| Reverse Recovery Charge | $Q_{rr}$ | 10 – 200 | nC |
| Maximum Reverse Voltage | $V_{RRM}$ | 50 – 1000 | V |
| Switching Frequency | $f_s$ | 1 – 100 | kHz |
| Junction Capacitance | $C_j$ | 10 – 200 | pF |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of Power Loss in a High-Frequency Diode

The total power dissipated by the diode in a switching circuit has **two main components**, each arising from a distinct physical mechanism.

#### A. Conduction Loss ($P_{cond}$)
This loss occurs because the diode is not an ideal short-circuit when forward-biased — it has a finite forward voltage drop $V_F$.

The conduction loss is given by:

$$P_{cond} = V_F \cdot I_{F(avg)}$$

In a chopper or inverter leg where the diode conducts only for a fraction of the switching cycle, the loss is duty-cycle dependent:

$$P_{cond} = V_F \cdot I_{F(avg)} \cdot D$$

where $D$ is the duty ratio of the current through the diode.

#### B. Switching Loss / Reverse Recovery Loss ($P_{sw}$)
This loss occurs **only during the turn-OFF transient**. When the diode is suddenly reverse-biased, it continues to conduct for a short time in the reverse direction, with a peak reverse recovery current $I_{RR}$. The product of the blocking voltage $V_R$ that appears across the diode and the reverse recovery current $I_{RR}$ during $t_{rr}$ results in energy dissipation.

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_R \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot f_s$$

This is the **dominant loss component at high frequencies**, and is the primary reason engineers prefer Schottky diodes for low-voltage, high-frequency applications.

#### C. Total Diode Power Loss
$$P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw}$$

### 2.2 Reverse Recovery Time — Detailed Physics

The reverse recovery transient has **two sub-intervals** that appear on an oscilloscope trace:

1. **Storage Time ($t_s$):** The period during which the excess minority carriers stored in the PN junction are being removed. The reverse current rises from zero to its peak value $I_{RR}$.
2. **Transition Time ($t_t$):** The period during which the junction capacitance charges up to the reverse blocking voltage $V_R$. The reverse current falls from $I_{RR}$ back to zero.

$$t_{rr} = t_s + t_t$$

The **reverse recovery charge** is the area under the reverse current curve:

$$Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_{rr} = t_s + t_t$ | Total reverse recovery time | ns |
| 2 | $Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr}$ | Reverse recovery charge | nC |
| 3 | $P_{cond} = V_F \cdot I_{F(avg)} \cdot D$ | Conduction (static) loss | W |
| 4 | $P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_R \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot f_s$ | Switching (dynamic) loss | W |
| 5 | $P_{sw} = V_R \cdot Q_{rr} \cdot f_s$ | Switching loss in terms of charge | W |
| 6 | $P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw}$ | Total diode power loss | W |
| 7 | $\eta_{conv} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss}}$ | Conversion efficiency (overall) | % |
| 8 | $f_{max} \approx \frac{0.25}{t_{rr}}$ | Max usable switching frequency (rule of thumb) | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition:** The switching loss formula assumes a **linear approximation** of the current and voltage waveforms during $t_{rr}$. For highly inductive loads where $di/dt$ is externally clamped, $I_{RR} \approx 2 \cdot I_F$ (a commonly used practical approximation).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Switch-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS):** Output rectifier diodes in flyback, forward, and bridge converters operate at $f_s = 50$–$500$ kHz. Diode losses directly affect the converter efficiency (which is mandated by international standards like 80 PLUS).
- **Solar PV Inverters:** High-frequency diodes used in MPPT boost converters determine the overall conversion efficiency.
- **Electric Vehicle Chargers:** Fast-recovery diodes in PFC (Power Factor Correction) stages minimize switching losses.
- **RF and Microwave Circuits:** Schottky diodes are preferred as mixers and detectors due to their negligible $t_{rr}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Reverse Recovery Loss

**Starting Premise:** During the turn-off of a diode carrying forward current $I_F$ in an inductive switching circuit, the voltage across the diode transitions from approximately $0$ to the reverse blocking voltage $V_R$ during the recovery interval $t_{rr}$.

**Step 1:** Assume the reverse current $i_R(t)$ rises linearly from $0$ to peak $I_{RR}$ during the storage time $t_s$:

$$i_R(t) = \frac{I_{RR}}{t_s} \cdot t, \quad 0 \le t \le t_s$$

**Step 2:** The charge removed during this interval is the area under the curve:

$$Q_1 = \int_0^{t_s} i_R(t) \, dt = \int_0^{t_s} \frac{I_{RR}}{t_s} \cdot t \, dt = \frac{I_{RR} \cdot t_s}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Similarly, the charge removed during the transition interval $t_t$ is:

$$Q_2 = \int_{t_s}^{t_{rr}} i_R(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_t$$

**Step 4:** Total reverse recovery charge:

$$Q_{rr} = Q_1 + Q_2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_{RR} \cdot (t_s + t_t) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr}$$

**Step 5:** The energy dissipated in the diode per switching event is the time-integral of instantaneous power $p(t) = v_D(t) \cdot i_R(t)$. With $v_D(t) \approx V_R$ during most of $t_{rr}$:

$$E_{rr} = \int_0^{t_{rr}} V_R \cdot i_R(t) \, dt = V_R \cdot Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_R \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr}$$

**Step 6:** The average power loss (switching loss) is the energy per cycle multiplied by the switching frequency:

$$P_{sw} = E_{rr} \cdot f_s = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_R \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot f_s$$

**Step 7:** Total diode loss (conduction + switching):

$$P_{total} = V_F \cdot I_{F(avg)} \cdot D + \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_R \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot f_s$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A fast-recovery diode (MUR460) is used as a freewheeling diode in a buck chopper. The input voltage is $V_s = 100$ V, the load current is $I_L = 5$ A (continuous), the duty cycle is $D = 0.4$, the switching frequency is $f_s = 50$ kHz, and the measured reverse recovery time is $t_{rr} = 60$ ns with a peak reverse recovery current $I_{RR} = 8$ A. The diode forward drop is $V_F = 1.25$ V. Calculate the total diode power loss and compare with theoretical estimate.

**Step 1 — Conduction Loss Calculation:**

The diode conducts when the chopper switch is OFF, i.e., for a duration $(1 - D) \cdot T_s$.

$$I_{F(avg)} = I_L \cdot (1 - D) = 5 \cdot (1 - 0.4) = 3.0 \text{ A}$$

$$P_{cond} = V_F \cdot I_{F(avg)} = 1.25 \cdot 3.0 = 3.75 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Switching Loss Calculation:**

The reverse voltage across the diode during turn-off is approximately equal to the source voltage $V_R = V_s = 100$ V.

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_R \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot f_s$$

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 100 \cdot 8 \cdot (60 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (50 \times 10^3)$$

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 100 \cdot 8 \cdot 60 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 50 \times 10^{3}$$

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 100 \cdot 8 \cdot 3.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 100 \cdot 0.024 = 1.20 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Total Diode Loss:**

$$P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw} = 3.75 + 1.20 = 4.95 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical Estimate from Datasheet:**

The MUR460 datasheet specifies $t_{rr} = 50$ ns (typical) at $I_F = 1$ A. Adjusting to our operating point (using $t_{rr} \propto \sqrt{I_F}$ as a first-order correction):

$$t_{rr(theory)} \approx 50 \cdot \sqrt{5/1} = 50 \cdot 2.236 = 111.8 \text{ ns}$$

$$P_{sw(theory)} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 100 \cdot 8 \cdot (111.8 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (50 \times 10^3) \approx 2.236 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{total(theory)} = 3.75 + 2.236 = 5.986 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with Schottky Diode (MBR2045, 45 V, 20 A):**

For a Schottky diode, $t_{rr} \approx 0$ (negligible, as it is a majority-carrier device), so $P_{sw} \approx 0$. However, $V_F \approx 0.57$ V at $I_F = 5$ A.

$$P_{cond(Schottky)} = 0.57 \cdot 3.0 = 1.71 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{total(Schottky)} = 1.71 + 0 = 1.71 \text{ W}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Inference:** The Schottky diode is **NOT suitable** here because its reverse voltage rating (45 V) is less than the circuit's $V_R = 100$ V. For low-voltage applications ($V_R \le 45$ V), however, the Schottky diode would have dramatically lower total loss.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — High-Frequency Diode vs. Schottky Diode

| Parameter | High-Frequency PN Diode (MUR460) | Schottky Diode (MBR2045) | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conduction Mechanism | Minority carrier | Majority carrier | Schottky has no stored charge |
| Reverse Recovery Time $t_{rr}$ | 25 – 500 ns | < 10 ns (often considered 0) | Schottky suitable for $>$100 kHz |
| Forward Voltage Drop $V_F$ | 0.9 – 1.5 V | 0.3 – 0.7 V | Schottky lower conduction loss |
| Reverse Voltage Rating $V_{RRM}$ | Up to 1000 V | Typically $\le$ 200 V | PN diode for high-voltage use |
| Leakage Current $I_R$ | Low (µA) | Higher (mA) | PN better for high-temperature |
| Switching Loss $P_{sw}$ | Significant at high $f_s$ | Negligible | Schottky preferred at high $f_s$ |
| Cost | Lower | Higher | Application-dependent |
| Typical Use | SMPS output rectifiers, inverters | Low-voltage synchronous rectifiers, RF | Trade-off based decision |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Experimental Test Setup for High-Frequency Diode Characterization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[DC Power Supply V_s] --> node2[Power Circuit Under Test]
    node3[Function Generator PWM Signal] --> node4[Gate Driver Circuit]
    node4 --> node2
    node2 --> node5[Diode Under Test DUT]
    node2 --> node6[Inductive Load R-L]
    node5 --> node7[Current Probe for I_F and I_RR]
    node2 --> node8[Voltage Probe for V_R]
    node7 --> node9[Digital Storage Oscilloscope DSO Channel 1]
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10[Measure t_rr from Waveform]
    node9 --> node11[Capture V_F and I_F for Conduction Loss]
    node10 --> node12[Calculate P_sw from Formula]
    node11 --> node13[Calculate P_cond from Formula]
    node12 --> node14[Compute P_total]
    node13 --> node14
    node14 --> node15[Compare with Theoretical Datasheet Value]
    node14 --> node16[Compare with Schottky Diode Result]
    node15 --> node17[Tabulate and Plot Loss vs Frequency]
    node16 --> node17
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Buck Chopper with Freewheeling Diode

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[DC Source V_s] --> nodeB[MOSFET Switch S]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Switching Node]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Inductor L]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Load Resistor R]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Ground Reference]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Freewheeling Diode D_UT]
    nodeG --> nodeF
    nodeH[PWM from Function Generator] --> nodeI[Gate Driver]
    nodeI --> nodeB
    nodeJ[DSO Channel A across Diode] --> nodeK[Display V_F and V_R]
    nodeL[Current Probe at Diode] --> nodeM[Display I_F and I_RR]
```

### 4.3 Waveform Reference — Reverse Recovery Transient

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[Time Axis t] --> nodeQ[Forward Current I_F Phase]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Reverse Current Rises to I_RR]
    nodeR --> nodeS[Storage Time t_s Interval]
    nodeS --> nodeT[Transition Time t_t Interval]
    nodeT --> nodeU[Reverse Current Decays to Zero]
    nodeU --> nodeV[Blocking Voltage V_R Established Across Diode]
    nodeV --> nodeW[Diode Fully OFF]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **DSO Setup Tip (Practical Lab Note):** Use the **invert function** on Channel 2 of the oscilloscope so that the reverse current appears as a positive spike above the forward current baseline. This makes the measurement of $t_{rr}$ much easier on the graticule.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define the term **reverse recovery time ($t_{rr}$)** of a power diode. Why is it a critical parameter in high-frequency switching applications?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The reverse recovery time $t_{rr}$ of a power diode is defined as the time interval between the instant at which the forward current $I_F$ passes through zero during turn-off, and the instant at which the reverse recovery current $I_{RR}$ has decayed back to 25% of its peak value (or to a specified small threshold, depending on the datasheet standard).

Mathematically:

$$t_{rr} = t_s + t_t$$

where $t_s$ is the storage time and $t_t$ is the transition time.

It is critical in high-frequency applications because the switching loss is directly proportional to $t_{rr}$:

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_R \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot f_s$$

As the switching frequency $f_s$ increases (e.g., in SMPS operating at 100 kHz), even a small $t_{rr}$ contributes significantly to the total diode loss, reducing converter efficiency. A smaller $t_{rr}$ allows higher $f_s$ operation and better efficiency.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Compare a **Schottky diode** with a **fast-recovery PN-junction diode** in terms of conduction mechanism, reverse recovery, and typical applications.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Schottky Diode | Fast-Recovery PN Diode |
|---|---|---|
| Conduction mechanism | Majority carrier (metal-semiconductor junction) | Minority carrier (PN junction) |
| Reverse recovery time | Negligible (ns-level) | 25 – 500 ns |
| Forward voltage drop $V_F$ | Low (0.3 – 0.7 V) | Higher (0.9 – 1.5 V) |
| Reverse voltage rating | Low (typically $\le$ 200 V) | High (up to 1000 V) |
| Typical applications | Low-voltage SMPS rectifiers, RF mixers, solar bypass | High-voltage SMPS, motor drives, inverters |

The Schottky diode is preferred in low-voltage, high-frequency applications due to its negligible switching loss, while the fast-recovery PN diode is used in high-voltage circuits where Schottky diodes are not available with sufficient $V_{RRM}$ rating.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram and waveforms, explain the **reverse recovery characteristics** of a high-frequency power diode. Derive the expression for the **reverse recovery charge** $Q_{rr}$. (7 Marks)

**b)** A fast-recovery diode is used in a chopper circuit. The following data is given: forward current $I_F = 10$ A, reverse voltage $V_R = 200$ V, switching frequency $f_s = 20$ kHz, measured reverse recovery time $t_{rr} = 75$ ns, peak reverse recovery current $I_{RR} = 12$ A, and forward voltage drop $V_F = 1.4$ V. The duty cycle of the chopper is $D = 0.6$. Calculate:
   (i) Conduction loss $P_{cond}$
   (ii) Switching loss $P_{sw}$
   (iii) Total diode power loss $P_{total}$
   (iv) Maximum allowable switching frequency (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part (a) — Reverse Recovery Characteristics (7 Marks):**

When a forward-conducting PN diode is suddenly reverse-biased, it does not turn off instantly. This is because excess minority carriers are stored in the P and N regions near the junction during forward conduction. The turn-off process involves two phases:

**Phase 1 — Storage Time ($t_s$):** The reverse voltage removes the excess minority carriers from the junction. During this time, the diode continues to conduct, and a reverse current $I_{RR}$ flows. The current rises from zero to a peak value $I_{RR}$.

**Phase 2 — Transition Time ($t_t$):** Once the stored charge is removed, the junction begins to support a reverse voltage. The reverse current decays from $I_{RR}$ back to zero, and the reverse voltage $V_R$ builds up across the diode.

**Derivation of $Q_{rr}$:** Assuming a linear approximation of the reverse current waveform, the reverse recovery charge is the area under the $i_R$ vs $t$ curve during $t_{rr}$:

$$Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr}$$

**Waveform sketch (verbal description for the answer script):**
- X-axis: time $t$
- Y-axis (dual trace): Diode current $i_D$ and Diode voltage $v_D$
- During $0 \le t \le t_1$: $i_D = +I_F$ (forward), $v_D = +V_F$
- At $t = t_1$: current crosses zero
- During $t_1 \le t \le t_1 + t_s$: reverse current grows to $I_{RR}$, $v_D$ still $\approx V_F$
- During $t_1 + t_s \le t \le t_1 + t_{rr}$: reverse current falls from $I_{RR}$ to 0, $v_D$ rises to $V_R$
- After $t_1 + t_{rr}$: diode fully OFF, $v_D = V_R$, $i_D = -I_R$ (leakage)

**(b) Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $I_F = 10$ A, $V_R = 200$ V, $f_s = 20$ kHz, $t_{rr} = 75$ ns, $I_{RR} = 12$ A, $V_F = 1.4$ V, $D = 0.6$.

**(i) Conduction Loss:**
The diode conducts during the OFF period of the chopper switch, i.e., for $(1 - D) = 0.4$ of the cycle.

$$I_{F(avg)} = I_F \cdot (1 - D) = 10 \cdot 0.4 = 4.0 \text{ A}$$

$$P_{cond} = V_F \cdot I_{F(avg)} = 1.4 \cdot 4.0 = 5.6 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) Switching Loss:**

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_R \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot f_s$$

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 200 \cdot 12 \cdot (75 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (20 \times 10^3)$$

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 200 \cdot 12 \cdot 75 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 20 \times 10^3$$

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 200 \cdot 12 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 200 \cdot 0.018 = 1.8 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Total Diode Power Loss:**

$$P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw} = 5.6 + 1.8 = 7.4 \text{ W}$$

**(iv) Maximum Allowable Switching Frequency:**

Using the rule of thumb that $t_{rr}$ should not exceed 25% of the switching period to avoid overlapping transients:

$$f_{max} \approx \frac{0.25}{t_{rr}} = \frac{0.25}{75 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.33 \text{ MHz}$$

(For conservative design, $f_{max} \approx 1$ MHz is recommended.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**a)** Explain with a neat sketch, the **experimental procedure to measure the reverse recovery time and power loss of a high-frequency diode** in the power electronics lab. List the instruments required. (7 Marks)

**b)** Discuss the **comparison of high-frequency PN-junction diode with a Schottky diode** in terms of construction, switching behavior, losses, voltage rating, and typical applications. Under what conditions would you prefer a Schottky diode over a fast-recovery PN diode, and vice versa? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part (a) — Experimental Procedure (7 Marks):**

**Aim:** To measure the reverse recovery time ($t_{rr}$) and total power loss ($P_{loss}$) of a high-frequency diode and to compare with theoretical estimates and a Schottky diode.

**Apparatus Required:**
- Power MOSFET/IGBT based chopper circuit kit
- High-frequency diode under test (e.g., MUR460) and Schottky diode (e.g., MBR2045)
- DC power supply (0 – 30 V or as per circuit)
- PWM function generator (1 Hz – 1 MHz)
- Gate driver circuit
- R-L load (suitable for the rating)
- Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO, 100 MHz or higher)
- Current probe (for measuring $I_F$ and $I_{RR}$)
- Voltage probe (for measuring $V_F$ and $V_R$)
- Connecting wires, breadboard, heatsink

**Circuit Diagram (Verbal Description):** A standard buck chopper circuit is used. The freewheeling diode is the device under test (DUT). An inductive load is connected to the switching node.

**Procedure:**
1. Connect the circuit as per the diagram. Mount the DUT on a heatsink.
2. Set the function generator to produce a PWM waveform with the required frequency (e.g., 20 kHz) and duty cycle (e.g., 50%).
3. Apply the gate signal through the gate driver to the MOSFET.
4. Switch on the DC supply and increase it gradually to the rated voltage.
5. Observe the current waveform (using a current probe) and voltage waveform (using a voltage probe) on the DSO across the diode.
6. Measure the **forward current $I_F$** (steady positive value), **peak reverse recovery current $I_{RR}$**, and **forward voltage drop $V_F$** from the waveform.
7. Measure the **reverse recovery time $t_{rr}$** as the time between the zero-crossing of the current and its decay to 25% of $I_{RR}$.
8. Measure the **reverse blocking voltage $V_R$** from the voltage waveform.
9. Calculate $P_{cond}$ and $P_{sw}$ using the formulas, and sum to get $P_{total}$.
10. Repeat the experiment with a **Schottky diode** under the same conditions and tabulate the results.
11. Compare the experimental values with the theoretical values obtained from the manufacturer's datasheet.

**Precautions:**
- Always use an isolated probe when measuring the switching node.
- Ensure the diode is properly heatsunk.
- Do not exceed the diode's $V_{RRM}$ or $I_{F(avg)}$ ratings.
- Use proper grounding techniques on the DSO.

**(b) Part (b) — Comparison Discussion (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Fast-Recovery PN Diode | Schottky Diode |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | PN junction with epitaxial layer | Metal-semiconductor junction |
| Conduction | Minority carrier | Majority carrier |
| Reverse recovery $t_{rr}$ | 25 – 500 ns | Negligible (< 10 ns) |
| Forward voltage $V_F$ | 0.9 – 1.5 V | 0.3 – 0.7 V |
| Reverse leakage $I_R$ | Very low (µA) | Higher (mA), increases with $V_R$ |
| Reverse voltage $V_{RRM}$ | Up to 1000 V or more | Typically $\le$ 200 V |
| Switching loss $P_{sw}$ | Significant at high $f_s$ | Almost zero |
| Temperature coefficient of $V_F$ | Negative (good for paralleling) | Positive (poor for paralleling) |
| Cost per ampere | Lower | Higher |

**When to prefer Schottky diode:**
- When the reverse voltage is low ($V_R \le 100$ V, e.g., in 12 V or 24 V SMPS output rectifiers).
- When the switching frequency is very high ($f_s > 100$ kHz), to eliminate switching loss.
- In solar PV systems as bypass diodes.
- In RF and microwave applications.

**When to prefer Fast-Recovery PN diode:**
- When the reverse voltage is high ($V_R > 200$ V, e.g., in 230 V AC mains-fed converters).
- In high-temperature environments (Schottky leakage increases with temperature).
- In high-current applications where the cost per ampere matters.
- In motor drive inverters and three-phase rectifiers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in $t_{rr}$:** A majority of students forget to convert nanoseconds to seconds before substituting into the switching loss formula. This leads to an answer that is off by a factor of $10^9$. **Always write $t_{rr}$ in seconds** in the calculation.
> 2. **Confusing $I_{RR}$ with $I_F$:** The reverse recovery current $I_{RR}$ is **NOT** equal to the forward current $I_F$. In a hard-switched inductive circuit, $I_{RR} \approx 1.5$ to $2 \times I_F$ depending on $di/dt$. Using $I_F$ in place of $I_{RR}$ underestimates the switching loss.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 0.5:** The switching loss formula has a factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ arising from the linear approximation. Skipping this factor gives a loss value that is double the correct answer.
> 4. **Schottky Diode Misapplication:** Recommending a Schottky diode for high-voltage applications is a common mistake. Always check $V_{RRM}$ first.
> 5. **Waveform Orientation on DSO:** Students often report incorrect $t_{rr}$ because they measure on the wrong edge or do not invert the current probe channel. Always confirm the current direction before measuring $t_{rr}$.
> 6. **Theoretical vs Practical Mismatch:** It is **expected** that the practical $t_{rr}$ differs from the datasheet value. The examiner expects a discussion of why — usually because datasheet conditions ($I_F$, $di/dt$, junction temperature) differ from your experimental conditions. **Do not** mark it as an "error"; instead, explain the deviation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **High-Frequency Diode Definition:** A diode with low reverse recovery time (typically $<$ 500 ns), optimized for switching applications above 1 kHz.
- **Reverse Recovery Time ($t_{rr}$):** Sum of storage time $t_s$ and transition time $t_t$. Critical figure-of-merit for switching applications.
- **Reverse Recovery Charge ($Q_{rr}$):** $Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr}$. Represents the excess minority carriers that must be removed at turn-off.
- **Two Loss Components:** Conduction loss (depends on $V_F$ and $I_F$) and switching loss (depends on $V_R$, $I_{RR}$, $t_{rr}$, and $f_s$).
- **Switching Loss Formula (MUST memorize):** $P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_R \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot f_s$
- **Total Loss:** $P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw}$
- **Schottky Diode Advantage:** Zero reverse recovery → zero switching loss → ideal for high-frequency, low-voltage applications.
- **Schottky Diode Limitation:** Low reverse voltage rating ($V_{RRM} \le 200$ V typically) and higher leakage current.
- **Comparison Rule of Thumb:** Schottky for $V_R \le 100$ V and $f_s > 100$ kHz; Fast-recovery PN for $V_R > 200$ V and high-temperature environments.
- **Experimental Setup:** A chopper circuit with the diode as the freewheeling element, R-L load, and DSO-based measurement using current and voltage probes.
- **Typical Lab Values:** $t_{rr}$ for MUR460 $\approx$ 50–100 ns, for MBR2045 $<$ 10 ns. Switching frequency in the lab: 10–50 kHz. Forward current: 2–5 A.
- **Practical vs Theoretical:** Differences are expected and should be explained using datasheet condition mismatch (junction temperature, $I_F$ value, $di/dt$).
- **Conversion Efficiency Impact:** Higher $f_s$ → smaller magnetic components but higher switching loss. Diode $t_{rr}$ is a key bottleneck.
- **Modern Trend:** Synchronous rectification (using a MOSFET in place of the diode) has replaced Schottky diodes in many high-efficiency converters (e.g., $> 95\%$ efficient laptop adapters).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single-Phase Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier Feeding R/RL Load with Line-Synchronized R and RC Firing Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL507 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Single-Phase Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier Feeding R/RL Load with Line-Synchronized R and RC Firing Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Phase Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier:** A phase-controlled power electronic converter that uses a single Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR/Thyristor) connected in series between a single-phase AC supply and the load. The rectifier allows conduction of the load current only during the positive half-cycle of the input voltage, and only after the SCR is triggered by a gate pulse at a controllable instant called the **firing angle (α)**.

**Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard):**
- **Firing Angle (α):** The angle (in electrical degrees or radians) measured from the instant the AC supply voltage crosses zero in the positive direction to the instant the SCR is gated ON.
- **Conduction Angle (θ_c):** The angular duration (in degrees) for which the SCR conducts current (θ_c = π - α for R load; θ_c = π + γ - α for RL load).
- **Extinction Angle (γ):** For RL loads, the angle at which the load current naturally falls to zero and the SCR turns OFF.
- **Line Synchronization:** The technique of deriving the firing circuit reference from the same AC mains that powers the load, ensuring that α is always measured from the same zero-crossing instant of the supply.

> [!NOTE]
> **Half-Wave Rectifier Output Frequency:** The fundamental ripple frequency of the output equals the **supply frequency (f = 50 Hz)** because only one output pulse is produced per input cycle. This is in contrast to a full-wave rectifier where the ripple frequency is **2f = 100 Hz**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a **single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier** like a **single water tap on a half-day pipeline**:

1. **The Pipeline (AC Source):** Water (electrons) flows back and forth rhythmically — forward for half the day, backward for the other half. The pipe only carries useful water during the **forward flow (positive half-cycle)**.
2. **The Hand-Operated Valve (SCR):** A valve that **stays shut by itself** even when water is flowing forward. Someone (the firing circuit) must **push the lever (gate trigger)** to open it. Once open, it only closes when the water stops pushing forward.
3. **The Scheduling Lever (Firing Angle α):** The valve-operator can wait any number of minutes (degrees) after the forward flow begins before pushing the lever. **Push early (small α) → more water passes; push late (large α) → less water passes.**
4. **The Bucket (R Load):** A simple bucket that fills whenever the valve is open and holds water until the next forward flow.
5. **The Inertial Water Wheel (L Load):** A heavy spinning wheel that keeps turning even after the forward flow stops. The water tap (SCR) stays open as long as the wheel is pushing water backward, producing a **negative voltage tail** (extinction angle γ).

**Real-world parallel:** This is exactly how a **lamp dimmer (domestic fan regulator)** works — the knob controls *how late* in each half-cycle the thyristor is allowed to pass current, smoothly varying the lamp's brightness.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Supply Frequency:** **f = 50 Hz** (India/Kerala standard) → **ω = 2πf = 314.159 rad/s**
- **Peak Supply Voltage:** **V_m = √2 × V_rms = 1.414 × V_rms** (e.g., for **230 V rms**, **V_m = 325.27 V**)
- **Maximum Average Output (R Load, α = 0):** **V_dc,max = V_m / π ≈ 0.318 V_m**
- **Thyristor Hold-on Current (typical):** **I_H ≈ 10–50 mA**
- **Latching Current (typical):** **I_L ≈ 20–100 mA**
- **Standard Range of α:** **0° ≤ α ≤ 180°** (positive half-cycle conduction)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCEOL507):** The experiment demands measurement of load voltage, load current, and firing angle for both **R load** and **RL load**, using *both* an **R-firing circuit** and an **RC-firing circuit** to demonstrate line synchronization.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Topology & Operating Principle

A single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier feeding an **R load** has the following structure:

```
AC Source (v_s = V_m sin ωt) → SCR (Anode-Cathode) → Load (R) → Back to Source Neutral
                                                              ↑
                                              Gate Pulse from Firing Circuit
                                              (delayed by α from zero-crossing)
```

**Step-by-Step Operating Logic:**

1. For **0 ≤ ωt < α**: The SCR is forward-biased (anode positive), but **no gate pulse is applied**. The SCR remains in the OFF state (blocking mode). Output voltage **v_o = 0** and load current **i_o = 0**.
2. At **ωt = α**: The firing circuit delivers a gate pulse. If **i_g > I_GT** and **v_s > 0**, the SCR latches ON.
3. For **α ≤ ωt ≤ π**: The SCR conducts. Assuming an **ideal SCR** (zero forward drop), **v_o = v_s = V_m sin ωt**, and **i_o = v_o / R = (V_m / R) sin ωt**.
4. At **ωt = π**: The supply voltage reverses polarity. The SCR becomes reverse-biased and naturally turns OFF. **i_o = 0** for the rest of the cycle.
5. For **π < ωt < 2π**: SCR is OFF; **v_o = 0** and **i_o = 0**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is conduction possible for *any* α between 0 and π?** The SCR can only be triggered while it is forward-biased. The supply voltage is positive (forward-bias condition for the SCR) from 0 to π. Hence the gate pulse is effective only in this window, giving the natural range **0° ≤ α ≤ 180°**.

## 2.2 Behavior with RL Load (The Critical Distinction)

With an inductive load, the load current **cannot change instantaneously** due to stored magnetic energy in L. This causes two important phenomena:

1. **Conduction extension beyond π:** The SCR keeps conducting past π as long as i_o > 0, because the inductor maintains current flow even when v_s becomes negative.
2. **Negative voltage region:** From ωt = π to ωt = (π + γ), the output voltage v_o is **negative** because v_s is negative but the SCR is still conducting.
3. **Current extinction at γ:** The current naturally falls to zero at ωt = π + γ, at which point the SCR turns OFF (assuming i_o < I_H).
4. **Discontinuous conduction:** The SCR remains OFF until the next gate pulse arrives at (2π + α), so the load voltage and current are discontinuous.

**Extinction angle γ is found from the transcendental equation:**

$$\sin(\alpha + \gamma) = \sin(\alpha) \cdot e^{-\gamma/\omega\tau}$$

where **τ = L / R** is the load time constant and **ωτ** is the normalized time constant (called the *load angle* or *extinction parameter*).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet for KTU University Exam (ESE) & Lab Viva:** All formulas below are required to solve both numerical and analytical questions on this experiment.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|----------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | Source Voltage (instantaneous) | $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ | $V_m$ in Volts, $\omega t$ in rad |
| 2 | Average Output Voltage (R Load) | $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | Volts |
| 3 | RMS Output Voltage (R Load) | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ | Volts |
| 4 | Average Load Current (R Load) | $I_{dc} = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{R} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi R}(1+\cos\alpha)$ | Amperes |
| 5 | RMS Load Current (R Load) | $I_{rms} = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{R}$ | Amperes |
| 6 | Average Output Voltage (RL Load) | $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}\bigl[\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha+\gamma)\bigr]$ | Volts |
| 7 | RMS Output Voltage (RL Load) | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi-\alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha - \sin 2(\alpha+\gamma)}{2} + \gamma\right]}$ | Volts |
| 8 | Extinction Angle Equation (RL Load) | $\sin(\alpha + \gamma) = \sin(\alpha)\cdot e^{-\gamma/\omega\tau}$ | $\tau = L/R$ |
| 9 | Maximum V_dc (R Load, α=0) | $V_{dc,max} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.318\,V_m$ | Volts |
| 10 | Output Ripple Frequency | $f_o = f_{supply} = 50\text{ Hz}$ | Hz |
| 11 | Form Factor (R Load) | $FF = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Rectification Efficiency (R Load) | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{dc}^2}{V_{rms}^2} \times 100\,\%$ | Percentage |
| 13 | R-Firing Circuit: Firing Angle | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{R_1}{R}\right)$ | Depends on bias |
| 14 | RC-Firing Circuit: Firing Angle | $\alpha \approx \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{R}{X_C}\right) = \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$ | Approximate |
| 15 | Thyristor Conduction (R Load) | $\theta_c = \pi - \alpha$ | Radians |
| 16 | Thyristor Conduction (RL Load) | $\theta_c = \pi + \gamma - \alpha$ | Radians |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DC Motor Speed Control (Low-Power):** Small fractional-HP series/shunt motors historically used half-wave rectifiers for cost-effective armature voltage control.
- **Battery Chargers (Trickle Charging):** Phase-controlled half-wave rectifiers provide a simple, low-cost charger for lead-acid batteries in UPS backup systems.
- **Lamp Dimming & Fan Regulators:** Domestic fan regulators (the classic "regulator box" on the wall) use exactly this topology with a DIAC-TRIAC combination — TRIAC is essentially two SCRs in anti-parallel.
- **Temperature Controllers:** Low-power resistive heating elements (incubators, soldering iron control) use half-wave phase control.
- **Educational Foundation:** This is the *gateway circuit* taught in every power electronics lab worldwide because every concept (firing angle, commutation, line synchronization) appears in its simplest possible form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why R AND RC Firing Circuits?** The R-firing circuit has a **limited range of α (typically 0° to 90°)** because beyond ~90° the UJT/breakover voltage cannot be reached. The **RC-firing circuit extends the range to nearly 180°**, making it the practical choice for controlling outputs down to nearly zero.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Output Voltage V_dc for R Load

The instantaneous output voltage for a half-wave controlled rectifier with an R load is:

$$v_o(\omega t) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \le \omega t < \alpha \\ V_m \sin(\omega t), & \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi \\ 0, & \pi < \omega t < 2\pi \end{cases}$$

The average (DC) value over one full cycle (0 to 2π) is:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

Since v_o = 0 outside [α, π], this reduces to:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 1:** Pull out the constant V_m.

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 2:** Integrate.

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\Bigl[-\cos(\omega t)\Bigr]_{\alpha}^{\pi}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the limits.

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\Bigl[-\cos(\pi) + \cos(\alpha)\Bigr]$$

**Step 4:** Substitute cos(π) = −1.

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\Bigl[-(-1) + \cos(\alpha)\Bigr] = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\bigl(1 + \cos\alpha\bigr)$$

**Final Result (boxed for KTU answer sheets):**

$$\boxed{V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\bigl(1 + \cos\alpha\bigr)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: RMS Output Voltage V_rms for R Load

The RMS value is defined as:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o^{2}(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute v_o = 0 outside [α, π].

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^{2}\sin^{2}(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 2:** Use the identity $\sin^{2}\theta = \dfrac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2}$.

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}\, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 3:** Integrate.

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{4\pi}\left[(\omega t) - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2}\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate limits. At ωt = π: π − sin(2π)/2 = π − 0 = π. At ωt = α: α − sin(2α)/2.

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{4\pi}\left[\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]$$

**Step 5:** Take the square root.

$$\boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Average Output Voltage for RL Load

For an RL load, conduction extends from α to (π + γ), so:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi+\gamma} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 1:** Integrate directly.

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\Bigl[-\cos(\omega t)\Bigr]_{\alpha}^{\pi+\gamma}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the limits.

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\Bigl[-\cos(\pi+\gamma) + \cos(\alpha)\Bigr]$$

**Step 3:** Use the identity cos(π + γ) = −cos(γ).

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\bigl[\cos(\gamma) + \cos(\alpha)\bigr]$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\bigl[\cos\alpha + \cos\gamma\bigr]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative equivalent form** (using the extinction equation $\sin(\alpha+\gamma) = \sin\alpha\cdot e^{-\gamma/\omega\tau}$): Some textbooks write $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}[\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha+\gamma)]$. This is mathematically equivalent because $-\cos(\alpha+\gamma) = \cos\gamma + \cos\alpha - 2\cos\gamma$ … wait, this is NOT always equivalent; the second form is a *misprint* in some books. The correct form is the boxed one above. Students should verify using the identity $\cos A - \cos B = -2\sin\bigl(\tfrac{A+B}{2}\bigr)\sin\bigl(\tfrac{A-B}{2}\bigr)$ which yields the alternative form found in some references.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Extinction Angle (γ) for RL Load

The load current during conduction satisfies the differential equation:

$$L\frac{di_o}{dt} + Ri_o = V_m\sin(\omega t), \quad \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi+\gamma$$

**Step 1:** Rewrite in terms of angular variable (t = ωt/ω, so d/dt = ω d/d(ωt)).

$$\frac{di_o}{d(\omega t)} + \frac{R}{\omega L}\, i_o = \frac{V_m}{\omega L}\sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2:** Define the load angle φ = tan⁻¹(ωL/R) and time constant τ = L/R. The solution is:

$$i_o(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z}\bigl[\sin(\omega t - \phi) + Ce^{-\omega t/\omega\tau}\bigr]$$

where $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2}$.

**Step 3:** Apply boundary condition i_o(α) = 0 (current cannot be discontinuous; it was zero at α just before triggering).

$$0 = \frac{V_m}{Z}\bigl[\sin(\alpha - \phi) + Ce^{-\alpha/\omega\tau}\bigr]$$

**Step 4:** Solve for C.

$$C = -\sin(\alpha - \phi)\cdot e^{\alpha/\omega\tau}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the second boundary condition i_o(π + γ) = 0 (current falls to zero at extinction).

$$0 = \frac{V_m}{Z}\bigl[\sin(\pi+\gamma-\phi) + Ce^{-(\pi+\gamma)/\omega\tau}\bigr]$$

**Step 6:** Substitute C and simplify. Using sin(π+γ−φ) = −sin(γ−φ) and expanding:

$$\sin(\gamma - \phi) = \sin(\alpha - \phi)\cdot e^{-(\pi+\gamma-\alpha)/\omega\tau}$$

**Step 7:** In the special case where L is small (φ → 0, φ ≈ sin φ), this simplifies to the canonical KTU form:

$$\boxed{\sin(\alpha + \gamma) = \sin(\alpha)\cdot e^{-\gamma/\omega\tau}}$$

This is a **transcendental equation** solved numerically (Newton-Raphson method) or graphically.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier is supplied by **V_rms = 230 V, f = 50 Hz**. The firing angle is **α = 45°**, and the load is purely resistive **R = 10 Ω**. Find V_dc, V_rms, I_dc, I_rms, Form Factor, and Rectification Efficiency.

**Step 1:** Compute V_m.
$$V_m = \sqrt{2}\times 230 = 325.27\text{ V}$$

**Step 2:** Convert α to radians.
$$\alpha = 45° = \pi/4 = 0.7854\text{ rad}$$

**Step 3:** Compute V_dc.
$$V_{dc} = \frac{325.27}{2\pi}\bigl(1+\cos 45°\bigr) = \frac{325.27}{2\pi}(1+0.7071) = \frac{325.27}{2\pi}\times 1.7071$$

$$V_{dc} = 51.76 \times 1.7071 = 88.34\text{ V}$$

**Step 4:** Compute V_rms.
$$V_{rms} = \frac{325.27}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\pi - 0.7854 + \frac{\sin 90°}{2}\right]}$$

$$V_{rms} = 162.64\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[3.1416 - 0.7854 + 0.5\right]}$$

$$V_{rms} = 162.64\sqrt{\frac{2.8562}{3.1416}} = 162.64\sqrt{0.9091} = 162.64 \times 0.9535 = 155.08\text{ V}$$

**Step 5:** Compute I_dc and I_rms.
$$I_{dc} = V_{dc}/R = 88.34/10 = 8.834\text{ A}$$
$$I_{rms} = V_{rms}/R = 155.08/10 = 15.508\text{ A}$$

**Step 6:** Form Factor.
$$FF = V_{rms}/V_{dc} = 155.08/88.34 = 1.756$$

**Step 7:** Rectification Efficiency.
$$\eta = \frac{V_{dc}^{2}}{V_{rms}^{2}}\times 100\% = \frac{88.34^{2}}{155.08^{2}}\times 100\% = \frac{7803.96}{24049.81}\times 100\% = 32.45\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** Maximum theoretical efficiency for a half-wave rectifier is 40.6% (at α = 0). Our 32.45% at α = 45° is consistent — efficiency drops as α increases. KTU students should remember: **Half-wave rectifiers are inherently inefficient** because half the input power is rejected.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: R vs RL Load

| Parameter | R Load | RL Load |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Conduction range | α to π (≈π−α) | α to π+γ (≈π+γ−α) |
| Output contains negative portion? | **No** (v_o ≥ 0) | **Yes** (v_o < 0 from π to π+γ) |
| Average voltage V_dc | $\frac{V_m}{2\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$ | $\frac{V_m}{2\pi}[\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha+\gamma)]$ |
| Maximum V_dc (α=0) | $V_m/\pi$ | Less than $V_m/\pi$ (because cos γ < 1) |
| Current waveform | Sinusoidal pulse | Distorted, lags voltage |
| Discontinuous current? | Always | Only if load is not too inductive |
| α range practical | 0° to 180° | 0° to ~150° (γ limits further) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete System Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional architecture of the complete half-wave controlled rectifier system with line-synchronized RC firing circuit — the *exact hardware stack* you assemble on the lab bench.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ACMAINS["AC MAINS 230 V, 50 Hz"]
        A1["v_s = V_m sin ωt"]
        A2["Isolation Transformer (Step-down)"]
    end

    subgraph SYNC["LINE SYNCHRONIZATION BLOCK"]
        B1["Zero-Crossing Detector (ZCD)"]
        B2["Squaring Circuit using Op-Amp / Transistor"]
        B3["Synchronized Reference Pulses @ ωt = 0, 2π, ..."]
    end

    subgraph FIRING["FIRING ANGLE GENERATOR"]
        C1["R-Firing Circuit: Variable R + Diac"]
        C2["RC-Firing Circuit: Potentiometer R_p + Capacitor C"]
        C3["UJT Relaxation Oscillator (Optional)"]
        C4["Phase-Shift Network Output"]
    end

    subgraph PULSE["PULSE SHAPING & ISOLATION"]
        D1["Pulse Transformer (Galvanic Isolation)"]
        D2["Gate Pulse Amplifier"]
        D3["Gate Drive to SCR Gate-Cathode"]
    end

    subgraph POWSTAGE["POWER STAGE"]
        E1["SCR T1 (Anode-Cathode in series with Load)"]
        E2["Freewheeling Diode D_FW (across RL load, optional)"]
    end

    subgraph LOAD["LOAD BANK"]
        F1["R Load (Rheostat / Lamp)"]
        F2["RL Load (R + Series Inductor L)"]
        F3["Measurement: Voltmeter, Ammeter, CRO"]
    end

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C2 --> C4
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> E1
    A2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1
    E1 --> F2
    F1 --> F3
    F2 --> F3
    E1 -.-> E2
    E2 -.-> F2
```

## 4.2 State Diagram: SCR Operating States

The SCR has three discrete states during a complete AC cycle. This Mermaid state diagram clarifies the transitions.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OFF_FORWARD: ωt = 0 (start of cycle)

    OFF_FORWARD : Forward-biased, no gate pulse
    OFF_FORWARD : v_s > 0, v_o = 0, i_o = 0
    OFF_FORWARD : Wait for gate trigger

    OFF_FORWARD --> ON_CONDUCTING: Gate pulse at ωt = α

    ON_CONDUCTING : SCR conducting
    ON_CONDUCTING : v_o = v_s (ideal SCR)
    ON_CONDUCTING : i_o = v_o / Z_load

    ON_CONDUCTING --> ON_CONDUCTING_RL: For RL: i_o > 0 past π
    ON_CONDUCTING_RL : SCR still ON, v_s < 0
    ON_CONDUCTING_RL : Negative voltage region
    ON_CONDUCTING_RL : Energy returned to source

    ON_CONDUCTING --> OFF_NATURAL: ωt = π (R load)
    ON_CONDUCTING_RL --> OFF_NATURAL: ωt = π + γ (RL load)

    OFF_NATURAL : Reverse-biased or i_o < I_H
    OFF_NATURAL : v_o = 0, i_o = 0
    OFF_NATURAL : Holds until next cycle

    OFF_NATURAL --> OFF_FORWARD: ωt = 2π (cycle restart)
    OFF_NATURAL --> [*]: Experiment ends
```

## 4.3 RC Firing Circuit — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Stage"]
        I1["AC Supply v_s"]
        I2["Bridge Rectifier (full-wave)"]
    end

    subgraph SYNC2["Synchronization"]
        S1["R_S limits current"]
        S2["Zener D_Z clips to ±V_Z"]
        S3["Square wave at ZCD"]
    end

    subgraph PHASESHIFT["Phase-Shift Network"]
        P1["Variable Resistor R_p"]
        P2["Capacitor C"]
        P3["RC Charge Curve v_C t"]
    end

    subgraph TRIGGER["Trigger Device"]
        T1["DIAC (V_BO ≈ 30 V)"]
        T2["UJT (η = 0.6)"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT2["Gate Drive"]
        O1["Pulse to SCR Gate"]
        O2["SCR T1 fires"]
    end

    I1 --> I2
    I2 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> T1
    P3 --> T2
    T1 --> O1
    T2 --> O1
    O1 --> O2

    P3 -.alpha control.-> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Tip:** In the actual PCEOL507 lab, the **R-firing circuit** uses a single potentiometer with a DIAC, while the **RC-firing circuit** adds a fixed capacitor in series with the potentiometer to extend α beyond 90°. Verify the firing angle by measuring the time delay between the ZCD pulse and the gate pulse on a **dual-trace CRO** and using **α = (Δt / T) × 360°**, where **T = 20 ms** at 50 Hz.

## 4.4 Waveform Reference (Schematic Description for Sketching)

Since waveforms are inherently time-domain signals, here is a text-anchored description that students can convert to a hand-drawn sketch in the lab record:

| Time (ωt) | Event | v_s | v_o (R Load) | v_o (RL Load) | i_o (R Load) | i_o (RL Load) |
|-----------|-------|-----|--------------|---------------|--------------|---------------|
| 0 | Start of positive half-cycle | 0 | 0 (SCR OFF) | 0 (SCR OFF) | 0 | 0 |
| α | Gate pulse applied | V_m sin α | Jumps to v_s | Jumps to v_s | Jumps to v_s/R | Rises exponentially |
| π/2 | Peak of supply | V_m (max) | V_m | V_m | V_m/R (max) | Near max |
| π | Zero crossing | 0 | 0 (SCR OFF) | 0 (still ON!) | 0 | Falling |
| π to π+γ | Negative supply | Negative | 0 (SCR OFF) | Negative | 0 | Decaying |
| π+γ | Current extinction | Negative | 0 | 0 (SCR OFF) | 0 | 0 |
| 2π | End of cycle | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 2π+α | Next gate pulse | V_m sin α | Repeats | Repeats | Repeats | Repeats |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define firing angle and extinction angle as applied to a single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier. How does the extinction angle arise?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Firing Angle (α):** The firing angle is defined as the angle (measured in electrical degrees from the instant the AC supply voltage crosses zero in the positive direction) at which the thyristor is triggered into conduction by the application of a gate pulse. Mathematically, $\alpha = \omega t_{gate}$, where $\omega t_{gate}$ is the angular position of the gate pulse. The range is $0° \le \alpha \le 180°$ for a half-wave rectifier. **(1.5 marks)**

**Extinction Angle (γ):** The extinction angle is the angle (measured from the same zero-crossing reference) at which the load current naturally falls to zero, causing the SCR to turn OFF. **(1 mark)**

**How γ arises:** With an RL load, the load current cannot change instantaneously due to the inductor's stored energy ($E = \frac{1}{2}Li^{2}$). The current therefore continues to flow even after the supply voltage reverses at π, and the SCR remains conducting until the current decays below the holding current. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Why is line synchronization necessary in the firing circuit of a controlled rectifier? Mention the role of a zero-crossing detector (ZCD).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Necessity of Line Synchronization:** The firing angle α must be measured from a *fixed and consistent* reference point in every AC cycle. If the firing circuit is asynchronous with the supply, α will drift cycle-to-cycle, causing unstable output, sub-harmonic oscillations, and possibly firing failure. Line synchronization locks the firing circuit to the supply frequency so that α is identical cycle after cycle. **(1.5 marks)**

**Role of ZCD:** The ZCD detects the precise instant the AC supply voltage crosses zero. It produces a sharp logic-level pulse (e.g., a square wave edge) that the firing circuit uses as the **time-origin** for measuring α. Typical implementations: op-amp comparator, transistor clipper, or optocoupler-based isolator. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — R Load Analysis

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / KTU Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the average and RMS values of the output voltage of a single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier supplying a purely resistive load R, with firing angle α. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier is fed from a **230 V, 50 Hz** AC supply and delivers power to a **R = 50 Ω** resistive load. The firing angle is set to **α = 60°**. Calculate: **(i)** V_dc, **(ii)** V_rms, **(iii)** I_dc, **(iv)** I_rms, **(v)** Form Factor, and **(vi)** Rectification Efficiency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

- Statement of piecewise output voltage equation. **(1 mark)**
- Average value derivation: $V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$. **(3 marks)**
- RMS value derivation: $V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}$. **(3 marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Given: V_rms = 230 V → $V_m = 230\sqrt{2} = 325.27$ V; R = 50 Ω; α = 60° = π/3 rad. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** $V_{dc} = \frac{325.27}{2\pi}(1+\cos 60°) = \frac{325.27}{2\pi}(1+0.5) = 51.76 \times 1.5 = 77.64$ V. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 3:** $V_{rms} = \frac{325.27}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\pi - \frac{\pi}{3} + \frac{\sin 120°}{2}\right]} = 162.64\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\frac{2\pi}{3} + 0.4330\right]}$
$= 162.64\sqrt{\frac{2.0944 + 0.4330}{3.1416}} = 162.64\sqrt{0.8038} = 162.64 \times 0.8966 = 145.83$ V. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 4:** $I_{dc} = V_{dc}/R = 77.64/50 = 1.553$ A. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5:** $I_{rms} = V_{rms}/R = 145.83/50 = 2.917$ A. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common error is to compute $I_{rms}$ using $V_m$ instead of $V_{rms}$ in the ratio $V_{rms}/R$. The current waveform is **not** a pure sinusoid — its RMS must come from the actual V_rms. Also, students often forget to convert α to radians when using sin and cos in calculators set to "RAD" mode — the KTU lab exam typically allows degree mode, but the answer scripts must be consistent. (1 mark deduction per error)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — RL Load & Firing Circuit (Internal Choice Alternative)

`[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram and waveforms, explain the operation of a single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier supplying an **R-L load**. Derive the expression for the average output voltage and the extinction angle equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat block diagram the operation of a **line-synchronized RC firing circuit** for a single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier. Compare the R-firing and RC-firing circuits in terms of the range of firing angle and circuit complexity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — RL Load Operation (7 marks):**

**Circuit Description:** Single SCR T1 in series with the series combination of R and L across a single-phase AC source v_s = V_m sin ωt. Gate pulse arrives at ωt = α. **(1 mark)**

**Operation (with waveforms described):**
- For 0 ≤ ωt < α: SCR OFF; v_o = 0, i_o = 0.
- For α ≤ ωt ≤ π: SCR ON; v_o = v_s, i_o rises.
- For π ≤ ωt ≤ π+γ: SCR ON; v_o = v_s (negative), i_o decays to zero.
- For π+γ ≤ ωt ≤ 2π+α: SCR OFF; v_o = 0, i_o = 0.

**(3 marks)**

**Average Voltage Derivation:**
$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi+\gamma} \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}[\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha+\gamma)]$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**Extinction Angle Equation:** By solving the load differential equation with boundary conditions $i_o(\alpha) = 0$ and $i_o(\pi+\gamma) = 0$, the extinction angle satisfies:
$$\sin(\alpha + \gamma) = \sin(\alpha)\cdot e^{-\gamma/\omega\tau}, \quad \tau = L/R$$
**(1.5 marks)**

**Part (b) — Line-Synchronized RC Firing Circuit (7 marks):**
