**Block Diagram Description:** A complete RC firing circuit has the following blocks in cascade: **(3 marks)**
1. **Synchronization transformer** (steps down and isolates).
2. **Zero-Crossing Detector (ZCD):** Op-amp comparator or transistor-based; produces a square wave synchronized to the supply.
3. **Phase-Shift Network:** Variable R and fixed C; produces a time-delayed ramp.
4. **Trigger Device:** DIAC (V_BO ≈ 30 V) or UJT; when the ramp reaches the breakover voltage, it generates a sharp pulse.
5. **Gate Pulse Output:** Isolated by a pulse transformer and applied to the SCR gate.

**R vs RC Firing Circuit Comparison Table: (4 marks)**

| Feature | R-Firing Circuit | RC-Firing Circuit |
|---------|------------------|-------------------|
| Components | R (variable) + DIAC | R (variable) + C (fixed) + DIAC |
| Range of α | 0° to ~90° | 0° to ~170° |
| Linearity of α control | Poor (α = tan⁻¹(R₁/R)) | Better (α ∝ ln(RC)) |
| Cost & Complexity | Low | Slightly higher |
| Suitability for high α | Not suitable | Excellent |
| Typical application | Simple dimmers | Phase-controlled motor drives |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Top 3 Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting to draw waveforms** in part (a): A "neat circuit diagram AND waveforms" means BOTH are required. Marks are split 50-50; missing waveforms costs ~3 marks.
> 2. **Writing the wrong extinction angle form:** Some students write $i_o(\pi+\gamma) = I_L$ (latching current) instead of $i_o(\pi+\gamma) = 0$. The correct condition is that current decays to **zero** (below holding current, which we approximate as zero for analytical purposes).
> 3. **Confusing R-firing and RC-firing range limits:** Students often claim the R-firing circuit can fire up to 180°. It **cannot** — the maximum is limited by the DIAC breakover voltage and the supply RMS voltage. Beyond ~90°, the RC voltage never reaches V_BO. (1 mark deduction for this error.)

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This and Stick in Your Lab Record!)**

- [x] **Half-wave controlled rectifier** uses **ONE SCR** in series with the load. Conducts only in the **positive half-cycle** after gate pulse at α.
- [x] **Output ripple frequency = supply frequency = 50 Hz** (not 100 Hz, which is full-wave).
- [x] **V_dc for R load:** $V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$
- [x] **V_dc for RL load:** $V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}[\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha+\gamma)]$
- [x] **Maximum V_dc (R load, α=0):** $V_m/\pi \approx 0.318 V_m \approx 103.5$ V for 230 V mains.
- [x] **Conduction angle (R load):** $\theta_c = \pi - \alpha$. **(Conduction angle (RL load):** $\theta_c = \pi + \gamma - \alpha$**.
- [x] **For α = 0:** R-load gives the **largest** V_dc; increasing α **reduces** V_dc.
- [x] **For α = 180°:** V_dc = 0 (no conduction).
- [x] **Extinction angle equation:** $\sin(\alpha+\gamma) = \sin\alpha \cdot e^{-\gamma/\omega\tau}$ with $\tau = L/R$.
- [x] **Line synchronization** is mandatory: the firing circuit must "know" when ωt = 0 every cycle, done via a **ZCD**.
- [x] **R-firing range:** 0° to ~90°. **RC-firing range:** 0° to ~170°. **Prefer RC for full-range control.**
- [x] **Maximum theoretical rectification efficiency of half-wave rectifier: 40.6%** (at α=0, R load). This is fundamentally limited — full-wave is better.
- [x] **Form factor FF = V_rms / V_dc**; for half-wave at α=0, FF ≈ 1.57.
- [x] **Power delivered to R load:** $P = I_{rms}^{2} \cdot R$ (use RMS, not average!).
- [x] **SCR is OFF** whenever v_s < 0 (reverse-biased) OR no gate pulse (forward-biased but not triggered).
- [x] **Freewheeling diode** across RL load eliminates the negative voltage region (v_o ≥ 0 always) and reduces γ → 0.
- [x] **Lab CRO measurement:** Use **dual-trace mode** — Channel 1 on v_s, Channel 2 on gate pulse. Time delay $\Delta t$ gives $\alpha = (\Delta t / 20\text{ ms}) \times 360°$.
- [x] **Safety:** Always use an **isolation transformer** between mains and the SCR. Never touch the circuit while energized; thyristor voltages are lethal.
- [x] **L vs R load identification:** If V_dc measured < theoretical R-load V_dc for the same α, the load is inductive (energy is being returned to source during π to π+γ).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single-Phase Half-Controlled (Semi-Converter) and Fully-Controlled Rectifier Feeding R/RL Loads with UJT, Ramp, and Digital Firing Circuits
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL507 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Single-Phase Half-Controlled (Semi-Converter) and Fully-Controlled Rectifier Feeding R/RL Loads with UJT, Ramp, and Digital Firing Circuits

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Single-Phase Fully-Controlled Rectifier (Full Converter)

A **single-phase fully-controlled bridge rectifier** is a power electronic circuit that uses **four Silicon Controlled Rectifiers (SCRs / Thyristors)** arranged in a full-bridge topology to convert single-phase AC supply into variable DC output. The output DC voltage is regulated by varying the **firing angle (α)** of the thyristors through gate pulses applied at the appropriate instants in each half-cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A fully-controlled converter is one in which all the switching devices in the bridge are controllable (SCRs), and the average output voltage can be varied from positive to negative by varying the firing angle α from 0° to 180° (rectifier → inverter mode).

## 1.2 Single-Phase Half-Controlled Rectifier (Semi-Converter)

A **semi-converter (half-controlled rectifier)** is a hybrid bridge using **two SCRs and two power diodes**. Because two devices are uncontrolled, the output voltage can only vary between a maximum positive value and zero; it cannot become negative. This makes it suitable for **unidirectional power flow** applications like DC motor drives (single-quadrant operation).

## 1.3 Firing Circuits — UJT, Ramp Comparator, and Digital

A **firing circuit** (or trigger circuit) generates the gate pulses required to turn ON the SCRs at the desired firing angle α. Three common types are studied in this experiment:

| Firing Circuit | Core Element | Operating Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **UJT Firing Circuit** | Unijunction Transistor (UJT) | RC time constant charges; when voltage reaches UJT peak point, it fires and discharges producing a pulse. |
| **Ramp Comparator Firing** | Op-Amp + Ramp Signal | A sawtooth/ramp is compared with a reference DC level; output switches when ramp equals reference, producing firing pulse. |
| **Digital Firing Circuit** | Microcontroller / FPGA / DSP | Time delay is computed digitally from zero-crossing detection; pulse is generated using a timer interrupt. |

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Water Tap Model**
> Imagine an AC waveform as a constantly reversing water flow (positive and negative). An **SCR is like a one-way tap that only opens when you press a button (gate pulse)**. If you press the button at the **start** of the flow (α = 0°), the tap stays open for the full useful half — maximum water collected. If you press it **late** (α = 90°), you collect only half — less output.
> 
> - **Fully-controlled rectifier:** Both positive and negative flows are controlled taps → you can even *push* water back (inverter mode).
> - **Semi-converter:** Only positive flow uses a tap; negative flow freely passes through a diode — you cannot push back, only regulate forward.
> - **R load:** A simple faucet — water stops instantly when the tap closes (discontinuous flow at high α).
> - **RL load:** A spring-loaded faucet — water keeps flowing briefly even after the tap closes due to stored magnetic energy, requiring a **freewheeling diode** to safely dissipate it.

## 1.5 Standard Physical & Electrical Quantities

- **RMS line voltage:** $V_s = 230$ V (standard Indian single-phase domestic supply) at **50 Hz**
- **Peak supply voltage:** $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_s \approx 325.27$ V
- **Standard firing angle range:** $\alpha = 0°$ to $180°$ (full converter), $\alpha = 0°$ to $180°$ (semi-converter, output is always non-negative)
- **Average output voltage** is the key controlled parameter (in Volts DC)
- **Critical load inductance** $L_c$ distinguishes continuous from discontinuous conduction

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Single-Phase Fully-Controlled Rectifier — Circuit Topology

The bridge consists of four SCRs: **T1, T2, T3, T4**. The pairs (T1, T2) and (T3, T4) conduct during the positive and negative half-cycles respectively.

**Conduction Sequence (with R load):**
- For $\omega t = \alpha \rightarrow \pi$: T1 & T2 conduct → $V_o = +V_s$
- For $\omega t = \pi \rightarrow (\pi + \alpha)$: T3 & T4 conduct → $V_o = -V_s$
- Beyond $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$, no device conducts for R load → discontinuous mode begins.

## 2.2 Single-Phase Semi-Converter — Circuit Topology

The bridge has SCRs **T1, T3** in the upper arms and **diodes D2, D4** in the lower arms.

**Conduction Sequence (with R load):**
- $\omega t = \alpha \rightarrow \pi$: T1 & D2 conduct → $V_o = +V_s$
- $\omega t = \pi \rightarrow (\pi + \alpha)$: D3 & D4 conduct → $V_o = -V_s$ → effectively $V_o = 0$ (because output terminals reverse)
- Output is **always non-negative** due to diode action.

## 2.3 Effect of RL Load — Continuous vs. Discontinuous Conduction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> With a pure R load, output voltage becomes discontinuous (zero) for $\alpha > 0$ in the full converter because once the supply crosses zero, no device is forward-biased. With an **RL load**, the load current continues to flow beyond the voltage zero due to inductor energy, leading to devices remaining in conduction past the natural commutation point.

The **critical inductance** $L_c$ is defined as the minimum inductance required to maintain continuous conduction:

$$L_c = \frac{R}{\omega} \cdot \left( \tan\alpha - \alpha \right)$$

(For KTU board problems, the analysis assumes **continuous conduction** unless stated otherwise.)

## 2.4 Freewheeling Diode (FWD) Action

A **freewheeling diode** placed across the RL load provides a path for the inductor current when the supply voltage reverses. It:
- Eliminates the negative output voltage portion (improves average output).
- Improves the input power factor.
- Prevents discontinuous conduction for moderate R values.

## 2.5 UJT Firing Circuit — Operating Principle

The UJT (Unijunction Transistor) has three terminals — Emitter (E), Base-1 (B1), Base-2 (B2). The intrinsic stand-off ratio $\eta$ determines the peak point voltage:

$$\eta = \frac{R_{B1}}{R_{B1} + R_{B2}}$$

$$V_P = \eta \cdot V_{BB} + V_D$$

where $V_D \approx 0.7$ V is the diode drop.

**RC timing:** The capacitor charges through R; when $V_E = V_P$, the UJT fires, producing a sharp pulse at B1 used to trigger the SCR. The firing angle is controlled by varying R or C.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Topology / Quantity | Formula | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Avg. output of **full converter**, R or RL (continuous) load | $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha$ | $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$ |
| 2 | Avg. output of **semi-converter**, R or RL (continuous) load | $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi} (1 + \cos\alpha)$ | $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$ |
| 3 | Avg. output of **semi-converter with FWD** | $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi} (1 + \cos\alpha)$ | Same as without FWD in continuous mode |
| 4 | RMS output of full converter, RL continuous | $V_{o(rms)} = V_s$ | Independent of α! |
| 5 | RMS output of semi-converter, R load | $V_{o(rms)} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{\pi} + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$ | — |
| 6 | UJT firing angle relation | $\alpha = \omega RC \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - \eta}\right)$ | Derivation from RC charging |
| 7 | Critical load inductance | $L_c = \dfrac{R}{\omega} (\tan\alpha - \alpha)$ | Boundary of continuous conduction |
| 8 | Rectification efficiency (full converter) | $\eta_{rec} = \dfrac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \approx 81.05\%$ | R load only |
| 9 | Transformer Utilization Factor (TUF) | $\text{TUF} = \dfrac{P_{dc}}{V_s I_s} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi^2} \approx 0.2864$ | R load |
| 10 | Ripple frequency (full-wave) | $f_r = 2 f_s$ | $f_s$ = supply freq. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These rectifiers form the front-end of nearly every adjustable DC drive (lathe spindles, paper mills, rolling mills), HVDC transmission links (3-phase version), battery chargers, and electroplating rectifiers. Modern SMPS also use the full-bridge topology with IGBTs/MOSFETs in place of SCRs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Output Voltage of Fully-Controlled Rectifier (R Load)

**Setup:** Single-phase full-bridge with 4 SCRs fed from $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. SCR pair T1-T2 fires at $\omega t = \alpha$, T3-T4 fires at $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$.

**Output voltage expression:**

$$v_o(\omega t) = \begin{cases} +V_m \sin(\omega t), & \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi \\ 0, & \pi \le \omega t \le \pi + \alpha \quad \text{(R load, no conduction)} \end{cases}$$

**Step 1 — Definition of average value over one full cycle ($2\pi$):**

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Perform the integration:**

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\omega t) \Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute limits:**

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Big( -\cos\pi + \cos\alpha \Big) = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \big( 1 + \cos\alpha \big)$$

**Wait — correction:** This is the **half-wave average**. For full-bridge, the negative half cycle also contributes, doubling the contribution:

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} (1 + \cos\alpha) \quad \text{[Full converter, R load, discontinuous mode]}$$

**For continuous conduction (large L):**

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Average Output Voltage of Semi-Converter (R Load)

**Conduction sequence:**
- $\alpha \to \pi$: T1 & D2 ON → $v_o = V_m \sin(\omega t)$
- $\pi \to \pi + \alpha$: D3 & D4 ON → output terminals are shorted (across load) → $v_o = 0$
- $\pi + \alpha \to 2\pi$: T3 & D4 ON → $v_o = -V_m \sin(\omega t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ (since sin is negative here) — actually this is positive due to the diode clamp.

**Average over full cycle:**

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi + \alpha}^{2\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \right]$$

**Step 1 — Symmetry simplification:** Since $\sin(\omega t)$ is symmetric about $\pi$, both integrals are equal:

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Integration:**

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\omega t) \Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} (1 + \cos\alpha)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The semi-converter average output has the **same expression** as the full-converter discontinuous mode average — but importantly, in the semi-converter, the output is always **non-negative**, which is preferred for DC motor drives.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: UJT Firing Angle Relationship

**RC charging of the emitter capacitor $C_E$:**

$$v_E(t) = V_{BB} \left( 1 - e^{-t/RC} \right)$$

**Condition for firing:** $v_E(t_1) = V_P = \eta V_{BB} + V_D$

For simplicity (ignoring $V_D$):

$$V_{BB}(1 - e^{-t_1/RC}) = \eta V_{BB}$$

$$1 - e^{-t_1/RC} = \eta$$

$$e^{-t_1/RC} = 1 - \eta$$

$$t_1 = RC \ln\left( \frac{1}{1 - \eta} \right)$$

**Firing angle (in radians):**

$$\alpha = \omega t_1 = \omega RC \ln\left( \frac{1}{1 - \eta} \right)$$

**Conversion to degrees:** $\alpha_{deg} = \dfrac{180}{\pi} \cdot \omega RC \ln\left( \dfrac{1}{1 - \eta} \right)$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Example (KTU Frequently Asked)

**Problem:** A single-phase full converter feeds an RL load with $V_s = 230$ V, 50 Hz, $R = 10 \ \Omega$, $L$ very large, and $\alpha = 60°$. Find (a) $V_{o(avg)}$, (b) $V_{o(rms)}$, (c) Average thyristor current, (d) Rectification efficiency.

**Given:**

$$V_s = 230 \ \text{V}, \quad V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot 230 = 325.27 \ \text{V}, \quad \alpha = 60° = \pi/3$$

**Step (a) — Average output voltage:**

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha = \frac{2 \times 325.27}{\pi} \cos 60°$$

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{650.54}{\pi} \times 0.5 = 103.53 \ \text{V}$$

**Step (b) — RMS output voltage (continuous RL):**

$$V_{o(rms)} = V_s = 230 \ \text{V}$$

**Step (c) — Average load current:**

$$I_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_{o(avg)}}{R} = \frac{103.53}{10} = 10.353 \ \text{A}$$

Average thyristor current (each SCR conducts for half the cycle):

$$I_{T(avg)} = \frac{I_{o(avg)}}{2} = 5.18 \ \text{A}$$

**Step (d) — Rectification efficiency:**

$$P_{dc} = V_{o(avg)} \cdot I_{o(avg)} = 103.53 \times 10.353 = 1071.8 \ \text{W}$$

$$P_{ac} = V_{o(rms)} \cdot I_{o(rms)} = V_{o(rms)} \cdot \frac{V_{o(rms)}}{R} = \frac{(230)^2}{10} = 5290 \ \text{W}$$

$$\eta_{rec} = \frac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \frac{1071.8}{5290} = 20.26\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The efficiency is low at high α because although DC power reduces, AC (RMS) power is unchanged. This is a classic KTU question pattern.

## 3.5 Critical Load Inductance Derivation

For a full converter with RL load, the boundary between continuous and discontinuous conduction is found by setting $i_L(\omega t = \pi + \alpha) = 0$:

$$\int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} \left( V_m \sin\omega t - i_L R \right) d(\omega t) = 0$$

Solving:

$$L_c = \frac{R}{\omega} (\tan\alpha - \alpha)$$

For $\alpha = 60°$ and $R = 10 \ \Omega$, $\omega = 2\pi \cdot 50 = 314.16$ rad/s:

$$L_c = \frac{10}{314.16} \times (\tan 60° - \pi/3) = 0.03183 \times (1.732 - 1.047) = 0.0218 \ \text{H} = 21.8 \ \text{mH}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Single-Phase Full Converter with Firing Unit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AC[AC Supply Vs 230V 50Hz] --> ISO[Isolator MCB]
    ISO --> T1[Step Down Transformer Optional]
    T1 --> BR[Full Bridge T1 T2 T3 T4 SCRs]
    BR --> LOAD[R Load OR RL Load]
    BR -.feedback.-> CT[Current Transformer]
    BR -.feedback.-> PT[Potential Transformer]
    ZCD[Zero Crossing Detector] --> MCU[Microcontroller OR UJT Ramp Trigger]
    MCU --> PULSE[Isolated Gate Pulse Amplifier]
    PULSE --> BR
    LOAD --> METER[DC Voltmeter Ammeter CRO]
    METER --> OSC[Oscilloscope]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Half-Controlled (Semi-Converter) Bridge

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AC_SUPPLY
        A[AC Source Vs] --> N[Neutral]
    end
    subgraph SEMI_BRIDGE
        T1["SCR T1 Upper Left"] --- D2["Diode D2 Lower Left"]
        T3["SCR T3 Upper Right"] --- D4["Diode D4 Lower Right"]
        T1 --- T3
        D2 --- D4
    end
    A --> T1
    N --> D2
    T3 --> A
    D4 --> N
    subgraph LOAD
        POS[Positive Terminal] --> R[R Load] --> L[Inductor L Optional] --> NEG[Negative Terminal]
    end
    T1 --> POS
    D2 --> NEG
    T3 --> NEG
    D4 --> POS
    FWD["Freewheeling Diode FWD Optional"] -.parallel.-> LOAD
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — UJT Firing Circuit Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SYNC[Sync Transformer 230V to 12V] --> RECT[Rectifier Bridge to DC]
    RECT --> REG[Zener Regulator VBB]
    REG --> RES[R Variable Timing Resistor]
    RES --> CAP[Capacitor C E]
    CAP --> EMIT[UJT Emitter E]
    UJT[UJT Base 1 B1] --> PULSE1[Gate Pulse via Pulse Transformer]
    UJT --> BASE2[Base 2 B2 to VBB]
    PULSE1 --> SCR_GATE[SCR Gate Cathode]
    PHASE[Phase Shifting R2 R3] --> REG
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Ramp Comparator Firing Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SYNC[Sync Input from Supply] --> RAMP_GEN[Ramp Generator using Op-Amp Integrator]
    RAMP_GEN --> COMP_NEG[Inverting Input of Comparator Op-Amp]
    VREF[VREF Adjustable DC Reference] --> COMP_POS[Non Inverting Input of Comparator]
    COMP_NEG --> OP[Op-Amp LM741 Comparator Output]
    COMP_POS --> OP
    OP --> MONO[Monostable Multivibrator Pulse Widener]
    MONO --> ISO_PULSE[Pulse Transformer Isolation]
    ISO_PULSE --> SCR_G[SCR Gate]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Digital Firing Circuit (Microcontroller Based)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AC_LINE[AC Mains] --> ZCD[Zero Crossing Detector LM358 Comparator]
    ZCD --> INT[Interrupt to Microcontroller]
    MCU[Microcontroller 8051 OR Arduino OR DSP TMS320]
    INT --> MCU
    KEY[Keyboard OR Potentiometer for Alpha Setting] --> MCU
    MCU --> TIMER[Internal Timer Counter]
    TIMER --> DELAY[Software Delay T = alpha by omega]
    DELAY --> GPIO[GPIO Pin Pulse Output]
    GPIO --> DRIVER[Gate Driver Optocoupler MOC3021]
    DRIVER --> SCR_G[SCR Gate Pulse]
    LCD[LCD Display 16x2 for Alpha Vo] --> MCU
```

## 4.6 Expected Output Waveform — R Load, Full Converter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WAVEFORM
    T0["t=0 alpha=0 waveform is full sine blocks"] --> T1["t=alpha first SCR pair fires"]
    T1 --> T2["t=pi natural commutation"]
    T2 --> T3["t=pi plus alpha second pair fires"]
    T3 --> T4["t=2pi cycle complete"]
    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**
**Differentiate between a single-phase fully-controlled rectifier and a single-phase semi-converter with respect to device count, output voltage waveform, and mode of operation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Full Converter | Semi-Converter |
|---|---|---|
| **SCRs** | **4** (all controllable) | **2** (T1, T3) |
| **Diodes** | **0** | **2** (D2, D4) |
| **Output voltage range** | $+V_o$ to $-V_o$ (reversible) | **0 to $+V_o$** (unidirectional) |
| **Operation modes** | Rectifier and Inverter (2-quadrant) | Rectifier only (1-quadrant) |
| **Average output** | $\frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | $\frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ |

(1 mark for each correct row of comparison; final mark for a clean concluding statement.)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**
**Explain the role of a freewheeling diode in a single-phase semi-converter feeding an RL load. Mention any two advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **freewheeling diode (FWD)** is connected across the RL load with cathode at the positive terminal. When the supply voltage becomes negative, the FWD becomes forward-biased and provides a path for the decaying inductor current, short-circuiting the load terminals. (2 marks)

**Advantages (1 mark for any two):**
1. Prevents reverse voltage across the load (improves average output).
2. Improves input power factor of the converter.
3. Protects SCRs from high reverse dv/dt.
4. Allows discontinuous RL to behave like continuous mode for moderate L.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** A single-phase full converter feeds a highly inductive load with $R = 5 \ \Omega$, $L \to \infty$, from a **230 V, 50 Hz** supply. The firing angle is **45°**. Determine:
  1. Average and RMS output voltage.
  2. Average and RMS thyristor current.
  3. Active and reactive power drawn from the supply.
  4. Power factor of the converter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **single-phase semi-converter with R load**. Draw the input supply, output voltage, and SCR gate pulse waveforms. Derive the expression for average output voltage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a)

**Given:** $V_s = 230$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $R = 5 \ \Omega$, $\alpha = 45°$, continuous conduction.

**Step 1 — Peak voltage and standard quantities:**

$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27 \ \text{V}, \quad \omega = 2\pi f = 314.16 \ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Average output voltage:**

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha = \frac{2 \times 325.27}{\pi} \times \cos 45°$$

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{650.54}{3.1416} \times 0.7071 = 146.45 \ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — RMS output voltage (full converter, continuous RL):**

$$V_{o(rms)} = V_s = 230 \ \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Currents:**

$$I_{o(rms)} = \frac{V_{o(rms)}}{R} = \frac{230}{5} = 46 \ \text{A}$$

$$I_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_{o(avg)}}{R} = \frac{146.45}{5} = 29.29 \ \text{A}$$

Each SCR conducts for π radians per cycle:

$$I_{T(rms)} = \frac{I_{o(rms)}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{46}{\sqrt{2}} = 32.53 \ \text{A}$$

$$I_{T(avg)} = \frac{I_{o(avg)}}{2} = 14.64 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 5 — Power:**

$$P = I_{o(rms)}^2 R = (46)^2 \times 5 = 10,580 \ \text{W} = 10.58 \ \text{kW}$$

**Step 6 — Supply RMS current (full converter, full-wave):**

$$I_s = I_{o(rms)} = 46 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 7 — Apparent power:**

$$S = V_s I_s = 230 \times 46 = 10,580 \ \text{VA}$$

**Step 8 — Power factor:**

$$\cos\phi = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{10,580}{10,580} = 1.0 \ \text{(apparent at this α — note: this ideal case ignores overlap)}$$

(For non-ideal with overlap, $\cos\phi = \cos\alpha$.)

---

#### Model Solution (b)

**Circuit Diagram Description (1 mark):**
A single-phase semi-converter has 2 SCRs (T1, T3) in the top arms and 2 power diodes (D2, D4) in the bottom arms, forming an H-bridge. The AC supply connects across one diagonal, the load across the other.

**Operation (3 marks):**
- **$\alpha$ to $\pi$:** SCR T1 and diode D2 are forward-biased; conduction begins when T1 receives gate pulse at $\omega t = \alpha$. Output follows the supply: $v_o = V_m \sin\omega t$.
- **$\pi$ to $\pi + \alpha$:** Supply reverses, but T3 has not yet been fired. Diodes D3 (in some configurations) and D4 conduct, shorting the load through the source — $v_o = 0$.
- **$\pi + \alpha$ to $2\pi$:** T3 fired; T1 turns off. D2 and T3 conduct; output again follows the negative half sine, which becomes positive at the load due to the bridge — $v_o = -V_m \sin\omega t$ (but with sign change at load = positive).

**Waveform description (2 marks):** Mention the timing relationship of gate pulses at $\alpha$ in each half-cycle and the resulting $v_o$ waveform with zero regions for $0$ to $\alpha$ and $\pi$ to $\pi + \alpha$.

**Derivation (1 mark — already covered in Section 3.2):**

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} (1 + \cos\alpha)$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, describe the operation of a **UJT-based firing circuit** for a single-phase fully-controlled rectifier. Derive the relationship between firing angle and the RC time constant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-phase full converter is operated from **230 V, 50 Hz** source and feeds a load of $R = 8 \ \Omega$ in series with a very large inductance (continuous conduction). If the converter is operated in the **inverter mode** with $\alpha = 150°$, determine:
  1. Average and RMS output voltage (state the sign convention).
  2. Average and RMS thyristor current.
  3. Power fed back to the source (negative power = regeneration). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a)

**Circuit (2 marks):** A UJT relaxation oscillator consists of a sync transformer (230 V → 12 V), a rectifier bridge, a Zener regulator providing $V_{BB}$ (≈ 18 V), a charging resistor R, a capacitor $C_E$, the UJT, and a pulse transformer for gate isolation.

**Operation (3 marks):**
1. On each half-cycle, the sync transformer produces a 12 V sinusoidal voltage; the bridge + Zener converts it to a constant $V_{BB}$ clamped DC.
2. $C_E$ charges through R exponentially: $v_E = V_{BB}(1 - e^{-t/RC})$.
3. When $v_E$ reaches the peak point $V_P = \eta V_{BB} + V_D \approx \eta V_{BB}$, the UJT fires (emitter-to-B1 breakdown).
4. $C_E$ rapidly discharges through the UJT and primary of the pulse transformer, producing a sharp positive pulse at the SCR gate.
5. The cycle resets; $C_E$ starts charging again. The pulse-train continues synchronously with the mains.
6. By varying R, the time to reach $V_P$ is controlled, thereby varying $\alpha$.

**Derivation (2 marks — see Section 3.3):**

$$\alpha = \omega RC \ln\left( \frac{1}{1 - \eta} \right)$$

---

#### Model Solution (b)

**Given:** $V_s = 230$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $R = 8 \ \Omega$, $\alpha = 150°$, continuous conduction, **inverter mode** ($\alpha > 90°$).

**Step 1:**

$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27 \ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Average output voltage:**

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha = \frac{2 \times 325.27}{\pi} \times \cos 150°$$

$$\cos 150° = -\cos 30° = -0.866$$

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{650.54}{\pi} \times (-0.866) = -179.4 \ \text{V}$$

The **negative sign** indicates that the converter operates in the **inverter mode** — power flows from load (DC) back to source (AC). (2 marks)

**Step 3 — RMS output voltage:**

$$V_{o(rms)} = V_s = 230 \ \text{V} \ \text{(magnitude, independent of α in continuous RL mode)}$$

**Step 4 — Currents:**

$$I_{o(rms)} = \frac{V_{o(rms)}}{R} = \frac{230}{8} = 28.75 \ \text{A}$$

$$I_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_{o(avg)}}{R} = \frac{-179.4}{8} = -22.43 \ \text{A} \ \text{(negative indicates reverse flow)}$$

$$I_{T(rms)} = \frac{I_{o(rms)}}{\sqrt{2}} = 20.33 \ \text{A}$$

$$I_{T(avg)} = \frac{I_{o(avg)}}{2} = -11.21 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 5 — Power fed back:**

$$P_{dc} = V_{o(avg)} \times I_{o(avg)} = (-179.4) \times (-22.43) = +4023.7 \ \text{W}$$

The product of two negatives is positive, but the **direction of current is reversed** relative to voltage, so net power is **negative** with respect to the source. Regenerative power = 4.024 kW fed back. (1 mark for clear sign convention explanation)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers**
> 
> 1. **Sign Convention Confusion in Inverter Mode:** Students often report $V_{o(avg)}$ as a *positive* value when $\alpha > 90°$. **Always include the sign**; it carries 1 mark.
> 2. **RMS = $V_s$ ONLY for continuous RL:** This shortcut is invalid for R load or discontinuous mode.
> 3. **Semi-converter vs Full-converter formulas:** Many students mix them up. The semi-converter has the form $(1 + \cos\alpha)$ multiplied by $V_m/\pi$, while the full converter has $\cos\alpha$ multiplied by $2V_m/\pi$.
> 4. **UJT circuit — missing sync transformer:** A UJT circuit without sync to the mains is incomplete. Always show the sync input.
> 5. **Drawing FWD wrong way:** The freewheeling diode cathode must be at the **positive** load terminal; reversed FWD will short-circuit the source.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert α to radians** in UJT derivation — examiners check for $\omega RC$ term.
> 7. **Using DC voltmeter rating less than peak:** Always choose $V_m$ for the voltmeter, not $V_{rms}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 Experiment**
> 
> - **Full converter** = 4 SCRs; output can be positive or negative; **2-quadrant** operation.
> - **Semi-converter** = 2 SCRs + 2 Diodes; output is **unidirectional (0 to $+V_{max}$)**; 1-quadrant.
> - **Average output:**
>   * Full converter (RL continuous): $V_{o(avg)} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$
>   * Semi-converter (R or RL continuous): $V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$
> - **RMS output (full converter, continuous RL):** $V_{o(rms)} = V_s$ — **independent of α**!
> - **Freewheeling diode** prevents negative load voltage; **improves PF**; placed antiparallel to load.
> - **Inverter mode:** $\alpha > 90°$; $V_{o(avg)}$ becomes **negative**; power flows from DC → AC.
> - **UJT firing:** $\alpha = \omega RC \ln[1/(1-\eta)]$ where $\eta$ is the stand-off ratio (typically 0.6–0.8).
> - **Ramp comparator firing:** Firing occurs when the **ramp voltage = reference DC level**.
> - **Digital firing:** Uses **zero-crossing detection** + software timer delay = α.
> - **Continuous conduction condition:** $L \ge L_c = \frac{R}{\omega}(\tan\alpha - \alpha)$.
> - **R load** → discontinuous above $\alpha = 0°$ for full converter; **RL load** → can be continuous.
> - **Rectification efficiency** (full converter, R load) = $\frac{8}{\pi^2} \approx 81.05\%$.
> - **TUF** (full converter, R load) = $\frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi^2} \approx 0.2864$.
> - **Ripple frequency** = $2f_s$ (full-wave) = **100 Hz** for 50 Hz supply.
> - **Each SCR conducts for π** (half-cycle) in a full-bridge.
> - **Lab safety:** Always use an **isolation transformer** before touching SCR gates; SCRs latch ON once fired — only turn OFF when current falls below holding current.
> - **Pulse transformer** isolation is **mandatory** in firing circuits to protect the control side from the power side.
> - **Displays in lab:** CRO is used for waveform observation (CH1 = $v_s$, CH2 = $v_o$ or $v_g$); DC voltmeter for $V_{o(avg)}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Source Inductance in Single-Phase Controlled Rectifier Feeding Highly Inductive Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL507 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Effect of Source Inductance in Single-Phase Controlled Rectifier Feeding Highly Inductive Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a **single-phase fully-controlled bridge rectifier**, the AC source is ideally modeled as a perfect sinusoidal voltage source with **zero internal impedance**. In practice, however, the supply possesses a finite **source inductance (Lₛ)** arising from the leakage reactance of the step-down transformer, the transmission/distribution line reactance, and any series filter inductance. When the rectifier feeds a **highly inductive load** (R-L with $L \to \infty$, i.e., a freewheeling-free DC motor armature or large inductor), the load current $I_0$ is assumed **constant and ripple-free**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Source Inductance (Lₛ):** The lumped series inductance of the AC supply, comprising transformer leakage inductance and line inductance, that opposes any sudden change in thyristor current.

The presence of $L_s$ prevents the instantaneous transfer (commutation) of load current from the outgoing thyristor to the incoming thyristor. Consequently, **both thyristors conduct simultaneously for a finite interval**, called the **commutation period** or **overlap period (γ)**. During this interval, the instantaneous output voltage departs from the ideal waveform, and the **average DC output voltage is reduced**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Commutation:** The process of transferring load current from one thyristor (or phase) to another in a controlled rectifier. In an ideal source (Lₛ = 0), this is instantaneous; in a practical source, it takes a finite angle γ.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Tap Water Tank" Intuition

Imagine two large overhead water tanks, A and B, connected to a common delivery pipe. A and B alternate in supplying water to the consumer.

- **Ideal case (Lₛ = 0):** The instant you open Tank B's valve and close Tank A's valve, the consumer feels an instantaneous switch — water flows from B with no interruption.
- **Real case (Lₛ ≠ 0):** Because of the inertia of the water column (analogous to magnetic flux/current inertia in an inductor), both valves must remain partially open for a brief moment. **Both tanks supply water together** for a short overlap time. The total "head" (pressure) during this overlap is the **average** of the two individual heads, and the **flowing volume** delivered to the consumer is less than expected — analogous to the **voltage drop** observed in the rectifier.

The brief period when both tanks are open is the **overlap angle γ**, and the loss of head/pressure corresponds to the **commutation voltage drop**:

$$\Delta V_0 \;=\; \frac{\omega L_s}{\pi}\, I_0$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** "Lₛ causes a *soft handover* between thyristors. The softer (larger) Lₛ, the longer the handover (γ), and the bigger the voltage you *lose*."

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Bolded Highlights)

- **Source Inductance ($L_s$)** — typically **1–10 mH** for laboratory transformer-rectifier setups.
- **Source Reactance ($X_s = \omega L_s$)** — the AC opposition offered by $L_s$ at supply frequency (e.g., for 50 Hz, $X_s = 314 \times L_s$).
- **Overlap Angle (γ)** — measured in **electrical degrees (°)** or **radians (rad)**.
- **Firing Angle (α)** — controlled by the gate-triggering circuit.
- **Continuous Conduction Condition (Highly Inductive Load):** $i_o(t) = I_0 =$ constant, with $L_{load} \to \infty$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Flow

Consider a single-phase fully-controlled bridge rectifier (T1, T2, T3, T4) with source inductance $L_s$ in series with each AC line, feeding a highly inductive load (R + large L) so that load current is **constant and continuous** at $I_0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For analysis, $L_s$ is split: $L_s/2$ on each AC line. Thus, when T1–T2 (positive half) and T3–T4 (negative half) commutate, the **commutating inductance is $2L_s$** in the loop.

1. **Steady state — Positive half (0 to α):** T1 and T2 are OFF (or not yet triggered). Source voltage is positive; load current freewheels through the previously conducting pair (T3, T4) or through a freewheeling diode, sustaining $I_0$.
2. **At ωt = α:** T1 and T2 are fired. Ideally, T3 and T4 should turn OFF immediately, but **source inductors oppose the current transfer**.
3. **Commutation phase (α to α + γ):**
   - Current in T1–T2 **rises** from 0 to $I_0$.
   - Current in T3–T4 **falls** from $I_0$ to 0.
   - **Both pairs conduct simultaneously.**
   - The two source inductors are effectively in series across the supply, **short-circuiting the source** through the four thyristors.
   - The instantaneous output voltage is the **average of the incoming and outgoing phase voltages**:
   
   $$v_o(\omega t) \;=\; \frac{v_s(\omega t) \;+\; (-v_s(\omega t))}{2} \;=\; 0$$
   
   for full bridge; equivalently, during commutation, $v_o$ is the mean of the two incoming and outgoing thyristor voltages.
4. **Post-commutation (α + γ to π + α):** T3–T4 are OFF, T1–T2 carry the full $I_0$, and $v_o = v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.
5. **Negative half (π + α to 2π + α):** Symmetric operation with T3–T4 triggered at $\pi + \alpha$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Range | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|---------|
| 1 | Ideal (no $L_s$) average output voltage | $V_{0,ideal} = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos(\alpha)$ | Volts | Continuous conduction, full bridge |
| 2 | Average output voltage **with** $L_s$ | $V_0 = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos(\alpha) - \dfrac{\omega L_s}{\pi}\, I_0$ | Volts | Highly inductive load, $I_0$ = const |
| 3 | Commutation (overlap) angle equation | $\cos(\alpha) - \cos(\alpha + \gamma) = \dfrac{2 \omega L_s\, I_0}{V_m} = \dfrac{2 X_s\, I_0}{V_m}$ | Dimensionless | Defines γ implicitly |
| 4 | Source reactance | $X_s = \omega L_s = 2 \pi f L_s$ | Ohms (Ω) | At supply frequency $f$ |
| 5 | Voltage drop due to commutation | $\Delta V_0 = \dfrac{X_s}{\pi}\, I_0$ | Volts | Per commutation per cycle (full bridge has **2** commutations) |
| 6 | Continuous conduction limit | $\alpha + \gamma \leq \pi \;(180^\circ)$ | Degrees | If exceeded, conduction becomes discontinuous |
| 7 | RMS output voltage | $V_{or} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{\gamma}{\pi}}$ (approx. for small γ) | Volts | KTU lab: compare measured vs. theoretical |
| 8 | Peak thyristor current | $I_T = I_0$ | Amperes | Steady-state thyristor current |
| 9 | Average thyristor current | $I_{T,avg} = I_0/2$ | Amperes | Half-wave conduction per thyristor |
| 10 | Power delivered to load | $P_0 = V_0 \times I_0$ | Watts | Practical measurement target |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Consistency:** Use **radians** for trigonometric arguments in equations, but convert to **degrees** for KTU exam answers. Always specify whether α, γ are in degrees or radians.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HV DC Transmission:** Line inductance is unavoidable, and γ (typically **15°–25°**) is compensated by **advancing the firing angle** (β-mode operation, where $\beta = \pi - \alpha$) to maintain the desired DC voltage.
- **DC Motor Drives (Phase-Fired Converters):** Commutation voltage drop affects the torque-speed characteristic; modern drives compensate digitally.
- **Battery Chargers / UPS Rectifiers:** Source inductance limits the maximum charging current at low α; design must ensure $\alpha + \gamma < 180^\circ$.
- **STATCOM and Active Rectifiers:** Same overlap equations apply; the drop is sometimes modeled as a fictitious source resistance $R_c = (2 X_s)/\pi$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Overlap Angle Equation (γ)

**Given:** Single-phase full converter with source inductance $L_s$ on each AC line, firing angle α, highly inductive load with constant $I_0$.

**Step 1 — Equivalent circuit during commutation.**

At the instant T1–T2 are fired (positive commutation, α to α + γ), both T1–T2 and T3–T4 conduct. The two source inductors appear in the short-circuit loop. The KVL around the commutation loop is:

$$v_s(\omega t) \;=\; L_s\, \frac{di_{T1}}{dt} \;-\; L_s\, \frac{di_{T3}}{dt}$$

Using $\dfrac{di_{T1}}{dt} - \dfrac{di_{T3}}{dt} = \dfrac{di_{T1}}{dt} + \dfrac{di_{T1}}{dt} = 2\,\dfrac{di_{T1}}{dt}$ (since $i_{T1} + i_{T3} = I_0$), we have:

$$v_s(\omega t) \;=\; 2 L_s\, \frac{di_{T1}}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from ωt = α to ωt = α + γ.**

$$V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) \;=\; 2 \omega L_s\, di_{T1}$$

$$\int_{\alpha}^{\alpha+\gamma} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) \;=\; 2 \omega L_s \int_{0}^{I_0} di_{T1}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_m \bigl[-\cos(\omega t)\bigr]_{\alpha}^{\alpha+\gamma} &\;=\; 2 \omega L_s\, I_0 \\
V_m \bigl[\cos(\alpha) - \cos(\alpha + \gamma)\bigr] &\;=\; 2 \omega L_s\, I_0 \\
\cos(\alpha) - \cos(\alpha + \gamma) &\;=\; \frac{2 \omega L_s\, I_0}{V_m} \\
\cos(\alpha) - \cos(\alpha + \gamma) &\;=\; \frac{2 X_s\, I_0}{V_m}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **overlap-angle transcendental equation** — the heart of the source-inductance analysis.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Average Output Voltage with Source Inductance

**Step 1 — Identify the output voltage waveform segments.**

The instantaneous output voltage has three regions per half-cycle:

- **0 to α:** (Previous pair conducting or freewheel) → $v_o = 0$ (for full bridge, depending on freewheeling action).
- **α to α + γ:** **Commutation** → $v_o = 0$ (the mean of $v_s$ and $-v_s$ is zero for a full bridge).
- **α + γ to π + α:** T1–T2 carrying full $I_0$ → $v_o = v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.

**Step 2 — Express the average over a full cycle (2π).**

$$V_0 \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\, \int_{\alpha+\gamma}^{\pi+\alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$V_0 \;=\; \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\, \bigl[-\cos(\omega t)\bigr]_{\alpha+\gamma}^{\pi+\alpha}$$

$$V_0 \;=\; \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\, \bigl[\cos(\alpha+\gamma) - \cos(\pi + \alpha)\bigr]$$

Since $\cos(\pi + \alpha) = -\cos(\alpha)$:

$$V_0 \;=\; \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\, \bigl[\cos(\alpha+\gamma) + \cos(\alpha)\bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the commutation identity.**

Using $\cos(\alpha) - \cos(\alpha+\gamma) = \dfrac{2 \omega L_s I_0}{V_m}$, we can also write:

$$\cos(\alpha+\gamma) \;=\; \cos(\alpha) - \frac{2 \omega L_s I_0}{V_m}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_0 &\;=\; \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\, \left[\cos(\alpha) - \frac{2 \omega L_s I_0}{V_m} + \cos(\alpha)\right] \\
V_0 &\;=\; \frac{V_m}{\pi}\, \cos(\alpha) - \frac{\omega L_s}{\pi}\, I_0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form.**

$$\boxed{\,V_0 \;=\; \frac{2 V_m}{\pi}\, \cos(\alpha) \;-\; \frac{\omega L_s}{\pi}\, I_0\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **linear superposition**: the average output voltage = ideal no-$L_s$ voltage − resistive-like drop $\dfrac{\omega L_s I_0}{\pi}$. The drop is **proportional to load current**, mimicking a resistor of value $\dfrac{\omega L_s}{\pi}$ (or equivalently $\dfrac{2 X_s}{\pi}$ for a full bridge with two commutations per cycle).

## 3.3 Sample Worked Numerical (Model Calculation)

**Problem:** Single-phase full converter, $V_s = 230$ V (rms), 50 Hz, $L_s = 5$ mH, $\alpha = 30^\circ$, $I_0 = 10$ A. Find γ and $V_0$.

**Solution:**

$$V_m \;=\; 230\sqrt{2} \;\approx\; 325.27 \text{ V}$$

$$X_s \;=\; 2\pi \times 50 \times 5\times 10^{-3} \;\approx\; 1.571 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 1 — Set up the transcendental equation:**

$$\cos(30^\circ) - \cos(30^\circ + \gamma) \;=\; \frac{2 \times 1.571 \times 10}{325.27}$$

$$0.8660 - \cos(30^\circ + \gamma) \;=\; 0.0966$$

$$\cos(30^\circ + \gamma) \;=\; 0.8660 - 0.0966 \;=\; 0.7694$$

**Step 2 — Solve for γ:**

$$30^\circ + \gamma \;=\; \cos^{-1}(0.7694) \;\approx\; 39.74^\circ$$

$$\gamma \;\approx\; 9.74^\circ \;(\approx 0.170 \text{ rad})$$

**Step 3 — Compute $V_0$:**

$$V_0 \;=\; \frac{2 \times 325.27}{\pi} \cos(30^\circ) - \frac{2 \times 1.571 \times 10}{\pi}$$

$$V_0 \;\approx\; 207.07 \times 0.8660 - 10.0 \;\approx\; 169.3 \text{ V}$$

**Comparison with ideal (Lₛ = 0):**

$$V_{0,ideal} \;=\; 207.07 \times 0.8660 \;\approx\; 179.3 \text{ V}$$

**Voltage drop = 179.3 − 169.3 ≈ 10.0 V**, exactly matching the formula $\Delta V_0 = X_s I_0 /\pi$? Actually for one commutation per half-cycle and 2 per cycle, $\Delta V_0 = (2 X_s I_0)/\pi = 10.0$ V. ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Varying Parameters

| Parameter Increased | Effect on γ | Effect on $V_0$ | Practical Observation |
|--------------------|-------------|------------------|------------------------|
| Firing angle α (up to 90°) | **Decreases** | **Decreases** (cos α) | DC voltage drops; commutation is faster |
| Firing angle α (> 90°, inversion) | **Increases** | Increases (negative to positive) | Used in DC motor regenerative braking |
| Source inductance $L_s$ | **Increases** | **Decreases** (via $\omega L_s I_0 / \pi$) | Softer handover, more voltage loss |
| Load current $I_0$ | **Increases** | **Decreases** | Heavier load → more drop |
| Supply frequency $f$ | **Increases** | **Decreases** | Higher $X_s$ at same $L_s$ |
| Supply voltage $V_m$ (relative to $I_0$) | **Decreases** | Increases (linearly) | Stronger source resists voltage loss |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Block Architecture (Mermaid)

The following diagram depicts the functional power flow and commutation logic of a single-phase full-controlled bridge with source inductance feeding a highly inductive load.

```mermaid
graph TD
    ACSRC[AC Source 230V 50Hz] --> LSA[Source Inductance Ls on Line A]
    ACSRC --> LSB[Source Inductance Ls on Line B]
    LSA --> T1[Thyristor T1]
    LSA --> T3[Thyristor T3]
    LSB --> T2[Thyristor T2]
    LSB --> T4[Thyristor T4]
    T1 --> DCPLUS[DC Bus Positive]
    T2 --> DCPLUS
    T3 --> DCMINUS[DC Bus Negative]
    T4 --> DCMINUS
    DCPLUS --> LOAD[Highly Inductive Load R plus Large L]
    DCMINUS --> LOAD
    LOAD --> IOUT[Constant DC Current I0]
    GATECLK[Gate Pulse Generator alpha firing] --> T1
    GATECLK --> T2
    GATECLK --> T3
    GATECLK --> T4
    COMMUT[Commutation Logic alpha to alpha plus gamma] --> T1
    COMMUT --> T3
```

## 4.2 Output Voltage Waveform Schematic (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Interval 1: alpha to alpha+gamma Overlap ON] -->|v_o = 0| S2[Interval 2: alpha+gamma to pi Conduction ON]
    S2 -->|v_o = Vm sin wt| S3[Interval 3: pi to pi+alpha Negative Half Off]
    S3 -->|v_o = 0| S4[Interval 4: pi+alpha to pi+alpha+gamma Overlap ON]
    S4 -->|v_o = 0| S5[Interval 5: pi+alpha+gamma to 2pi Conduction ON]
    S5 -->|v_o = -Vm sin wt| S6[Interval 6: 2pi to 2pi+alpha Off]
    S6 -->|Cycle Repeats| S1
```

## 4.3 ASCII Waveform Sketch (Quick-Reference for Lab Notebook)

```
v_s, v_o, i_T
   ^                          
v_s|    ____          ____    
   |   /    \        /    \   
   |  /      \      /      \  
   | /        \    /        \ 
   |/          \  /          \ 
0--+------------\/------------\+----> ωt
   |   α   ↑    /\  π+α   ↑   /\
   |  / \  |   /  \       |  /  \
i_T1| /   \ |  /    \      | /    \
   |/     \| /      \     |/      \ 
   |        \/        \    +-------- 
   |        α+γ        \  /          
   |                    \/           
   |  T3, T4 currents (mirror)      
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** During the brief overlap (α to α+γ), the load voltage $v_o$ is **clamped near zero** (or the mean of the two phase voltages), and both pairs of thyristors share $I_0$.

## 4.4 Phasor-Level Equivalent Model

For steady-state DC-side modeling, the source inductance is approximated as an **equivalent resistance** $R_c$:

$$R_c \;=\; \frac{2 X_s}{\pi} \;=\; \frac{2 \omega L_s}{\pi}$$

So the practical converter is modeled as an **ideal rectifier in series with $R_c$** feeding the DC load — this is a standard simplification used in **DC drive textbooks** (R. Krishnan, M. H. Rashid) and in KTU ESE problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the terms "commutation" and "overlap angle (γ)" in the context of a single-phase controlled rectifier with source inductance. Why does overlap occur?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Commutation (1 mark):** The process of transferring load current from one conducting thyristor (or thyristor pair) to the next.
- **Overlap angle γ (1 mark):** The electrical angle (in degrees or radians) during which both the outgoing and incoming thyristors conduct simultaneously.
- **Reason for overlap (1 mark):** Source inductance $L_s$ opposes sudden change in current. Since $V = L \, di/dt$, the current through an inductor cannot change instantaneously, so the outgoing thyristor cannot turn OFF until its current decays to zero, while the incoming thyristor current ramps up — both must conduct together for the finite angle γ.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State the equation relating α, γ, $X_s$, $I_0$, and $V_m$ for a single-phase full converter with source inductance. What happens to γ as load current $I_0$ increases?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Equation (2 marks):** $\cos(\alpha) - \cos(\alpha + \gamma) = \dfrac{2 X_s I_0}{V_m}$
- **Trend (1 mark):** As $I_0$ increases, the right-hand side increases, so $\cos(\alpha + \gamma)$ must decrease, meaning $(\alpha + \gamma)$ increases, so **γ increases**. (Larger current → longer overlap.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is provided — students answer **either Question A or Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** With the help of equivalent circuit and waveforms, **explain the commutation process** in a single-phase full-controlled bridge rectifier feeding a highly inductive load when the source has inductance $L_s$. Derive the expression for the **overlap angle γ** in terms of α, $X_s$, $I_0$, and $V_m$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Draw circuit with split source inductance ($L_s$ on each line) and mark T1–T4, load (R + large L), and the commutation loop.
- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Mark waveform: $v_s$, $v_o$, $i_{T1}$, $i_{T3}$ — show the overlap region (α to α+γ) clearly.
- **Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply KVL during commutation: $v_s = 2 L_s \, \dfrac{di_{T1}}{dt}$. Substitute $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, integrate from α to α+γ with $i_{T1}: 0 \to I_0$.
- **Step 4 (2 marks):** Obtain $\cos(\alpha) - \cos(\alpha+\gamma) = \dfrac{2 \omega L_s I_0}{V_m} = \dfrac{2 X_s I_0}{V_m}$ — write final boxed equation.
- **Step 5 (1 mark):** Note the limiting condition $\alpha + \gamma \leq 180^\circ$ for continuous conduction.

**(b)** A single-phase full converter is supplied from a **230 V, 50 Hz** source through a transformer with total leakage inductance of **4 mH**. The converter operates at firing angle $\alpha = 45^\circ$ and supplies a constant DC current of **15 A** to a highly inductive load. **Calculate** (i) the overlap angle γ, (ii) the average output voltage $V_0$, and (iii) the commutation voltage drop $\Delta V_0$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** $V_m = 230\sqrt{2} = 325.27$ V, $X_s = 2\pi \times 50 \times 4 \times 10^{-3} = 1.257$ Ω.
- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply the equation: $\cos(45^\circ) - \cos(45^\circ + \gamma) = \dfrac{2 \times 1.257 \times 15}{325.27}$
  
  $$0.7071 - \cos(45^\circ + \gamma) = 0.1159$$
  
  $$\cos(45^\circ + \gamma) = 0.5912$$
  
  $$45^\circ + \gamma = \cos^{-1}(0.5912) = 53.76^\circ \Rightarrow \gamma = 8.76^\circ$$
- **Step 3 (2 marks):** $V_0 = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos(\alpha) - \dfrac{X_s I_0}{\pi} \times 2$
  
  $$V_0 = \frac{2 \times 325.27}{\pi} \times 0.7071 - \frac{2 \times 1.257 \times 15}{\pi}$$
  
  $$V_0 = 146.45 - 12.00 = 134.45 \text{ V}$$
  
  (Alternatively use $V_0 = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}[\cos(\alpha+\gamma) + \cos(\alpha)]$: $V_0 = \frac{325.27}{2\pi}(0.5912 + 0.7071) = 134.45$ V ✓)
- **Step 4 (2 marks):** $\Delta V_0 = V_{0,ideal} - V_0 = 146.45 - 134.45 = 12.00$ V. This matches $\Delta V_0 = \dfrac{2 X_s I_0}{\pi} = 12.00$ V. Conclude with the comment: "The commutation overlap reduces the output voltage by ~8.2%."

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme Alternative]*

**(a)** **Derive** the expression for the **average output voltage** of a single-phase full-controlled rectifier feeding a highly inductive load, including the effect of source inductance $L_s$. Show that the commutation voltage drop is $\Delta V_0 = \dfrac{2 X_s I_0}{\pi}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** State the piecewise waveform of $v_o$ over one cycle: zero during overlap, $V_m \sin(\omega t)$ otherwise.
- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Write the average integral: $V_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha+\gamma}^{\pi+\alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t) d(\omega t)$ and evaluate to get $V_0 = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}[\cos(\alpha+\gamma) + \cos(\alpha)]$.
- **Step 3 (2 marks):** Use $\cos(\alpha+\gamma) = \cos(\alpha) - \dfrac{2 X_s I_0}{V_m}$ to obtain $V_0 = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}\cos(\alpha) - \dfrac{2 X_s I_0}{\pi}$.
- **Step 4 (2 marks):** Identify $\Delta V_0 = \dfrac{2 X_s I_0}{\pi}$ and interpret as an equivalent resistance $R_c = \dfrac{2 X_s}{\pi}$ (write this boxed).

**(b)** A laboratory single-phase full converter is operated with the following parameters: $V_s = 110$ V (rms), $f = 50$ Hz, $L_s = 3$ mH, $I_0 = 8$ A, and the converter is fired at $\alpha = 60^\circ$. **Calculate** (i) the overlap angle γ, (ii) the average output voltage with and without source inductance, and (iii) plot the trend of γ vs. $I_0$ for $I_0 = 5, 10, 15, 20$ A. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** $V_m = 110\sqrt{2} = 155.56$ V, $X_s = 2\pi(50)(3\times 10^{-3}) = 0.9425$ Ω.
- **Step 2 (1 mark):** For $I_0 = 8$ A: $\cos(60^\circ) - \cos(60^\circ + \gamma) = \dfrac{2(0.9425)(8)}{155.56} = 0.0969$
  
  $\cos(60^\circ + \gamma) = 0.4031 \Rightarrow 60^\circ + \gamma = 66.23^\circ \Rightarrow \gamma \approx 6.23^\circ$
- **Step 3 (2 marks):**
  - $V_{0,ideal} = \dfrac{2(155.56)}{\pi}\cos(60^\circ) = 99.03 \times 0.5 = 49.51$ V
  - $V_0 = 49.51 - \dfrac{2(0.9425)(8)}{\pi} = 49.51 - 4.80 = 44.71$ V
  - $\Delta V_0 = 4.80$ V (≈ 9.7% reduction)
- **Step 4 (3 marks):** For the table:
  
  | $I_0$ (A) | RHS = $2 X_s I_0 / V_m$ | $\cos(60^\circ+\gamma)$ | $\gamma$ (°) |
  |-----------|--------------------------|------------------------|--------------|
  | 5 | 0.0606 | 0.4394 | 4.83 |
  | 10 | 0.1212 | 0.3788 | 9.74 |
  | 15 | 0.1817 | 0.3183 | 14.74 |
  | 20 | 0.2423 | 0.2577 | 20.07 |
  
  **Observation (bonus mark if mentioned):** γ increases monotonically and nonlinearly with $I_0$; the rate of increase accelerates as $I_0$ grows.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost 2–3 marks each — avoid these!**
>
> 1. **Mixing up the L value:** $L_s$ is on **each AC line**, so the **commutating inductance is $2L_s$**, not $L_s$. Wrong substitution gives γ off by 50%.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $V_0$ drop:** For a single-phase full bridge, there are **2 commutations per cycle**, so $\Delta V_0 = 2 X_s I_0 / \pi$, not $X_s I_0 / \pi$. Half-controlled bridges have **only 1** commutation per cycle (natural + line commutation) — read the question carefully.
> 3. **Degree vs. radian confusion:** $\cos(\alpha)$ requires α in **radians** for calculator entry; for written answers, **convert back to degrees** and clearly state units.
> 4. **Missing continuous-conduction condition:** Always check $\alpha + \gamma \leq 180^\circ$. If violated, the derived formulas break down (discontinuous mode).
> 5. **Not commenting on practical $V_0$ drop:** Examiners expect the qualitative remark: *"Lₛ causes a voltage drop that is proportional to I₀, equivalent to a resistance of $2X_s/\pi$ in series."*
> 6. **Lab-record pitfall:** Many students forget to **label axes, units, and zero levels** in the oscilloscope waveform sketch. Marks are deducted even if the waveform is correct.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the day of exam**

- ✅ **Source inductance $L_s$** is the **practical** non-ideality of the AC supply; it always exists due to transformer leakage and line reactance.
- ✅ **Overlap angle γ** is the heart of the analysis — both thyristors conduct together for γ during commutation.
- ✅ **Two key equations to memorize verbatim:**
  
  $$\cos(\alpha) - \cos(\alpha + \gamma) = \frac{2 X_s I_0}{V_m} \quad\text{and}\quad V_0 = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi}\cos(\alpha) - \frac{2 X_s I_0}{\pi}$$
  
- ✅ **Commutating inductance = $2L_s$** (for split source inductance on each line).
- ✅ **Equivalent resistance model:** $R_c = 2 X_s / \pi$ in series with the ideal DC source.
- ✅ **Continuous conduction condition:** $\alpha + \gamma \leq 180^\circ$.
- ✅ **As $I_0 \uparrow$ → γ $\uparrow$ → $V_0 \downarrow$.** As $\alpha \uparrow$ (toward 90°) → γ $\downarrow$ and $V_0 \downarrow$.
- ✅ **During overlap, $v_o$ is the mean of incoming and outgoing phase voltages** — not zero (in single-phase full bridge, it becomes zero; in three-phase it equals the mean of two phase voltages).
- ✅ **Lab measurement tips:** Use current shunt or Hall-effect sensor (never across an inductor directly); trigger scope on gate pulse; synchronize the time-base to the supply frequency.
- ✅ **KTU's favourite follow-up:** "How is commutation voltage drop compensated in HVDC?" — Answer: **margin angle** (δ) or **advance angle** (β) in inverter mode.
- ✅ **Half-wave vs. full-wave distinction:** For a half-controlled (semi-converter) bridge, the overlap equation has the same form but γ occurs only once per cycle → drop formula changes accordingly.
- ✅ **Always state assumptions** in derivations: continuous conduction, constant $I_0$, sinusoidal source, lumped $L_s$.
- ✅ **Avoid units confusion:** $L_s$ in **H (Henry)**, $X_s$ in **Ω (Ohm)**, $V_m$ in **V (Volt)**, $I_0$ in **A (Ampere)**.

---

> **END OF NOTES — KTU 2024 SCHEME | PCEOL507 | Module Experiment 2**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL507 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Single-Phase Half-Controlled/Fully-Controlled Rectifier Fed PMDC/Separately Excited DC Motor Drive

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Single-Phase Controlled Rectifier Fed DC Motor Drive** is a power electronic converter system that uses **thyristor-based AC-DC converters** to deliver a variable DC voltage to a DC motor from a fixed-magnitude, fixed-frequency single-phase AC supply. The converter is employed to vary the average armature voltage, thereby controlling the speed and torque of the motor.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Canonical):**
> A controlled rectifier uses **thyristors (SCRs)** as switching elements, and the average output DC voltage is regulated by varying the **firing angle (α)** — the delay angle from the natural commutation instant of the diode equivalent circuit.

The two principal configurations studied in this experiment are:

| Configuration | Abbreviation | Number of SCRs | Freewheeling Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fully-Controlled Bridge Rectifier (Full Converter)** | 1-φ Full Converter | 4 SCRs | No internal FWD; requires external FWD for discontinuous conduction |
| **Half-Controlled Bridge Rectifier (Semi-Converter)** | 1-φ Semi-Converter | 2 SCRs + 2 Diodes | Natural freewheeling through the diode leg |

The motor load in the experiment is either:
- **PMDC Motor** — Permanent Magnet DC motor (field flux is constant, set by permanent magnets).
- **Separately Excited DC Motor** — Field winding is energized by an independent DC source; flux can be varied.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world analogy: The "Dimmer Switch for a DC Fan"**
> Imagine a ceiling fan running on DC. Instead of a simple switch, you use a **light dimmer-style electronic knob** that chops the AC mains and reshapes it into a variable DC voltage. Turning the knob delays the instant the power is "let through" each half-cycle — the larger the delay (firing angle), the lower the average DC voltage reaching the fan, and therefore the slower the fan rotates. This delay knob is exactly the **firing angle α** in a controlled rectifier.

A simple "tap on the wire" analogy:
- A **diode** is like a **one-way valve** that opens automatically at the right time — you have no control.
- A **thyristor (SCR)** is like a **one-way valve that opens only when you "tap" it** (apply a gate pulse). The **timing of the tap** is the firing angle α.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Supply frequency:** $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$ (India), giving $T = 20 \text{ ms}$, $\omega = 2\pi f = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$.
- **Peak supply voltage:** $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms}$.
- **Firing angle range:** $0° \le \alpha \le 180°$ (full converter), $0° \le \alpha \le 180°$ (semi-converter).
- **Conduction modes:** Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM) and Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM).
- **Speed reference frame:** rad/s (mechanical) or RPM (1 RPM = 2π/60 rad/s).

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus highlight:** The 2024 KTU scheme emphasizes **waveform analysis**, **average output voltage derivation**, and **speed-torque characteristic plotting** for both converter topologies driving both motor types. Continuous conduction is the default assumption unless otherwise stated.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step by Step

### A. Single-Phase Fully-Controlled Bridge (Full Converter)

1. **Bridge structure:** Four SCRs arranged as T1–T2 (positive half-cycle pair) and T3–T4 (negative half-cycle pair).
2. **Natural commutation points:** At $\omega t = 0°, 180°, 360°, \ldots$ — these are the instants the diode-equivalent bridge would start conducting.
3. **Firing sequence:** T1, T2 are fired at $\alpha$; T3, T4 are fired at $\pi + \alpha$.
4. **Output voltage polarity:** Always positive (rectified). Output current direction depends on load.
5. **Output voltage waveform for $\alpha \le 90°$:** Average voltage $V_0 > 0$ → rectifier (motoring) mode.
6. **Output voltage waveform for $\alpha > 90°$:** Average voltage $V_0 < 0$ → inverter (regenerative braking) mode.
7. **Discontinuous conduction:** Appears at light loads; without an external freewheeling diode (FWD), the current becomes discontinuous when back-EMF dominates.

### B. Single-Phase Half-Controlled Bridge (Semi-Converter)

1. **Bridge structure:** Two SCRs (T1, T3) on the top/bottom of one leg, and two diodes (D2, D4) on the other leg.
2. **Freewheeling path:** When the supply voltage reverses polarity, the diodes D2 and D4 conduct simultaneously, providing a natural freewheeling path — **no external FWD required**.
3. **Output voltage range:** Always $V_0 \ge 0$ regardless of $\alpha$. Cannot operate in inverter mode.
4. **Conduction:** Firing T1 and T2 (or equivalent pair) at $\alpha$ controls the positive output; the negative half is naturally clipped.
5. **Waveform advantage:** Lower ripple, smoother current, simpler control — preferred for **low-power DC drives**.

### C. Motor Action

- **Armature reaction:** At higher armature currents, the armature flux distorts the main field. In a separately excited motor, this is mitigated by **compensating windings** or by limiting armature current.
- **Back-EMF:** $E_b = K \Phi \omega$ — opposes the applied voltage and is proportional to speed and flux.
- **Steady-state speed equation:**
$$N = \frac{V_0 - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$$
- **Torque equation:** $T = K \Phi I_a$ — torque is directly proportional to armature current.
- **Speed-torque characteristic** is a **straight line with negative slope** when $V_0$ and $\Phi$ are held constant. Lower $V_0$ shifts the line parallel downward.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table** — Memorize this for the lab viva and ESE.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Peak supply voltage | $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_s$ | $V_s$ = RMS supply |
| 2 | Average output voltage — Full Converter (CCM) | $V_0 = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha$ | Valid for $0° \le \alpha \le 180°$ |
| 3 | Average output voltage — Semi-Converter (CCM) | $V_0 = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi} (1 + \cos \alpha)$ | Valid for $0° \le \alpha \le 180°$ |
| 4 | RMS output voltage — Full Converter | $V_{0,rms} = V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ | Used for harmonic loss calculation |
| 5 | RMS output voltage — Semi-Converter | $V_{0,rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ | — |
| 6 | Back-EMF | $E_b = K \Phi \omega$ | $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 7 | Armature voltage equation | $V_0 = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Neglecting brush drop |
| 8 | Speed (rad/s) | $\omega = \dfrac{V_0 - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$ | — |
| 9 | Speed in RPM | $N = \dfrac{60 (V_0 - I_a R_a)}{2 \pi K \Phi}$ | $K \Phi$ in V·s/rad |
| 10 | Developed torque | $T = K \Phi I_a$ | $K \Phi$ constant for PMDC |
| 11 | Mechanical power developed | $P_m = T \omega = E_b I_a$ | $E_b I_a$ = electrical → mechanical |
| 12 | Discontinuous conduction angle (Full Conv.) | $\beta - \alpha = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\cos \alpha - \dfrac{2 I_a R_a}{V_m}\right)$ | $\beta$ = extinction angle |
| 13 | Critical (continuous) current | $I_{cr} = \dfrac{V_m \sin \alpha}{2 \omega L_a}$ (full conv.) | Threshold for CCM |
| 14 | No-load speed | $N_0 = \dfrac{60 V_0}{2 \pi K \Phi}$ | At $I_a = 0$ |
| 15 | Full-load speed | $N_{fl} = N_0 - \dfrac{60 I_{a,fl} R_a}{2 \pi K \Phi}$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on table syntax:** All absolute-value bars are rendered with `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact — this is the KTU-Premier-Engine formatting safeguard.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial DC drives** for paper mills, rolling mills, elevators, and printing machines where a wide, smooth speed range is required.
- **Traction drives** (older locomotives, electric vehicles with DC traction motors) — use controlled rectifiers for soft start and regenerative braking.
- **Battery chargers** with controlled voltage and current profiles.
- **Servo systems** in CNC machines — DC motors with controlled-rectifier supplies provide precise torque and speed regulation.
- **Renewable systems** — small DC generators (wind/Pico-hydro) charging batteries through controlled rectifiers with MPPT-style voltage tracking.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Output Voltage of 1-φ Full Converter

**Problem setup:** A single-phase full-bridge rectifier with thyristors T1, T2, T3, T4 is fed from $v_s = V_m \sin \omega t$. T1 and T2 are fired at $\omega t = \alpha$, T3 and T4 at $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$. Derive the average output voltage expression $V_0 = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha$.

**Step 1 — Identify the conduction intervals.**

During $\alpha \le \omega t \le \pi + \alpha$, T1 and T2 conduct, so the output voltage equals the source voltage:

$$v_0(\omega t) = V_m \sin(\omega t), \quad \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi + \alpha$$

During $\pi + \alpha \le \omega t \le 2\pi + \alpha$, T3 and T4 conduct, again giving $v_0 = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ because $\sin(\omega t)$ is already negative in that interval. So the output waveform repeats with period $\pi$.

**Step 2 — Write the average value definition over one output period.**

The output period is $\pi$ (one-half of the supply period). Average value:

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration.**

$$V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[-\cos(\omega t)\Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the limits.**

$$V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[-\cos(\pi + \alpha) + \cos \alpha\Big]$$

Using the identity $\cos(\pi + \alpha) = -\cos \alpha$:

$$V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[-(-\cos \alpha) + \cos \alpha\Big]$$

**Step 5 — Simplify.**

$$V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \cdot 2 \cos \alpha = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha$$

**Step 6 — Sign analysis.**

- For $0° \le \alpha < 90°$: $\cos \alpha > 0 \Rightarrow V_0 > 0$ → **rectifier (motoring)** mode.
- For $\alpha = 90°$: $V_0 = 0$.
- For $90° < \alpha \le 180°$: $\cos \alpha < 0 \Rightarrow V_0 < 0$ → **inverter (regeneration)** mode.

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{\,V_0 = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha \quad \text{(1-φ Full Converter, CCM)}\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Average Output Voltage of 1-φ Semi-Converter

**Step 1 — Output waveform description.**

The semi-converter produces a positive output $v_0 = V_m \sin \omega t$ from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\omega t = \pi$, and then **freewheels at zero** from $\omega t = \pi$ to $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$ (i.e., from $\pi$ until the next firing). It repeats every $\pi$ radians.

**Step 2 — Average value over one period ($\pi$):**

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) + \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\pi}^{\pi + \alpha} 0 \, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the first integral:**

$$V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[-\cos(\omega t)\Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[-\cos \pi + \cos \alpha\Big]$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\cos \pi = -1$:**

$$V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi} (1 + \cos \alpha)$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{\,V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi} (1 + \cos \alpha) \quad \text{(1-φ Semi-Converter, CCM)}\,}$$

Note: $V_0 \ge 0$ for all $\alpha$ — no inverter operation, so the semi-converter is restricted to **motoring and freewheeling** only.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Speed Equation of Separately Excited / PMDC Motor Fed by Controlled Rectifier

**Step 1 — Armature loop KVL.**

The motor armature is fed by $V_0$, with back-EMF $E_b$, armature resistance $R_a$, and inductance $L_a$. For a steady-state DC analysis (DC values):

$$V_0 = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the back-EMF relation.**

$$V_0 = K \Phi \omega + I_a R_a$$

**Step 3 — Solve for angular speed $\omega$.**

$$\omega = \frac{V_0 - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to RPM using $\omega = 2 \pi N / 60$:**

$$N = \frac{60}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{V_0 - I_a R_a}{K \Phi} = \frac{60 (V_0 - I_a R_a)}{2 \pi K \Phi}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $V_0$ from the converter formula. For a full converter:**

$$N = \frac{60}{2 \pi K \Phi} \left(\frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha - I_a R_a\right)$$

**Step 6 — No-load speed ($I_a \to 0$):**

$$N_0 = \frac{60 V_0}{2 \pi K \Phi} = \frac{60}{2 \pi K \Phi} \cdot \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha$$

**Step 7 — Key observation:** $N_0 \propto V_0 \propto \cos \alpha$. So **firing angle α controls no-load speed linearly with $\cos \alpha$**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Example (Typical KTU 3-Mark or 7-Mark Part Question)**

**Given:**
- 1-φ full converter, $V_s = 230 \text{ V (RMS)}$, $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$.
- PMDC motor: $R_a = 0.5 \text{ }\Omega$, $K \Phi = 0.05 \text{ V·s/rad}$.
- Firing angle $\alpha = 30°$, motor current $I_a = 10 \text{ A}$.

**Find:** (a) Average output voltage, (b) Motor speed in RPM, (c) Developed torque.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**

$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27 \text{ V}$$

$$V_0 = \frac{2 \times 325.27}{\pi} \cos 30° = \frac{650.54}{\pi} \times 0.8660 \approx 179.3 \text{ V}$$

**Part (b):**

$$\omega = \frac{V_0 - I_a R_a}{K \Phi} = \frac{179.3 - 10 \times 0.5}{0.05} = \frac{174.3}{0.05} = 3486 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$N = \frac{60 \times 3486}{2 \pi} = 33{,}288 \text{ RPM}$$

**Part (c):**

$$T = K \Phi \cdot I_a = 0.05 \times 10 = 0.5 \text{ N·m}$$

**Answer Summary:**
- $V_0 \approx 179.3 \text{ V}$
- $N \approx 33{,}288 \text{ RPM}$
- $T = 0.5 \text{ N·m}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** A very common error is to compute $V_m$ using peak-to-peak or to confuse degrees with radians in the cosine term. Always verify the calculator is in **degree mode** for these standard problems.

---

## 3.5 Discontinuous Conduction Analysis (Full Converter)

**Setup:** The armature inductance $L_a$ is finite, and the current can become zero during the off period if the back-EMF is high. Extinction angle $\beta$ satisfies:

$$V_m \sin \beta = E_b + I_a R_a$$

After $\beta$, current is zero and $v_0 = E_b$ (motor coasts).

**Energy balance equation (current must integrate to its average value):**

$$I_a = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} \frac{V_m \sin \omega t - E_b}{R_a} d(\omega t) \quad \text{(steady state)}$$

Solving for $E_b$ (or $I_a$) yields the discontinuous boundary curves, plotted as $V_0$ vs $I_a$ for fixed $\alpha$. The **boundary between CCM and DCM** is the locus of the critical current:

$$I_{cr} = \frac{V_m \sin \alpha}{2 \omega L_a}$$

Above $I_{cr}$ → CCM; below → DCM.
