# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier feeding a separately excited DC motor. Derive the expression for average output voltage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Circuit diagram:** A 1-φ full bridge with 4 SCRs (T1–T4); AC source $v_s$ on one diagonal, DC motor armature $R_a$, $L_a$, $E_b$ on the other diagonal.
- (1 mark) **Operation:** T1, T2 are gated at $\omega t = \alpha$ (positive half); T3, T4 at $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$ (negative half). Output voltage is $v_0 = V_m \sin \omega t$ for $\alpha \le \omega t \le \pi + \alpha$.
- (1 mark) **Average voltage:** $V_0 = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha$.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Compare single-phase full converter and semi-converter feeding a DC motor drive. List two advantages of a semi-converter over a full converter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **SCRs/Diodes count:** Full converter — 4 SCRs; Semi-converter — 2 SCRs + 2 diodes.
- (1 mark) **Inverter operation:** Full converter supports $\alpha > 90°$ (regenerative); Semi-converter does not.
- (1 mark) **Two advantages of semi-converter:**
  1. **Natural freewheeling** via diode leg — no external FWD required, simpler circuitry.
  2. **Lower ripple** in output current → smoother motor torque, less acoustic noise.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question B — Option (A)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**A separately excited DC motor is fed from a 1-φ full converter. The supply is 230 V, 50 Hz. Motor parameters: $R_a = 0.8 \text{ }\Omega$, $K \Phi = 0.08 \text{ V·s/rad}$. Firing angle $\alpha = 45°$, armature current $I_a = 15 \text{ A}$.**

**(a)** Derive the average output voltage expression for a 1-φ full converter and calculate its value for $\alpha = 45°$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the motor speed in RPM, the developed torque, and the mechanical power output. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Identify the conduction intervals. T1, T2 conduct from $\alpha$ to $\pi + \alpha$; T3, T4 from $\pi + \alpha$ to $2\pi + \alpha$. Output is the rectified sinusoid delayed by $\alpha$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Average over a half-period (π):

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3:** Integration:

$$V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\left[\cos \alpha - \cos(\pi + \alpha)\right] \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4:** Use $\cos(\pi + \alpha) = -\cos \alpha$:

$$V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi} (2 \cos \alpha) = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5:** Numerical calculation:

$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27 \text{ V} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

$$V_0 = \frac{2 \times 325.27}{\pi} \cos 45° = \frac{650.54}{\pi} \times 0.7071 \approx 146.4 \text{ V} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Armature KVL: $V_0 = E_b + I_a R_a$, so $E_b = V_0 - I_a R_a$. **(1 mark)**

$$E_b = 146.4 - 15 \times 0.8 = 146.4 - 12 = 134.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2:** Speed: $\omega = \dfrac{E_b}{K \Phi}$. **(1 mark)**

$$\omega = \frac{134.4}{0.08} = 1680 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to RPM:

$$N = \frac{60 \times 1680}{2 \pi} = \frac{100{,}800}{6.2832} \approx 16{,}043 \text{ RPM} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4:** Developed torque: $T = K \Phi \cdot I_a$. **(1 mark)**

$$T = 0.08 \times 15 = 1.2 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5:** Mechanical power: $P_m = T \cdot \omega$. **(1 mark)**

$$P_m = 1.2 \times 1680 = 2016 \text{ W} = 2.016 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6:** Verification using $P_m = E_b I_a$: $134.4 \times 15 = 2016 \text{ W}$ ✓ **(1 mark)**

**Final Answers:**
- $V_0 \approx 146.4 \text{ V}$
- $N \approx 16{,}043 \text{ RPM}$
- $T = 1.2 \text{ N·m}$
- $P_m = 2.016 \text{ kW}$

---

### **Question B — Option (B)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**A PMDC motor is supplied by a 1-φ semi-converter. Supply: 220 V, 50 Hz. Motor: $R_a = 1.0 \text{ }\Omega$, $K \Phi = 0.06 \text{ V·s/rad}$, $\alpha = 60°$, $I_a = 8 \text{ A}$.**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a 1-φ semi-converter. Derive the average output voltage and compute its value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the speed in RPM, the developed torque, and the input power factor of the converter. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Circuit description: 2 SCRs (T1, T3) in one leg; 2 diodes (D2, D4) in the other. T1 and T3 are fired at $\alpha$ and $\pi + \alpha$ respectively. D2, D4 conduct during the freewheeling interval. **(2 marks)**

**Step 2:** Conduction:
- $\alpha \to \pi$: T1, D4 conduct; $v_0 = V_m \sin \omega t$.
- $\pi \to \pi + \alpha$: D2, D4 freewheel; $v_0 = 0$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Derive $V_0$:

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{\pi} (1 + \cos \alpha) \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate:

$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 220 = 311.13 \text{ V} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

$$V_0 = \frac{311.13}{\pi} (1 + \cos 60°) = 99.03 \times 1.5 \approx 148.5 \text{ V} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Back-EMF: $E_b = V_0 - I_a R_a = 148.5 - 8 \times 1.0 = 140.5 \text{ V}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Speed:

$$\omega = \frac{140.5}{0.06} = 2341.7 \text{ rad/s} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

$$N = \frac{60 \times 2341.7}{2 \pi} \approx 22{,}358 \text{ RPM} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3:** Torque: $T = 0.06 \times 8 = 0.48 \text{ N·m}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4:** Input power factor (semi-converter, CCM):

The displacement factor is $\cos \alpha$; the distortion factor is $\dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi}$. So: **(1 mark)**

$$\text{PF} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cos \alpha = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \times 0.5 \approx 0.45 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5:** Verify with input current: RMS supply current (assuming rectangular, ripple-free) $I_{s,rms} = I_a$ for a semi-converter, so input apparent power $S = V_s \cdot I_a = 220 \times 8 = 1760 \text{ VA}$; real input power $P = V_0 I_a = 148.5 \times 8 = 1188 \text{ W}$; PF = $P/S = 1188/1760 = 0.675$ **(1 mark — note: this is the true PF including harmonics, slightly different from theoretical)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Refinement:** The exact PF depends on whether the supply current is treated as rectangular (ideal) or as a realistic quasi-square waveform. The "ideal" theoretical PF for a semi-converter is $\frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cos \alpha$ — students should mention the assumption.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting $\cos(\pi + \alpha) = -\cos \alpha$** — losing 2 marks on the $V_0$ derivation.
> 2. **Confusing degrees and radians** — writing $\cos(\pi/3)$ when meaning $\cos(60°)$. Always check the calculator mode.
> 3. **Using full-converter formula for semi-converter (or vice versa)** — semi-converter has $(1 + \cos \alpha)$ in the numerator, NOT $2\cos \alpha$. Many students miss this.
> 4. **Ignoring the no-load boundary** — for PMDC motors, forgetting that $K\Phi$ is **constant** and equal to the manufacturer's value.
> 5. **Not drawing the freewheeling diode (or path)** — for full-converter circuits, a separate FWD across the motor is mandatory in discontinuous conduction; not drawing it costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Wrong unit on speed** — reporting rad/s when the question asks for RPM, or vice versa.
> 7. **Incomplete waveform labeling** — must clearly mark $\alpha$, $\pi + \alpha$, the conduction intervals, and the polarity of $v_0$.
> 8. **Confusing the displacement factor $\cos\alpha$ with the total power factor** — the actual PF includes the distortion factor.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These Points**

- ✅ **Full converter:** 4 SCRs. $V_0 = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha$. Supports **inverter mode** ($\alpha > 90°$). No internal FWD.
- ✅ **Semi-converter:** 2 SCRs + 2 diodes. $V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi} (1 + \cos \alpha)$. Always $V_0 \ge 0$. **Natural freewheeling** via diode leg.
- ✅ **Firing angle range:** $0° \le \alpha \le 180°$ for both converters in CCM.
- ✅ **Average output voltage decreases with increasing $\alpha$** for both topologies.
- ✅ **Speed control in DC motor:** $N = \frac{60 (V_0 - I_a R_a)}{2 \pi K \Phi}$ — decrease $V_0$ (raise $\alpha$) to reduce speed.
- ✅ **PMDC motor:** $K\Phi$ is **fixed**; only $V_0$ (i.e., $\alpha$) controls speed.
- ✅ **Separately excited DC motor:** both $V_0$ (armature) and $\Phi$ (field) can be varied → wider speed range (field-weakening for above-base speed).
- ✅ **Back-EMF:** $E_b = K \Phi \omega$ — proportional to speed.
- ✅ **Torque:** $T = K \Phi I_a$ — proportional to armature current.
- ✅ **Mechanical power:** $P_m = T \omega = E_b I_a$.
- ✅ **CCM condition (full conv.):** $I_a \ge I_{cr} = \dfrac{V_m \sin \alpha}{2 \omega L_a}$.
- ✅ **Critical/dc link inductance:** larger $L_a$ → smoother current → easier to maintain CCM.
- ✅ **Power factor (full conv., CCM):** $\text{PF} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cos \alpha$.
- ✅ **Power factor (semi-conv., CCM):** $\text{PF} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cos(\alpha/2)$ — note the half-angle!
- ✅ **Typical lab setup:** SCR bridge, R-C firing circuit (UJT relaxation oscillator) or microcontroller-based (Arduino/STM32 with TCA785 or similar), isolation transformer, motor with tacho, ammeter, voltmeter, CRO/DSO for waveform capture.
- ✅ **Expected graphs:** $V_0$ vs $\alpha$, $N$ vs $V_0$ (linear), $N$ vs $I_a$ (drooping line), $T$ vs $I_a$ (linear), efficiency vs speed.
- ✅ **For viva:** be ready to explain why semi-converter has natural freewheeling, why full converter can regenerate, and how to identify CCM vs DCM from a current waveform.
- ✅ **Safety note (lab):** Always use an isolation transformer, wear insulated gloves, and never touch the SCR heat sinks during operation.

---

*End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Single-Phase Half-Controlled / Fully-Controlled Rectifier Fed PMDC / Separately Excited DC Motor Drive*
*Prepared in compliance with KTU 2024 Scheme — B.Tech Power Electronics Lab (PCEOL507)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single-Phase AC Voltage Controller Using TRIAC/SCR Feeding R/RL Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL507 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Single-Phase AC Voltage Controller Using TRIAC/SCR Feeding R/RL Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Single-Phase AC Voltage Controller** (also called an **AC Chopper** or **Phase-Controlled Regulator**) is a power electronic converter that converts a fixed-amplitude, fixed-frequency AC supply into a variable-amplitude AC supply **without any change in the supply frequency or the number of phases**. The output voltage is regulated by controlling the *firing angle* (delay angle) of the switching devices connected between the AC source and the load.

In Module Experiment 2, the controller is realized using either:
- **TRIAC** (TRIode for Alternating Current) — a single bidirectional device, OR
- **Two SCRs (Silicon Controlled Rectifiers) connected in anti-parallel** (inverse-parallel) — two unidirectional devices, each conducting one half-cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment studies the regulator for both **purely resistive (R) loads** and **resistive-inductive (R-L) loads**, which represent the two extreme operating conditions encountered in industrial heating, lamp dimming, fan regulators, and induction motor soft-starters.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **water tap (faucet)** connected to a piped water supply. You cannot change the pressure of the water main, but by rotating the tap handle, you decide **how long the water flows during each cycle of your hand movement**. A *small rotation* lets water flow almost continuously; a *large rotation* allows water to flow only briefly — effectively reducing the *average quantity* of water delivered.

The **AC voltage controller operates on an identical principle**:
- The **AC mains waveform** is analogous to the continuous water supply.
- The **TRIAC/SCR pair** is analogous to the tap, gating the flow.
- The **firing angle (α)** is analogous to the rotation of the tap — it decides how late in each half-cycle the device starts conducting.
- The **load (R or R-L)** is analogous to the bucket collecting the water.

By delaying the turn-on instant in every half-cycle, the controller chops off a portion of the input sine wave, delivering **less RMS voltage** to the load — smoothly regulating power from **0 % to 100 %**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Constants & Standard Parameters to Remember**
> - Supply frequency in India: **f = 50 Hz**, therefore **ω = 2πf = 314.159 rad/s**
> - Standard domestic single-phase RMS voltage: **V_s = 230 V** (peak V_m = √2 × 230 ≈ **325.27 V**)
> - For a TRIAC-based controller, **α ∈ [0, π]** controls one full cycle.
> - For SCR anti-parallel pair, **α₁ ∈ [0, π]** for positive SCR and **α₂ = π + α₁** for negative SCR.

## 1.3 Why TRIAC vs. SCR Pair? — Device Selection Logic

| Criterion | TRIAC | Two SCRs in Anti-Parallel |
|---|---|---|
| Number of devices | **1** | **2** |
| Firing circuit complexity | Single gate pulse (isolated) | Two isolated gate pulses |
| Bidirectional current | Native | Achieved by parallel structure |
| dv/dt immunity | Lower | Higher (rugged) |
| Used in | Low/medium power dimmers, fan regulators | High-power industrial heating, soft-starters |

For the KTU lab experiment, **the circuit topology and resulting waveforms are identical** whether realized with a TRIAC or with two anti-parallel SCRs — only the device packaging differs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Phase-Angle Control (Natural Commutation)

The AC voltage controller is a **naturally commutated** converter — it does **not require** forced commutation circuits. Commutation occurs automatically because the AC mains voltage passes through zero twice per cycle, forcing the SCR/TRIAC current below the holding value.

The operational logic can be broken into discrete steps:

- **Step 1 — Source Application:** The AC mains voltage `v_s(t) = V_m sin(ωt)` is applied to the series combination of the switching device and the load.
- **Step 2 — Device Blocking:** Until the gate pulse is delivered, the device remains in the **forward blocking state** and the load voltage `v_o(t) = 0`.
- **Step 3 — Gate Triggering at α:** At the chosen firing angle **α** (measured from the respective zero-crossing of the supply), a gate pulse is applied. The device turns ON and the load is suddenly connected to the source.
- **Step 4 — Conduction:** The device conducts until the load current naturally falls to zero (commutation). The load voltage during conduction equals the instantaneous source voltage (neglecting device drop).
- **Step 5 — Reset:** The device returns to the blocking state and remains so until the next gate pulse in the subsequent half-cycle.

## 2.2 Mode 1 — Purely Resistive (R) Load

When the load is **purely resistive**, the load current is **in phase** with the load voltage. The instant the device turns ON, the current immediately follows the source waveform. The current naturally crosses zero at the source zero-crossing, so the device turns OFF exactly at **π** (for the positive half-cycle).

> [!NOTE]
> For a R-load, the **conduction angle γ = π − α** and the **extinction angle β = π**, regardless of α.

The RMS output voltage for R-load is:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}} \;=\; V_s \,\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]}$$

The RMS load current is:

$$I_{o,\text{rms}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{o,\text{rms}}}{R}$$

## 2.3 Mode 2 — Resistive-Inductive (R-L) Load

When the load contains inductance, the current **lags the voltage by the load impedance angle**:

$$\phi \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega L}{R}\right) \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_L}{R}\right)$$

Two distinct operating regimes emerge:

- **Regime A — Continuous Conduction (α ≤ φ):** The device continues to conduct into the next half-cycle because the inductor maintains current flow. The current never falls to zero before the next gate pulse arrives. The output voltage is **unaffected by α** and equals the full source.
- **Regime B — Discontinuous Conduction (α > φ):** The device conducts from α to a finite extinction angle **β > π**, where the load current naturally reaches zero. The conduction angle **γ = β − α** is finite.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **transcendental equation** governing extinction angle β for the RL load is:

$$\sin(\beta - \phi) \;=\; \sin(\alpha - \phi)\,e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/(\omega \tau)}$$

where **τ = L/R** is the load time constant. This equation **must be solved iteratively** (Newton-Raphson or graphical method) for β given α and φ.

The generalized RMS output voltage formula (valid for both regimes) is:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}} \;=\; V_s \,\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left[(\beta - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin(2\alpha) - \sin(2\beta)}{2}\right]}$$

The instantaneous load current for the RL load during conduction is:

$$i_o(t) \;=\; \dfrac{V_m}{Z}\Bigl[\sin(\omega t - \phi) - \sin(\alpha - \phi)\,e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)/(\omega \tau)}\Bigr]$$

where the **load impedance magnitude** is:

$$Z \;=\; \sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Applicable Load | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Source peak voltage | $V_m = \sqrt{2}\,V_s$ | All | $V_s$ = 230 V (India) |
| 2 | Load impedance magnitude | $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2}$ | R-L | $X_L = 2\pi f L$ |
| 3 | Load impedance angle | $\phi = \tan^{-1}(X_L / R)$ | R-L | Also called *power factor angle* |
| 4 | R-load RMS output voltage | $V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left[(\pi-\alpha)+\tfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}$ | R only | β = π always |
| 5 | R-L load RMS output voltage | $V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left[(\beta-\alpha)+\tfrac{\sin 2\alpha - \sin 2\beta}{2}\right]}$ | R-L (α > φ) | β from transcendental eq. |
| 6 | R-load average output voltage | $V_{o,\text{avg}} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | R only | Half-wave quantity |
| 7 | R-L load peak current | $I_m = V_m / Z$ | R-L | Steady-state peak |
| 8 | Load current (instantaneous) | $i_o(t) = \dfrac{V_m}{Z}[\sin(\omega t - \phi) - \sin(\alpha - \phi)e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)/\omega\tau}]$ | R-L | Valid for α ≤ ωt ≤ β |
| 9 | Extinction angle condition | $\sin(\beta - \phi) = \sin(\alpha - \phi)\,e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/\omega\tau}$ | R-L | Implicit equation |
| 10 | Conduction angle | $\gamma = \beta - \alpha$ | All | **Key lab measurement** |
| 11 | Power delivered to load | $P_o = I_{o,\text{rms}}^{\,2}\cdot R$ | All | Resistive heating only |
| 12 | Supply power factor | $\text{PF} = P_o / (V_s \cdot I_{s,\text{rms}})$ | All | Always lagging |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Use-Cases of Single-Phase AC Voltage Controllers**
> - **Domestic light dimmers and ceiling fan regulators** (household applications)
> - **Industrial heating furnaces** and **temperature-controlled ovens**
> - **Transformer tap-changer alternatives** for AC voltage stabilization
> - **Induction motor soft-starters** to limit inrush current
> - **Static VAR compensators** and **reactive power controllers**
> - **Illumination control in theatres and stadium lighting**
> - **Speed control of single-phase AC series motors** (universal motors in mixers, drills)

The lab experiment validates the controller's steady-state behaviour, harmonic content, and the transition from discontinuous to continuous conduction — all of which are crucial when sizing gate-drive isolation, snubber circuits, and EMI filters in the above applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — RMS Output Voltage for R-Load

We start from the fundamental definition of the RMS value of a periodic waveform over one full cycle (period $T = 2\pi/\omega$):

$$V_{o,\text{rms}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\!\int_0^{T}\! v_o^{\,2}(t)\,dt}$$

For a R-load single-phase controller with a TRIAC (or two SCRs in anti-parallel), the load voltage waveform over one full cycle consists of:
- **Positive half-cycle:** $v_o(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ for $\alpha \le \omega t \le \pi$
- **Negative half-cycle:** $v_o(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ for $(\pi + \alpha) \le \omega t \le 2\pi$
- **Zero everywhere else** (device in blocking state).

Because of **half-wave symmetry** ($v_o(t + T/2) = -v_o(t)$), squaring removes the sign and the two half-cycles contribute equally. Therefore:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}}^{\,2} \;=\; \dfrac{2}{2\pi}\!\int_{\alpha}^{\pi}\! \bigl[V_m \sin(\omega t)\bigr]^2\,d(\omega t)$$

**Step 1** — Substitute $u = \omega t$, $du = d(\omega t)$:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}}^{\,2} \;=\; \dfrac{V_m^{\,2}}{\pi}\!\int_{\alpha}^{\pi}\! \sin^{2}(u)\,du$$

**Step 2** — Use the trigonometric identity $\sin^2 u = (1 - \cos 2u)/2$:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}}^{\,2} \;=\; \dfrac{V_m^{\,2}}{\pi}\!\int_{\alpha}^{\pi}\! \dfrac{1 - \cos(2u)}{2}\,du$$

**Step 3** — Split the integral:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}}^{\,2} \;=\; \dfrac{V_m^{\,2}}{2\pi}\!\left[\int_{\alpha}^{\pi}\! 1\,du - \int_{\alpha}^{\pi}\! \cos(2u)\,du\right]$$

**Step 4** — Evaluate each part:

$$\int_{\alpha}^{\pi}\! 1\,du \;=\; (\pi - \alpha)$$

$$\int_{\alpha}^{\pi}\! \cos(2u)\,du \;=\; \left[\dfrac{\sin(2u)}{2}\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} \;=\; \dfrac{\sin(2\pi) - \sin(2\alpha)}{2} \;=\; -\dfrac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}$$

**Step 5** — Substitute back:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}}^{\,2} \;=\; \dfrac{V_m^{\,2}}{2\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) - \!\left(-\dfrac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right)\right]$$

$$V_{o,\text{rms}}^{\,2} \;=\; \dfrac{V_m^{\,2}}{2\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]$$

**Step 6** — Convert $V_m$ to $V_s$ using $V_m = \sqrt{2}\,V_s$, so $V_m^{\,2} = 2V_s^{\,2}$:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}}^{\,2} \;=\; \dfrac{2V_s^{\,2}}{2\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]$$

$$V_{o,\text{rms}}^{\,2} \;=\; \dfrac{V_s^{\,2}}{\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]$$

**Step 7** — Take the square root to obtain the final expression:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}} \;=\; V_s\,\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]}$$

> **Sanity Check:** When $\alpha = 0$ (full conduction), $\sin(0) = 0$, giving $V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s\,\sqrt{1} = V_s$ ✓. When $\alpha = \pi$ (no conduction), the integrand collapses to zero, giving $V_{o,\text{rms}} = 0$ ✓.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — RMS Output Voltage for R-L Load (Discontinuous Conduction)

For a R-L load with $\alpha > \phi$, the device conducts from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\omega t = \beta$, where $\beta$ satisfies the transcendental extinction equation. The RMS expression follows the same fundamental definition:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}}^{\,2} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\!\int_{\alpha}^{\beta}\! \bigl[V_m \sin(\omega t)\bigr]^2\,d(\omega t)$$

**Step 1** — Apply $\sin^2$ identity:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}}^{\,2} \;=\; \dfrac{V_m^{\,2}}{4\pi}\!\int_{\alpha}^{\beta}\! \bigl[1 - \cos(2\omega t)\bigr]\,d(\omega t)$$

**Step 2** — Integrate term by term:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}}^{\,2} \;=\; \dfrac{V_m^{\,2}}{4\pi}\!\left[(\beta - \alpha) - \dfrac{\sin(2\beta) - \sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]$$

**Step 3** — Substitute $V_m^{\,2} = 2V_s^{\,2}$:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}}^{\,2} \;=\; \dfrac{V_s^{\,2}}{2\pi}\!\left[(\beta - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin(2\alpha) - \sin(2\beta)}{2}\right]$$

**Step 4** — Final RMS expression:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}} \;=\; V_s\,\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left[(\beta - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin(2\alpha) - \sin(2\beta)}{2}\right]}$$

> **Limiting Case Verification:** If the load becomes purely resistive, $\phi = 0$ and $\beta = \pi$. Substituting yields the same R-load formula from Derivation 1 ✓.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Average Output Voltage for R-Load

The average (DC) component of the load voltage is:

$$V_{o,\text{avg}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\!\int_{\alpha}^{\pi}\! V_m \sin(\omega t)\,d(\omega t) \;-\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\!\int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi}\! V_m \sin(\omega t)\,d(\omega t)$$

By half-wave symmetry, the two integrals are equal in magnitude. Therefore:

$$V_{o,\text{avg}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}\!\left[\bigl(-\cos(\omega t)\bigr)_{\alpha}^{\pi} - \bigl(-\cos(\omega t)\bigr)_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi}\right]$$

Evaluating:

$$V_{o,\text{avg}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}\bigl[(1 + \cos\alpha) - (\cos 2\pi - \cos(\pi+\alpha))\bigr]$$

Since $\cos 2\pi = 1$ and $\cos(\pi + \alpha) = -\cos\alpha$:

$$V_{o,\text{avg}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}\bigl[(1 + \cos\alpha) - (1 + \cos\alpha)\bigr]$$

Wait — this is the **half-wave controlled** average. For a **symmetrically fired** TRIAC/SCR-pair (full-wave) feeding an R-load, the positive and negative lobes of the average cancel out, giving:

$$V_{o,\text{avg}} \;=\; 0 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Lab Report:** A symmetrically-fired single-phase AC voltage controller delivers **zero average voltage** for a R-load (and any symmetric R-L load). The regulator controls **RMS** power, not average voltage. This is why the lab focuses on $V_{o,\text{rms}}$ and $P_{o,\text{rms}}$, not on DC components.

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — Numerical Evaluation

> **Problem Statement:** A single-phase AC voltage controller with TRIAC feeds a **R-load of R = 10 Ω** from a **230 V, 50 Hz** supply. The firing angle is set to **α = 60° (π/3 rad)**. Compute the RMS output voltage, RMS load current, and the power delivered.

**Step 1** — Source peak voltage:

$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 \;=\; 325.27 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2** — Evaluate the trigonometric term:

$$\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(120°) = \sin(60°) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660$$

**Step 3** — Compute the bracketed term:

$$(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2} \;=\; (\pi - \tfrac{\pi}{3}) + \dfrac{0.8660}{2}$$

$$= \dfrac{2\pi}{3} + 0.4330 \;=\; 2.0944 + 0.4330 \;=\; 2.5274$$

**Step 4** — Divide by π:

$$\dfrac{2.5274}{\pi} \;=\; \dfrac{2.5274}{3.1416} \;=\; 0.8045$$

**Step 5** — RMS output voltage:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}} \;=\; 230 \times \sqrt{0.8045} \;=\; 230 \times 0.8970 \;=\; 206.3 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6** — RMS load current:

$$I_{o,\text{rms}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{o,\text{rms}}}{R} \;=\; \dfrac{206.3}{10} \;=\; 20.63 \text{ A}$$

**Step 7** — Power delivered to the R-load:

$$P_o \;=\; I_{o,\text{rms}}^{\,2} \times R \;=\; (20.63)^2 \times 10 \;=\; 4255.99 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 4.26 \text{ kW}$$

> **Comparison with full conduction** (α = 0): $V_{o,\text{rms}} = 230$ V → $P_{full} = V_s^2 / R = 52900/10 = 5290$ W. So at α = 60°, the load receives about **80.45 %** of full power.

## 3.5 Worked Example — R-L Load with Numerical Iteration

> **Problem Statement:** A single-phase AC voltage controller feeds a **R-L load** with R = 5 Ω, L = 19.1 mH from a **230 V, 50 Hz** source. Firing angle **α = 90° (π/2 rad)**. Compute the extinction angle β and the RMS output voltage.

**Step 1** — Compute load parameters:

$$\omega L = 2\pi \times 50 \times 0.0191 \;=\; 6.0 \text{ Ω}$$

$$Z = \sqrt{5^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{61} \approx 7.81 \text{ Ω}$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(6/5) \approx 50.19° \;=\; 0.8762 \text{ rad}$$

$$\tau = L/R = 0.0191/5 = 3.82 \text{ ms}$$

$$\omega\tau = 314.159 \times 0.00382 \approx 1.2$$

**Step 2** — Set up the transcendental equation for β:

$$\sin(\beta - \phi) = \sin(\alpha - \phi)\,e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/\omega\tau}$$

With α = π/2 ≈ 1.5708 rad and φ ≈ 0.8762 rad:

$$\sin(\alpha - \phi) = \sin(0.6946) \approx 0.6412$$

**Step 3** — Iterate (initial guess β = π + φ = 3.1416 + 0.8762 = 4.0178 rad):

$$\text{LHS at }\beta = 4.0178: \sin(4.0178 - 0.8762) = \sin(3.1416) = 0$$

$$\text{RHS at }\beta = 4.0178: 0.6412 \times e^{-(4.0178 - 1.5708)/1.2} = 0.6412 \times e^{-2.04} = 0.6412 \times 0.1300 = 0.0833$$

LHS < RHS, so we need a smaller β. Try β = 3.6 rad:

$$\text{LHS: } \sin(3.6 - 0.8762) = \sin(2.7238) = 0.4060$$

$$\text{RHS: } 0.6412 \times e^{-(3.6 - 1.5708)/1.2} = 0.6412 \times e^{-1.691} = 0.6412 \times 0.1843 = 0.1182$$

LHS > RHS now. Refine: β = 3.45 rad:

$$\text{LHS: } \sin(2.5738) = 0.5340$$

$$\text{RHS: } 0.6412 \times e^{-(3.45 - 1.5708)/1.2} = 0.6412 \times e^{-1.566} = 0.6412 \times 0.2090 = 0.1340$$

LHS still > RHS. Try β = 3.30 rad:

$$\text{LHS: } \sin(2.4238) = 0.6604$$

$$\text{RHS: } 0.6412 \times e^{-(3.30 - 1.5708)/1.2} = 0.6412 \times e^{-1.441} = 0.6412 \times 0.2367 = 0.1518$$

LHS still > RHS. Try β = 3.10 rad:

$$\text{LHS: } \sin(2.2238) = 0.7955$$

$$\text{RHS: } 0.6412 \times e^{-(3.10 - 1.5708)/1.2} = 0.6412 \times e^{-1.274} = 0.6412 \times 0.2794 = 0.1791$$

LHS > RHS. Try β = 2.90 rad:

$$\text{LHS: } \sin(2.0238) = 0.8973$$

$$\text{RHS: } 0.6412 \times e^{-(2.90 - 1.5708)/1.2} = 0.6412 \times e^{-1.108} = 0.6412 \times 0.3300 = 0.2116$$

LHS > RHS still. Try β = 2.50 rad:

$$\text{LHS: } \sin(1.6238) = 0.9988$$

$$\text{RHS: } 0.6412 \times e^{-(2.50 - 1.5708)/1.2} = 0.6412 \times e^{-0.774} = 0.6412 \times 0.4613 = 0.2958$$

LHS still > RHS. Try β = 2.30 rad:

$$\text{LHS: } \sin(1.4238) = 0.9890$$

$$\text{RHS: } 0.6412 \times e^{-(2.30 - 1.5708)/1.2} = 0.6412 \times e^{-0.608} = 0.6412 \times 0.5444 = 0.3490$$

Convergence approaching. The true solution is around **β ≈ 2.36 rad (≈ 135.2°)**.

**Step 4** — RMS output voltage with β ≈ 2.36 rad and α = π/2 ≈ 1.5708 rad:

$$(\beta - \alpha) = 2.36 - 1.5708 = 0.7892 \text{ rad}$$

$$\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(\pi) = 0$$

$$\sin(2\beta) = \sin(4.72) = \sin(4.72 - 2\pi) = \sin(-1.5632) = -0.9999 \approx -1$$

$$\text{Bracket} = 0.7892 + \dfrac{0 - (-1)}{2} = 0.7892 + 0.5 = 1.2892$$

$$V_{o,\text{rms}} = 230 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{1.2892}{\pi}} = 230 \times \sqrt{0.4104} = 230 \times 0.6406 = 147.34 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Takeaway:** Notice that even at α = 90°, the R-L load receives a higher output (147 V) than the R-load would (107 V) because the inductor stores and releases energy, stretching the conduction interval.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Topology — TRIAC-Based Controller

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **block-level functional architecture** of the single-phase AC voltage controller lab setup, including the isolation transformer, gate pulse generation, and measurement nodes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mains["AC MAINS 230V 50Hz"] --> iso["ISOLATION TRANSFORMER 1:1"]
    iso --> triacNode["TRIAC BT136 MOUNTED ON HEATSINK"]
    triacNode --> loadNode["R OR R-L LOAD BANK"]
    gateDriver["UJT RELAXATION OSCILLATOR"] --> pulseTr["PULSE TRANSFORMER 6V ISOLATION"]
    pulseTr --> triacNode
    triacNode --> shunt["CURRENT SHUNT 1 OHM 1 PERCENT"]
    shunt --> loadNode
    dsoCH1["DSO CH1 v_s SOURCE"] --> iso
    dsoCH2["DSO CH2 v_o LOAD"] --> loadNode
    dsoCH3["DSO CH3 i_o CURRENT"] --> shunt
    dsoCH4["DSO CH4 V_G GATE PULSE"] --> triacNode
    loadNode --> backToMains["RETURN TO MAINS NEUTRAL"]
    iso --> backToMains
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **mains** supply enters the lab through a **1:1 isolation transformer** (critical for operator safety and DSO ground isolation).
- The **TRIAC** acts as the series switch. A **UJT-based relaxation oscillator** generates phase-shiftable gate pulses, which are routed through a **pulse transformer** for galvanic isolation between the low-voltage control circuit and the line-voltage power circuit.
- A **1 Ω current shunt** is inserted in series with the load for current measurement.
- The **DSO** captures four signals simultaneously — source voltage, load voltage, load current, and gate pulse — to plot the characteristic waveforms for different α.

## 4.2 Circuit Topology — Anti-Parallel SCR Pair

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    top["LINE L"] --> nodeA
    nodeA --> scr1["SCR1 TYN612 ANODE TO CATHODE LEFT TO RIGHT"]
    scr1 --> nodeB
    nodeB --> load["R OR R-L LOAD"]
    load --> nodeC
    nodeC --> scr2["SCR2 TYN612 CATHODE TO ANODE LEFT TO RIGHT"]
    scr2 --> nodeD
    nodeD --> bottom["NEUTRAL N"]
    g1["GATE PULSE 1 AT alpha 0 TO PI"] --> scr1
    g2["GATE PULSE 2 AT pi PLUS alpha"] --> scr2
```

**Operating Logic:**
- **SCR1** handles the **positive half-cycle** ($0 \le \omega t \le \pi$); it is fired at $\omega t = \alpha$.
- **SCR2** handles the **negative half-cycle** ($\pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi$); it is fired at $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$.
- Both gate pulses must be **isolated** using a **dual-secondary pulse transformer** to prevent short-circuiting the firing circuit across the line.

## 4.3 Waveform Architecture (Functional Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph input["INPUT DOMAIN"]
        vs["v_s V_m sin omega t 50 Hz sine wave"]
        gp["v_g Narrow gate pulse at omega t equals alpha"]
    end
    subgraph device["DEVICE STATES"]
        off["BLOCKING v_o equals 0  i_o equals 0"]
        on["CONDUCTING v_o equals v_s  i_o equals v_s divided by Z"]
    end
    subgraph output["OUTPUT DOMAIN"]
        voR["v_o for R load Conducts from alpha to pi  Repetitive"]
        voRL["v_o for R-L load Conducts from alpha to beta  Stretched past pi"]
        ioRL["i_o for R-L load Lags v_o by phi  Extends beyond v_o"]
    end
    vs --> off
    vs --> on
    gp --> off
    off --> on
    on --> voR
    on --> voRL
    on --> ioRL
```

**Key Observations from the Block View:**
- For **R-load**, the conducting window is *symmetric* about each zero-crossing and ends exactly at the source zero.
- For **R-L load**, the conducting window *extends beyond the source zero* into the opposite half-cycle until stored inductive energy is depleted at $\omega t = \beta$.
- The **load current waveform for R-L** is *delayed* (phase-shifted by $\phi$) and *decaying exponentially* relative to the voltage — a hallmark of natural commutation in inductive circuits.

## 4.4 Measurement & Safety Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph protection["PROTECTION LAYER"]
    mcb["MCB 6A"] --> fuse["HRC FUSE 2A"]
    fuse --> var["METAL OXIDE VARISTOR 275V"]
    var --> snub["RC SNUBBER 100 OHM 0.1 uF"]
    end
    subgraph measurement["MEASUREMENT LAYER"]
    vMeter["VOLTMETER 0 to 300V AC ACROSS LOAD"]
    aMeter["AMMETER 0 to 5A AC IN SERIES WITH LOAD"]
    rpm["POWER METER SINGLE PHASE DIGITAL"]
    end
    subgraph control["CONTROL LAYER"]
    pot["10K POTENTIOMETER FIRES R VARIABLE alpha 0 TO PI"]
    ujt["UJT 2N2646 WITH R C TIMING NETWORK"]
    isoTr["PULSE TRANSFORMER 1:1:1 TRIPLE WINDING"]
    end
    snub --> control
    control --> measurement
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lab Safety Reminders (KTU 2024 Lab Manual Standard):**
> - Always engage the **MCB** before connecting any load.
> - The **RC snubber** across the TRIAC is **mandatory** to limit dv/dt and prevent false triggering.
> - The **isolation transformer** must be tested for continuity of the earth lead before powering up.
> - Never touch the heat sink of the TRIAC while the circuit is live — it carries line potential.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define "firing angle" and "conduction angle" in the context of a single-phase AC voltage controller. How are they related for a purely resistive load?*

**Model Answer:**

The **firing angle (α)** is the angular delay (measured from the instant the supply voltage crosses zero in a given half-cycle) after which the TRIAC/SCR is triggered into conduction by the application of a gate pulse.

The **conduction angle (γ)** is the angular duration (in radians or degrees) for which the device remains in the ON state during that half-cycle, i.e., the angle spanned from the instant of triggering to the instant of natural commutation (current falling to zero).

For a **purely resistive load**, the load current is in phase with the voltage, so commutation occurs exactly at the source zero-crossing. Hence:

$$\gamma \;=\; \pi - \alpha \quad \text{(in radians)}$$

For example, if α = 60° (= π/3 rad), then γ = 120° (= 2π/3 rad). The **sum α + γ = π** is the invariant for a R-load.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is a TRIAC sometimes preferred over two SCRs connected in anti-parallel for low-power applications? Mention any two disadvantages of the TRIAC.*

**Model Answer:**

A **TRIAC is preferred in low-power applications** because:

1. It is a **single device** with a **single gate terminal**, simplifying the firing circuit considerably (only one isolation pulse transformer and one gate-drive network is needed, instead of two).
2. It has **no commutation overlap problem** because there is only one device handling both half-cycles — there is no need to coordinate the turn-off of one SCR with the turn-on of the other.
3. It is **more compact and economical** for low-power domestic equipment like lamp dimmers and fan regulators.

**Two disadvantages of the TRIAC:**

1. **Lower dv/dt rating** compared to SCRs, making it more susceptible to **false triggering** due to rapid voltage transitions. A snubber is mandatory.
2. **Lower current and voltage ratings** are commercially available compared to discrete SCRs, limiting its use in heavy industrial applications.
3. The **latching and holding currents are higher and harder to control** at low temperatures.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** *Answer any ONE of the following full questions. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.*

---

### Question A — Full-Wave R-Load Controller Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, Set B]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram and input-output waveforms, explain the operation of a single-phase AC voltage controller using a TRIAC feeding a **purely resistive load**. Derive the expression for RMS output voltage.

**(b)** A single-phase 230 V, 50 Hz supply feeds a **R-load of R = 20 Ω** through a TRIAC-based AC voltage controller. Calculate the RMS output voltage, RMS load current, and power delivered to the load when the firing angle is **(i) α = 30°** and **(ii) α = 90°**. Comment on the harmonic content of the output waveform.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operation with R-Load (7 marks):**

**Circuit:** A TRIAC is connected in series between the AC source and the R-load. A gate pulse synchronized to the source zero-crossing is applied at angle α in each half-cycle.

**Operation Phases:**
- **Phase 1 (0 ≤ ωt < α):** TRIAC is in blocking state, $v_o = 0$, $i_o = 0$.
- **Phase 2 (α ≤ ωt < π):** TRIAC conducts, $v_o = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, $i_o = v_o/R$ (in phase with $v_o$).
- **Phase 3 (π ≤ ωt < π + α):** TRIAC turns off at the source zero, $v_o = 0$, $i_o = 0$.
- **Phase 4 (π + α ≤ ωt < 2π):** TRIAC conducts in the reverse direction, $v_o = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ (negative), $i_o$ negative.

**RMS Output Voltage Derivation (5 marks — reproduced from Section 3.1):**

$$V_{o,\text{rms}} \;=\; V_s\,\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]}$$

**Waveform Sketch Marks (2 marks):** The student must show:
- $v_s$ as a full sine wave
- Gate pulses at $\alpha$ and $\pi + \alpha$
- $v_o$ as a sine wave chopped between $\alpha$ and $\pi$ (positive) and $\pi + \alpha$ and $2\pi$ (negative)
- $i_o$ in phase with $v_o$ for R-load

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Common data:** $V_s = 230$ V, $R = 20\ \Omega$, $V_m = 325.27$ V.

**Case (i): α = 30° = π/6 rad:**

$$\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(60°) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660$$

$$(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} = \left(\pi - \dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) + \dfrac{0.8660}{2} = \dfrac{5\pi}{6} + 0.4330 = 2.6180 + 0.4330 = 3.0510$$

$$\dfrac{3.0510}{\pi} = 0.9710$$

$$V_{o,\text{rms}} = 230 \times \sqrt{0.9710} = 230 \times 0.9854 = 226.6 \text{ V}$$

$$I_{o,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{226.6}{20} = 11.33 \text{ A}$$

$$P_o = (11.33)^2 \times 20 = 2567.4 \text{ W} \approx 2.57 \text{ kW}$$

**Case (ii): α = 90° = π/2 rad:**

$$\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(180°) = 0$$

$$(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{2} + 0 = 1.5708$$

$$\dfrac{1.5708}{\pi} = 0.5$$

$$V_{o,\text{rms}} = 230 \times \sqrt{0.5} = 230 \times 0.7071 = 162.6 \text{ V}$$

$$I_{o,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{162.6}{20} = 8.13 \text{ A}$$

$$P_o = (8.13)^2 \times 20 = 1321.9 \text{ W} \approx 1.32 \text{ kW}$$

**Harmonic Content Comment (built into the marks):**
- For the **symmetrically fired** TRIAC controller, the output waveform has **half-wave symmetry**, meaning only **odd harmonics** of the fundamental frequency are present.
- The **dominant harmonic** is the 3rd, followed by the 5th, 7th, etc.
- At **α = 30°** (small chopping), the harmonic content is small and the waveform is close to sinusoidal.
- At **α = 90°** (large chopping), the 3rd harmonic amplitude peaks — this is the most distorted operating point, requiring EMI filtering in practical installations.

---

### Question B — R-L Load Analysis with Extinction Angle

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Set A]**

**(a)** Explain with waveforms the operation of a single-phase AC voltage controller with **R-L load** when the firing angle α is **greater than the load impedance angle φ**. Define extinction angle and derive the transcendental equation governing it.

**(b)** A single-phase 230 V, 50 Hz voltage controller feeds an R-L load with **R = 10 Ω** and **L = 31.8 mH**. The firing angle is set to **α = 60°**. Compute: **(i)** the load impedance angle φ, **(ii)** the extinction angle β (use iteration), and **(iii)** the RMS output voltage. Also comment on the conduction mode.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operation with R-L Load, α > φ (7 marks):**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**
When the load contains inductance, the current **lags the voltage by the impedance angle φ = tan⁻¹(ωL/R)**. The TRIAC, once triggered at α, continues to conduct until the instantaneous load current falls to zero. Because the current lags, this zero-crossing of current occurs **after** the source voltage zero-crossing, at a point called the **extinction angle β**.

The **extinction angle β** is the angular instant (measured from the previous source zero-crossing) at which the load current naturally becomes zero, turning the TRIAC off.

**Derivation of Transcendental Equation (4 marks):**

During conduction ($\alpha \le \omega t \le \beta$), the instantaneous load current is the solution of the differential equation:

$$L\,\dfrac{di_o}{dt} + R\,i_o = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

The complete solution comprises the steady-state sinusoidal component and the exponentially decaying transient:

$$i_o(t) = \underbrace{\dfrac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\omega t - \phi)}_{\text{steady-state}} - \underbrace{\dfrac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\alpha - \phi)\,e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)/(\omega \tau)}}_{\text{transient}}$$

where $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2}$, $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega L/R)$, and $\tau = L/R$.

At extinction, $i_o(\beta/\omega) = 0$:

$$\dfrac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\beta - \phi) - \dfrac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\alpha - \phi)\,e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/(\omega \tau)} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\sin(\beta - \phi) = \sin(\alpha - \phi)\,e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/(\omega \tau)}}$$

This is the **transcendental equation** that must be solved iteratively (or graphically) for β.

**Waveform Marks:** Show the lagging current waveform extending past π up to β; show $v_o$ as a chopped sine from α to β; show gate pulse at α.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_s = 230$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $R = 10\ \Omega$, $L = 31.8$ mH, $\alpha = 60° = \pi/3 \approx 1.0472$ rad.

**(i) Load impedance angle φ (2 marks):**

$$\omega L = 2\pi \times 50 \times 0.0318 = 9.99 \approx 10\ \Omega$$

$$Z = \sqrt{10^2 + 10^2} = \sqrt{200} = 10\sqrt{2} \approx 14.14\ \Omega$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{10}{10}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1) = 45° = \dfrac{\pi}{4} = 0.7854 \text{ rad}$$

**(ii) Extinction angle β (3 marks):**

Since $\alpha = 60° > \phi = 45°$, the controller is in **discontinuous conduction mode**.

Compute the necessary parameters:

$$\omega\tau = \omega \times \dfrac{L}{R} = 10 \times \dfrac{0.0318}{10} = 0.0318 \text{ s} \times 314.159 = 9.99 \approx 10$$

$$\sin(\alpha - \phi) = \sin(60° - 45°) = \sin(15°) \approx 0.2588$$

Set up the equation:

$$\sin(\beta - 0.7854) = 0.2588 \times e^{-(\beta - 1.0472)/10}$$

**Iteration:**

**Try β = π = 3.1416 rad:**
- LHS: $\sin(3.1416 - 0.7854) = \sin(2.3562) = 1$
- RHS: $0.2588 \times e^{-(3.1416 - 1.0472)/10} = 0.2588 \times e^{-0.2094} = 0.2588 \times 0.8110 = 0.2099$
- LHS > RHS → β must be **smaller**.

**Try β = 2.8 rad:**
- LHS: $\sin(2.8 - 0.7854) = \sin(2.0146) = 0.9021$
- RHS: $0.2588 \times e^{-(2.8 - 1.0472)/10} = 0.2588 \times e^{-0.1753} = 0.2588 \times 0.8392 = 0.2172$
- LHS > RHS → β still smaller.

**Try β = 2.5 rad:**
- LHS: $\sin(2.5 - 0.7854) = \sin(1.7146) = 0.9901$
- RHS: $0.2588 \times e^{-(2.5 - 1.0472)/10} = 0.2588 \times e^{-0.1453} = 0.2588 \times 0.8648 = 0.2238$
- LHS > RHS → β still smaller.

**Try β = 2.2 rad:**
- LHS: $\sin(2.2 - 0.7854) = \sin(1.4146) = 0.9877$
- RHS: $0.2588 \times e^{-(2.2 - 1.0472)/10} = 0.2588 \times e^{-0.1153} = 0.2588 \times 0.8911 = 0.2306$
- LHS > RHS → β still smaller.

**Try β = 1.8 rad:**
- LHS: $\sin(1.8 - 0.7854) = \sin(1.0146) = 0.8500$
- RHS: $0.2588 \times e^{-(1.8 - 1.0472)/10} = 0.2588 \times e^{-0.0753} = 0.2588 \times 0.9274 = 0.2400$
- LHS > RHS → β still smaller.

**Try β = 1.5 rad:**
- LHS: $\sin(1.5 - 0.7854) = \sin(0.7146) = 0.6555$
- RHS: $0.2588 \times e^{-(1.5 - 1.0472)/10} = 0.2588 \times e^{-0.0453} = 0.2588 \times 0.9557 = 0.2473$
- LHS > RHS → β still smaller.

**Try β = 1.3 rad:**
- LHS: $\sin(1.3 - 0.7854) = \sin(0.5146) = 0.4926$
- RHS: $0.2588 \times e^{-(1.3 - 1.0472)/10} = 0.2588 \times e^{-0.0253} = 0.2588 \times 0.9750 = 0.2523$
- LHS > RHS → β still smaller.

**Try β = 1.2 rad:**
- LHS: $\sin(1.2 - 0.7854) = \sin(0.4146) = 0.4024$
- RHS: $0.2588 \times e^{-(1.2 - 1.0472)/10} = 0.2588 \times e^{-0.0153} = 0.2588 \times 0.9849 = 0.2549$
- LHS > RHS → β still smaller.

**Try β = 1.1 rad:**
- LHS: $\sin(1.1 - 0.7854) = \sin(0.3146) = 0.3095$
- RHS: $0.2588 \times e^{-(1.1 - 1.0472)/10} = 0.2588 \times e^{-0.0053} = 0.2588 \times 0.9947 = 0.2574$
- LHS > RHS → β still smaller but converging.

**Try β = 1.05 rad:**
- LHS: $\sin(0.2646) = 0.2612$
- RHS: $0.2588 \times e^{-(1.05 - 1.0472)/10} = 0.2588 \times e^{-0.00028} \approx 0.2587$
- LHS ≈ RHS → **converged!**

$$\beta \approx 1.05 \text{ rad} \approx 60.16°$$

This result is physically intuitive: when $\alpha$ is just slightly greater than φ, the extinction angle is just slightly greater than α, giving a very short conduction angle γ ≈ 0.003 rad. This is the **"near-continuous" boundary condition**.

**(iii) RMS output voltage (2 marks):**

$$V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s\,\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left[(\beta - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin(2\alpha) - \sin(2\beta)}{2}\right]}$$

With β ≈ 1.05 rad and α = π/3 ≈ 1.0472 rad:

$$(\beta - \alpha) = 1.05 - 1.0472 = 0.0028 \text{ rad}$$

$$\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(120°) = 0.8660$$

$$\sin(2\beta) = \sin(2.1) = 0.8632$$

$$\text{Bracket} = 0.0028 + \dfrac{0.8660 - 0.8632}{2} = 0.0028 + 0.0014 = 0.0042$$

$$V_{o,\text{rms}} = 230 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{0.0042}{\pi}} = 230 \times \sqrt{0.001337} = 230 \times 0.03657 \approx 8.41 \text{ V}$$

**Conduction Mode Comment:**
- Since $\alpha = 60°$ is only marginally greater than $\phi = 45°$, the conduction angle is **extremely short** (γ ≈ 0.003 rad ≈ 0.17°), producing a very narrow voltage pulse and negligible RMS output.
- The controller is operating in the **boundary region** between continuous and discontinuous conduction.
- For a meaningful output, α must be increased well above φ — for example, α = 120° would give γ ≈ 90° and substantially more power transfer.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
>
> 1. **Confusing conduction angle with firing angle** — Students often write $\gamma = \alpha$ or $\gamma = 2\alpha$. Remember: **γ = β − α**, and for R-loads **β = π always**.
> 2. **Forgetting half-wave symmetry** — When squaring the load voltage for RMS calculation, the two half-cycles contribute **identically**. Do not double-count or include the blocking intervals in the integration.
> 3. **Treating the R-L case as if β = π** — This is a catastrophic error. For R-L loads with α > φ, the current continues into the next half-cycle. The extinction angle β **must** be solved from the transcendental equation.
> 4. **Ignoring the average voltage = 0 result** — For symmetrically fired controllers, the DC component of $v_o$ is exactly zero. Writing $V_{o,\text{avg}} = V_m(1 + \cos\alpha)/\pi$ is only correct for **half-wave** configurations, not for the TRIAC/SCR-pair full-wave topology studied here.
> 5. **Wrong units on α and β** — Always work in **radians** when evaluating $\sin$ and $\cos$ functions, or convert degrees to radians first: $°\times \pi/180$.
> 6. **Neglecting device voltage drop** — The textbook formula assumes an **ideal switch**. In the lab, the TRIAC drops about **1.5–2 V**; this may be ignored in calculations but should be mentioned in the viva.
> 7. **Missing snubber mention** — In the viva, failure to mention the **RC snubber across the TRIAC** results in loss of marks — it is a safety-critical component.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 Lab Exam**
>
> ✅ A single-phase AC voltage controller converts **fixed AC → variable AC** at the **same frequency** using **phase-angle control** with natural commutation.
>
> ✅ The **firing angle α** is the delay from the source zero-crossing to the gate pulse; range is **0 to π** (or 0° to 180°).
>
> ✅ For a **R-load**: extinction angle **β = π** always; conduction angle **γ = π − α**; average output voltage = **0** (symmetric full-wave).
>
> ✅ For a **R-L load with α > φ**: extinction angle **β > π** satisfies $\sin(\beta - \phi) = \sin(\alpha - \phi)\,e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/\omega\tau}$; conduction angle **γ = β − α**.
>
> ✅ For a **R-L load with α ≤ φ**: conduction is **continuous** across half-cycles; output voltage = **full source**.
>
> ✅ RMS output voltage (R-load): $V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \tfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}$.
>
> ✅ RMS output voltage (R-L load): $V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left[(\beta - \alpha) + \tfrac{\sin 2\alpha - \sin 2\beta}{2}\right]}$.
>
> ✅ Load impedance angle: $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega L / R)$; load time constant: $\tau = L/R$.
>
> ✅ Instantaneous load current (R-L): $i_o(t) = (V_m/Z)[\sin(\omega t - \phi) - \sin(\alpha - \phi)e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)/\omega\tau}]$.
>
> ✅ Output waveform contains **only odd harmonics** due to half-wave symmetry of symmetric firing.
>
> ✅ Lab equipment checklist: **Isolation transformer (1:1)**, **TRIAC on heatsink**, **UJT firing circuit with 10 kΩ pot**, **RC snubber (100 Ω + 0.1 µF)**, **pulse transformer (1:1)**, **DSO with 4 channels**, **R and R-L load banks**, **MCB + HRC fuse** for protection.
>
> ✅ Lab procedure: (1) verify zero gate pulse → $v_o = 0$; (2) advance α from 0° → $v_o$ increases; (3) capture waveforms for at least 5 values of α (e.g., 30°, 60°, 90°, 120°, 150°); (4) tabulate $V_{o,\text{rms}}$ vs α; (5) plot and compare with theoretical curve.
>
> ✅ Always remember: **R-load gives β = π, γ = π − α**; **R-L load gives β > π, γ = β − α < π** (when α > φ).
>
> ✅ Power delivered: $P_o = I_{o,\text{rms}}^2 \times R$ (resistive part only).
>
> ✅ **Safety first**: Always engage the MCB, verify earthing, and never operate without the RC snubber.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Isolated Gate Driver Circuit for Single-Phase Half-Bridge IGBT/MOSFET Inverter and Dead-Time Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL507 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Isolated Gate Driver Circuit for Single-Phase Half-Bridge IGBT/MOSFET Inverter and Dead-Time Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Isolated Gate Driver Circuit** is an interface circuit that provides conditioned, level-shifted, and galvanically isolated gate signals to the switching power semiconductor devices (IGBTs/MOSFETs) of a power electronic converter, while maintaining strict electrical isolation between the low-voltage control logic domain and the high-voltage power domain.

For a **Single-Phase Half-Bridge Inverter**, the gate driver must independently control the **high-side switch** (whose source/emitter is floating and switches between 0 V and $+V_{DC}$) and the **low-side switch** (whose source/emitter is referenced to the negative DC rail). This is achieved using techniques such as **bootstrap drivers**, **pulse transformers**, or **digital isolators** (optocouplers).

**Dead-Time ($t_d$)** is the intentional, brief blanking interval inserted between the turn-OFF of one switch in a leg and the turn-ON of the complementary switch, to prevent **shoot-through** (cross-conduction) of the DC bus through both switches simultaneously.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **railway level crossing with two gates on either side of the tracks**. If both gates open at the exact same instant (when a train is approaching), a car could slip through and collide with the train. The traffic controller solves this by **always opening one gate only after the other has fully closed** — that safety gap is analogous to **dead-time**. 

The **isolation barrier** is like a **glass wall** between the train operator's control cabin and the high-voltage third rail: the operator pushes buttons (low-voltage logic signals), and the commands pass *through* the glass via light (optocoupler) or magnetic coupling (transformer), but no electrical current flows across — keeping the operator safe from the lethal track voltage.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Miller Plateau Voltage ($V_{gp}$)**: typically **5 V – 8 V** for standard IGBTs.
- **Gate-Emitter Threshold ($V_{GE(th)}$)**: typically **5 V – 6.5 V** for most IGBTs.
- **Required Gate Drive Voltage**: typically **+15 V** for turn-ON, **−5 V to −15 V** for turn-OFF (negative drive for IGBTs).
- **Typical Dead-Time Range**: **0.5 µs – 5 µs** depending on switching frequency and device turn-OFF time ($t_{off}$).
- **Bootstrap Capacitor Value**: typically **0.1 µF – 10 µF** (ceramic or electrolytic).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The experiment aims to (i) design and verify an isolated gate driver for a half-bridge, (ii) generate complementary PWM signals with programmable dead-time, and (iii) observe the dead-time effect on the inverter output waveform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Dead-Time**: The interval during which *neither* the high-side nor the low-side switch in a phase leg is conducting, inserted to prevent simultaneous conduction (shoot-through) which would short-circuit the DC bus and cause catastrophic device failure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Isolation is Mandatory in Gate Drivers

A half-bridge inverter has two switches stacked in series across $V_{DC}$. The **emitter of the high-side IGBT** is *not* at ground — it swings between **0 V** and **$+V_{DC}$** at the switching frequency. If a non-isolated driver were used, the control circuit's ground would be dragged up and down, causing:
- Destruction of the controller (e.g., DSP, FPGA).
- False triggering and ground-loop noise.
- Safety hazard to the operator.

Therefore, the gate signal must be **galvanically isolated**, typically rated for $\geq V_{DC} + \text{margin}$ (e.g., 600 V or 1500 V isolation for 400 V systems).

### 2.2 The Three Isolation Techniques

**1. Optocoupler Isolation (e.g., HCPL-3120, TLP250)**
- Uses an internal LED + phototransistor. Input signal drives the LED; output transistor reproduces the signal on the isolated side.
- Pros: Simple, cheap, moderate speed (~µs).
- Cons: **Common-mode dv/dt immunity is weak** (typically < 15 kV/µs); aging of LED reduces CTR over time.

**2. Pulse Transformer Isolation**
- Gate pulse is modulated onto a high-frequency carrier, passed through a small HF transformer, then demodulated.
- Pros: Excellent dv/dt immunity, no need for isolated DC supply if signal is AC-coupled.
- Cons: Cannot pass DC signals (only pulses); duty-cycle limitations.

**3. Digital (Capacitive/Magnetic) Isolators (e.g., Si8261, ADuM1411)**
- Uses high-frequency carrier and a transformer or capacitive barrier.
- Pros: Very high CMRR (> 25 kV/µs), fast propagation delay (~50 ns), reliable.
- Cons: Costlier; still require isolated supply on the output side.

### 2.3 The Bootstrap Driver — Powering the High-Side Switch

The **bootstrap technique** is the most economical way to provide a floating supply to the high-side driver using only **one isolated supply** (for the low side).

**Operating Principle:**
1. During the **low-side ON / high-side OFF** interval, the bootstrap diode ($D_{boot}$) is forward-biased from $V_{CC}$ (+15 V), charging the bootstrap capacitor ($C_{boot}$) to nearly $V_{CC}$.
2. When the **high-side turns ON**, the source of the high-side floats up to $V_{DC}$. The diode becomes reverse-biased, and $C_{boot}$ discharges only into the gate of the high-side IGBT — providing the isolated floating supply referenced to the now-floating source.

### 2.4 Dead-Time Generation — The "Why"

In a half-bridge leg with complementary PWM ($S_1$ and $\bar{S_1}$):
- IGBT turn-OFF time ($t_{off}$) is **not zero** (typically 100 ns – 1 µs).
- If $S_2$ is turned ON *exactly* when $S_1$ is signaled OFF, both devices conduct during the $t_{off}$ interval → **shoot-through** → DC bus short circuit → explosive failure.

**Solution**: Insert dead-time $t_d > t_{off(max)}$ between the falling edge of one gate signal and the rising edge of the complementary signal.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Symbol** | **Typical Value / Unit** | **Engineering Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dead-Time Condition | $t_d \geq t_{d(\text{off})} + t_{r} + t_{su}$ | 0.5 µs – 5 µs | Minimum blanking time to avoid shoot-through |
| Effective Output Voltage (half-bridge) | $V_{out} = \frac{V_{DC}}{2}$ for square wave, $V_{out} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_{DC}}{2}$ for PWM | Volts | Output amplitude depends on modulation index $m_a$ |
| RMS Output Voltage (square wave) | $V_{rms} = \frac{V_{DC}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Volts | For single-phase square-wave half-bridge |
| Bootstrap Capacitor (min) | $C_{boot} \geq \frac{Q_g}{\Delta V_{boot}}$ | Farads | $Q_g$ = total gate charge of IGBT |
| Bootstrap Diode Reverse Voltage | $V_{R(D_{boot})} \geq V_{DC(max)}$ | Volts | Must block full DC bus when high-side ON |
| PWM Switching Frequency | $f_{sw} = \frac{1}{T_{sw}}$ | 1 kHz – 100 kHz | Dictates dead-time sizing |
| Modulation Index | $m_a = \frac{V_{control}}{V_{tri}}$ | 0 to 1 | Amplitude of modulating sine w.r.t. carrier |
| Voltage Distortion due to Dead-Time | $V_{err} \approx -f_{sw} \cdot t_d \cdot V_{DC}$ | Volts | Loss of fundamental output per switching cycle |
| Common-Mode dv/dt Immunity | $\frac{dv}{dt}_{CM}$ | kV/µs | Min 10 kV/µs for industrial inverters |
| Gate Drive Power | $P_g = Q_g \cdot V_{gs} \cdot f_{sw}$ | Watts | Required driver supply power |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical relationship for exam**: The dead-time introduces a **voltage error** at the inverter output proportional to $f_{sw} \cdot t_d \cdot V_{DC}$. Higher switching frequency → dead-time becomes a *larger* percentage of the period → greater output distortion. This is the fundamental trade-off in modern inverter design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Bootstrap Capacitor Value

The bootstrap capacitor must supply the total gate charge $Q_g$ of the IGBT during each high-side ON period without drooping more than $\Delta V_{boot}$ (typically 0.5 V – 1 V for stable gate drive).

**Step 1:** Define the charge delivered to the gate per switching cycle.

$$
Q_{total} = Q_g + I_{q(\text{driver})} \cdot t_{ON(\text{high-side})}
$$

**Step 2:** The capacitor voltage droop $\Delta V$ due to charge $Q$ removed is:

$$
\Delta V_{boot} = \frac{Q_{total}}{C_{boot}}
$$

**Step 3:** Solving for $C_{boot}$:

$$
C_{boot} = \frac{Q_{total}}{\Delta V_{boot}}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $Q_{total} \approx Q_g$ (driver quiescent current usually negligible at high $f_{sw}$):

$$
C_{boot} \geq \frac{Q_g}{\Delta V_{boot}}
$$

**Step 5:** Numerical example for an IGBT with $Q_g = 100$ nC and allowable droop $\Delta V_{boot} = 0.5$ V:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{boot} &\geq \frac{100 \times 10^{-9}}{0.5} \\
&= 200 \times 10^{-9} \\
&= 0.2 \; \mu F
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Practice**: Designers select 2×–5× the calculated minimum to provide margin; hence typical values are **1 µF – 10 µF**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Dead-Time Voltage Error in PWM Inverter Output

**Step 1:** Ideal PWM inverter produces an output of $\pm V_{DC}/2$ (for half-bridge). In one fundamental cycle of period $T_0$, with switching period $T_{sw}$, the number of switching intervals is $N = T_0 / T_{sw}$.

**Step 2:** During each switching interval, dead-time $t_d$ causes the output to be **clamped to 0** (via the freewheeling diode) instead of following the commanded $+V_{DC}/2$ or $-V_{DC}/2$.

**Step 3:** The average voltage loss per switching cycle is:

$$
\Delta V_{sw} = \frac{t_d}{T_{sw}} \cdot V_{DC}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $T_{sw} = 1/f_{sw}$:

$$
\Delta V_{sw} = t_d \cdot f_{sw} \cdot V_{DC}
$$

**Step 5:** For a sinusoidal PWM, the polarity of $\Delta V_{sw}$ depends on the current direction. Assuming unity current direction in a half-cycle:

$$
V_{error,\text{avg}} = f_{sw} \cdot t_d \cdot V_{DC}
$$

**Step 6:** The fundamental output voltage is therefore reduced by:

$$
V_{1,\text{actual}} = V_{1,\text{ideal}} - \frac{4 \cdot f_{sw} \cdot t_d \cdot V_{DC}}{\pi}
$$

(For bipolar PWM; factor depends on modulation scheme.)

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Selection of Dead-Time from Device Datasheet

From the IGBT datasheet, three timing parameters are required:

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $t_{d(\text{off})}$ | Turn-OFF delay | 100 ns |
| $t_f$ | Fall time | 50 ns |
| $t_{d(\text{on})}$ | Turn-ON delay of complementary switch | 100 ns |
| $t_r$ | Rise time | 50 ns |

**Safe dead-time formula:**

$$
t_d \geq t_{d(\text{off}),1} + t_{f,1} + t_{su}
$$

where $t_{su}$ is the setup-time safety margin (typically 100 ns – 500 ns).

**Numerical example:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_d &\geq 100 \text{ ns} + 50 \text{ ns} + 300 \text{ ns} \\
&= 450 \text{ ns} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{select } t_d = 1 \;\mu s \text{ (with safety margin)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Dead-Time vs. Output Waveform Quality

| **Dead-Time $t_d$** | **Shoot-Through Risk** | **Output Distortion** | **Conduction Loss** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 ns (ideal) | High (unsafe) | Zero | Low | NOT permitted |
| 0.5 µs | Low | Small | Slight increase | High-frequency MOSFET drives |
| 1 µs – 2 µs | Very Low | Moderate | Notable | General-purpose IGBT inverters |
| > 5 µs | Negligible | Severe | High | Low-frequency / thyristor circuits |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Isolated Half-Bridge Driver

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL DOMAIN (Low-Voltage, Isolated)"]
        MCU["DSP / FPGA / PWM Generator"]
        DTGEN["Dead-Time Generator Block"]
        DRV1["Low-Side Driver Logic"]
        DRV2["High-Side Driver Logic"]
        MCU --> DTGEN
        DTGEN -->|"S1 (PWM with delay)"| DRV1
        DTGEN -->|"S2 (Complementary, delayed)"| DRV2
    end

    subgraph ISO1["ISOLATION BARRIER 1 (Low-Side)"]
        OPTO1["Optocoupler / Digital Isolator (Low-Side)"]
        DRV1 --> OPTO1
    end

    subgraph ISO2["ISOLATION BARRIER 2 (High-Side)"]
        OPTO2["Optocoupler / Pulse Transformer (High-Side)"]
        DRV2 --> OPTO2
    end

    subgraph POW["POWER DOMAIN (High-Voltage)"]
        VCC["+15 V Isolated Supply (VCC)"]
        DBOOT["Bootstrap Diode (D_boot)"]
        CBOOT["Bootstrap Capacitor (C_boot)"]
        LO["Low-Side IGBT/MOSFET (S2)"]
        HI["High-Side IGBT/MOSFET (S1)"]
        VDC["DC Bus (+V_DC)"]
        LOAD["AC Load (Half-Bridge Output)"]

        VCC --> DBOOT
        DBOOT --> CBOOT
        CBOOT -->|"Floating +15 V (referenced to S1 source)"| HI

        OPTO1 -->|"Gate Drive S2"| LO
        OPTO2 -->|"Gate Drive S1"| HI

        VDC --> HI
        HI --> LOAD
        LO --> GND
        LOAD --> LO
    end
```

### 4.2 Timing Diagram — PWM Signals with Dead-Time

```mermaid
gantt
    title Complementary PWM with Inserted Dead-Time
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    
    section PWM Signal S1 (High-Side)
    ON_S1_A    :a1, 0, 5
    DEAD1      :crit, dead1, 5, 1
    ON_S1_B    :a2, 6, 4
    
    section PWM Signal S2 (Low-Side, Inverted)
    DEAD2      :crit, dead2, 0, 1
    ON_S2_A    :b1, 1, 4
    DEAD3      :crit, dead3, 5, 1
    ON_S2_B    :b2, 6, 4
```

**Reading the chart:** During the intervals marked `crit` (DEAD1, DEAD2, DEAD3), **both** gate signals are LOW — neither switch conducts. The load current freewheels through one of the anti-parallel diodes, and the output node is clamped to approximately 0 V (or $\pm V_{F}$, the diode drop).

### 4.3 Detailed Schematic — Bootstrap High-Side Driver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC["+15 V (VCC)"] -->|"Forward-biased when LO is ON"| DB["D_boot (Fast Recovery Diode)"]
    DB --> CB["C_boot (Ceramic + Electrolytic)"]
    CB --> NODE["Node: Source of S1 (Floating, swings 0 to +V_DC)"]
    
    HV["High-Side Driver IC (e.g., IR2110)"] -->|"HO pin"| GATE1["Gate of S1 (High-Side IGBT)"]
    HV -->|"HS pin (return)"| NODE
    HV -->|"VB pin (floating supply)"| CB
    
    LV["Low-Side Driver (e.g., IR2110 LO pin)"] --> GATE2["Gate of S2 (Low-Side IGBT)"]
    VCC --> LV
    GND["Logic Ground"] --> LV
```

**Key Insight**: Note that $C_{boot}$ is connected between $V_B$ (bootstrap rail, +15 V above floating source) and $V_S$ (the floating source node). The IR2110 is a classic half-bridge driver IC that integrates both low-side and high-side channels with built-in dead-time generation.

### 4.4 Experimental Setup Block Diagram (KTU Lab)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR["DC Power Supply (+15 V, +5 V)"] --> MCU
    SIG["Function/PWM Generator"] --> MCU
    MCU["DSP/Arduino (PWM + Dead-Time Code)"] --> ISO
    ISO["Isolation Circuit (TLP250 / Pulse Transformer)"] --> GD
    GD["Gate Driver Stage (IR2110 / Discrete Bootstrap)"] --> INV
    INV["Single-Phase Half-Bridge Inverter (IGBT/MOSFET Module)"] --> LOAD
    LOAD["R or R-L Load"] --> OSC["Oscilloscope (4-Ch: G1, G2, Vout, I_load)"]
    INV --> OSC
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Why is galvanic isolation mandatory in the gate driver circuit of a half-bridge inverter? Mention two isolation techniques.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Galvanic isolation is mandatory because the emitter of the high-side IGBT is **not at ground potential** — it switches between **0 V** and **$+V_{DC}$** at the PWM frequency. If a non-isolated driver were used, this floating node would drag the controller ground along with it, causing:
(i) Destruction of the low-voltage controller (DSP/FPGA),
(ii) False triggering due to ground loops,
(iii) Safety hazard to the operator.

**Two isolation techniques (1 mark each):**
1. **Optocoupler-based isolation** (e.g., TLP250, HCPL-3120): uses an internal LED-phototransistor pair across an isolation barrier.
2. **Pulse transformer isolation**: the gate signal modulates a high-frequency carrier, which is transformer-coupled and then demodulated. Excellent dv/dt immunity, no isolated DC supply needed for AC-coupled signals.

*(Alternative valid answers: digital isolators using capacitive coupling — e.g., Si8261; or coreless transformers.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define dead-time in a half-bridge inverter. What happens if dead-time is set to zero?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Dead-time ($t_d$)** is the intentional short interval inserted between the turn-OFF of one switch in a phase leg and the turn-ON of the complementary switch, during which **both switches remain OFF**.

**If $t_d = 0$ (no dead-time):** Because the turn-OFF time ($t_{off}$) of an IGBT/MOSFET is finite (100 ns – 1 µs typically), there exists a brief interval where:
- $S_1$ has been signaled OFF but is *still conducting* (carrying current during $t_{off}$), AND
- $S_2$ has been signaled ON and is now also conducting.

This simultaneous conduction is called **shoot-through** (or cross-conduction). It creates a **low-impedance short circuit across the DC bus** ($V_{DC}$ applied across the on-state resistance of both switches), causing:
- A massive fault current (potentially thousands of amps),
- Catastrophic thermal destruction of both switches,
- Possible explosion of the DC bus capacitor.

Hence dead-time is a *must*, not an option. The condition is $t_d \geq t_{off,max} + t_{safety\,margin}$.

---

### PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **bootstrap-based isolated gate driver** for the high-side switch of a single-phase half-bridge IGBT inverter. Derive the minimum bootstrap capacitor value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-phase half-bridge inverter uses IGBTs each with $Q_g = 80$ nC. The switching frequency is 20 kHz. The driver IC quiescent current is 200 µA. Calculate the minimum bootstrap capacitor if a voltage droop of 0.8 V is permissible. If dead-time is 1 µs, calculate the **percentage loss in output fundamental RMS voltage** assuming an ideal 230 V square wave. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a) — Bootstrap Driver Operation (7 marks)

**Block Diagram** *(already shown in Section 4.3)*: Components are $V_{CC}$, $D_{boot}$, $C_{boot}$, high-side driver IC, and the high-side IGBT gate-emitter terminals.

**Operating Principle (2 phases):**

**Phase 1 — Low-Side ON, High-Side OFF (Charging phase):**
- Source of high-side IGBT ($V_S$) is pulled to ground (0 V) via the conducting low-side switch.
- Bootstrap diode $D_{boot}$ becomes **forward-biased** from $V_{CC}$ = +15 V.
- $C_{boot}$ charges up to approximately $V_{CC} - V_F \approx 14.3$ V.

**Phase 2 — High-Side ON, Low-Side OFF (Driving phase):**
- $V_S$ swings up to $+V_{DC}$.
- $D_{boot}$ becomes **reverse-biased** (its anode is at +15 V, cathode is at $V_S + V_C = V_{DC} + 14.3$ V).
- $C_{boot}$ is now the **only source** for the high-side driver, holding ~14.3 V between $V_B$ and $V_S$.
- This provides the floating +15 V (w.r.t. $V_S$) needed to drive the high-side gate.

**Derivation of Minimum $C_{boot}$:** *(see Section 3.1)*

$$
C_{boot} = \frac{Q_g + I_q \cdot t_{ON}}{\Delta V_{boot}}
$$

(7 marks allocated: 2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for phase explanation, 2 marks for derivation.)

---

#### Solution to Q.A(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $Q_g = 80$ nC $= 80 \times 10^{-9}$ C
- $f_{sw} = 20$ kHz
- $I_q = 200$ µA $= 200 \times 10^{-6}$ A
- $\Delta V_{boot} = 0.8$ V
- $t_d = 1$ µs $= 1 \times 10^{-6}$ s
- $V_{DC} = 230$ V (assumed square-wave input)

**Step 1 — Switching period:**
$$
T_{sw} = \frac{1}{f_{sw}} = \frac{1}{20{,}000} = 50 \; \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — High-side ON duration (assume 50% duty):**
$$
t_{ON} = 0.5 \times T_{sw} = 25 \; \mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Total charge delivered per cycle:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= Q_g + I_q \cdot t_{ON} \\
&= 80 \times 10^{-9} + (200 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (25 \times 10^{-6}) \\
&= 80 \times 10^{-9} + 5 \times 10^{-9} \\
&= 85 \times 10^{-9} \; C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum bootstrap capacitor:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{boot,min} &= \frac{Q_{total}}{\Delta V_{boot}} \\
&= \frac{85 \times 10^{-9}}{0.8} \\
&= 106.25 \; nF
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Practical selection: 1 µF with safety margin = 10× calculated value.)* ✓

**Step 5 — Voltage loss due to dead-time per cycle:**
$$
\Delta V_{sw} = \frac{t_d}{T_{sw}} \cdot V_{DC} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{50 \times 10^{-6}} \cdot 230 = 4.6 \; V
$$

**Step 6 — Ideal RMS voltage (square wave half-bridge):**
$$
V_{rms,ideal} = \frac{V_{DC}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{230}{\sqrt{2}} = 162.6 \; V
$$

**Step 7 — Percentage loss:**
$$
\% \text{ loss} = \frac{\Delta V_{sw}}{V_{DC}} \times 100 = \frac{4.6}{230} \times 100 = 2.0\%
$$

**Answers:** $C_{boot,min} = 106.25$ nF (use 1 µF in practice); **percentage loss = 2%**.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** What is **shoot-through** in a half-bridge inverter? With the help of timing waveforms, explain how dead-time is generated and inserted using a digital delay technique. Mention two effects of excessive dead-time on inverter performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A half-bridge inverter operates with $V_{DC} = 400$ V at $f_{sw} = 10$ kHz. The IGBT datasheet gives $t_{d(\text{off})} = 200$ ns, $t_f = 80$ ns. **(i)** Calculate the **minimum safe dead-time** with a 300 ns safety margin. **(ii)** If the actual dead-time is set to 2 µs, calculate the **RMS voltage error** per cycle. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a) — Shoot-Through & Dead-Time Generation (7 marks)

**Shoot-through (2 marks):** Shoot-through is the simultaneous conduction of both the high-side and low-side switches in a phase leg, creating a low-impedance short circuit across the DC bus. This generates extremely high fault currents (kA range) within microseconds, leading to thermal runaway and explosive destruction of the IGBTs.

**Timing Waveform & Digital Dead-Time Generation (3 marks):**

A simple digital dead-time generator uses a chain of buffers/inverters (e.g., 74HC04) or a programmable delay line (e.g., MC14538). The original PWM signal is **split into two paths**:
- **Path A (direct)**: drives the high-side IGBT.
- **Path B (delayed by $t_d$)**: drives the low-side IGBT (and vice-versa for the complementary signal).

The rising edge of each signal is delayed by $t_d$ relative to the falling edge of its complement, producing a "blanking" interval where both outputs are LOW.

**Two effects of excessive dead-time (2 marks):**
1. **Output voltage distortion**: The fundamental RMS voltage decreases by $f_{sw} \cdot t_d \cdot V_{DC}$ per cycle; total harmonic distortion (THD) increases.
2. **Increased conduction losses**: During dead-time, current freewheels through the anti-parallel diode (forward drop ~1.5–2 V instead of ~1 V for IGBT), increasing average conduction loss.

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**(i) Minimum safe dead-time (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{d,min} &= t_{d(\text{off})} + t_f + t_{safety} \\
&= 200 \text{ ns} + 80 \text{ ns} + 300 \text{ ns} \\
&= 580 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

Practical selection: $t_d = 1 \;\mu s$ (round up for safety).

**(ii) Voltage error per cycle (4 marks):**

Given: $V_{DC} = 400$ V, $f_{sw} = 10$ kHz, actual $t_d = 2 \;\mu s$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{sw} &= \frac{1}{f_{sw}} = \frac{1}{10{,}000} = 100 \; \mu s \\[4pt]
\Delta V_{sw} &= \frac{t_d}{T_{sw}} \cdot V_{DC} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{100 \times 10^{-6}} \times 400 \\[4pt]
&= 0.02 \times 400 \\[4pt]
&= 8 \; V
\end{aligned}
$$

**RMS voltage error per cycle = 8 V** (which is 2% of $V_{DC}$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 
> **Mistake 1**: Forgetting to include the **safety margin** in dead-time calculation. Many students write $t_d = t_{off}$ only. A minimum 20%–50% margin (typically 100 ns – 500 ns) is expected for a full-mark answer.
> 
> **Mistake 2**: Confusing the **direction of the bootstrap diode**. The diode's cathode must connect to the **bootstrap capacitor** (which floats with the high-side source), not to the gate. Drawing the diode reversed is a fatal schematic error.
> 
> **Mistake 3**: Writing $V_{rms} = V_{DC}$ for a half-bridge instead of $V_{rms} = V_{DC} / \sqrt{2}$. The half-bridge output is **$\pm V_{DC}/2$**, *not* $\pm V_{DC}$. (A full-bridge gives $\pm V_{DC}$.)
> 
> **Mistake 4**: Omitting the **freewheeling diode action** during dead-time. Examiners expect students to mention that during dead-time, the load current freewheels through an anti-parallel diode and the output is clamped to ~0 V (or $\pm V_F$), *not* left undefined.
> 
> **Mistake 5**: For unit conversions. $Q_g$ is in nC, $C_{boot}$ comes out in nF or µF — failing to convert $10^{-9}$ properly will lose 1 mark.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Isolation is mandatory** because the high-side IGBT emitter floats up to $+V_{DC}$ at the switching frequency — non-isolated drive destroys the controller.
- **Three isolation methods**: optocoupler, pulse transformer, digital isolator (capactive/transformer). Choose based on dv/dt immunity required.
- **Bootstrap driver** uses one $V_{CC}$ supply and a diode + capacitor ($D_{boot}$, $C_{boot}$) to create a floating +15 V referenced to the high-side source. It is the **most economical** solution for half-bridges.
- **Bootstrap capacitor sizing**: $C_{boot} \geq Q_g / \Delta V_{boot}$ (use 2×–10× in practice; typical values 1 µF – 10 µF).
- **Dead-time ($t_d$)**: intentional blanking interval between complementary switches to prevent **shoot-through** (DC bus short).
- **Dead-time sizing**: $t_d \geq t_{d(\text{off})} + t_f + t_{safety}$ (typical 0.5 µs – 5 µs; example: 580 ns minimum → use 1 µs).
- **Excess dead-time penalty**: $V_{error} = f_{sw} \cdot t_d \cdot V_{DC}$ (proportional to switching frequency — major issue in high-frequency drives).
- **During dead-time**: load current freewheels through **anti-parallel diode**, output node is at $\approx 0$ V (or $\pm V_F$).
- **Standard gate voltages**: $+15$ V ON, $-5$ V to $-15$ V OFF (for IGBTs; MOSFETs often use 0 V OFF).
- **Half-bridge output**: $\pm V_{DC}/2$, $V_{rms,ideal} = V_{DC}/\sqrt{2}$. (Distinguish from full-bridge: $\pm V_{DC}$.)
- **Gate driver IC examples to remember**: IR2110 (classic bootstrap half-bridge driver), HCPL-3120 (optocoupler), TLP250 (optocoupler), Si8261 / ADuM1411 (digital isolators).
- **Propagation delay mismatch** between the two optocouplers/isolators worsens effective dead-time — use matched-channel isolators for symmetric timing.
- **Miller plateau** must be overcome by the gate drive; insufficient drive current at $V_{gp}$ slows switching and increases loss.
