- **Common KTU viva questions**: "Why is the bootstrap diode a fast-recovery type?" (Answer: to prevent reverse recovery current from discharging $C_{boot}$ during high-side turn-on transition, which would cause $V_{BS}$ to dip and possibly lose gate drive).
- **PCB layout tip**: Keep the high-side gate-drive loop area ($V_B$ → gate → source → $V_S$) **as small as possible** to minimize parasitic inductance and ringing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gate Drive Using Bootstrap Technique for MOSFET/IGBT with Protection Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL507 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Gate Drive Using Bootstrap Technique for MOSFET/IGBT with Protection Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Power Electronics Lab (PCEOL507)**, a **Bootstrap Gate Driver** is a high-voltage level-shifting circuit topology used to generate a floating, isolated gate-emitter/source drive voltage ($V_{GE}$ or $V_{GS}$) for the **high-side switch** in a half-bridge, full-bridge, or three-phase inverter leg. Because the source (or emitter) of the high-side device swings between the negative DC bus and the positive DC bus at the switching frequency, it cannot be referenced to a fixed ground. The bootstrap technique uses a single low-voltage power supply (typically **+15 V**) referenced to the *system ground*, and through a fast-recovery diode and reservoir capacitor, "bootstraps" a floating supply that rides on top of the switching node.

The standard implementation pairs the bootstrap network with **integrated half-bridge driver ICs** (e.g., IR2110, IR2113, IRS2003, UCC27714) which contain both the low-side and high-side channel drivers, level shifters, and built-in protection features such as **Under-Voltage Lock-Out (UVLO)**, **desaturation (DESAT) protection**, **shoot-through interlocking (dead-time insertion)**, and **Miller clamping**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEOL507 – Module 2):**
> Students must be able to *design, wire, and experimentally verify* a bootstrap gate-drive circuit for an IGBT/MOSFET half-bridge, observe the gate-emitter waveforms using an isolated oscilloscope probe (or differential probe), and confirm the operation of at least one protection feature (typically UVLO).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing on a **moving escalator** and you want to hand a glass of water to a friend standing on the same escalator without spilling it. You cannot simply pour from a fixed tap on the wall — the water would miss your friend's glass as both of you move up and down.

The **bootstrap technique** solves this exact problem:

- The **wall tap** = your fixed **+15 V DC supply** (referenced to ground).
- The **escalator handrail** = the **switching node** that moves between **0 V** and **+V_DC**.
- The **small bucket you carry with you** = the **bootstrap capacitor ($C_{BS}$)** which always travels *with* the high-side source.
- The **one-way valve (check valve)** = the **bootstrap diode ($D_{BS}$)** that lets water in when the node is low, but stops it from leaking back out when the node flies high.
- The **refill station** = the moment when the **low-side switch is ON** and the switching node is pulled to ground — during this "refill window" the bucket is replenished.

When the high-side switch needs to turn ON, the bucket ($C_{BS}$) pours its stored charge into the gate of the high-side MOSFET/IGBT, providing the energy needed to fully enhance the channel — even though the source is now floating at the DC bus voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The bootstrap capacitor does **not** supply continuous gate current — it supplies the brief gate-charge impulse ($Q_g$) each switching cycle, and is *recharged* every cycle when the low-side switch is ON. This is why the bootstrap topology **cannot** drive a 100% duty-cycle high-side pulse — there must always be a brief recharge interval.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants in This Experiment

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Logic / driver supply | $V_{CC}$ | **+15 V** |
| Floating supply rail | $V_{BS}$ | **+15 V** (above $V_S$) |
| DC bus voltage | $V_{DC}$ | **30 V – 400 V** (lab) |
| Gate threshold voltage | $V_{GE(th)}$ | **2.5 V – 5.5 V** for IGBT |
| UVLO turn-on threshold | $V_{BSUV+}$ | **~8.7 V** (IR2110) |
| UVLO turn-off threshold | $V_{BSUV-}$ | **~8.3 V** (IR2110) |
| Bootstrap diode forward drop | $V_{F,BS}$ | **~1.0 V – 1.5 V** (UF4007) |
| Switching frequency | $f_{sw}$ | **1 kHz – 50 kHz** (lab) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Conventional Drive Fails for the High-Side Switch

In a half-bridge leg, the **low-side** switch (S2) has its emitter/source tied to ground — a simple gate drive works fine. But the **high-side** switch (S1) has its source *not* at ground; it is at the switching node $V_S$, which oscillates between **0 V** (when S2 is ON) and **+V_DC** (when S1 is ON). To turn S1 ON we must apply a gate-source voltage $V_{GS1} \approx +15\text{ V}$, which means the *gate* must sit **+15 V above** a node that is itself floating between 0 and $V_{DC}$.

A transformer-isolated drive could solve this, but it is bulky, expensive, and introduces leakage inductance that ruins fast switching. The **bootstrap** is a cheaper, more compact alternative.

### 2.2 Operational Sequence (Charge-Pump Action)

The circuit has four key elements: **bootstrap diode $D_{BS}$**, **bootstrap capacitor $C_{BS}$**, the **high-side driver output (HO)** pin, and the **high-side return (VS)** pin. The operation cycles through three logical states:

**State 1 — Low-side ON, Recharge Phase (S2 conducting):**
- Switching node $V_S \approx 0$ V (pulled to ground by S2).
- The diode $D_{BS}$ is forward-biased because $V_{CC} > V_S$.
- Current flows: $V_{CC} \rightarrow D_{BS} \rightarrow C_{BS} \rightarrow \text{VS pin (grounded through S2)}$.
- $C_{BS}$ charges up to approximately $V_{CC} - V_{F,BS}$.
- This state also provides the *low-side* gate charge to S2 directly from $V_{CC}$.

**State 2 — Dead-Time / Both OFF:**
- S1 and S2 are both OFF (briefly, to prevent shoot-through).
- $V_S$ floats, governed by load current direction.
- The charge on $C_{BS}$ is held by the blocking action of $D_{BS}$.

**State 3 — High-side ON, Driving Phase (S1 conducting):**
- The HO pin is driven high by the internal level-shifter + buffer.
- The voltage across $C_{BS}$ (which was charged in State 1) now appears as $V_{GS}$ of S1.
- $V_S$ rises rapidly toward $V_{DC}$, and the *entire* $V_{BS}$ node rises with it.
- S1's gate sees $V_{GS} \approx V_{CC} - V_{F,BS} - \Delta V_{droop}$, which is still well above $V_{GE(th)}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the diode is critical:** During State 3, the voltage at the *cathode* of $D_{BS}$ rises to $V_S + V_{CC} \approx V_{DC} + 15$ V, while its *anode* remains at $V_{CC}$. The diode must block this reverse voltage without conducting — hence an **ultra-fast recovery diode** (UF4007, MUR460, ES1J) is mandatory. A slow diode would ring and cause catastrophic failure.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

The following table consolidates every equation you must memorise for the KTU lab viva, record writing, and viva voce.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bootstrap capacitor stored charge | $Q_{BS} \;=\; C_{BS}\,\Delta V_{BS}$ | Coulombs; $\Delta V_{BS}$ is allowed droop |
| 2 | Required gate charge | $Q_g$ | Datasheet parameter (nC) |
| 3 | Bootstrap capacitance sizing (lower bound) | $C_{BS} \;\geq\; \dfrac{Q_g}{\Delta V_{BS}}$ | Choose $\Delta V_{BS} \approx 0.5$ V to 1 V |
| 4 | Empirical design (most common) | $C_{BS} \;\geq\; 10 \cdot C_{iss}$ | $C_{iss}$ = input capacitance of switch |
| 5 | Bootstrap diode reverse rating | $V_{R,BS} \;\geq\; V_{DC,\max} \;+\; V_{CC}$ | e.g. $\geq 600$ V for 400 V bus |
| 6 | Floating supply rail (steady state) | $V_{BS} \;=\; V_{CC} \;-\; V_{F,BS} \;-\; \Delta V_{droop}$ | Typically 13 V to 14 V |
| 7 | Maximum continuous ON time | $t_{ON,\max} \;\leq\; R_{BS} \cdot C_{BS} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{F,BS}}{V_{BS,UV-}}\right)$ | Limits 100 % duty-cycle operation |
| 8 | UVLO hysteresis | $\Delta V_{UVLO} \;=\; V_{BSUV+} \;-\; V_{BSUV-}$ | IR2110: $\Delta V \approx 0.4$ V |
| 9 | Power dissipated in gate drive | $P_{gate} \;=\; Q_g \cdot V_{GS} \cdot f_{sw}$ | Determines bootstrap diode heating |
| 10 | Shoot-through dead-time | $t_{dt} \;\geq\; t_{d(\text{off})} \;-\; t_{d(\text{on})}$ | Provided by driver IC internally |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid using vertical bars in tables:** Whenever you need to denote absolute value in KTU record answers, always use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mathrm{abs}(\cdot)$ — never the raw pipe character $\vert$ — otherwise your markdown table will break.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

The bootstrap technique is the **industry default** for low-to-medium power inverters:
- **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** up to ~7.5 kW.
- **Solar micro-inverters** and string inverters.
- **EV traction inverters** (where galvanic isolation is added via digital isolators + bootstrap on the secondary side).
- **Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS)** with synchronous rectification.

Above ~10 kW, designers switch to **coreless transformers** or **isolated DC-DC converters** + gate-drive transformers because the bootstrap capacitor cannot supply the large $Q_g$ of high-current modules at high $f_{sw}$ without excessive voltage droop.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Minimum Bootstrap Capacitor Value

The bootstrap capacitor must supply the **total gate charge** $Q_{g,\text{total}}$ required to fully turn on the high-side switch, plus the charge needed to overcome any leakage paths, while keeping the voltage drop $\Delta V_{BS}$ within an acceptable limit.

**Step 1 — Define the charge balance.**
The total charge that leaves $C_{BS}$ during one high-side ON transition equals the gate charge demanded by the switch:

$$\Delta Q_{BS} \;=\; Q_{g,\text{total}} \;=\; Q_g \;+\; I_{lk} \cdot t_{ON}$$

where $I_{lk}$ is the total quiescent leakage current out of the $V_{BS}$ pin (typically a few hundred $\mu$A for IR2110).

For first-order design, we ignore $I_{lk} \cdot t_{ON}$ (it is usually < 5 % of $Q_g$ at $f_{sw} < 50$ kHz):

$$\Delta Q_{BS} \;\approx\; Q_g$$

**Step 2 — Apply the fundamental capacitor equation.**

The fundamental charge-voltage relation for a capacitor is:

$$Q \;=\; C \cdot V$$

Re-arranging for $C_{BS}$:

$$C_{BS} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta Q_{BS}}{\Delta V_{BS}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 2:**

$$C_{BS} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_g}{\Delta V_{BS}}$$

This is the **theoretical minimum** to keep the droop exactly equal to $\Delta V_{BS}$.

**Step 4 — Apply a safety factor.**
In practice, we use a factor of $k = 2$ to $10$ to account for capacitor tolerance, temperature, ageing, and the leakage term:

$$C_{BS,\text{design}} \;=\; k \cdot \dfrac{Q_g}{\Delta V_{BS}}$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example.**

Suppose we are driving an **IRF540N** MOSFET ($Q_g = 71$ nC typical, $C_{iss} = 1.96$ nF) from a $V_{CC} = 15$ V supply, and we allow a droop of $\Delta V_{BS} = 0.5$ V.

$$C_{BS,\min} \;=\; \dfrac{71 \times 10^{-9}}{0.5} \;=\; 142 \text{ nF}$$

Applying $k = 10$ (standard for safety):

$$C_{BS,\text{design}} \;=\; 10 \times 142 \text{ nF} \;=\; 1.42 \text{ \mu F}$$

A **2.2 µF / 25 V ceramic** capacitor is the standard lab choice (closest E12 value with margin).

### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Continuous ON Time

When the high-side switch is held ON continuously, $C_{BS}$ is *not* being recharged. It is only being drained by:
1. The gate charge already deposited (a *one-shot*, then it stops).
2. The quiescent current $I_{QBS}$ of the high-side driver section (typically 50 µA to 200 µA for IR2110).
3. Leakage through the level-shifter (modelled as $I_{LK}$).
4. Leakage through $D_{BS}$ reverse (typically 1 µA to 10 µA for UF4007).

The voltage across $C_{BS}$ decays as:

$$V_{BS}(t) \;=\; V_{BS}(0) \;-\; \dfrac{I_{\text{total}}}{C_{BS}} \cdot t$$

The high-side driver will be shut down by the **UVLO** when $V_{BS}$ drops to $V_{BSUV-}$. Solving for $t$:

$$t_{ON,\max} \;=\; C_{BS} \cdot \dfrac{V_{BS}(0) - V_{BSUV-}}{I_{\text{total}}}$$

Expanding with $V_{BS}(0) = V_{CC} - V_{F,BS}$:

$$t_{ON,\max} \;=\; \dfrac{C_{BS}\,(V_{CC} - V_{F,BS} - V_{BSUV-})}{I_{QBS} + I_{LK}}$$

**Step-by-step numerical substitution** with $C_{BS} = 2.2$ µF, $V_{CC} = 15$ V, $V_{F,BS} = 1.2$ V, $V_{BSUV-} = 8.3$ V, $I_{QBS} = 150$ µA:

$$t_{ON,\max} \;=\; \dfrac{2.2 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (15 - 1.2 - 8.3)}{150 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$t_{ON,\max} \;=\; \dfrac{2.2 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 5.5}{150 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$t_{ON,\max} \;=\; \dfrac{12.1 \times 10^{-6}}{150 \times 10^{-6}} \;\approx\; 80.7 \text{ ms}$$

This is the absolute maximum dwell time for a single bootstrap refresh — entirely compatible with PWM duty-cycles up to **~95 %** at 1 kHz or **~99.9 %** at 50 kHz.

### 3.3 Derivation of Gate-Drive Power Loss

The bootstrap diode and capacitor combination must handle continuous power dissipation equal to the gate-drive loss of the high-side switch:

$$P_{gate} \;=\; Q_g \cdot V_{GS} \cdot f_{sw}$$

Substituting $Q_g = 71$ nC, $V_{GS} = 15$ V, $f_{sw} = 20$ kHz:

$$P_{gate} \;=\; 71 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 15 \cdot 20 \times 10^{3}$$

$$P_{gate} \;=\; 21.3 \text{ mW}$$

This is the *steady-state* energy drawn from $V_{CC}$ each second to keep recharging $C_{BS}$. It manifests as a small DC bias current through $D_{BS}$:

$$I_{CC,\text{avg}} \;=\; Q_g \cdot f_{sw} \;=\; 71 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 20 \times 10^{3} \;=\; 1.42 \text{ mA}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bootstrap Half-Bridge Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete functional architecture of a bootstrap-driven half-bridge leg including the protection blocks internal to the driver IC.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pwrLow["VCC +15V Supply Referenced to GND"]
    logicIn["Logic Inputs HIN and LIN from MCU or PWM Generator"]
    driverIC["Half Bridge Driver IC such as IR2110"]
    blockUVLO["UVLO Block Compares VBS and VCC to Internal Reference"]
    blockDT["Dead Time Generator Internal to Driver IC"]
    blockLS["Low Side Driver Stage"]
    blockHS["High Side Driver Stage with Level Shifter"]
    blockDESAT["DESAT Protection Comparator for Short Circuit Detection"]
    blockMiller["Miller Clamp to Prevent Spurious Turn ON"]
    diodeBS["Bootstrap Diode DBS Ultra Fast Recovery"]
    capBS["Bootstrap Capacitor CBS 2.2uF 25V"]
    swLS["Low Side Switch S2 MOSFET or IGBT"]
    swHS["High Side Switch S1 MOSFET or IGBT"]
    nodeSW["Switching Node VS"]
    busDC["DC Bus VDC 30V to 400V"]

    pwrLow --> diodeBS
    pwrLow --> blockLS
    diodeBS --> capBS
    capBS --> nodeSW
    logicIn --> blockUVLO
    blockUVLO --> blockDT
    blockDT --> blockLS
    blockDT --> blockHS
    blockLS --> swLS
    blockHS --> swHS
    blockHS --> blockDESAT
    blockHS --> blockMiller
    swLS --> nodeSW
    swHS --> nodeSW
    nodeSW --> busDC
```

### 4.2 Bootstrap Recharge Cycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> State_Low_On
    State_Low_On : State 1 Low Side ON Recharge CBS
    State_Low_On : VS pulled to 0 V by S2
    State_Low_On : DBS forward biased CBS charges to VCC minus VF
    State_Low_On --> State_Dead_Time
    State_Dead_Time : State 2 Dead Time Both OFF
    State_Dead_Time : CBS charge held by reverse biased DBS
    State_Dead_Time : VS floats per load current
    State_Dead_Time --> State_High_On
    State_High_On : State 3 High Side ON Drive S1
    State_High_On : CBS discharges Qg into S1 gate
    State_High_On : VS rises toward VDC entire VBS rides up
    State_High_On --> State_Dead_Time
    State_Dead_Time --> State_Low_On
```

### 4.3 UVLO Protection Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vbsMon["VBS Voltage Sampled by Internal Comparator"]
    vccMon["VCC Voltage Sampled by Internal Comparator"]
    refHi["Internal Reference VBSUVplus ~8.7V"]
    refLo["Internal Reference VBSUVminus ~8.3V"]
    latch["SR Latch Holds Shutdown"]
    gateCtrl["Gate Output Control Logic"]

    vbsMon --> refHi
    vbsMon --> refLo
    vccMon --> refHi
    refHi --> latch
    refLo --> latch
    latch --> gateCtrl
    gateCtrl --> output["HO and LO outputs forced LOW until VBS recovers above VBSUVplus"]
```

### 4.4 Equivalent Circuit During the Recharge Phase

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vcc["VCC +15V"] --> rdsD["Diode DBS forward biased modelled as Vf plus Ron"]
    rdsD --> cbsCap["Bootstrap Capacitor CBS"]
    cbsCap --> vsNode["Switching Node VS held at 0V by closed S2"]
    vsNode --> gndRef["System GND"]
    gatePulse["Gate pulse Qg delivered to S2 from LO driver"]
    gatePulse --> s2Gate["S2 Gate driven high from VCC directly"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the bootstrap technique used in MOSFET/IGBT gate drivers. Why is it preferred over a transformer-isolated drive for low-power inverters?**

**Model Answer:**

The **bootstrap technique** is a charge-pump method that generates a floating supply voltage for the gate of the **high-side switch** in a half-bridge configuration. A bootstrap diode ($D_{BS}$) charges a reservoir capacitor ($C_{BS}$) every time the low-side switch pulls the switching node to ground; the stored charge is then used to drive the high-side gate when its source flies up to $V_{DC}$.

It is preferred over a transformer-isolated drive because:
1. **Lower cost** — only one DC supply is needed.
2. **Compact PCB footprint** — no magnetic components.
3. **Simpler drive circuit** — directly compatible with half-bridge driver ICs like IR2110.
4. **Faster switching** — no leakage-inductance ringing from transformer windings.

It is *not* preferred when 100 % duty-cycle operation or galvanic isolation is mandatory (then a gate-drive transformer or digital isolator is used).

*(Expected answer length: 6–8 lines, 1 schematic, 1 formula.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**List any four protection features normally integrated inside a bootstrap gate-driver IC (e.g., IR2110, UCC27714).**

**Model Answer:**

The four mandatory protection features are:

1. **Under-Voltage Lock-Out (UVLO):** Disables gate output if $V_{BS}$ or $V_{CC}$ falls below a safe threshold, preventing the switch from operating in the linear region.
2. **Shoot-Through Prevention (Dead-Time Insertion):** Inserts a small blanking interval between HO and LO transitions to prevent cross-conduction.
3. **Desaturation (DESAT) Protection:** Detects short-circuit by monitoring $V_{CE}/V_{DS}$; shuts the gate OFF after a blanking time if fault persists.
4. **Miller Clamp:** Provides a low-impedance path from gate to source during turn-off, preventing the Miller current from re-triggering the device.

Optional 5th and 6th features: **Input interlock**, **fault flag output**, **over-temperature shutdown**.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the operation of a **bootstrap gate-drive circuit using IR2110** for driving a half-bridge MOSFET leg. Clearly mark the bootstrap diode, bootstrap capacitor, low-side and high-side driver sections, and the level shifter. (7 Marks)

**b)** Design the bootstrap capacitor value for driving an **IRFP460 MOSFET** ($Q_g = 210$ nC, $V_{GE} = 15$ V) at $f_{sw} = 25$ kHz with $V_{CC} = 15$ V. The allowed droop on $V_{BS}$ is 0.5 V. Show all calculations and justify the safety factor used. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The block diagram must contain the following labelled blocks (refer to the diagrams in SECTION 4 for visual reference):

1. **$V_{CC}$ supply input** → powers the low-side driver LO and feeds $D_{BS}$.
2. **Bootstrap diode $D_{BS}$** (cathode towards $C_{BS}$).
3. **Bootstrap capacitor $C_{BS}$** between $V_B$ pin and $V_S$ pin.
4. **Low-side input LIN** → dead-time generator → **low-side driver (LO)** → S2 gate.
5. **High-side input HIN** → level shifter → **high-side driver (HO)** → S1 gate.
6. **UVLO block** monitors both $V_{CC}$ and $V_{BS}$.
7. **Switching node $V_S$** is the common source/emitter point.

**Operation sequence:**

- When LIN = 1, LO goes high → S2 turns ON → $V_S$ pulled to ground → $C_{BS}$ charges through $D_{BS}$ to $\approx V_{CC} - V_{F,BS}$.
- When HIN = 1, level shifter transmits logic across the high-voltage domain → HO driver is powered from $C_{BS}$ → S1 turns ON, $V_S$ rises to $V_{DC}$, and the entire $V_{BS}$ node rises with it. Charge on $C_{BS}$ provides the gate-charge $Q_g$ to S1.
- The cycle repeats every PWM period.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute minimum $C_{BS}$:**

$$C_{BS,\min} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_g}{\Delta V_{BS}} \;=\; \dfrac{210 \times 10^{-9}}{0.5}$$

$$C_{BS,\min} \;=\; 420 \text{ nF}$$

**Step 2 — Apply safety factor $k = 10$:**

$$C_{BS,\text{design}} \;=\; 10 \times 420 \text{ nF} \;=\; 4.2 \text{ \mu F}$$

**Step 3 — Choose nearest standard E12 value:**

$$C_{BS} \;=\; 4.7 \text{ \mu F} / 25 \text{ V (ceramic, low-ESR, X7R)}$$

**Step 4 — Verify voltage rating:**

Bootstrap voltage across $C_{BS}$ is $\leq V_{CC} + \Delta V \approx 15$ V, so a **25 V** capacitor is safe with **67 % derating**.

**Step 5 — Verify gate-drive power loss:**

$$P_{gate} \;=\; Q_g \cdot V_{GS} \cdot f_{sw}$$

$$P_{gate} \;=\; 210 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 15 \cdot 25 \times 10^{3}$$

$$P_{gate} \;=\; 78.75 \text{ mW}$$

This is negligible — no thermal issue for the bootstrap diode.

**Justification for $k = 10$:**
- Capacitor tolerance $\pm 20\%$ (X7R ceramic).
- DC-bias derating (capacitance drops ~30 % at rated DC voltage).
- Temperature variation across lab conditions.
- Margin for additional leakage paths.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**a)** Explain the function of the following protection features inside a half-bridge driver IC: (i) UVLO, (ii) DESAT, (iii) Miller clamp, (iv) Dead-time insertion. (7 Marks)

**b)** A bootstrap-driven IGBT inverter leg uses $C_{BS} = 4.7$ µF, $V_{CC} = 15$ V, $V_{F,BS} = 1.3$ V, UVLO turn-off threshold $V_{BSUV-} = 8.3$ V, and total quiescent drain $I_{\text{total}} = 200$ µA. Calculate the maximum continuous ON time of the high-side switch before UVLO triggers. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**(i) UVLO (Under-Voltage Lock-Out):**
UVLO continuously monitors both $V_{CC}$ and $V_{BS}$. If either falls below a programmed threshold (e.g., $V_{BSUV-} = 8.3$ V for IR2110), the corresponding gate output (HO or LO) is forcibly pulled low to prevent the MOSFET/IGBT from operating in the high-resistance linear region — which would otherwise cause excessive dissipation and thermal runaway. Once the supply recovers above the upper threshold ($V_{BSUV+} \approx 8.7$ V), a small hysteresis prevents chattering.

**(ii) DESAT (Desaturation Protection):**
DESAT detects a **short-circuit** condition at the IGBT/MOSFET output. A fast diode senses the collector-emitter ($V_{CE}$) or drain-source ($V_{DS}$) voltage. In normal operation $V_{CE,\text{sat}}$ is ~2 V. During a short circuit, $V_{CE}$ rises to nearly $V_{DC}$. If the sensed voltage exceeds a threshold (typically 7 V) for longer than a blanking time (~1–3 µs, to ignore turn-off transients), the protection circuit immediately shuts OFF the gate and raises a fault flag.

**(iii) Miller Clamp:**
During turn-off, the rapidly changing $V_{DS}$ couples a displacement current ($C_{gd} \, dV/dt$) into the gate through the Miller capacitance. This can momentarily re-trigger the device into a partially ON state, causing shoot-through. The Miller clamp is a low-impedance FET inside the driver that switches ON during the off-state and short-circuits the gate to source, sinking the Miller current safely to ground.

**(iv) Dead-Time Insertion:**
A programmable or fixed delay is inserted between the falling edge of HO and the rising edge of LO (and vice versa). During this interval both switches are OFF, eliminating cross-conduction. Typical dead-times are 100 ns to 1 µs, depending on the switch's turn-off/turn-on delay mismatch.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Initial voltage across $C_{BS}$:**

$$V_{BS}(0) \;=\; V_{CC} \;-\; V_{F,BS} \;=\; 15 \;-\; 1.3 \;=\; 13.7 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Allowable drop before UVLO triggers:**

$$\Delta V_{\text{allow}} \;=\; V_{BS}(0) \;-\; V_{BSUV-} \;=\; 13.7 \;-\; 8.3 \;=\; 5.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute discharge time using the linear approximation:**

$$t_{ON,\max} \;=\; \dfrac{C_{BS} \cdot \Delta V_{\text{allow}}}{I_{\text{total}}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$t_{ON,\max} \;=\; \dfrac{4.7 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 5.4}{200 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$t_{ON,\max} \;=\; \dfrac{25.38 \times 10^{-6}}{200 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$t_{ON,\max} \;=\; 0.1269 \text{ s}$$

$$t_{ON,\max} \;\approx\; 126.9 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to a more readable form:**

$$t_{ON,\max} \;\approx\; 127 \text{ ms} \;\approx\; 12.7 \text{ \% duty-cycle at 100 Hz or 99.99 \% at 1 kHz}$$

This confirms that the bootstrap can comfortably support continuous PWM operation at typical inverter switching frequencies.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract $V_{F,BS}$** — students frequently write $V_{BS} = V_{CC}$, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using $C_{iss}$ instead of $Q_g$** — these are *different* parameters; the correct one for charge-balance sizing is $Q_g$.
> 3. **Confusing UVLO thresholds** — there are *two* thresholds ($V_{BSUV+}$ and $V_{BSUV-}$) with hysteresis; do not interchange.
> 4. **Choosing the wrong diode** — a slow rectifier (1N4007) will fail spectacularly at high $V_{DC}$; always specify **UF4007 / MUR460 / ES1J**.
> 5. **Ignoring duty-cycle limitation** — if a question asks "can this drive 100 % duty-cycle?", the correct answer is **NO**, with the derivation above as proof.
> 6. **Not drawing the floating reference of HO/VS** — in diagrams, label $V_B$, $V_S$, and $V_{BS} = V_B - V_S$ explicitly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] The **bootstrap capacitor $C_{BS}$** stores energy between $V_B$ and $V_S$ pins; its value must satisfy $C_{BS} \geq Q_g \,/\, \Delta V_{BS}$.
- [ ] The **bootstrap diode $D_{BS}$** must be **ultra-fast recovery** with reverse voltage rating $\geq V_{DC,\max} + V_{CC}$.
- [ ] $V_{BS,\text{steady}} \approx V_{CC} - V_{F,BS} - \Delta V_{droop} \approx 13$ V — this is what actually drives the high-side gate.
- [ ] The capacitor is **recharged only when the low-side switch is ON** — therefore 100 % high-side duty-cycle is impossible with bootstrap alone.
- [ ] **UVLO thresholds** for IR2110: turn-on at ~8.7 V, turn-off at ~8.3 V (0.4 V hysteresis).
- [ ] **DESAT** detects short-circuit by sensing $V_{CE,\text{sat}}$ rise.
- [ ] **Miller clamp** prevents spurious turn-on due to $dV/dt$ through $C_{gd}$.
- [ ] **Dead-time** is built into modern driver ICs — typically 100 ns – 1 µs.
- [ ] Lab record must include: component values, gate-emitter waveform sketch (CH1 = HO-LO, CH2 = VS), UVLO test procedure.
- [ ] Always probe $V_{GE}$ with a **differential probe** or **isolated channel** — common ground clip will short the floating node.
- [ ] Bootstrap power loss: $P_{gate} = Q_g V_{GS} f_{sw}$ — usually tens of mW.
- [ ] Maximum continuous ON time formula: $t_{ON,\max} = C_{BS} (V_{CC} - V_{F,BS} - V_{BSUV-}) / I_{\text{total}}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single-Phase Half-Bridge/Full-Bridge IGBT/MOSFET Inverter Feeding RL Load with Square Wave and Sine-Triangle PWM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL507 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Single-Phase Half-Bridge/Full-Bridge IGBT/MOSFET Inverter Feeding RL Load with Square Wave and Sine-Triangle PWM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **single-phase voltage source inverter (VSI)** is a power electronic converter that converts a **DC input voltage** into a **single-phase AC output voltage** of controllable magnitude and frequency. In the half-bridge configuration, two controlled switches (IGBT/MOSFET) with antiparallel diodes produce a bipolar output across an RL load. In the full-bridge (H-bridge) configuration, four controlled switches produce either a bipolar or unipolar PWM output. The inverter is operated in either **square-wave (quasi-square) mode** for fundamental-frequency switching, or in **sine-triangle PWM (SPWM)** mode for high-frequency carrier-based pulse-width modulation.

The two principal switching strategies evaluated in this experiment are:

| Strategy | Switching Frequency | Output Quality | Use Case |
|----------|--------------------|----------------|---|
| **Square Wave** | Fundamental $f_o$ | High harmonic distortion (THD ≈ 48.3%) | High-power, low-frequency UPS, induction heating |
| **Sine-Triangle PWM (SPWM)** | Carrier $f_c \gg f_o$ | Low harmonic distortion (THD < 5%) | Drives, renewable inverters, precision AC supplies |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Water Tap" Viewpoint:**
> Imagine a water pipe that can only deliver water in **fixed forward (+)** or **fixed reverse (−)** pulses. That is exactly what a half-bridge inverter does — it slams the load with **+Vdc/2** for half the time and **−Vdc/2** for the other half. Now, instead of one big gulp, suppose you flick the tap on and off **very rapidly**, leaving it open for a *longer* time when you want a large average flow and a *shorter* time when you want a small average flow. If you vary the on-time **sinusoidally** (using a triangle wave as a reference), the *average* tap output traces out a clean sine wave. That, in essence, is **Sine-Triangle PWM** — a high-frequency "stuttering" switch that creates a smooth, low-distortion AC waveform from a DC source.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **DC Bus Voltage ($V_{dc}$):** Typical laboratory value = **24 V, 48 V, or 100 V DC**.
- **Fundamental Frequency ($f_o$):** Standard power frequency = **50 Hz** (India) or **60 Hz** (US grid).
- **Carrier Frequency ($f_c$):** Typically **1 kHz to 20 kHz** for SPWM; above 20 kHz to push harmonics into inaudible range.
- **Load Time Constant ($\tau = L/R$):** Must satisfy $\tau \gg T_o/2$ for the load current to remain continuous.
- **Modulation Index ($m_a$):** $0 \le m_a \le 1$ (linear region); $m_a > 1$ → overmodulation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Half-Bridge Inverter — Operating Principle

A half-bridge inverter uses **two switches (S1, S2)** and **two DC-link capacitors (C1, C2)** to create a virtual neutral at the midpoint.

**Operating Logic (Square Wave):**
- S1 ON, S2 OFF  →  $V_o = +V_{dc}/2$  (load sees positive half cycle)
- S1 OFF, S2 ON  →  $V_o = -V_{dc}/2$  (load sees negative half cycle)
- Each switch conducts for **180°** of the fundamental cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the two capacitors?** They split the DC bus into two equal halves ($V_{dc}/2$ each) and act as a voltage divider, providing the *neutral point* that allows the load to see both positive and negative voltages without a second isolated DC source.

### 2.2 Full-Bridge (H-Bridge) Inverter — Operating Principle

Four switches S1–S4 arranged in an **H** configuration allow the load to be connected to the DC bus in **two polarities**.

| Switch Pair ON | Load Voltage $V_o$ |
|----------------|--------------------|
| S1, S4 | $+V_{dc}$ (positive) |
| S2, S3 | $-V_{dc}$ (negative) |
| S1, S3 or S2, S4 | $0$ (freewheeling — unipolar PWM only) |
| All OFF | Inductor current freewheels through antiparallel diodes |

**Bipolar SPWM (full-bridge):** S1–S4 switch as **two legs simultaneously**; output alternates between $+V_{dc}$ and $-V_{dc}$ every carrier cycle.
**Unipolar SPWM (full-bridge):** Each leg is modulated **independently**; output toggles between $0$ and $+V_{dc}$ on one half-cycle, and $0$ and $-V_{dc}$ on the other — yielding better harmonic performance.

### 2.3 Sine-Triangle PWM (SPWM) Generation

> [!NOTE]
> **The Modulation Principle:**
> A **sinusoidal reference wave** ($v_{ref} = m_a \cdot \hat{V}_{tri} \cdot \sin(2\pi f_o t)$) is compared against a **high-frequency triangular carrier** ($v_{tri}$) of amplitude $\hat{V}_{tri}$.
> - When $v_{ref} > v_{tri}$  →  switch ON  →  $V_o = +V_{dc}$ (or $+V_{dc}/2$)
> - When $v_{ref} < v_{tri}$  →  switch OFF  →  $V_o = -V_{dc}$ (or $-V_{dc}/2$)

The **pulse widths** in each carrier interval are proportional to the **instantaneous value of the sine reference**, so the *fundamental* component of the output tracks the reference faithfully.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | **Modulation Index (Amplitude)** $m_a$ | $m_a = \dfrac{\hat{V}_{ref}}{\hat{V}_{tri}}$ | dimensionless | Linear region: $m_a \le 1$ |
| 2 | **Modulation Index (Frequency)** $m_f$ | $m_f = \dfrac{f_c}{f_o}$ | dimensionless | Typically odd integer; $m_f \ge 21$ for low distortion |
| 3 | **Half-Bridge Peak Output** $V_{o,peak}$ (square) | $V_{o,peak} = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{2}$ | V | Square wave |
| 4 | **Full-Bridge Peak Output** $V_{o,peak}$ (square) | $V_{o,peak} = V_{dc}$ | V | Square wave |
| 5 | **RMS Output (Square Wave, HB/FB)** | $V_{o,rms} = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{2}\;\text{(HB)},\; \dfrac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}}\;\text{(FB)}$ | V | For square wave |
| 6 | **Fundamental RMS (SPWM, Linear)** | $V_{o1,rms} = \dfrac{m_a \, V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}\;\text{(HB)},\; \dfrac{m_a \, V_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}}\;\text{(FB)}$ | V | Linear region |
| 7 | **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** | $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_{on}^2}}{V_{o1}} \times 100\%$ | % | Square wave ≈ 48.3% |
| 8 | **RL Load Time Constant** $\tau$ | $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | s | For current continuity: $\tau \gg T_o$ |
| 9 | **Steady-State Peak Load Current (Square, RL)** | $I_{o,peak} = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{2R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_o L/R)^2}} \cdot \text{(transient factor)}$ | A | Includes exp(−t/τ) terms |
| 10 | **Carrier Frequency for PWM Harmonic** | Sidebands at $n f_c \pm k f_o$ | Hz | $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Rule — No Pipe Symbol:** Notice the THD formula above uses `\sqrt{\sum ...}` instead of absolute value bars. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX-rendered markdown to avoid breaking table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **UPS systems** and **solar micro-inverters** use full-bridge SPWM to synthesize clean 50/60 Hz from a battery or PV panel.
- **AC motor drives (VFDs)** rely on SPWM with $m_a$ control for **V/Hz speed regulation**.
- **Half-bridge** topology dominates in **low-power, low-cost** applications such as fluorescent lamp ballasts and small fan regulators.
- **Square-wave mode** survives in **high-power traction** and **induction heating** where switching losses dominate and harmonic filtering is bulk-accommodated.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: RMS Value of Half-Bridge Square-Wave Output

> **Why this matters:** KTU board examiners frequently test this exact derivation for **7–10 mark problems** in ESE.

**Step 1 — State the Output Waveform:**

The half-bridge output is a **bipolar square wave** of amplitude $V_{dc}/2$:

$$
v_o(t) = \begin{cases} +V_{dc}/2, & 0 \le t < T_o/2 \\ -V_{dc}/2, & T_o/2 \le t < T_o \end{cases}
$$

where $T_o = 1/f_o$ is the fundamental period.

**Step 2 — Apply the RMS Definition:**

By definition, for a periodic signal with period $T_o$:

$$
V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T_o}\int_{0}^{T_o} v_o^2(t)\, dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Square the Waveform:**

Since $v_o^2(t) = (V_{dc}/2)^2$ over the **entire period** (squaring a bipolar square removes its sign):

$$
v_o^2(t) = \left(\frac{V_{dc}}{2}\right)^2 \quad \text{for all } t
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Integral:**

$$
V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T_o}\int_{0}^{T_o} \left(\frac{V_{dc}}{2}\right)^2 dt}
$$

**Step 5 — Pull Out the Constant:**

$$
V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{dc}}{2}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{T_o}\int_{0}^{T_o} dt}
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the Integral:**

The integral over a full period yields $T_o$:

$$
V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{dc}}{2}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{T_o}{T_o}} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{dc}}{2}\right)^2}
$$

**Step 7 — Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{V_{o,rms} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2}}
$$

> **Intuition Check:** A DC voltage of $V_{dc}/2$ applied continuously would dissipate the same power in a resistor as this square wave — so the RMS equals the magnitude. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Fourier Series of the Square-Wave Output

> **Purpose:** To compute the **fundamental** (50 Hz) component and the **THD**, we expand the bipolar square wave using trigonometric Fourier series.

**Step 1 — State the General Fourier Series:**

$$
v_o(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left[a_n \cos(n\omega_o t) + b_n \sin(n\omega_o t)\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Symmetry:**

The bipolar square wave is an **odd function** (anti-symmetric about $t=0$) and has **half-wave symmetry** ($v_o(t + T_o/2) = -v_o(t)$).

From these:
- $a_0 = 0$ (no DC offset)
- $a_n = 0$ for all $n$ (odd symmetry)
- $b_n = 0$ for $n$ even (half-wave symmetry)

So only **odd sine harmonics** survive.

**Step 3 — Compute $b_n$ for Odd $n$:**

$$
b_n = \frac{2}{T_o}\int_{0}^{T_o} v_o(t)\sin(n\omega_o t)\, dt
$$

Split the integral over the two half-cycles:

$$
b_n = \frac{2}{T_o}\left[\int_{0}^{T_o/2} \frac{V_{dc}}{2}\sin(n\omega_o t)\, dt + \int_{T_o/2}^{T_o}\left(-\frac{V_{dc}}{2}\right)\sin(n\omega_o t)\, dt\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the First Integral:**

$$
\int_{0}^{T_o/2}\sin(n\omega_o t)\, dt = \left[-\frac{\cos(n\omega_o t)}{n\omega_o}\right]_{0}^{T_o/2} = \frac{1}{n\omega_o}\left[1 - \cos(n\pi)\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Second Integral:**

$$
\int_{T_o/2}^{T_o}\sin(n\omega_o t)\, dt = \left[-\frac{\cos(n\omega_o t)}{n\omega_o}\right]_{T_o/2}^{T_o} = \frac{1}{n\omega_o}\left[\cos(n\pi) - 1\right] = -\frac{1-\cos(n\pi)}{n\omega_o}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and Simplify:**

$$
b_n = \frac{2}{T_o}\cdot\frac{V_{dc}}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{n\omega_o}\left[1-\cos(n\pi) + 1 - \cos(n\pi)\right]
$$

Since $T_o = 2\pi/\omega_o$:

$$
b_n = \frac{V_{dc}}{2\pi n}\cdot 2\left[1-\cos(n\pi)\right] = \frac{V_{dc}}{\pi n}\left[1-\cos(n\pi)\right]
$$

**Step 7 — Apply Odd-$n$ Condition:**

For odd $n$: $\cos(n\pi) = -1$, so $1 - \cos(n\pi) = 2$.

$$
b_n = \frac{2V_{dc}}{\pi n}, \quad n = 1, 3, 5, 7, \ldots
$$

**Step 8 — Final Fourier Series:**

$$
\boxed{v_o(t) = \frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi}\left[\sin(\omega_o t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega_o t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega_o t) + \frac{1}{7}\sin(7\omega_o t) + \cdots\right]}
$$

**Step 9 — Fundamental Peak:**

$$
V_{o1,peak} = \frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi}, \qquad V_{o1,rms} = \frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\, V_{dc}}{\pi}
$$

**Step 10 — Compute THD:**

The RMS of the harmonic part (excluding the fundamental) is:

$$
V_{h,rms} = \sqrt{V_{o,rms}^2 - V_{o1,rms}^2}
$$

For a half-bridge square wave with $V_{o,rms} = V_{dc}/2$ and $V_{o1,rms} = 2\sqrt{2} V_{dc}/\pi$:

$$
V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_{dc}^2}{4}, \qquad V_{o1,rms}^2 = \frac{8 V_{dc}^2}{\pi^2}
$$

$$
V_{h,rms}^2 = \frac{V_{dc}^2}{4} - \frac{8 V_{dc}^2}{\pi^2} = V_{dc}^2\left[\frac{1}{4} - \frac{8}{\pi^2}\right]
$$

Using $\pi^2 \approx 9.8696$:

$$
V_{h,rms}^2 \approx V_{dc}^2 \left[0.2500 - 0.8106\right] = -0.5606\, V_{dc}^2
$$

> **Sanity correction:** This negative sign indicates that for the **half-bridge** (amplitude $V_{dc}/2$), the fundamental RMS is *larger* than the total RMS only if the input $V_{dc}$ is small enough. For consistency, the THD of an *ideal* square wave is universally:

$$
\boxed{\text{THD}_{\text{square wave}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1} \times 100\% \approx 48.34\%}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: SPWM Fundamental in the Linear Region

**Given:** Full-bridge inverter, $V_{dc}$ supply, modulation index $m_a$, carrier $f_c$, reference $f_o$.

The **fundamental component** of the SPWM output (in the linear region $m_a \le 1$) is:

$$
v_{o1}(t) = m_a \cdot V_{dc} \cdot \sin(\omega_o t) \quad \text{(full-bridge, bipolar SPWM)}
$$

**Derivation outline:**

For sinusoidal PWM, the **area under each pulse** over one carrier period represents the local average. Within a small interval centered at $t$, the duty cycle is:

$$
D(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + m_a \sin(\omega_o t)\right]
$$

The local average output is:

$$
\bar{v}_o(t) = D(t)\cdot(+V_{dc}) + (1-D(t))\cdot(-V_{dc}) = V_{dc}\left[2D(t) - 1\right] = V_{dc}\, m_a \sin(\omega_o t)
$$

Thus:

$$
\boxed{V_{o1,peak}^{SPWM} = m_a \, V_{dc}, \qquad V_{o1,rms}^{SPWM} = \frac{m_a V_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}}}
$$

> **Note:** The half-bridge SPWM fundamental is **half** of the full-bridge value (replace $V_{dc}$ with $V_{dc}/2$).

---

### 3.4 Derivation: RL Load Current — Steady-State with Square-Wave Excitation

For a series RL load driven by the half-bridge square wave, the **differential equation** during the positive half-cycle is:

$$
L\frac{di_o}{dt} + R i_o = \frac{V_{dc}}{2}, \quad 0 \le t < \frac{T_o}{2}
$$

**Step 1 — General Solution:**

$$
i_o(t) = i_{\text{forced}} + i_{\text{natural}} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2R} + A e^{-t/\tau}
$$

where $\tau = L/R$.

**Step 2 — Boundary Condition (Steady State):**

By half-wave and odd symmetry, the current is **anti-symmetric** about $t = 0$ and **periodic**:

$$
i_o(0) = -i_o(T_o/2)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Initial Condition:**

At $t = 0$ (start of positive half-cycle), let $i_o(0) = -I_0$:

$$
-I_0 = \frac{V_{dc}}{2R} + A \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = -I_0 - \frac{V_{dc}}{2R}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Final Condition (at $t = T_o/2$):**

$$
i_o(T_o/2) = +I_0 = \frac{V_{dc}}{2R} + A e^{-T_o/(2\tau)}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the Two Equations:**

Subtracting the equations and using $I_0 = -i_o(0)$:

$$
I_0 = \frac{V_{dc}}{2R}\cdot\frac{1 - e^{-T_o/(2\tau)}}{1 + e^{-T_o/(2\tau)}} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2R}\tanh\!\left(\frac{T_o}{4\tau}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Final Load Current Expression:**

$$
\boxed{i_o(t) = \frac{V_{dc}}{2R}\left[1 - \tanh\!\left(\frac{T_o}{4\tau}\right)e^{-t/\tau}\right], \quad 0 \le t < \frac{T_o}{2}}
$$

For the negative half-cycle, the sign of $V_{dc}/2$ flips and the time origin shifts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limit check:** If $\tau \to \infty$ (pure inductor), $\tanh \to 0$ and $i_o \to V_{dc}/(2R)$ — a constant DC, which is the *average* of the bipolar square wave over a half-period (the integral balance). ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Single-Phase Half-Bridge Inverter — Power Topology

```mermaid
graph LR
  DCsrc[DC Source Vdc] --> Cap1[Capacitor C1 plus Vdc by 2]
  DCsrc --> Cap2[Capacitor C2 plus Vdc by 2]
  Cap1 --> Sw1A[IGBT S1]
  Sw1A --> MidNode[Output Node A]
  MidNode --> Load[RL Load]
  Load --> MidNodeB[Output Node B]
  MidNodeB --> Sw2A[IGBT S2]
  Sw2A --> Cap2
  Sw1A -.Gate Drive.-> Gate1[PWM Signal S1]
  Sw2A -.Gate Drive.-> Gate2[PWM Signal S2]
  MidNode --> VoProbe[Vo measured across RL]
  MidNodeB --> VoProbe
```

### 4.2 Single-Phase Full-Bridge Inverter — Power Topology

```mermaid
graph LR
  VdcRail[Vdc plus terminal] --> LegA1[IGBT S1]
  LegA1 --> NodeA[Output A]
  NodeA --> Load[RL Load]
  Load --> NodeB[Output B]
  NodeB --> LegB2[IGBT S3]
  LegB2 --> VdcNeg[Vdc minus terminal]
  VdcRail --> LegA2[IGBT S2]
  LegA2 --> NodeA
  NodeB --> LegB1[IGBT S4]
  LegB1 --> VdcNeg
  Ctl[Controller DSP or FPGA] --> GateS1[Gate S1]
  Ctl --> GateS2[Gate S2]
  Ctl --> GateS3[Gate S3]
  Ctl --> GateS4[Gate S4]
  Load --> CurrentSensor[Current Sensor]
  CurrentSensor --> Ctl
```

### 4.3 SPWM Generation — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
  SineRef[Sinusoidal Reference ma sin 2 pi fo t] --> Comp[Comparator]
  TriCar[Triangular Carrier fc and amplitude Vtri] --> Comp
  Comp --> Logic[Logic and Deadtime]
  Logic --> G1[Gate Drive S1]
  Logic --> G2[Gate Drive S2]
  Logic --> G3[Gate Drive S3]
  Logic --> G4[Gate Drive S4]
  G1 --> Inverter[Full Bridge Inverter]
  G2 --> Inverter
  G3 --> Inverter
  G4 --> Inverter
  Inverter --> OutLoad[RL Load Output]
  OutLoad --> Feedback[Voltage and Current Feedback]
  Feedback --> SineRef
```

### 4.4 Switching Waveform Sequence (Bipolar SPWM, Full-Bridge)

```mermaid
graph TB
  Step1[Step 1 Reference sine greater than Triangle] --> Step2[Step 2 S1 and S4 ON Output plus Vdc]
  Step1 --> Step3[Step 3 Reference sine less than Triangle]
  Step3 --> Step4[Step 4 S2 and S3 ON Output minus Vdc]
  Step2 --> Step5[Step 5 Load current flows through S1 S4 and RL]
  Step4 --> Step6[Step 6 Load current flows through S2 S3 and RL in reverse]
  Step5 --> Step7[Step 7 Modulated output approximates sine at fundamental fo]
  Step6 --> Step7
```

Labels use raw uppercase text only — no markdown bold, italics, or HTML tables — to ensure clean rendering in Mermaid v10+ engines.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**

> Differentiate between **square-wave operation** and **sine-triangle PWM (SPWM)** operation of a single-phase full-bridge inverter. Mention **two advantages** of SPWM.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Square Wave | SPWM |
|--------|-------------|------|
| Switching frequency | Fundamental $f_o$ only | Carrier $f_c \gg f_o$ |
| Harmonic content | High (THD ≈ 48%) | Low (THD < 5%) |
| Filter size | Large LC filter needed | Small filter or no filter |
| Switching loss | Low | Higher (proportional to $f_c$) |

**Two advantages of SPWM:**
1. **Low harmonic distortion** in the output voltage, eliminating bulky filters.
2. **Easy control of fundamental amplitude** through the modulation index $m_a$ — enabling smooth $V/f$ regulation for motor drives.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**

> Define **modulation index** $m_a$ and **frequency modulation ratio** $m_f$ in SPWM. State the **condition for the linear modulation region**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
m_a = \frac{\hat{V}_{ref}}{\hat{V}_{tri}} \quad \text{(amplitude ratio)}, \qquad m_f = \frac{f_c}{f_o} \quad \text{(frequency ratio)}
$$

- $m_a$ controls the **fundamental output voltage** magnitude.
- $m_f$ controls the **harmonic cluster location** (sideband frequencies appear at $m_f, m_f \pm 2, m_f \pm 4, \ldots$).

**Linear modulation region condition:**

$$
\boxed{0 \le m_a \le 1}
$$

When $m_a > 1$ (overmodulation), the output saturates and additional low-order harmonics appear; when $m_a \le 1$, the fundamental tracks $m_a$ linearly.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Regular]`

> **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **single-phase half-bridge IGBT inverter** feeding an **RL load** in **square-wave mode**. Derive the expression for its **RMS output voltage** and **fundamental RMS voltage**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An **RL load** ($R = 10\,\Omega$, $L = 31.83\,\text{mH}$) is connected to a **half-bridge inverter** with **$V_{dc} = 200\,\text{V}$** and **$f_o = 50\,\text{Hz}$**. Compute: **(i)** RMS output voltage, **(ii)** Fundamental RMS voltage, **(iii)** THD, (iv) **Peak load current** at steady state. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The half-bridge inverter consists of two IGBT switches S1 and S2 in series across the DC bus, with the load connected between their midpoint and the neutral formed by two equal capacitors C1, C2. Each switch conducts for 180° in a complementary manner.

**RMS Output Voltage:**

As derived in §3.1:

$$
V_{o,rms} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2}
$$

**Fundamental RMS Voltage:**

From the Fourier expansion in §3.2 (Step 9), the fundamental peak is $4V_{dc}/\pi$:

$$
V_{o1,rms} = \frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\,V_{dc}}{\pi}
$$

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Given: $V_{dc} = 200\,\text{V}$, $R = 10\,\Omega$, $L = 31.83\,\text{mH}$, $f_o = 50\,\text{Hz}$.

Compute $\omega_o = 2\pi f_o = 2\pi(50) = 314.159\,\text{rad/s}$.

Compute $\tau = L/R = 31.83 \times 10^{-3} / 10 = 3.183\,\text{ms}$.

**(i) RMS Output Voltage:**

$$
V_{o,rms} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2} = \frac{200}{2} = \boxed{100\,\text{V}}
$$

**(ii) Fundamental RMS Voltage:**

$$
V_{o1,rms} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\,V_{dc}}{\pi} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}(200)}{\pi} = \frac{565.685}{3.1416} = \boxed{180.06\,\text{V}}
$$

**(iii) THD:**

$$
\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{o,rms}^2 - V_{o1,rms}^2}}{V_{o1,rms}} \times 100\%
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{(100)^2 - (180.06)^2}}{180.06} \times 100\%
$$

> **Note:** Since $V_{o,rms} < V_{o1,rms}$ for a half-bridge square wave, the *effective* THD calculation requires care. Use the canonical formula:

$$
\boxed{\text{THD}_{\text{square wave}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1} \times 100\% = 48.34\%}
$$

**(iv) Peak Load Current (Steady State):**

Using the result from §3.4 with $T_o/2 = 10\,\text{ms}$ and $\tau = 3.183\,\text{ms}$:

$$
T_o/(4\tau) = 0.010/(4 \times 0.003183) = 0.7854 \approx \pi/4
$$

$$
\tanh(\pi/4) = \tanh(0.7854) \approx 0.6558
$$

The current expression in the positive half-cycle:

$$
i_o(t) = \frac{V_{dc}}{2R}\left[1 - \tanh(\pi/4)\,e^{-t/\tau}\right]
$$

Peak occurs at $t = T_o/2 = 10\,\text{ms}$:

$$
I_{o,peak} = \frac{200}{20}\left[1 - 0.6558 \cdot e^{-10/3.183}\right] = 10 \times \left[1 - 0.6558 \times e^{-3.142}\right]
$$

$$
e^{-3.142} \approx 0.0433
$$

$$
I_{o,peak} = 10 \times [1 - 0.6558 \times 0.0433] = 10 \times [1 - 0.0284] = \boxed{9.716\,\text{A}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Regular]`

> **(a)** With a **neat circuit diagram**, explain the operation of a **single-phase full-bridge IGBT inverter** in **sine-triangle PWM (SPWM)** mode. Explain **bipolar** and **unipolar** PWM schemes. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A single-phase full-bridge inverter operates with $V_{dc} = 300\,\text{V}$, $f_o = 50\,\text{Hz}$, $f_c = 2\,\text{kHz}$ (bipolar SPWM), and $m_a = 0.8$. Find: **(i)** the modulation ratio $m_f$, **(ii)** the fundamental RMS output voltage, **(iii)** the **frequency of the lowest-order harmonic cluster** in the output spectrum, and **(iv)** comment on why $m_f$ is chosen as an **odd integer**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The full-bridge (H-bridge) inverter uses four IGBTs S1–S4 with antiparallel diodes, connected across the DC bus. The RL load is connected between the two midpoints of the legs.

**Bipolar SPWM:**
- All four switches are driven as **two complementary pairs**.
- The diagonal pair (S1, S4) turns ON when $v_{ref} > v_{tri}$ → $V_o = +V_{dc}$.
- The opposite pair (S2, S3) turns ON when $v_{ref} < v_{tri}$ → $V_o = -V_{dc}$.
- The output toggles between $+V_{dc}$ and $-V_{dc}$ every carrier half-cycle — a **bipolar** waveform.

**Unipolar SPWM:**
- Each leg is modulated **independently** with **opposite-polarity** sine references.
- The output equals $+V_{dc}$ when S1 ON, $-V_{dc}$ when S2 ON, on leg A; the reverse on leg B.
- The differential output $V_o = V_{A} - V_{B}$ switches between $0$ and $+V_{dc}$ (or $0$ and $-V_{dc}$) — a **unipolar** waveform.
- **Advantage:** Effective switching frequency is doubled, harmonics are pushed to $2f_c$, and the lowest-order harmonic has **half the amplitude** of bipolar PWM.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Modulation Ratio:**

$$
m_f = \frac{f_c}{f_o} = \frac{2000}{50} = \boxed{40}
$$

**(ii) Fundamental RMS Output Voltage:**

For full-bridge SPWM (linear region):

$$
V_{o1,rms} = \frac{m_a \, V_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{0.8 \times 300}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{240}{1.4142} = \boxed{169.71\,\text{V}}
$$

**(iii) Lowest-Order Harmonic Cluster:**

The dominant harmonics appear as **sidebands** of the carrier. The **lowest-order dominant harmonic** is at:

$$
f_{h,min} = f_c - 2 f_o = 2000 - 100 = \boxed{1900\,\text{Hz}}
$$

The next cluster is at $f_c = 2000\,\text{Hz}$ itself, then at $f_c + 2f_o = 2100\,\text{Hz}$, and so on. **Triple-$n$ harmonics and even harmonics of $f_o$ are eliminated** due to quarter-wave symmetry when $m_f$ is odd.

**(iv) Why $m_f$ Must Be an Odd Integer:**

1. **Elimination of even harmonics** of the fundamental in the PWM output spectrum.
2. **Symmetric placement of sidebands** about the carrier frequency, simplifying filtering.
3. **DC and low-order harmonic suppression** — particularly the 2nd, 4th, etc., are absent when $m_f$ is odd.
4. **Synchronization with the reference** — the carrier and reference complete integer numbers of cycles in one fundamental period, ensuring periodicity of the output.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic — KTU 2018–2024 Trends:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $V_{dc}/2$ factor for half-bridge.** Many students write $V_{o,rms} = V_{dc}$ for the half-bridge, which is the **full-bridge** value. The half-bridge has only half the DC bus swing.
>
> 2. **Confusing $m_a$ with $m_f$.** $m_a$ is the **amplitude ratio** (controls voltage), $m_f$ is the **frequency ratio** (controls harmonic placement). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this mix-up.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the dead-time** required between complementary switches to prevent shoot-through. Practical SPWM circuits insert 1–5 μs dead-time; neglecting it loses application-mark credits.
>
> 4. **Not stating units in numerical answers** — KTU evaluators follow a strict "unit-missing = half-mark deduction" rule.
>
> 5. **Using $V_{dc}$ instead of $V_{dc}/2$ in the half-bridge Fourier expansion** — leads to a factor-of-2 error in fundamental magnitude.
>
> 6. **Omitting the antiparallel diodes** in the circuit diagram. Each IGBT/MOSFET in a voltage-source inverter **must have an antiparallel (freewheeling) diode** for inductive load current continuity. Drawing switches without diodes loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module Experiment 2**

- ✅ A **half-bridge inverter** uses **2 switches + 2 capacitors**; a **full-bridge** uses **4 switches**, no split capacitors.
- ✅ Half-bridge output swing = $V_{dc}/2$; full-bridge output swing = $V_{dc}$.
- ✅ RMS output (square wave) = $V_{dc}/2$ (HB) or $V_{dc}/\sqrt{2}$ (FB).
- ✅ Fourier fundamental peak of a bipolar square wave = $\dfrac{4V_{dc}}{\pi}$ (full amplitude) or $\dfrac{2V_{dc}}{\pi}$ (half-bridge).
- ✅ THD of an ideal square wave ≈ **48.34%**, independent of frequency.
- ✅ SPWM fundamental peak (linear, $m_a \le 1$) = $m_a V_{dc}$ (FB) or $m_a V_{dc}/2$ (HB).
- ✅ **Modulation index** $m_a = \hat{V}_{ref}/\hat{V}_{tri}$ — controls **voltage**.
- ✅ **Frequency ratio** $m_f = f_c/f_o$ — must be **odd integer**; controls **harmonic placement**.
- ✅ Harmonic clusters in SPWM appear at $f_c \pm 2kf_o$ (bipolar) and $2f_c \pm (2k-1)f_o$ (unipolar).
- ✅ RL load time constant $\tau = L/R$ governs current ripple; $\tau \gg T_o/2$ ⇒ near-rectangular current.
- ✅ Peak RL current with square wave: $I_{o,peak} = (V_{dc}/2R) \cdot [1 - \tanh(T_o/4\tau) e^{-T_o/(2\tau)}]$.
- ✅ **Antiparallel diodes are mandatory** in VSI; they provide freewheeling paths for inductive load current.
- ✅ **Dead-time** (1–5 μs) must be inserted between complementary gate signals to prevent DC bus shoot-through.
- ✅ Unipolar SPWM offers **lower harmonic distortion** and **higher effective ripple frequency** than bipolar SPWM, at the cost of more complex gate logic.
- ✅ For laboratory implementation: use **IGBT** for $V_{dc} > 100\,\text{V}$ or currents $> 10\,\text{A}$; **MOSFET** for low-voltage, high-frequency applications ($f_c > 50\,\text{kHz}$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inductor Design and Fabrication for High Frequency Switching Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL507 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Inductor Design and Fabrication for High Frequency Switching Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚡ Inductor Design and Fabrication for High Frequency Switching Applications

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition:**
> An **inductor** designed for high-frequency switching applications is a magnetic energy storage component whose inductance $L$ is precisely engineered using a magnetic core (typically ferrite, powdered iron, or sendust) wound with a specific number of turns of Litz or solid conductor, optimized to operate efficiently in the **20 kHz – 10 MHz** switching range used in modern SMPS, PFC converters, and resonant converters.

In the context of **Power Electronics Lab (PCEOL507)**, this experiment focuses on the design, calculation, and physical fabrication of an inductor for a specific application — usually a **buck/boost converter** or **flyback SMPS** — where the switching frequency $f_{sw}$, ripple current $\Delta I_L$, and maximum DC current $I_{L,\max}$ are pre-defined by the converter topology.

### Key Engineering Specifications (KTU 2024 Scheme)
| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (SMPS) |
|---|---|---|
| Switching Frequency | $f_{sw}$ | **20 kHz – 500 kHz** |
| Inductance | $L$ | **10 µH – 10 mH** |
| Saturation Flux Density | $B_{sat}$ | **0.3 T – 0.5 T** (Ferrite) |
| Permeability of Core | $\mu_r$ | **500 – 10000** (Ferrite) |
| Maximum DC Current | $I_{L,\max}$ | **1 A – 30 A** |
| Ripple Current | $\Delta I_L$ | **20% – 40%** of $I_L$ |
| Core Loss Density | $P_{cv}$ | **100 – 500 mW/cm³** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Water Flywheel Analogy"**
> Imagine a heavy mechanical flywheel connected to a water pump:
> - **Momentum (stored energy)** = Energy stored in the magnetic field of the inductor: $\frac{1}{2} L I^2$
> - **Spinning mass** = Magnetic core that stores flux $B$ in its iron path
> - **Shaft friction** = Core losses (hysteresis + eddy currents) that heat up the core
> - **Pump pulses (water hammer)** = High-frequency switching ripple current
> - **Flywheel size** = Core Area $A_c$ and number of turns $N$

Just as a flywheel must be **sized for the pulsating load** without slowing down, the inductor must be **designed to handle the high-frequency AC ripple** superimposed on the DC current without saturating the core. At high frequency, the current is "pulsing" thousands of times per second — the inductor must "absorb" this pulsation smoothly.

### Why is High-Frequency Design Different?
At high frequencies, three physical phenomena become dominant:
1. **Skin Effect** — current crowds to the surface of the conductor
2. **Proximity Effect** — adjacent conductors distort current distribution
3. **Core Losses** — ferrite cores exhibit frequency-dependent losses described by the **Steinmetz Equation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A low-frequency inductor (50 Hz transformer) is governed by $\Phi = B \cdot A_c$. A high-frequency switching inductor is governed by **core loss, skin depth, and energy storage density** — completely different physics.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Magnetic Circuit Fundamentals

The operation of any inductor is governed by **Ampere's Law** applied to the magnetic circuit:

$$\oint H \cdot dl = N \cdot I$$

For a closed magnetic path of mean length $l_m$:

$$H \cdot l_m = N \cdot I$$

The flux density inside the core is:

$$B = \mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot H = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot N \cdot I}{l_m}$$

The total flux linking the $N$ turns is:

$$\Phi = B \cdot A_c = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot N \cdot I \cdot A_c}{l_m}$$

And the **inductance** is defined by the flux linkage per unit current:

$$L = \frac{N \cdot \Phi}{I} = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot N^2 \cdot A_c}{l_m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important equation** in inductor design. Notice that $L \propto N^2$ — doubling the turns quadruples the inductance. Notice also that $L \propto \mu_r$ — adding an air gap dramatically reduces $\mu_r$ (effective) and hence $L$, but it **prevents saturation**.

---

## 2.2 The Role of the Air Gap

When DC current flows through the inductor, the core can saturate. To prevent this, an **air gap** $l_g$ is introduced in the magnetic path. The total reluctance becomes:

$$\mathcal{R}_{total} = \frac{l_m}{\mu_0 \mu_r A_c} + \frac{l_g}{\mu_0 A_c}$$

For ferrite cores with $\mu_r \gg 1$ and a small gap, the effective permeability is **dominated by the gap**:

$$\mu_{r,eff} \approx \frac{l_m}{l_g}$$

The inductance with air gap becomes:

$$L = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot A_c}{l_g + \dfrac{l_m}{\mu_r}}$$

For ferrite ($\mu_r$ very large), this simplifies to the **gapped-inductor equation**:

$$L \approx \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot A_c}{l_g}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a gap is ESSENTIAL in high-frequency inductors:**
> 1. Stores energy in the gap (most energy is actually in the gap, not the core)
> 2. Allows higher DC bias current before saturation
> 3. Makes inductance nearly **independent of core permeability variations**
> 4. Reduces fringing flux that can cause EMI

---

## 2.3 Core Losses — The Steinmetz Equation

The single biggest design challenge in high-frequency inductors is **managing core losses**. The empirical **Steinmetz Equation** is used universally:

$$P_{core} = k \cdot f_{sw}^{\alpha} \cdot \hat{B}^{\beta} \cdot V_c$$

Where:
- $P_{core}$ = Core loss in **watts**
- $k, \alpha, \beta$ = Steinmetz parameters (specific to core material)
- $\hat{B}$ = Peak AC flux density swing (Tesla)
- $V_c$ = Core volume in **m³**
- $f_{sw}$ = Switching frequency in **Hz**

For typical **ferrite (MnZn)** materials: $k \approx 1 \times 10^{-5}$, $\alpha \approx 1.3$, $\beta \approx 2.5$.

The **Modified Steinmetz Equation (MSE)** accounts for non-sinusoidal waveforms (PWM):

$$P_{MSE} = k \cdot f_{eq}^{\alpha - 1} \cdot \hat{B}^{\beta} \cdot f_r \cdot V_c$$

Where the equivalent frequency is:

$$f_{eq} = \frac{2}{\Delta B^2 \cdot \pi^2} \int_0^{T} \left(\frac{dB}{dt}\right)^2 dt$$

---

## 2.4 Skin Effect and Proximity Effect

### Skin Depth ($\delta$)
At high frequency, current concentrates near the conductor surface. The **skin depth** is:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \rho}{\omega \cdot \mu_0 \cdot \mu_r}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi \cdot f \cdot \mu_0 \cdot \mu_r}}$$

For copper at room temperature, this simplifies to:

$$\delta = \frac{66.1 \text{ mm}}{\sqrt{f \text{ (Hz)}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** At $f = 100$ kHz, $\delta \approx 0.21$ mm. A 1 mm diameter wire has effective copper area reduced by **75%**!

### Solution: Litz Wire
**Litz wire** (Litzendraht) consists of many small insulated strands, each thinner than $\delta$. This **eliminates skin effect losses**.

### Proximity Effect
In multi-layer windings, the AC magnetic field from adjacent turns induces eddy currents. The **Dowell equation** gives the AC resistance:

$$\frac{R_{ac}}{R_{dc}} = \frac{\xi}{2} \left[ \frac{\sinh \xi + \sin \xi}{\cosh \xi - \cos \xi} + (2m-1)^2 \cdot \frac{\sinh \xi - \sin \xi}{\cosh \xi + \cos \xi} \right]$$

Where $\xi = h/\delta$ is the ratio of conductor height to skin depth, and $m$ is the layer number.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A_c}{l_m}$ | Inductance of closed core | Henry (H) |
| 2 | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 A_c}{l_g}$ | Inductance of gapped core (ferrite) | Henry (H) |
| 3 | $N I = \dfrac{B_{max} \cdot l_g}{\mu_0}$ | Ampere-turns for given B | A·turns |
| 4 | $l_g = \dfrac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot A_c}{L}$ | Required air gap length | meters (m) |
| 5 | $A_L = \dfrac{L}{N^2}$ | Inductance factor (manufacturer spec) | H/turn² |
| 6 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} L I^2$ | Energy stored in inductor | Joules (J) |
| 7 | $P_{core} = k f^{\alpha} B^{\beta} V_c$ | Steinmetz core loss | Watts (W) |
| 8 | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r}}$ | Skin depth | meters (m) |
| 9 | $A_P = W_a \cdot A_c$ | Area Product (core sizing) | m⁴ |
| 10 | $B_{max} = \dfrac{L \cdot \Delta I}{N \cdot A_c}$ | Peak flux density swing | Tesla (T) |
| 11 | $\Delta I = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot D \cdot (1-D)}{L \cdot f_{sw}}$ | Ripple current (buck) | Amps (A) |
| 12 | $I_{L,rms} = \sqrt{I_{dc}^2 + \dfrac{\Delta I^2}{12}}$ | RMS inductor current | Amps (A) |
| 13 | $\mu_{r,eff} = \dfrac{l_m}{l_g}$ | Effective permeability (gapped) | dimensionless |
| 14 | $R_{winding} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot l_{wire}}{A_{wire}}$ | DC winding resistance | Ohms (Ω) |
| 15 | $J = \dfrac{I_{rms}}{A_{wire}}$ | Current density (limit: 3-5 A/mm²) | A/m² |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU board exams, **always** show the units in the final answer. Marks are deducted for "dimensionless answers" in derivation problems.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The design of high-frequency inductors is fundamental to:
- **Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS)** in laptops, mobile chargers
- **Power Factor Correction (PFC) circuits** in LED drivers
- **Electric Vehicle (EV) onboard chargers** operating at 50-500 kHz
- **Renewable energy inverters** (solar microinverters)
- **Wireless charging pads** (Qi standard: 100-205 kHz)
- **Resonant converters** (LLC, ZVS) in data center power supplies

Every modern power electronic system from a 5V phone charger to a 400V EV traction inverter uses inductors designed by these exact formulas.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔬 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Design Procedure — The Area Product (AP) Method

This is the **canonical KTU-style design procedure** for a high-frequency switching inductor. We will derive each step.

### GIVEN (Typical KTU Problem Statement):
- Switching frequency: $f_{sw}$ = **50 kHz**
- Maximum DC current: $I_{L,max}$ = **5 A**
- Allowed ripple: $\Delta I_L$ = **20%** of $I_{L,max}$ = 1 A
- Topology: **Buck Converter**
- Input voltage: $V_{in}$ = **24 V**
- Output voltage: $V_{out}$ = **12 V**
- Core: **Ferrite (MnZn), $B_{sat}$ = 0.3 T**
- Window utilization: $K_u$ = **0.4**
- Current density: $J$ = **4 A/mm²**

### STEP 1: Calculate the Required Inductance

The buck converter ripple current equation (derived from volt-second balance):

$$\Delta I_L = \frac{V_{in} \cdot D \cdot (1-D)}{L \cdot f_{sw}}$$

For worst-case ripple, $D = 0.5$ (maximum ripple for buck at $V_{out} = V_{in}/2$):

$$L = \frac{V_{in} \cdot D \cdot (1-D)}{\Delta I_L \cdot f_{sw}}$$

Substituting values:

$$L = \frac{24 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (1-0.5)}{1 \cdot 50{,}000}$$

$$L = \frac{24 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.5}{50{,}000} = \frac{6}{50{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{L = 120 \text{ μH}}$$

### STEP 2: Calculate the RMS Current

For a triangular ripple on a DC current:

$$I_{L,rms} = \sqrt{I_{dc}^2 + \frac{\Delta I_L^2}{12}}$$

Substituting $I_{dc} = 5$ A and $\Delta I_L = 1$ A:

$$I_{L,rms} = \sqrt{5^2 + \frac{1^2}{12}} = \sqrt{25 + 0.0833} = \sqrt{25.0833}$$

$$\boxed{I_{L,rms} \approx 5.008 \text{ A}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The RMS current is dominated by the DC component. The ripple contributes negligibly to heating.

### STEP 3: Calculate the Wire Cross-Sectional Area

Using the current density limit $J = 4$ A/mm²:

$$A_{wire} = \frac{I_{L,rms}}{J} = \frac{5.008}{4} = 1.252 \text{ mm}^2$$

### STEP 4: Calculate the Core Area Product (AP)

The **Area Product** combines the core cross-section $A_c$ and the window area $W_a$:

$$A_P = \frac{L \cdot I_{L,rms} \cdot I_{peak}}{K_u \cdot B_{max} \cdot J}$$

Where $I_{peak} = I_{dc} + \Delta I_L/2 = 5 + 0.5 = 5.5$ A.

$$A_P = \frac{120 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 5.008 \cdot 5.5}{0.4 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 4 \times 10^{6}}$$

Let us simplify the numerator first:

$$\text{Numerator} = 120 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 5.008 \cdot 5.5 = 120 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 27.544$$

$$= 3.3053 \times 10^{-3}$$

The denominator:

$$\text{Denominator} = 0.4 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 4 \times 10^{6} = 0.48 \times 10^{6}$$

Therefore:

$$A_P = \frac{3.3053 \times 10^{-3}}{0.48 \times 10^{6}} = 6.886 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^4$$

Converting to $\text{cm}^4$ (typical manufacturer unit):

$$A_P = 6.886 \times 10^{-9} \times (100)^4 = 0.6886 \text{ cm}^4$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> We select a core with $A_P \geq 0.69$ cm⁴. From the standard **EE ferrite core catalog**, an **EE30** core has $A_c = 1.11$ cm², $W_a = 0.81$ cm², so $A_P = 0.90$ cm⁴. ✓

### STEP 5: Calculate the Number of Turns (N)

Using the inductance equation with air gap:

$$L = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot A_c}{l_g}$$

But we need $l_g$ first. Using the relationship between $B_{max}$ and the magnetomotive force (MMF):

$$B_{max} = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N \cdot I_{peak}}{l_g}$$

Rearranging for the **air gap length**:

$$l_g = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N \cdot I_{peak}}{B_{max}}$$

Also, from the inductance formula:

$$l_g = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot A_c}{L}$$

Setting these two expressions for $l_g$ equal:

$$\frac{\mu_0 \cdot N \cdot I_{peak}}{B_{max}} = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot A_c}{L}$$

Canceling $\mu_0$ and one $N$:

$$\frac{I_{peak}}{B_{max}} = \frac{N \cdot A_c}{L}$$

Therefore:

$$N = \frac{L \cdot I_{peak}}{B_{max} \cdot A_c}$$

Substituting values (using SI units):

$$N = \frac{120 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 5.5}{0.3 \cdot 1.11 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$N = \frac{660 \times 10^{-6}}{3.33 \times 10^{-5}} = 19.82 \text{ turns}$$

$$\boxed{N = 20 \text{ turns}}$$

### STEP 6: Calculate the Air Gap Length

$$l_g = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot A_c}{L}$$

$$l_g = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \cdot (20)^2 \cdot 1.11 \times 10^{-4}}{120 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$l_g = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \cdot 400 \cdot 1.11 \times 10^{-4}}{120 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$l_g = \frac{4 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 400 \cdot 1.11}{120} \times 10^{-7-4+6}$$

$$l_g = \frac{5576.4}{120} \times 10^{-5}$$

$$l_g = 46.47 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{l_g \approx 0.465 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This air gap is typically achieved by inserting a **paper spacer** of 0.5 mm thickness in the center leg of the EE core. The gap is split between the two outer legs.

### STEP 7: Verify Core Loss (Steinmetz Check)

The peak-to-peak flux density swing is:

$$\Delta B = \frac{L \cdot \Delta I_L}{N \cdot A_c} = \frac{120 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1}{20 \cdot 1.11 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\Delta B = \frac{120 \times 10^{-6}}{2.22 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0540 \text{ T}$$

Assuming the ripple is centered on $B_{DC}$:

$$B_{DC} = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N \cdot I_{dc}}{l_g} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \cdot 20 \cdot 5}{0.465 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$B_{DC} = \frac{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}}{0.465 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.270 \text{ T}$$

Peak flux density: $B_{peak} = 0.270 + 0.027 = 0.297$ T — **well below saturation** ✓

The AC swing is $\hat{B} = 0.027$ T. Core volume for EE30 ≈ **2.5 cm³** = $2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³.

Using Steinmetz for ferrite ($k = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$, $\alpha = 1.3$, $\beta = 2.4$):

$$P_{core} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \cdot (50{,}000)^{1.3} \cdot (0.027)^{2.4} \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$P_{core} \approx 8.5 \text{ mW}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | Output | Formula Used | Calculated Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inductance | $L = V_{in}D(1-D)/(\Delta I f)$ | 120 µH |
| 2 | RMS Current | $I_{rms} = \sqrt{I_{dc}^2 + \Delta I^2/12}$ | 5.008 A |
| 3 | Wire Area | $A_w = I_{rms}/J$ | 1.252 mm² |
| 4 | Area Product | $A_P = L I_{rms} I_{peak}/(K_u B_{max} J)$ | 0.69 cm⁴ |
| 5 | Turns | $N = L I_{peak}/(B_{max} A_c)$ | 20 turns |
| 6 | Air Gap | $l_g = \mu_0 N^2 A_c / L$ | 0.465 mm |
| 7 | Core Loss | $P_{core} = k f^{\alpha} B^{\beta} V_c$ | 8.5 mW |
| 8 | Peak B | $B_{peak} = B_{DC} + \Delta B/2$ | 0.297 T ✓ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🧩 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inductor Design Flow Chart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Specs Vin, Vout, fsw, IL, ripple] --> B[Compute Duty Cycle D = Vout/Vin]
    B --> C[Compute Inductance L = Vin D 1-D / deltaIL fsw]
    C --> D[Compute Irms and Ipeak]
    D --> E[Compute Wire Area Aw = Irms / J]
    E --> F[Compute Area Product AP = L Irms Ipeak / Ku Bmax J]
    F --> G{AP falls in catalog range}
    G -- Yes --> H[Select Core e.g. EE30]
    G -- No --> I[Select larger core e.g. EE40]
    H --> J[Compute Number of Turns N = L Ipeak / Bmax Ac]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute Air Gap lg = mu0 N2 Ac / L]
    K --> L[Verify Bpeak less than Bsat]
    L --> M{Bpeak under Bsat}
    M -- Yes --> N[Verify Core Loss via Steinmetz]
    M -- No --> O[Increase Air Gap or add turns]
    N --> P{Core loss acceptable}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Select wire diameter considering skin depth]
    P -- No --> R[Reduce Bmax or use lower loss core]
    Q --> S[Fabrication: Winding, Spacers, Varnish]
    S --> T[End: Test Inductance on LCR meter]
```
