# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📌 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Steinmetz Equation. Why is it critical in high-frequency inductor design?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The Steinmetz Equation is an empirical formula that quantifies **core losses** in magnetic materials operating under AC excitation:

$$P_{core} = k \cdot f^{\alpha} \cdot \hat{B}^{\beta} \cdot V_c$$

Where $P_{core}$ is the core loss in watts, $f$ is the operating frequency, $\hat{B}$ is the peak AC flux density, $V_c$ is the core volume, and $k, \alpha, \beta$ are material-specific constants.

It is **critical in high-frequency design** because:
1. Core losses scale as $f^{1.3}$ — losses rise dramatically with switching frequency
2. Core losses scale as $B^{2.4}$ — small flux increases cause large loss increases
3. At high frequency, core heating becomes the **dominant failure mechanism** of the inductor
4. It allows selection of appropriate core material (ferrite vs. powdered iron) before fabrication

---

### **Question 2: What is skin effect? How is it mitigated in high-frequency inductors?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Skin effect** is the phenomenon where AC current density is non-uniform across a conductor cross-section, with current concentrating near the surface as frequency increases. The skin depth is given by:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r}}$$

For copper at 100 kHz, $\delta \approx 0.21$ mm, meaning current flows only in a thin outer shell of the conductor, dramatically increasing effective resistance.

**Mitigation methods:**
1. **Litz wire** — multiple insulated strands, each thinner than $\delta$
2. **Flat copper foil** — large surface area, low AC resistance
3. **PCB embedded inductors** — wide copper traces
4. **Silver-plated conductors** — for very high frequencies (>1 MHz)

---

## 📌 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks)** — Inductor Design for Buck Converter
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted 2024 Scheme]`

> Design an inductor for a buck converter with the following specifications:
> - Input voltage $V_{in} = 48$ V, Output voltage $V_{out} = 24$ V
> - Switching frequency $f_{sw} = 100$ kHz
> - Load current $I_L = 8$ A
> - Allowed ripple $\Delta I_L = 30\%$ of $I_L$
> - Ferrite core: $B_{max} = 0.3$ T, $K_u = 0.4$, $J = 4$ A/mm²

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]: Calculate the inductance, RMS current, and wire area.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Duty Cycle**
$$D = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{24}{48} = 0.5$$

**Step 2: Inductance**
$$\Delta I_L = 0.3 \times 8 = 2.4 \text{ A}$$
$$L = \frac{V_{in} \cdot D \cdot (1-D)}{\Delta I_L \cdot f_{sw}} = \frac{48 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.5}{2.4 \cdot 100{,}000}$$
$$\boxed{L = 50 \text{ μH}}$$

**Step 3: RMS Current**
$$I_{L,rms} = \sqrt{8^2 + \frac{2.4^2}{12}} = \sqrt{64 + 0.48} = \sqrt{64.48} = 8.03 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4: Wire Area**
$$A_{wire} = \frac{I_{L,rms}}{J} = \frac{8.03}{4} = 2.01 \text{ mm}^2$$

Using AWG14 wire (2.08 mm²) or equivalent.

---

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]: Design the core, turns, and air gap. Verify peak flux density.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 5: Area Product**
$$I_{peak} = 8 + 1.2 = 9.2 \text{ A}$$
$$A_P = \frac{L \cdot I_{L,rms} \cdot I_{peak}}{K_u \cdot B_{max} \cdot J} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8.03 \cdot 9.2}{0.4 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 4 \times 10^{6}}$$

Numerator: $50 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 73.876 = 3.694 \times 10^{-3}$

Denominator: $0.48 \times 10^{6}$

$$A_P = 7.70 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^4 = 0.77 \text{ cm}^4$$

**Step 6: Core Selection** — Select **EE35** core ($A_c = 1.07$ cm², $W_a = 0.97$ cm², $A_P = 1.04$ cm⁴) ✓

**Step 7: Number of Turns**
$$N = \frac{L \cdot I_{peak}}{B_{max} \cdot A_c} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 9.2}{0.3 \cdot 1.07 \times 10^{-4}}$$
$$N = \frac{4.6 \times 10^{-4}}{3.21 \times 10^{-5}} = 14.33$$
$$\boxed{N = 15 \text{ turns}}$$

**Step 8: Air Gap**
$$l_g = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A_c}{L} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \cdot 225 \cdot 1.07 \times 10^{-4}}{50 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$l_g = \frac{3.024 \times 10^{-7}}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 6.05 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$
$$\boxed{l_g \approx 0.6 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 9: Verification**
$$B_{DC} = \frac{\mu_0 N I_{dc}}{l_g} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \cdot 15 \cdot 8}{0.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.251 \text{ T}$$
$$B_{AC} = \frac{L \cdot \Delta I_L}{2 N A_c} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 2.4}{2 \cdot 15 \cdot 1.07 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0374 \text{ T}$$
$$B_{peak} = 0.251 + 0.0374 = 0.288 \text{ T} < B_{sat} = 0.3 \text{ T} \checkmark$$

---

### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice: Core Loss & Fabrication
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Internal Choice]`

> A ferrite-cored inductor ($V_c = 3$ cm³) operates in a 100 kHz buck converter with $B_{AC} = 0.05$ T. Core material constants: $k = 2 \times 10^{-5}$, $\alpha = 1.4$, $\beta = 2.5$.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the core loss using Steinmetz Equation. Discuss the effect of doubling the frequency on losses.
> **(b)** Explain the complete fabrication procedure of a gapped ferrite inductor for this application. Include winding, insulation, and testing steps.

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]: Model Solution**

**Core Loss Calculation:**
$$P_{core} = k \cdot f^{\alpha} \cdot \hat{B}^{\beta} \cdot V_c$$

Convert $V_c$ to m³: $V_c = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ m³

$$P_{core} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \cdot (100{,}000)^{1.4} \cdot (0.05)^{2.5} \cdot 3 \times 10^{-6}$$

Compute each term:
- $(10^5)^{1.4} = 10^7 = 10{,}000{,}000$
- $(0.05)^{2.5} = (5 \times 10^{-2})^{2.5} = 5^{2.5} \times 10^{-5} = 55.90 \times 10^{-5} = 5.59 \times 10^{-4}$

$$P_{core} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 10^7 \cdot 5.59 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 3 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$P_{core} = 2 \times 3 \times 5.59 \times 10^{-5+7-4-6} = 33.54 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\boxed{P_{core} \approx 33.5 \text{ mW}}$$

**Effect of Doubling Frequency (to 200 kHz):**

Since $P \propto f^{1.4}$:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{200{,}000}{100{,}000}\right)^{1.4} = 2^{1.4} = 2.639$$

$$\boxed{P_{new} \approx 88.4 \text{ mW} \text{ (a 164% increase)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why high-frequency designs require **smaller cores** (to limit flux) and **better thermal management**.

---

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]: Fabrication Procedure Model Answer**

The fabrication of a gapped ferrite inductor involves the following steps:

1. **Core Selection and Preparation**: Select the EE or ETD ferrite core (e.g., EE35) per design. Inspect for cracks and clean with isopropyl alcohol.

2. **Air Gap Insertion**: Cut a non-magnetic spacer (kapton tape or kraft paper) of calculated thickness $l_g$. Insert it in the **center leg** of the bottom E-core. This creates the energy storage gap.

3. **Bobbin Preparation**: Place the plastic bobbin over the center leg of the bottom E-core. This provides insulation between winding and core.

4. **Winding**: Wind $N$ turns of AWG14 (or Litz wire) uniformly around the bobbin. Maintain **even layer distribution** to minimize proximity effect. Use insulation tape between layers.

5. **Tap Identification**: Identify start and end terminals. Use **sleeve markers** (yellow for start, red for end) per KTU lab convention.

6. **Core Clamping**: Place the top E-core on the bottom E-core with spacer. Clamp the assembly with a non-magnetic clamp or use **spring clips** to maintain mechanical stability and constant air gap.

7. **Varnishing and Curing**: Dip the wound inductor in **insulating varnish** (Class F or H) and cure at 80°C for 4 hours. This provides humidity protection and mechanical rigidity.

8. **Testing**:
   - Measure inductance on **LCR meter at 100 kHz** — verify design value
   - Measure DC resistance on **milliohm meter** — verify copper loss
   - Apply rated current — measure temperature rise after 30 minutes
   - Verify saturation by gradually increasing DC current and observing $L$ roll-off

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the final numerical answer — **deduct 0.5 mark**
> 2. **Not converting cm² to m²** in the AP formula — major error
> 3. **Using wrong Steinmetz parameters** for the material — material-specific
> 4. **Ignoring fringing flux** at the air gap — this affects EMI compliance
> 5. **Not verifying peak B** is below $B_{sat}$ — design may fail in practice
> 6. **Confusing $B_{max}$ with $\Delta B$** — one is peak swing, other is AC component

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam**

✅ **Core Equation:** $L = \mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A_c / l_m$ (no gap), $L = \mu_0 N^2 A_c / l_g$ (gapped)

✅ **Steinmetz Equation:** $P_{core} = k f^{\alpha} B^{\beta} V_c$ — used for **all high-frequency loss calculations**

✅ **Skin Depth for Copper:** $\delta = 66.1 / \sqrt{f \text{ (Hz)}}$ mm — use Litz wire if conductor diameter $> 2\delta$

✅ **Energy Stored:** $E = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$ — most energy is stored in the **air gap**, not the core

✅ **Air Gap ALWAYS Required** for inductors carrying DC current to prevent saturation

✅ **Design Procedure Order**: Compute L → Select core (AP method) → Calculate N → Calculate $l_g$ → Verify $B_{peak} < B_{sat}$ → Verify $P_{core}$ acceptable → Fabricate → Test

✅ **Typical Values to Memorize**: Ferrite $B_{sat} \approx 0.3-0.4$ T, Powdered Iron $B_{sat} \approx 1.0-1.5$ T, Current density $J = 3-5$ A/mm², Window utilization $K_u = 0.3-0.5$

✅ **Litz wire is mandatory** above 50 kHz for inductors carrying > 1 A

✅ **B-H Curve**: Air gap makes the B-H curve **sheared** — DC bias capability increases, but $L$ decreases

✅ **Practical Air Gap**: Achieved with paper, plastic, or ceramic spacers — gap length precision: ±5%

✅ **Test Equipment for Lab**: LCR meter (1 kHz to 100 kHz), oscilloscope with current probe, DC power supply with current limit, temperature sensor

✅ **Conversion Factors**: 1 cm² = 10⁻⁴ m², 1 cm⁴ = 10⁻⁸ m⁴, 1 T = 10⁴ Gauss

✅ **Energy Density Limit**: $E/V_c \leq 100$ mJ/cm³ for practical ferrite inductors (thermal limit)

---

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes**
> Module Experiment 2 | PCEOL507 Power Electronics Lab | KTU 2024 Scheme

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Set-Up of Buck/Boost/Buck-Boost Converter (Continuous Conduction Mode)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL507 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design and Set-Up of Buck/Boost/Buck-Boost Converter (Continuous Conduction Mode)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Switch-Mode DC-DC Converter:** A power electronic circuit that converts one DC voltage level to another DC voltage level using semiconductor switches (MOSFET/IGBT), energy storage elements (inductor and capacitor), and pulse-width modulation (PWM) control. Operation is classified as **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)** when the inductor current never falls to zero during one switching cycle.

The three fundamental non-isolated converter topologies covered in this experiment are:

| Converter | Function | Output Polarity |
|---|---|---|
| **Buck (Step-Down)** | $V_o < V_s$ | Same as input |
| **Boost (Step-Up)** | $V_o > V_s$ | Same as input |
| **Buck-Boost** | $V_o \lessgtr V_s$ | Inverted (negative) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Water-Tank Analogy (Buck Converter):** Imagine a water tank with an inlet pipe (source) and an outlet pipe (load). A **valve** at the inlet is opened and closed rapidly (the MOSFET switch). When the valve is open, water fills the tank (inductor stores energy as magnetic field). When the valve is closed, only the stored water flows to the load (inductor releases energy through the freewheeling diode). The **duty cycle (D)** is the fraction of time the valve stays open. A longer open-time → higher output pressure. This is exactly how a Buck converter steps down voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Buck-Boost as a Two-Stage Pump:** A Buck-Boost is like a bucket brigade — first the bucket (inductor) is *filled* from the source via the switch, then the bucket is *emptied into the load* in the opposite direction. The output voltage is therefore inverted, and its magnitude can be either above or below the input depending on the duty cycle.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Design Metrics

- **Switching Frequency ($f_s$):** Typically **20 kHz – 100 kHz** (above audible range to avoid noise)
- **Duty Cycle ($D$):** $0 < D < 1$ (always between 0 and 1 for CCM)
- **Ripple Factor:** Usually **20% – 40%** of average inductor current
- **Output Voltage Ripple ($\Delta V_o$):** Typically **1% – 5%** of $V_o$
- **Efficiency ($\eta$):** Ideal case = **100%**; Practical = **85% – 95%**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle – Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Heart of CCM Operation:** In Continuous Conduction Mode, the inductor current waveform is a periodic triangular wave that **never reaches zero**. This requires the energy stored in the inductor at the end of one switching period to be greater than zero, which is the CCM boundary condition.

**Common Switching Sequence (all three converters):**

1. **Switch ON (DT_s interval):** MOSFET is ON, diode is reverse-biased (OFF). Source delivers energy to the inductor.
2. **Switch OFF ((1-D)T_s interval):** MOSFET is OFF, diode is forward-biased (ON). Inductor releases stored energy to the load and capacitor.
3. **Capacitor:** Acts as a low-pass filter to smooth output voltage; charges when inductor current exceeds load current, discharges when it is less.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | **Buck Converter** | **Boost Converter** | **Buck-Boost Converter** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage Transfer Ratio ($M$)** | $M = D$ | $M = \dfrac{1}{1-D}$ | $M = \dfrac{D}{1-D}$ (magnitude) |
| **Switch Voltage Stress** | $V_{sw,max} = V_s$ | $V_{sw,max} = V_o$ | $V_{sw,max} = V_s + V_o$ |
| **Diode Voltage Stress** | $V_D = V_s$ | $V_D = V_o$ | $V_D = V_s + V_o$ |
| **Inductor Design ($L$)** | $L = \dfrac{V_o(1-D)}{f_s \, \Delta I_L}$ | $L = \dfrac{V_o \, D(1-D)}{f_s \, \Delta I_L}$ | $L = \dfrac{V_o(1-D)}{f_s \, \Delta I_L}$ |
| **Capacitor Design ($C$)** | $C = \dfrac{1-D}{8 L f_s^2} \cdot \dfrac{V_o}{\Delta V_o}$ | $C = \dfrac{D}{R \, f_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_o}{\Delta V_o}$ | $C = \dfrac{D}{R \, f_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_o}{\Delta V_o}$ |
| **CCM Boundary ($L_{min}$)** | $L_{min} = \dfrac{(1-D)R}{2 f_s}$ | $L_{min} = \dfrac{D(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$ | $L_{min} = \dfrac{(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$ |
| **Output Voltage ($V_o$)** | $V_o = D \cdot V_s$ | $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$ | $V_o = -\dfrac{D \, V_s}{1-D}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Notation:**
> - $D$ = Duty cycle (dimensionless)
> - $R$ = Load resistance (Ω)
> - $\Delta I_L$ = Inductor current ripple (A)
> - $\Delta V_o$ = Output voltage ripple (V)
> - $f_s$ = Switching frequency (Hz)
> - Negative sign in Buck-Boost indicates **inverted polarity**

### 2.3 CCM Boundary Condition – The Most Critical Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Boundary of Conduction:** A converter operates in CCM when $L > L_{min}$. At the boundary, the inductor current just touches zero at the end of the OFF period. Below $L_{min}$, the converter enters **Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM)**, where the voltage transfer ratio becomes load-dependent — a phenomenon that must be avoided in regulated power supply design.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Buck:** Laptop chargers, USB power delivery, LED drivers, point-of-load regulators in computer motherboards.
- **Boost:** Solar PV systems, hybrid electric vehicles, battery-powered flash circuits, regenerative braking.
- **Buck-Boost:** Power factor correction (PFC) pre-regulators, battery management systems (BMS), renewable energy MPPT systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Voltage Transfer Ratio – Buck Converter (Volts-Second Balance)

**Principle Used:** In steady-state CCM, the average voltage across the inductor over one switching period must be zero (otherwise the inductor would saturate). This is the **Volt-Second Balance** theorem.

**Step 1: Write the inductor voltage during the switch ON interval ($0 < t < D T_s$).**

The MOSFET is ON, so the source is directly connected to the inductor-input side. The voltage across the inductor is:

$$V_{L,ON} = V_s - V_o$$

**Step 2: Write the inductor voltage during the switch OFF interval ($D T_s < t < T_s$).**

The diode conducts, connecting the inductor to the load. The voltage across the inductor is:

$$V_{L,OFF} = -V_o$$

**Step 3: Apply the Volt-Second Balance (average $V_L$ = 0).**

$$\int_0^{T_s} V_L(t) \, dt = V_{L,ON} \cdot D T_s + V_{L,OFF} \cdot (1-D) T_s = 0$$

**Step 4: Substitute and divide by $T_s$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
(V_s - V_o) \cdot D + (-V_o) \cdot (1-D) &= 0 \\
D V_s - D V_o - V_o + D V_o &= 0 \\
D V_s - V_o &= 0 \\
\therefore \quad V_o &= D \cdot V_s
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **fundamental transfer ratio** for a Buck converter.

### 3.2 Derivation of Voltage Transfer Ratio – Boost Converter

**Step 1: Inductor voltage when switch is ON.**

When the MOSFET is ON, the input source is shorted across the inductor (diode is reverse-biased). The output is supported by the capacitor alone.

$$V_{L,ON} = V_s$$

**Step 2: Inductor voltage when switch is OFF.**

The inductor is now in series with the source supplying the load and capacitor.

$$V_{L,OFF} = V_s - V_o$$

**Step 3: Apply Volt-Second Balance.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_s \cdot D + (V_s - V_o) \cdot (1-D) &= 0 \\
D V_s + V_s - V_o - D V_s + D V_o &= 0 \\
V_s - V_o + D V_o &= 0 \\
V_s &= V_o (1-D) \\
\therefore \quad V_o &= \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Voltage Transfer Ratio – Buck-Boost Converter

**Step 1: Inductor voltage when switch is ON.**

The inductor is connected directly across the input; the diode is OFF, isolating the output.

$$V_{L,ON} = V_s$$

**Step 2: Inductor voltage when switch is OFF.**

The inductor is now in series with the load; current flows in the opposite direction through the output capacitor (causing the output to be negative).

$$V_{L,OFF} = V_o$$

**Step 3: Apply Volt-Second Balance.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_s \cdot D + V_o \cdot (1-D) &= 0 \\
V_o (1-D) &= -D V_s \\
\therefore \quad V_o &= -\dfrac{D \cdot V_s}{1-D}
\end{aligned}$$

The magnitude is $\vert V_o \vert = \dfrac{D \cdot V_s}{1-D}$, and the polarity is **inverted** with respect to the input.

### 3.4 Derivation of Inductor Design Equation (Buck Example)

**Step 1: Current ripple definition.**

The peak-to-peak inductor current ripple is determined by the ON-state voltage and the ON-time interval:

$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_{L,ON} \cdot D T_s}{L} = \dfrac{(V_s - V_o) \cdot D T_s}{L}$$

**Step 2: Substitute $V_o = D V_s$, so $V_s - V_o = V_s(1-D)$.**

$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s(1-D) \cdot D T_s}{L} = \dfrac{V_o(1-D) T_s}{L}$$

**Step 3: Rearrange to obtain $L$.**

$$L = \dfrac{V_o(1-D)}{f_s \cdot \Delta I_L}$$

(where $f_s = 1/T_s$). This equation is used to select the inductor value that keeps the ripple within the desired limit.

### 3.5 CCM Boundary Derivation

**Step 1: Define boundary condition.**

At the CCM/DCM boundary, the minimum inductor current equals zero:

$$I_{L,min} = I_L - \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_L = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2}$$

**Step 2: Average inductor current equals load current (Buck).**

For a Buck converter, $I_L = I_o = V_o / R$. Substituting:

$$\dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2} = \dfrac{V_o}{R}$$

**Step 3: Substitute $\Delta I_L$ from §3.4.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{V_o(1-D)}{f_s L} &= \dfrac{V_o}{R} \\
L_{min} &= \dfrac{(1-D) R}{2 f_s}
\end{aligned}$$

For CCM: $L > L_{min}$.

### 3.6 Numerical Design Example – Buck Converter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Given:** $V_s = 24$ V, $V_o = 12$ V, $I_o = 2$ A, $f_s = 50$ kHz, $\Delta I_L = 20\%$ of $I_L$, $\Delta V_o = 1\%$ of $V_o$.

**Step 1: Compute duty cycle.**

$$D = \dfrac{V_o}{V_s} = \dfrac{12}{24} = 0.5$$

**Step 2: Compute load resistance.**

$$R = \dfrac{V_o}{I_o} = \dfrac{12}{2} = 6 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3: Compute ripple current.**

$$\Delta I_L = 0.20 \times 2 = 0.4 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 4: Compute inductor value.**

$$L = \dfrac{12 \times (1 - 0.5)}{50000 \times 0.4} = \dfrac{6}{20000} = 300 \, \mu H$$

**Step 5: Verify CCM.**

$$L_{min} = \dfrac{(1 - 0.5) \times 6}{2 \times 50000} = \dfrac{3}{100000} = 30 \, \mu H$$

Since $L = 300 \, \mu H \gg L_{min} = 30 \, \mu H$, **the converter operates in CCM**. ✓

**Step 6: Compute output capacitor.**

$$C = \dfrac{(1 - 0.5)}{8 \times 300 \times 10^{-6} \times (50000)^2} \times \dfrac{12}{0.12} = 16.67 \, \mu F$$

Choose standard value: **22 µF, 25 V** electrolytic capacitor.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Buck Converter – Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Source Vs] -->|Positive terminal| B[MOSFET Switch S]
    B -->|Switch ON: Energy stored| C[Inductor L]
    C -->|Smoothed current| D[Output Capacitor C]
    D --> E[Load R]
    E -->|Return path| F[Freewheeling Diode D1]
    F -->|Cathode at Vs, Anode at ground| A
    G[PWM Controller] -->|Gate pulses at frequency fs| B
    D -->|Feedback voltage| G
```

### 4.2 Boost Converter – Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Source Vs] -->|Positive terminal| B[Inductor L]
    B -->|Charges during ON| C[MOSFET Switch S]
    C -->|Switch to ground| D[Return to Source]
    B -->|Releases during OFF| E[Diode D1]
    E -->|Rectified output| F[Output Capacitor C]
    F --> G[Load R]
    G -->|Return| D
    H[PWM Controller] -->|Gate signal| C
    F -->|Feedback| H
```

### 4.3 Buck-Boost Converter – Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Source Vs] -->|Positive terminal| B[MOSFET Switch S]
    B -->|Switch ON: L stores energy| C[Inductor L]
    C --> D[Common Ground]
    A -->|Negative terminal| D
    C -->|Switch OFF: L discharges in reverse| E[Diode D1]
    E -->|Inverted polarity -Vo| F[Output Capacitor C]
    F --> G[Load R]
    G -->|Return to common ground| D
    H[PWM Controller] -->|PWM signal| B
    F -->|Feedback voltage sensing| H
```

### 4.4 CCM Waveform Timing Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Phase1["Phase 1: Switch ON (0 to DT_s)"]
        W1[Gate Pulse: HIGH]
        W2[Inductor Voltage: V_L equals Vs minus Vo for BUCK]
        W3[Inductor Current: RAMPS UP linearly]
    end
    subgraph Phase2["Phase 2: Switch OFF (DT_s to T_s)"]
        X1[Gate Pulse: LOW]
        X2[Inductor Voltage: equals minus Vo]
        X3[Inductor Current: RAMPS DOWN linearly]
    end
    subgraph Boundary["CCM Condition"]
        Y1[IL minimum greater than 0]
        Y2[Average IL equals Io for BUCK]
    end
    Phase1 --> Phase2
    Phase2 --> Boundary
```

```mermaid
    A[230V AC Mains] --> B[Variac]
    B --> C[Rectifier with Filter]
    C --> D[DC Source Vs to Converter]
    D --> E[Gate Driver Circuit]
    F[Function Generator / PWM IC SG3525] -->|PWM pulses| E
    E -->|Gate signal| G[MOSFET IRF540]
    G --> H[Converter Power Stage]

    J[Digital Storage Oscilloscope] -->|Probe 1| K[Gate Pulse]

```

```mermaid
        Q1[MOSFET IRF540 N-Channel]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM) and Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM) in a DC-DC converter. State the condition that determines the boundary between them.

**Model Answer:**

> **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM):** A mode of operation of a switching converter in which the inductor current flows continuously throughout the entire switching period, i.e., the current never falls to zero.
>
> **Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM):** A mode of operation in which the inductor current falls to zero and remains zero for a finite portion of the switching period.
>
> **Boundary Condition:** The boundary between CCM and DCM occurs when the minimum inductor current just reaches zero, which is mathematically expressed as:
>
> $$I_{L,min} = I_L - \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2} = 0$$
>
> This gives $L = L_{min} = \dfrac{(1-D)R}{2f_s}$ for a Buck converter. For CCM: $L > L_{min}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Why is the output of a Buck-Boost converter inverted? What is the relationship between input and output voltage magnitudes?

**Model Answer:**

> The output of a Buck-Boost converter is inverted because the inductor, when discharging, releases current in the **opposite direction** to that during charging. This current, flowing through the diode, charges the output capacitor with a polarity opposite to the input. Hence, the output terminal referenced to ground is **negative**.
>
> The voltage relationship (magnitude) is:
>
> $$\vert V_o \vert = \dfrac{D \cdot V_s}{1-D}$$
>
> When $D < 0.5$, the converter acts as a Buck (steps down); when $D > 0.5$, it acts as a Boost (steps up).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the voltage transfer ratio of a Buck converter operating in CCM using the volt-second balance principle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a Buck converter with the following specifications: $V_s = 24$ V, $V_o = 15$ V, $I_o = 3$ A, $f_s = 25$ kHz, and peak-to-peak inductor current ripple of 30% of the average value. Calculate $D$, $L$, and the minimum value of $L$ to maintain CCM. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Voltage Transfer Ratio:**

Consider a Buck converter in steady-state CCM. Let the switching period be $T_s$ and the duty cycle be $D$.

**During Switch ON (0 ≤ t ≤ DT_s):**

The MOSFET conducts. The inductor sees the difference between source voltage and output voltage:

$$V_{L,ON} = V_s - V_o$$

**During Switch OFF (DT_s ≤ t ≤ T_s):**

The MOSFET is OFF, the diode conducts. The inductor sees only the output voltage with opposite polarity:

$$V_{L,OFF} = -V_o$$

**Applying Volt-Second Balance:**

In steady state, the average voltage across the inductor over one cycle must be zero:

$$V_{L,ON} \cdot D T_s + V_{L,OFF} \cdot (1-D) T_s = 0$$

Substituting:

$$(V_s - V_o) \cdot D + (-V_o) \cdot (1-D) = 0$$

Expanding:

$$D V_s - D V_o - V_o + D V_o = 0$$

$$D V_s - V_o = 0$$

$$\boxed{V_o = D \cdot V_s}$$

Thus the transfer ratio $M = V_o / V_s = D$.

**(b) Design Calculations:**

**Step 1: Compute the duty cycle.**

$$D = \dfrac{V_o}{V_s} = \dfrac{15}{24} = 0.625$$

**Step 2: Compute load resistance.**

$$R = \dfrac{V_o}{I_o} = \dfrac{15}{3} = 5 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3: Compute ripple current (30% of average).**

$$\Delta I_L = 0.30 \times 3 = 0.9 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 4: Compute the inductor value.**

$$L = \dfrac{V_o(1-D)}{f_s \cdot \Delta I_L} = \dfrac{15 \times (1 - 0.625)}{25000 \times 0.9} = \dfrac{15 \times 0.375}{22500} = 250 \, \mu H$$

**Step 5: Compute the minimum inductance for CCM.**

$$L_{min} = \dfrac{(1-D) R}{2 f_s} = \dfrac{(1 - 0.625) \times 5}{2 \times 25000} = \dfrac{1.875}{50000} = 37.5 \, \mu H$$

**Step 6: Verify CCM operation.**

Since $L = 250 \, \mu H \gg L_{min} = 37.5 \, \mu H$, the converter operates safely in CCM. ✓

**Final Design Values:** $D = 0.625$, $L = 250 \, \mu H$, $L_{min} = 37.5 \, \mu H$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a Boost converter in CCM. Derive the expression for its output voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Boost converter has $V_s = 12$ V, $V_o = 24$ V, $R = 20 \, \Omega$, $f_s = 50$ kHz. Determine: (i) duty cycle $D$, (ii) average inductor current, (iii) peak-to-peak inductor ripple current if $L = 200 \, \mu H$, and (iv) whether the converter operates in CCM. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of Boost Converter:**

> Refer to §4.2 for the circuit topology. The Boost converter operates in two states:
>
> **State 1 (Switch ON, duration $DT_s$):** The MOSFET is ON. The diode is reverse-biased. The input source charges the inductor through the switch. Energy is stored in the magnetic field. The output capacitor supplies the load.
>
> **State 2 (Switch OFF, duration $(1-D)T_s$):** The MOSFET is OFF. The diode is forward-biased. The inductor voltage reverses (acting as a source in series with $V_s$) and delivers energy to the load and recharges the output capacitor.
>
> **Derivation (Volt-Second Balance):**
>
> During ON: $V_{L,ON} = V_s$
> During OFF: $V_{L,OFF} = V_s - V_o$
>
> Applying volt-second balance:
>
> $$V_s \cdot D + (V_s - V_o) \cdot (1-D) = 0$$
>
> $$V_s D + V_s - V_s D - V_o + D V_o = 0$$
>
> $$V_s - V_o + D V_o = 0$$
>
> $$V_s = V_o (1-D)$$
>
> $$\boxed{V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}}$$

**(b) Numerical Design:**

**Step 1: Compute duty cycle from transfer ratio.**

$$V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 - D = \dfrac{V_s}{V_o} = \dfrac{12}{24} = 0.5$$

$$\therefore \quad D = 0.5$$

**Step 2: Compute average output current and average inductor current.**

$$I_o = \dfrac{V_o}{R} = \dfrac{24}{20} = 1.2 \, \text{A}$$

For a Boost converter, the average inductor current relates to output current by power balance: $I_L = \dfrac{I_o}{1-D} = \dfrac{1.2}{0.5} = 2.4 \, \text{A}$.

**Step 3: Compute peak-to-peak ripple current.**

$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s \cdot D}{f_s \cdot L} = \dfrac{12 \times 0.5}{50000 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{6}{10} = 0.6 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 4: Determine minimum inductance and verify CCM.**

$$L_{min} = \dfrac{D(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s} = \dfrac{0.5 \times (0.5)^2 \times 20}{2 \times 50000} = \dfrac{2.5}{100000} = 25 \, \mu H$$

Since $L = 200 \, \mu H \gg L_{min} = 25 \, \mu H$, the converter operates in CCM. ✓

**Step 5: Confirm with current check.**

$$I_{L,min} = I_L - \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2} = 2.4 - 0.3 = 2.1 \, \text{A} > 0$$

CCM is confirmed.

**Final Answers:** $D = 0.5$, $I_L = 2.4$ A, $\Delta I_L = 0.6$ A, Converter is in **CCM**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Buck-Boost:** Students often forget the negative sign in $V_o = -DV_s/(1-D)$. Examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for missing polarity inversion.
>
> 2. **Confusing $I_L$ with $I_o$:** In a **Boost** converter, the average inductor current $I_L$ is **NOT** equal to $I_o$. The correct relation is $I_L = I_o/(1-D)$ derived from power conservation. Many students incorrectly write $I_L = I_o$ — this is a major error worth **2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to verify CCM:** The problem statement may say "design for CCM" but students often compute $L$ without checking $L > L_{min}$. Examiners look for this verification step.
>
> 4. **Units Confusion:** $\mu H$ vs mH vs H is a frequent mistake. Always write units explicitly.
>
> 5. **Volts-Second Balance Setup:** Marks are awarded for clearly labeling $V_{L,ON}$ and $V_{L,OFF}$ before applying the balance equation. Skipping this labeling step costs 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**
>
> ✅ **Voltage Transfer Ratios (Memorize):**
> - Buck: $M = D$
> - Boost: $M = 1/(1-D)$
> - Buck-Boost: $\vert M \vert = D/(1-D)$ (output is **inverted**)
>
> ✅ **CCM Boundary Conditions (Critical for Design):**
> - Buck: $L_{min} = (1-D)R/(2f_s)$
> - Boost: $L_{min} = D(1-D)^2 R/(2f_s)$
> - Buck-Boost: $L_{min} = (1-D)^2 R/(2f_s)$
>
> ✅ **Always remember to verify $L > L_{min}$** after computing the inductor value.
>
> ✅ **Output Polarity:**
> - Buck → Non-inverting
> - Boost → Non-inverting
> - Buck-Boost → **Inverting** (negative output)
>
> ✅ **Inductor Current Relationship:**
> - Buck: $I_L = I_o$
> - Boost: $I_L = I_o / (1-D)$ (higher input current)
> - Buck-Boost: $I_L = I_o / (1-D)$
>
> ✅ **Practical Design Heuristics (Lab Tip):**
> - Choose $f_s \geq 20$ kHz to avoid audible noise
> - Keep inductor ripple at 20%–40% of average current
> - Keep output voltage ripple below 5% of $V_o$
> - Use **fast-recovery diodes** (e.g., MUR460) to minimize switching losses
> - Use **gate driver ICs** (TC4420, IR2110) for proper MOSFET switching
>
> ✅ **Experimental Procedure Outline (Lab Exam):**
> 1. Wire the converter circuit on a breadboard/PCB.
> 2. Apply a variable DC input voltage using a rectifier + variac setup.
> 3. Generate PWM signal using SG3525 / function generator.
> 4. Observe gate pulse, output voltage, inductor current on DSO.
> 5. Vary duty cycle, plot $V_o$ vs $D$ and compare with theoretical formula.
> 6. Verify CCM by observing the inductor current waveform (no zero crossing).
>
> ✅ **Key Takeaway:** Volt-Second Balance and Amp-Second Balance (for capacitor) are the **two fundamental principles** governing all switching converter analysis in CCM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speed Control of Permanent Magnet/Separately Excited DC Motor Using One-Quadrant/Two-Quadrant DC Chopper
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOL507 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Speed Control of Permanent Magnet/Separately Excited DC Motor Using One-Quadrant/Two-Quadrant DC Chopper # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **DC Chopper** is a static power electronic device (DC-to-DC converter) that converts a fixed DC input voltage into a variable DC output voltage by rapidly switching the supply ON and OFF at a high frequency, thereby achieving **pulse-width modulation (PWM)** of the average voltage delivered to the load. In the context of the KTU PCEOL507 lab, the experiment involves using a chopper to control the speed of a **Permanent Magnet (PM) DC Motor** or a **Separately Excited (SE) DC Motor** by varying the average armature voltage. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > A chopper is a high-speed ON/OFF semiconductor switch that connects the source to the load and disconnects it at a fast rate. The average value of the output voltage depends on the **duty cycle (α)**, defined as: > $$\alpha = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$ > where $T$ is the total switching period. ## 1.2 One-Quadrant vs Two-Quadrant Chopper Classification | Chopper Type | Class | Operating Quadrants | Output Voltage Range | Applications | |---|---|---|---|---| | **One-Quadrant Chopper** | Class A | Quadrant I (Forward Motoring) | $0$ to $+V_s$ | Single-direction drive, fans, pumps | | **Two-Quadrant Chopper** | Class C | Quadrant I & II (Motoring + Regenerative Braking) | $0$ to $+V_s$ or $-V_s$ to $0$ | Hoists, electric vehicles, elevators | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Faucet and Bucket" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** > Imagine a water tap (faucet) connected to a bucket. If you open the tap fully for the entire time, the bucket fills to the brim. Now, if you **repeatedly turn the tap ON for 1 second and OFF for 1 second**, the bucket receives water only half the time — effectively getting *half the average water flow*. > > A **chopper works exactly like this**: > - The **tap** = Power semiconductor switch (IGBT/MOSFET) > - **ON duration** ($T_{on}$) = Time water flows > - **OFF duration** ($T_{off}$) = Time tap is closed > - **Bucket fill level** = Average DC output voltage > > If you keep the ON-time longer than the OFF-time, the bucket fills more → higher average voltage → **motor runs faster**. If you make ON-time shorter, the motor slows down. ## 1.4 Why Use a Chopper for DC Motor Speed Control? Conventional methods of speed control include: 1. **Armature resistance control** — wastes power as heat (inefficient). 2. **Field flux control** — limited range, affects commutation. The **chopper-fed armature voltage control** method is preferred because: - **High efficiency** (>90%) — switching losses are minimal. - **Smooth and wide speed range** from near-zero to base speed. - **Regenerative braking capability** (in two-quadrant chopper) — returns energy to source. - **Compact, lightweight, and maintenance-free** (no moving parts like rheostats). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU 2024 Highlight:** > The speed of a DC motor below base speed is controlled by **varying the armature voltage** using a chopper, while speed above base speed is controlled by **field weakening** (only for separately excited motors, since PM motors have fixed flux). ## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Switching frequency** ($f$): typically **1 kHz to 20 kHz** for IGBT/MOSFET choppers. - **Duty cycle range**: **0 < α < 1** (theoretical), practically **0.05 to 0.95**. - **Back EMF constant** ($K\phi$): depends on motor rating (in V/(rad/s) or V/(RPM)). - **Standard supply voltage** ($V_s$): **230 V DC** (lab supply) or derived from **rectifier-fed DC link**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle of a One-Quadrant (Class A) Chopper The one-quadrant chopper consists of: - **One controllable switch** ($S$) — typically an IGBT or MOSFET. - **One freewheeling diode** ($D_F$) — placed across the armature terminals. - **DC source** ($V_s$) — from a rectifier. ### Mode 1: Switch ON (Duration $T_{on}$) - The source $V_s$ is connected directly across the motor armature. - Armature current $I_a$ rises exponentially: $$V_s = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$$ - The freewheeling diode $D_F$ is reverse-biased and does not conduct. ### Mode 2: Switch OFF (Duration $T_{off}$) - The switch disconnects the source. - Motor armature current cannot change instantaneously due to **inductance $L_a$**, so it freewheels through diode $D_F$. - The armature terminals are short-circuited through $D_F$, so $V_a = 0$. - Current decays: $$0 = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Observation:** > In a one-quadrant chopper, the **armature voltage is always positive** ($0 \le V_a \le V_s$) and the **armature current is always positive** ($I_a \ge 0$). Hence, the motor operates **only in Quadrant I** — forward motoring. Reversal of rotation and regenerative braking are **not possible**. ## 2.2 Operational Principle of a Two-Quadrant (Class C) Chopper The two-quadrant chopper (also called a **half-bridge or Class C chopper**) has: - **Two controllable switches** ($S_1$, $S_2$) — typically IGBTs. - **Two freewheeling diodes** ($D_1$, $D_2$). - The motor is connected between the **midpoint of the switches/diodes** and the **DC source**. ### Four Operating Modes: | Mode | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $V_a$ | Operation | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Motoring (Forward)** | ON | OFF | OFF | ON (freewheel) | $+V_s$ / 0 | Quadrant I | | **Braking (Regenerative)** | OFF | ON | ON (freewheel) | OFF | $-V_s$ / 0 | Quadrant II | | **Forward motoring via $D_1$** | OFF | OFF | ON | OFF | $+V_s$ | Energy return | | **Reverse motoring via $D_2$** | OFF | OFF | OFF | ON | $-V_s$ | Energy return | ### Average Output Voltage for Two-Quadrant Chopper: $$V_o = (2\alpha - 1) \cdot V_s$$ where $\alpha$ is the duty cycle of switch $S_1$. - If $\alpha > 0.5$: $V_o > 0$ → **Forward motoring** (Quadrant I). - If $\alpha < 0.5$: $V_o < 0$ → **Reverse motoring / Regenerative braking** (Quadrant II). - If $\alpha = 0.5$: $V_o = 0$ → **Zero voltage loop** (freewheeling). ## 2.3 Speed Control Equations for DC Motor The fundamental speed equation of a DC motor is: $$N = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K\phi}$$ where: - $N$ = speed in RPM (or rad/s) - $V_a$ = average armature voltage - $I_a$ = average armature current - $R_a$ = armature resistance - $K\phi$ = motor constant (flux × constant) The **back EMF** is: $$E_b = K\phi \cdot N = V_a - I_a R_a$$ The **electromagnetic torque** is: $$T_e = K\phi \cdot I_a$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Crucial Insight for PM vs SE Motors:** > - **PM Motor**: $K\phi$ is **fixed** (determined by permanent magnets). Speed control is **only** through $V_a$ variation. > - **SE Motor**: $K\phi$ is **variable** by changing field current $I_f$. Speed can be controlled by either $V_a$ (below base speed) or $\phi$ (above base speed — field weakening). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All key formulas for the lab exam are tabulated below. Memorize these.** | S.No | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\alpha = \frac{T_{on}}{T}$ | Duty cycle (one-quadrant) | dimensionless | | 2 | $V_{o,avg} = \alpha \cdot V_s$ | Average output voltage (1-Q chopper) | Volts (V) | | 3 | $V_{o,avg} = (2\alpha - 1) \cdot V_s$ | Average output voltage (2-Q chopper) | Volts (V) | | 4 | $N = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K\phi}$ | Speed equation of DC motor | RPM or rad/s | | 5 | $E_b = K\phi \cdot N$ | Back EMF equation | Volts (V) | | 6 | $T_e = K\phi \cdot I_a$ | Electromagnetic torque | N·m | | 7 | $\Delta I_a = \frac{V_s - E_b}{L_a} \cdot T_{on}$ | Current rise during $T_{on}$ | Amperes (A) | | 8 | $\Delta I_a = \frac{E_b}{L_a} \cdot T_{off}$ | Current fall during $T_{off}$ | Amperes (A) | | 9 | $I_{a,min} = I_a - \frac{\Delta I_a}{2}$ | Minimum armature current | Amperes (A) | | 10 | $I_{a,max} = I_a + \frac{\Delta I_a}{2}$ | Maximum armature current | Amperes (A) | | 11 | $f = \frac{1}{T}$ | Chopping frequency | Hz | | 12 | $I_{rms} = \sqrt{I_{min}^2 + \frac{(\Delta I_a)^2}{3}}$ | RMS armature current | Amperes (A) | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Chopper Type | Why? | |---|---|---| | **Electric Vehicles (EVs)** | Two-quadrant / Four-quadrant | Regenerative braking recovers kinetic energy | | **Battery-operated forklifts** | One-quadrant | Simple forward drive, battery-fed | | **DC servo drives in CNC machines** | Two-quadrant | Precise speed control with braking | | **Solar PV water pumps** | One-quadrant | Variable solar input → variable speed | | **Subway/Metro traction** | Two/Four-quadrant | Frequent start-stop with regeneration | | **Robotics (DC motor joints)** | One/Two-quadrant | Compact, efficient speed/torque control | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Output Voltage of One-Quadrant Chopper **Problem:** Derive the average output voltage equation for a one-quadrant chopper feeding a DC motor, starting from first principles. **Given:** - DC supply voltage: $V_s$ - Chopper switching period: $T = T_{on} + T_{off}$ - Duty cycle: $\alpha$ **Step 1:** During the ON period $T_{on}$, the output voltage equals the supply voltage. $$v_o(t) = V_s, \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le T_{on}$$ **Step 2:** During the OFF period $T_{off}$, the switch is open and the freewheeling diode conducts, short-circuiting the load. The output voltage is zero. $$v_o(t) = 0, \quad \text{for } T_{on} \le t \le T$$ **Step 3:** The average output voltage over one full switching period is defined as: $$V_{o,avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T v_o(t) \, dt$$ **Step 4:** Substituting the piecewise voltage function and splitting the integral: $$V_{o,avg} = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_0^{T_{on}} V_s \, dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0 \, dt \right]$$ **Step 5:** Evaluating the integrals: $$V_{o,avg} = \frac{1}{T} \left[ V_s \cdot T_{on} + 0 \right] = \frac{V_s \cdot T_{on}}{T}$$ **Step 6:** Recognizing that $\alpha = T_{on}/T$, we get the standard result: $$\boxed{V_{o,avg} = \alpha \cdot V_s}$$ **Step 7 (Physical Interpretation):** As $\alpha$ varies from 0 to 1, $V_{o,avg}$ varies linearly from 0 to $V_s$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Average Output Voltage of Two-Quadrant (Class C) Chopper **Given:** Two switches $S_1$ and $S_2$ operating complementarily in a half-bridge configuration. **Step 1:** When $S_1$ is ON (duration $\alpha T$), current flows from source to motor. $$v_o = +V_s$$ **Step 2:** When $S_2$ is ON (duration $(1-\alpha)T$), the motor terminals are effectively reversed relative to the source. $$v_o = -V_s$$ **Step 3:** Computing the average: $$V_{o,avg} = \frac{1}{T} \left[ V_s \cdot \alpha T + (-V_s)(1-\alpha)T \right]$$ **Step 4:** Simplifying: $$V_{o,avg} = V_s \left[ \alpha - (1-\alpha) \right] = V_s (2\alpha - 1)$$ **Step 5 (Result):** $$\boxed{V_{o,avg} = (2\alpha - 1) \cdot V_s}$$ **Step 6 (Verification):** - At $\alpha = 1$: $V_{o,avg} = V_s$ (full positive). - At $\alpha = 0.5$: $V_{o,avg} = 0$ (zero voltage). - At $\alpha = 0$: $V_{o,avg} = -V_s$ (full negative). This proves the **bidirectional voltage capability** of the two-quadrant chopper. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Steady-State Speed Equation and Torque-Speed Characteristics **Starting from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) on the armature circuit:** $$V_a(t) = E_b(t) + I_a(t) R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a(t)}{dt}$$ **Step 1:** In **steady state**, the average current is constant, so $dI_a/dt = 0$ on average: $$V_{a,avg} = E_b + I_a R_a$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $E_b = K\phi N$: $$V_{a,avg} = K\phi N + I_a R_a$$ **Step 3:** Solve for $N$: $$N = \frac{V_{a,avg} - I_a R_a}{K\phi}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute $V_{a,avg} = \alpha V_s$ (one-quadrant chopper): $$N = \frac{\alpha V_s - I_a R_a}{K\phi}$$ **Step 5:** At **no-load** ($I_a \approx 0$): $$N_0 = \frac{\alpha V_s}{K\phi}$$ **Step 6:** At **stall** ($N = 0$): $$\alpha V_s = I_{stall} R_a \implies I_{stall} = \frac{\alpha V_s}{R_a}$$ **Step 7: Torque-Speed Equation** — Since $T_e = K\phi I_a$, substitute $I_a$ from the speed equation: $$T_e = K\phi \cdot \frac{\alpha V_s - K\phi N}{R_a}$$ **Step 8:** Rearranging into the standard linear form $T = T_{stall} - k \cdot N$: $$T_e = \underbrace{\frac{K\phi \cdot \alpha V_s}{R_a}}_{T_{stall}} - \underbrace{\frac{(K\phi)^2}{R_a}}_{k} \cdot N$$ **Result:** The torque-speed characteristic is a **straight line** with: - **No-load speed** = $\alpha V_s / K\phi$ (varies with duty cycle $\alpha$) - **Slope** = $-(K\phi)^2 / R_a$ (independent of $\alpha$) > [!NOTE] > **Critical Insight:** > As $\alpha$ decreases, the no-load speed decreases, but the **slope of the torque-speed line remains the same**. This means the family of curves at different $\alpha$ values are **parallel lines**, all shifting horizontally. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Current Ripple in Continuous Conduction Mode **Step 1:** During $T_{on}$ (switch closed), the KVL gives: $$V_s = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$$ **Step 2:** Assuming $I_a R_a \ll V_s - E_b$ (ripple is small), the current rise is approximately linear: $$L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} \approx V_s - E_b$$ **Step 3:** Integrating over $T_{on}$: $$\Delta I_a = \frac{(V_s - E_b)}{L_a} \cdot T_{on}$$ **Step 4:** Similarly, during $T_{off}$ (switch open, diode freewheeling), $V_a = 0$: $$0 = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$$ **Step 5:** The current fall is: $$\Delta I_a = \frac{E_b}{L_a} \cdot T_{off}$$ **Step 6:** Equating the two expressions (steady-state requires equal rise and fall): $$\frac{(V_s - E_b)}{L_a} \cdot T_{on} = \frac{E_b}{L_a} \cdot T_{off}$$ **Step 7:** Solving for $E_b$: $$E_b = \alpha V_s$$ This confirms that in **steady-state continuous conduction**, the back EMF equals the **average** applied voltage (neglecting armature drop). **Step 8:** The peak-to-peak ripple current is: $$\Delta I_a = \frac{\alpha (1-\alpha) V_s}{L_a \cdot f}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Maximum ripple** occurs at $\alpha = 0.5$, giving $\Delta I_{a,max} = \frac{V_s}{4 L_a f}$. > > To reduce ripple, **increase chopping frequency** $f$ or **increase armature inductance** $L_a$. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Boundary Conditions for Continuous Conduction | Condition | Equation | Implication | |---|---|---| | **Continuous Conduction** | $I_{a,min} > 0$ | Current never reaches zero; smooth torque | | **Critical (Discontinuous Limit)** | $I_{a,min} = 0$ | Boundary case | | **Discontinuous Conduction** | $I_{a,min} < 0$ (i.e., $I_a$ touches zero) | Current becomes zero during part of cycle | | **Minimum load for CCM** | $I_{a,crit} = \frac{V_s \alpha (1-\alpha)}{2 L_a f}$ | Below this, operation is discontinuous | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** > Students often forget to check the **conduction mode**. If the load is too light or the duty cycle is too low, the motor operates in **discontinuous conduction**, where the simple equations $V_{o,avg} = \alpha V_s$ and $N = \alpha V_s / K\phi$ become **inaccurate** and require modification. --- ## 3.6 Numerical Example (Worked Out) **Problem:** A 220 V, 1500 RPM, 10 A separately excited DC motor has $R_a = 1.0 \, \Omega$ and $L_a = 50 \, mH$. It is fed from a one-quadrant chopper with $V_s = 220 \, V$ and $f = 1 \, kHz$. Find the speed at duty cycle $\alpha = 0.6$ and no-load. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Average output voltage. $$V_{o,avg} = \alpha V_s = 0.6 \times 220 = 132 \, V$$ **Step 2:** Motor constant from rated conditions. $$E_{b,rated} = V_{rated} - I_{rated} R_a = 220 - 10 \times 1.0 = 210 \, V$$ $$K\phi = \frac{E_{b,rated}}{N_{rated}} = \frac{210}{1500} = 0.14 \, V/RPM$$ **Step 3:** No-load speed at $\alpha = 0.6$ (ignoring small $I_a R_a$ drop). $$N = \frac{V_{o,avg}}{K\phi} = \frac{132}{0.14} = 942.86 \, RPM$$ **Step 4 (Verification with full equation):** At no-load, $I_a \approx 0$: $$N_0 = \frac{132 - 0}{0.14} = 942.86 \, RPM$$ **Result:** The motor runs at approximately **943 RPM** at $\alpha = 0.6$ under no-load. > [!NOTE] > This is a **typical KTU 2024 numerical problem** pattern. Practice changing $\alpha$ and observing the speed variation. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Speed Control System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[AC Mains Supply

IGBT or MOSFET] DFW[Freewheeling Diode DF] end subgraph MotorLoad [DC Motor] MA[Armature Inductance La] MR[Armature Resistance Ra] ME[Back EMF Eb] end VSp --> SW1 SW1 --> NODE1 NODE1 --> MA MA --> MR MR --> ME ME --> VSn DFW -.->|Reverse biased during ON| NODE1 DFW -.->|Conducts during OFF| VSn classDef sourceStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef switchStyle fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF classDef motorStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 class VSp,VSn sourceStyle class SW1,DFW switchStyle class MA,MR,ME motorStyle ``` **Operating States:** - **When $S_1$ is ON**: Current path: $V_s^+ \rightarrow S_1 \rightarrow \text{Armature} \rightarrow V_s^-$. Diode $D_F$ is reverse-biased. - **When $S_1$ is OFF**: Current path: Armature $\rightarrow D_F \rightarrow \text{Armature (freewheel)}$. Source is disconnected. --- ## 4.3 Two-Quadrant Chopper Circuit Topology (Class C) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph DCSource [DC Source Vs] VSp[Vs Positive] VSn[Vs Negative] end subgraph Leg1 [Upper Leg] S1[Switch S1] D1[Diode D1] end subgraph Leg2 [Lower Leg] S2[Switch S2] D2[Diode D2] end subgraph MotorLoad [DC Motor] MOT[Armature
La Ra Eb] end VSp --> S1 S1 --> MID[Midpoint M] MID --> MOT MOT --> VSn MID --> S2 S2 --> VSn D1 -.->|Parallel to S1 reversed| VSp D2 -.->|Parallel to S2 reversed| MID classDef powerStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef switchStyle fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF classDef motorStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 class VSp,VSn powerStyle class S1,S2,D1,D2 switchStyle class MOT motorStyle ``` **Operating Modes:** - **S1 ON, S2 OFF**: Current flows from $V_s$ through S1 to motor → **Forward motoring** (Q1). - **S1 OFF, S2 ON**: Source is shorted through S2 (current freewheels), or motor drives current back to source through D1 → **Braking/Regeneration** (Q2). - **Both OFF**: Motor current freewheels through D1 and D2. --- ## 4.4 Operating Waveforms — Timing Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TimeAxis [Time --> end A[Gate Pulse to S1
alpha T on, T off] -->|Triggers| B[Output Voltage Vao
Vs during ON, 0 during OFF] B -->|Causes| C[Armature Current Ia
Rises during ON, Falls during OFF] C -->|Produces| D[Speed N
Increases with Vao] classDef pulseStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000 classDef voltStyle fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef currStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef speedStyle fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 class A pulseStyle class B voltStyle class C currStyle class D speedStyle ``` **Waveform Description (Steady State Continuous Conduction):** 1. **Gate pulse**: Train of pulses with width $\alpha T$ and period $T$. 2. **Output voltage $V_a(t)$**: Square wave — $V_s$ during $T_{on}$, $0$ during $T_{off}$. 3. **Armature current $I_a(t)$**: Ripples between $I_{min}$ and $I_{max}$ around the mean value. **Rises** during $T_{on}$ (exponentially) and **falls** during $T_{off}$ (exponentially). 4. **Speed N**: Slowly varying DC value, follows the average of $V_a$. --- ## 4.5 Torque-Speed Characteristics Family of Curves ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Chopper Output
