With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of a one-quadrant chopper used for speed control of a DC motor.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A DC chopper is a static power electronic switch that converts a fixed DC input voltage to a variable DC output voltage by varying the **duty cycle $\alpha = T_{on}/T$**. **Circuit:** A one-quadrant chopper consists of a controllable switch (IGBT/MOSFET) in series with the DC source and the motor armature, and a **freewheeling diode (DF)** connected across the armature. **Working:** - **Mode 1 (Switch ON, duration $T_{on}$):** Source voltage $V_s$ is applied to the armature. Armature current rises through the switch. The freewheeling diode is reverse-biased. - **Mode 2 (Switch OFF, duration $T_{off}$):** The source is disconnected. The motor inductance forces the current to freewheel through diode $D_F$, which provides a path for the decaying current. The **average armature voltage** is $V_a = \alpha V_s$. By varying $\alpha$ from 0 to 1, the speed of the motor is controlled smoothly below its base speed. Since voltage and current are always unidirectional, the motor operates in **Quadrant I only**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part A):** > Students often forget to mention the **freewheeling diode** and its role. This costs 1 mark. Always draw the diode and explain its function. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Compare one-quadrant and two-quadrant choppers used for DC motor speed control. Mention any two applications of each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Feature | One-Quadrant Chopper | Two-Quadrant Chopper | |---|---|---| | **Switches** | 1 | 2 | | **Diodes** | 1 (freewheeling) | 2 | | **Operating Quadrant** | Quadrant I only | Quadrants I and II | | **Output Voltage Range** | $0$ to $+V_s$ | $-V_s$ to $+V_s$ | | **Average Voltage** | $V_o = \alpha V_s$ | $V_o = (2\alpha - 1) V_s$ | | **Regenerative Braking** | Not possible | Possible | | **Cost** | Lower | Higher | | **Complexity** | Simple | More complex control | **Applications:** - **One-Quadrant:** Battery-operated toys, small DC fans, low-power pumps. - **Two-Quadrant:** Electric vehicles, elevators, hoists, servo drives, CNC machines. --- ## PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### OPTION A: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] #### Question A: **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **two-quadrant (Class C) chopper** for speed control of a separately excited DC motor. Derive the expression for its average output voltage. (7 Marks) **(b)** A 220 V, 1000 RPM, 20 A separately excited DC motor has $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$ and $L_a = 30 \, mH$. It is fed from a one-quadrant chopper with $V_s = 220 \, V$ and switching frequency $f = 500 \, Hz$. Calculate the **no-load speed** and **peak-to-peak armature current ripple** at $\alpha = 0.4$. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** **Circuit Description (2 marks):** A Class C two-quadrant chopper consists of two controllable switches $S_1$ and $S_2$ (IGBTs) and two freewheeling diodes $D_1$ and $D_2$ arranged in a half-bridge configuration. The motor armature is connected between the midpoint of the bridge and the negative terminal of the source. **Modes of Operation (3 marks):** - **Mode 1 (S1 ON, S2 OFF):** The source $V_s$ drives the motor current through $S_1$. The motor receives $+V_s$ and operates in **forward motoring (Quadrant I)**. - **Mode 2 (S1 OFF, S2 ON):** The source is shorted through $S_2$, providing a freewheel path. The motor armature voltage is $0$, but the current continues to flow. - **Mode 3 (S1 OFF, S2 OFF, D1 conducts):** When the motor acts as a generator (during braking), the back EMF $E_b$ drives current back to the source through diode $D_1$. The armature voltage is effectively $+V_s$ from the source side, but the motor EMF opposes it — this is **regenerative braking (Quadrant II)**. **Derivation (2 marks):** When $S_1$ is ON for time $\alpha T$, $v_o = +V_s$. When $S_2$ is ON for time $(1-\alpha)T$, $v_o = -V_s$. $$V_{o,avg} = \frac{1}{T}\left[V_s \cdot \alpha T + (-V_s)(1-\alpha)T\right] = (2\alpha - 1)V_s$$ --- **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **Step 1: Calculate motor constant $K\phi$.** At rated conditions, back EMF: $$E_{b,rated} = V - I_a R_a = 220 - 20 \times 0.5 = 210 \, V$$ Motor constant: $$K\phi = \frac{E_{b,rated}}{N_{rated}} = \frac{210}{1000} = 0.21 \, V/RPM$$ **Step 2: Average output voltage at $\alpha = 0.4$.** $$V_{a,avg} = \alpha V_s = 0.4 \times 220 = 88 \, V$$ **Step 3: No-load speed (assuming $I_a \approx 0$ at no-load).** $$N_0 = \frac{V_{a,avg}}{K\phi} = \frac{88}{0.21} = 419.05 \, RPM$$ **Step 4: Back EMF at no-load** (since $I_a \approx 0$, $E_b \approx V_{a,avg}$). $$E_b = 88 \, V$$ **Step 5: Current ripple calculation.** $$T_{on} = \frac{\alpha}{f} = \frac{0.4}{500} = 0.8 \times 10^{-3} \, s = 0.8 \, ms$$ $$T_{off} = \frac{1-\alpha}{f} = \frac{0.6}{500} = 1.2 \, ms$$ Current rise during $T_{on}$: $$\Delta I_a = \frac{(V_s - E_b) \cdot T_{on}}{L_a} = \frac{(220 - 88) \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{30 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$\Delta I_a = \frac{132 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{30 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{105.6 \times 10^{-3}}{30 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.52 \, A$$ **Step 6: Final Results.** - **No-load speed:** $N_0 \approx 419 \, RPM$ - **Peak-to-peak ripple:** $\Delta I_a \approx 3.52 \, A$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):** > > 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students write $K\phi$ in V·s/rad (SI) but use it with N in RPM without conversion. Either use **consistent units** or check whether the formula expects RPM or rad/s. > > 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting that in the no-load condition, $I_a R_a$ is small but **not exactly zero**. For more accuracy, include a small no-load current (5% of rated) in the calculation. > > 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing $T_{on}$ and $T_{off}$ in the ripple formula. The rise happens during $T_{on}$ and fall during $T_{off}$. > > 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Not specifying **units clearly** in the final answer — this loses 1 mark. --- ### OPTION B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] #### Question B: **(a)** Explain the **four-quadrant operation** of a DC motor drive using choppers, with the help of circuit diagrams. Why is a two-quadrant chopper insufficient for full four-quadrant operation? (7 Marks) **(b)** A permanent magnet DC motor has the following ratings: 48 V, 3000 RPM, $R_a = 0.2 \, \Omega$, $K\phi = 0.015 \, V/(RPM)$. It is controlled by a one-quadrant chopper from a 48 V DC source. Find (i) the **motor speed** at $\alpha = 0.7$ with a load torque of 1.5 N·m, and (ii) the **duty cycle** required to run the motor at 1500 RPM under the same load. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** **Four-Quadrant Operation (5 marks):** A DC motor drive can operate in four modes depending on the direction of rotation (forward/reverse) and the nature of torque (motoring/braking): - **Quadrant I (Forward Motoring):** $V_a > 0$, $I_a > 0$, $N > 0$, $T > 0$. - **Quadrant II (Forward Braking/Regeneration):** $V_a < 0$, $I_a > 0$, $N > 0$, $T < 0$. - **Quadrant III (Reverse Motoring):** $V_a < 0$, $I_a < 0$, $N < 0$, $T < 0$. - **Quadrant IV (Reverse Braking/Regeneration):** $V_a > 0$, $I_a < 0$, $N < 0$, $T > 0$. A **full H-bridge (four-quadrant) chopper** with 4 switches and 4 diodes is required. Different switch combinations give different quadrants: - **S1, S4 ON** → Quadrant I (forward motoring). - **S2, S3 ON** → Quadrant III (reverse motoring). - **S1, S2 ON (with D2, D3 freewheeling)** → Quadrant II (forward braking). - **S3, S4 ON (with D1, D4 freewheeling)** → Quadrant IV (reverse braking). **Why Two-Quadrant is Insufficient (2 marks):** A two-quadrant (Class C) chopper produces voltages only in the range $-V_s$ to $+V_s$, but the current direction is **fixed** (one polarity). It can operate in Quadrants I and II, but cannot reverse the current direction, hence **cannot operate in Quadrants III and IV**. To achieve full four-quadrant control, an H-bridge with four switches is mandatory. --- **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **Step 1: Compute no-load back EMF constant.** $$K\phi = 0.015 \, V/RPM$$ **Step 2: Compute armature current for the given load torque.** $$T_e = K\phi \cdot I_a$$ $$I_a = \frac{T_e}{K\phi} = \frac{1.5}{0.015} = 100 \, A$$ **Part (b)(i): Speed at $\alpha = 0.7$.** **Step 3:** Average output voltage. $$V_{a,avg} = \alpha V_s = 0.7 \times 48 = 33.6 \, V$$ **Step 4:** Back EMF from KVL. $$E_b = V_{a,avg} - I_a R_a = 33.6 - 100 \times 0.2 = 33.6 - 20 = 13.6 \, V$$ **Step 5:** Motor speed. $$N = \frac{E_b}{K\phi} = \frac{13.6}{0.015} = 906.67 \, RPM$$ **Part (b)(ii): Duty cycle for N = 1500 RPM.** **Step 6:** Required back EMF. $$E_b = K\phi \cdot N = 0.015 \times 1500 = 22.5 \, V$$ **Step 7:** Required armature voltage. $$V_{a,avg} = E_b + I_a R_a = 22.5 + 100 \times 0.2 = 22.5 + 20 = 42.5 \, V$$ **Step 8:** Required duty cycle. $$\alpha = \frac{V_{a,avg}}{V_s} = \frac{42.5}{48} = 0.885$$ **Final Results:** - (i) Speed at $\alpha = 0.7$: $N \approx 907 \, RPM$ - (ii) Required duty cycle for 1500 RPM: $\alpha \approx 0.885$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B Option B):** > > 1. **Units of $K\phi$:** In this problem $K\phi$ is given as **V/RPM** — use it directly with N in RPM. Do not convert to SI without proper conversion factor. > > 2. **Torque-Current Relationship:** Many students confuse $T = K\phi I_a$ with $T = K\phi / I_a$. This is a fundamental error costing 2-3 marks. > > 3. **No-load assumption:** The problem specifies a **load torque** of 1.5 N·m, so you **cannot** use the no-load formula. The full speed equation $N = (V_{a,avg} - I_a R_a)/K\phi$ must be used. > > 4. **Two-quadrant insufficiency explanation:** Just saying "it cannot reverse" is not enough. You must mention the **current direction limitation** and specify which quadrants are covered. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **HIGH-FREQUENCY MARKS-LOSING MISTAKES (KTU 2024 Pattern):** > > 1. **Forgetting the Freewheeling Diode (FDM):** FDM is **mandatory** in one-quadrant choppers. Omitting it costs 1-2 marks. > > 2. **Confusing $T_{on}$ and $T_{off}$:** Always clarify: $T_{on}$ = switch closed; $T_{off}$ = switch open. The current **rises** during $T_{on}$ and **falls** during $T_{off}$. > > 3. **Not distinguishing 1-Q vs 2-Q choppers:** A 2-Q chopper has **two switches**, not one. The output voltage formula is $V_o = (2\alpha - 1)V_s$, NOT $\alpha V_s$. > > 4. **Mixing up PM and SE motors:** PM motors have **fixed flux** — no field winding. SE motors have **variable flux** through separate field control. > > 5. **Units inconsistency:** $K\phi$ in V/RPM vs V·s/rad — choose one and stick with it. > > 6. **Forgetting Continuous vs Discontinuous Conduction:** Always verify that the assumed mode (CCM) holds for the given load and duty cycle. > > 7. **Not showing intermediate steps:** KTU examiners award **step marks**. Even if the final answer is wrong, showing correct methodology can earn 4-5 out of 7 marks. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST:** ### Core Concepts - [ ] **DC Chopper** = Static DC-to-DC switch-mode converter using PWM. - [ ] **Duty cycle** $\alpha = T_{on}/T$; varies from 0 to 1. - [ ] **One-quadrant chopper:** $V_o = \alpha V_s$, Quadrant I only, has 1 switch + 1 freewheeling diode. - [ ] **Two-quadrant (Class C) chopper:** $V_o = (2\alpha - 1)V_s$, Quadrants I & II, has 2 switches + 2 diodes. - [ ] **Four-quadrant chopper:** H-bridge with 4 switches, all 4 quadrants possible. ### Speed Control Equations - [ ] $N = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K\phi}$ (master speed equation). - [ ] $E_b = K\phi N$ (back EMF). - [ ] $T_e = K\phi I_a$ (torque). - [ ] At no-load: $N_0 = \alpha V_s / K\phi$. - [ ] PM motor: flux is **fixed**; SE motor: flux is **variable** via $I_f$. ### Ripple and Conduction - [ ] Current rise: $\Delta I_a = (V_s - E_b) T_{on} / L_a$. - [ ] Current fall: $\Delta I_a = E_b \cdot T_{off} / L_a$. - [ ] Maximum ripple at $\alpha = 0.5$. - [ ] Higher $f$ → lower ripple; higher $L_a$ → lower ripple. - [ ] **CCM (Continuous Conduction Mode):** $I_{min} > 0$. - [ ] **DCM (Discontinuous Conduction Mode):** Current touches zero. ### Practical Points - [ ] Switching frequency: **1-20 kHz** (above audible range preferred). - [ ] Typical IGBT/MOSFET used as chopper switch. - [ ] Freewheeling diode is **fast-recovery type**. - [ ] Chopper is fed from a **rectifier-DC link** (e.g., diode bridge + capacitor). - [ ] Closed-loop control uses **tachogenerator or encoder** for speed feedback. ### Applications to Remember - [ ] **One-quadrant:** DC fans, pumps, toys. - [ ] **Two-quadrant:** Electric vehicles, elevators, hoists, servo drives. - [ ] **Four-quadrant:** CNC machines, robotics, full reversible drives. ### KTU Exam Strategy - [ ] Always **draw circuit diagrams** with proper labeling. - [ ] **Show all intermediate steps** — KTU gives step marks. - [ ] State **assumptions** clearly (e.g., continuous conduction, no-load). - [ ] Mention **units** in the final answer. - [ ] For numerical problems, **box the final answer** for visibility.

topics.title: Three-Phase IGBT/MOSFET Inverter Feeding RL Load with Sine-Triangle PWM and Third-Harmonic Injection PWM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOL507 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Three-Phase IGBT/MOSFET Inverter Feeding RL Load with Sine-Triangle PWM and Third-Harmonic Injection PWM ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Three-Phase IGBT/MOSFET Inverter A **Three-Phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** is a power electronic converter that converts a fixed DC input voltage into a three-phase AC output voltage of variable magnitude and frequency. The switching devices used are **Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistors (IGBTs)** or **Metal Oxide Semiconductor Field Effect Transistors (MOSFETs)**, depending on the power level. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A three-phase VSI consists of **three legs** (phases), each having **two controlled switches** (with anti-parallel diodes), making **six switches** total. The switches in each leg operate in a complementary fashion to avoid a short circuit of the DC bus. ### 1.2 The RL Load The **RL load** (Resistive-Inductive) is the most common type of load in industrial drives, comprising resistance (R) and inductance (L) in series. Induction motors, transformers, and reactor-loaded systems all exhibit RL behavior. - **Resistance (R):** Dissipates real power, contributes to the in-phase component of current. - **Inductance (L):** Stores magnetic energy, causes the current to **lag** the voltage. - **Power Factor:** Always **lagging** (less than unity), typically between **0.7 and 0.85**. > [!NOTE] > **Why RL loads matter in Power Electronics:** The inductive nature of the load forces the current to be **continuous** and **smooth**, which filters out high-frequency switching harmonics and produces a near-sinusoidal current waveform even with a stepped voltage output. ### 1.3 Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM) **Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)** is a modulation technique where a **sinusoidal reference wave** of desired output frequency is compared against a **high-frequency triangular carrier wave** to generate the gating pulses for the inverter switches. ### 1.4 Third-Harmonic Injection PWM (THIPWM) **Third-Harmonic Injection PWM (THIPWM)** is an advanced modulation technique where a **third-harmonic component (of the fundamental reference) is injected** into the sinusoidal reference signal before comparison with the carrier. This boosts the maximum achievable linear modulation index from **1.0 to 1.155** (i.e., $2/\sqrt{3}$). ### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — The "DJ Mixer" Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a DJ at a music console trying to deliver smooth music (pure sine wave) using a set of six drum beats (the six switches). SPWM is like asking the DJ to mimic the music using the beats as they are — there's a ceiling. **Third-harmonic injection** is like secretly adding a tiny "echo" at three times the main tune's frequency to the drums, allowing the DJ to push the volume higher without distortion. The ear (or the motor) does not "hear" the third harmonic in a three-phase system because it cancels out across the phases! --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Principle of the Three-Phase VSI The structure uses **six switches** ($S_1$ to $S_6$) arranged in **three half-bridge legs**: - **Leg A:** $S_1$ (top) and $S_4$ (bottom) - **Leg B:** $S_3$ (top) and $S_6$ (bottom) - **Leg C:** $S_5$ (top) and $S_2$ (bottom) Each leg produces a switched DC waveform of magnitude $\pm V_{dc}/2$ at the phase output relative to the **DC bus midpoint**. ### 2.2 Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) Operation — The "Why" and "How" **Why use SPWM?** - To synthesize a sinusoidal output voltage from a DC source. - To control the **fundamental amplitude** via the modulation index. - To place the dominant switching harmonics at **high frequencies** (multiples of $f_{carrier}$), which are easier to filter. **How does it work?** 1. Generate three sinusoidal reference signals: $V_{ra}$, $V_{rb}$, $V_{rc}$, each displaced by **120°**. 2. Generate a single high-frequency triangular carrier wave $V_{tri}$. 3. For each leg: switch ON the upper device if $V_{ref} > V_{tri}$; switch ON the lower device if $V_{ref} < V_{tri}$. 4. The result is a pulse train of varying width that follows the sine envelope. ### 2.3 Third-Harmonic Injection PWM (THIPWM) — The Concept **Why inject a third harmonic?** - In a **balanced three-phase system**, the third harmonic and all its odd multiples (triplen harmonics: 3rd, 9th, 15th...) sum to **zero** in the line-to-line voltage because they are co-phasal in the phase voltages. - We can **add a third harmonic to each phase** without affecting the line-to-line voltage. - This allows the **peak of the phase voltage to be reduced** for the same DC link, while the **fundamental is increased**. **How is the third harmonic injected?** The modified reference signal becomes: $$V_{ref,TH} = M \left[ \sin(\omega t) + \frac{1}{6}\sin(3\omega t) \right]$$ - The amplitude of the injected 3rd harmonic is **1/6 of the fundamental** (this is the optimal value). - This produces a "flat-topped" reference wave that has a **lower peak** than a pure sine, allowing $M > 1$. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Typical Value/Range** | |---|---|---|---| | Modulation Index (Amplitude) | $m_a = \dfrac{V_{ref,peak}}{V_{carrier,peak}}$ | Ratio of reference to carrier peak | SPWM: $m_a \leq 1.0$
THIPWM: $m_a \leq 1.155$ | | Frequency Modulation Ratio | $m_f = \dfrac{f_{carrier}}{f_{reference}}$ | Ratio of carrier to fundamental frequency | Odd integer, typically **15, 21, 39, 75** | | Maximum Linear Modulation (SPWM) | $m_{a,max} = 1.0$ | Limits fundamental to $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_{dc}$ | $0.866 \, V_{dc}$ | | Maximum Linear Modulation (THIPWM) | $m_{a,max} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155$ | Limits fundamental to $V_{dc}$ | $1.0 \, V_{dc}$ | | Peak Fundamental Phase Voltage | $V_{ph,peak} = m_a \cdot \dfrac{V_{dc}}{2}$ | RMS of fundamental phase voltage | Per phase | | Line-to-Line RMS (Fundamental) | $V_{LL,rms} = m_a \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot V_{dc}$ | For SPWM, linear region | $0.612 \, m_a \cdot V_{dc}$ | | Optimal 3rd Harm. Amplitude | $\dfrac{1}{6} V_{ref,peak}$ | Flat-top reference condition | $0.167$ of fundamental | | THD Definition | $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_{rms}^2 - V_1^2}}{V_1}$ | Total Harmonic Distortion | Typically **5–10%** with RL load | | RL Load Impedance | $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2}$ | Magnitude of load impedance | Frequency dependent | | Load Current Phase Angle | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\omega L}{R}\right)$ | Lag angle of current w.r.t. voltage | 0 to 90° | | Dominant Harmonic Frequency | $f_h = m_f \cdot f_1$ | First set of sideband harmonics | 50 Hz × 15 = 750 Hz | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL PITFALL:** In KTU valuation, students often confuse $m_a$ and $m_f$. Remember: $m_a$ controls the **amplitude (voltage)**, and $m_f$ controls the **frequency/spectrum of harmonics**. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial AC Motor Drives:** SPWM and THIPWM are the backbone of variable frequency drives (VFDs) controlling induction motors. - **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS):** Used to generate clean, low-THD sinusoidal output. - **Solar Inverters (PV Systems):** Convert DC from solar panels to grid-quality AC. - **Active Power Filters:** Use PWM techniques to synthesize compensating currents. - **Electric Vehicle Traction:** IGBT-based three-phase inverters drive the EV motors. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Maximum Linear Modulation Index for THIPWM **Step 1 — Start with the modified reference signal (per phase):** $$V_{ref}(t) = M \left[ \sin(\omega t) + k \cdot \sin(3\omega t) \right]$$ where $k$ is the ratio of the third-harmonic amplitude to the fundamental amplitude. **Step 2 — Condition to maximize linear modulation:** For linear operation, the **peak of $V_{ref}(t)$ must not exceed $V_{carrier,peak}$**. The peak occurs where $\dfrac{dV_{ref}}{dt} = 0$: $$\frac{dV_{ref}}{dt} = M \left[ \omega \cos(\omega t) + 3 k \omega \cos(3\omega t) \right] = 0$$ Therefore: $$\cos(\omega t) = -3k \cdot \cos(3\omega t)$$ **Step 3 — Use triple-angle identity to simplify:** $$\cos(3\omega t) = 4\cos^3(\omega t) - 3\cos(\omega t)$$ Substituting: $$\cos(\omega t) = -3k \left[ 4\cos^3(\omega t) - 3\cos(\omega t) \right]$$ $$\cos(\omega t) = -12k\cos^3(\omega t) + 9k\cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 4 — Collect terms and solve for $k$:** Let $c = \cos(\omega t)$: $$c = -12k c^3 + 9k c$$ $$1 = -12k c^2 + 9k$$ $$12k c^2 = 9k - 1$$ $$c^2 = \frac{9k - 1}{12k}$$ **Step 5 — Find the optimal $k$ that minimizes the peak (flattens the reference):** To find the optimal point, we differentiate the peak $V_{ref,peak}$ w.r.t. $k$ and set to zero. The well-known result (from standard texts) is: $$k = \frac{1}{6}$$ **Step 6 — Verify by computing the peak with $k = 1/6$:** Substituting $k = 1/6$: $$c^2 = \frac{9 \cdot (1/6) - 1}{12 \cdot (1/6)} = \frac{1.5 - 1}{2} = \frac{0.5}{2} = 0.25$$ $$c = \pm 0.5 \implies \cos(\omega t) = 0.5 \implies \omega t = 60°$$ **Step 7 — Calculate the peak value:** $$V_{ref,peak} = M \left[ \sin(60°) + \frac{1}{6}\sin(180°) \right] = M \left[ \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + 0 \right] = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} M$$ **Step 8 — Apply linear modulation condition:** For linear modulation: $V_{ref,peak} \leq V_{carrier,peak} = 1$ $$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} M \leq 1$$ $$M \leq \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} = 1.1547$$ **Conclusion:** $$\boxed{M_{max, THIPWM} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155}$$ This is a **15.5% improvement** over pure SPWM ($M_{max} = 1.0$). ### 3.2 Derivation of RMS Line-to-Line Voltage for SPWM (Linear Region) **Step 1 — The fundamental phase voltage (peak) in linear region:** $$V_{ph,1,peak} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Convert to RMS:** $$V_{ph,1,rms} = \frac{m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}$$ **Step 3 — Convert phase to line-to-line (multiply by $\sqrt{3}$ for sinusoidal fundamental):** $$V_{LL,1,rms} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph,1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical example** (for $V_{dc} = 400$ V, $m_a = 0.9$): $$V_{LL,1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \times 0.9 \times 400}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1.732 \times 0.9 \times 400}{2.828} \approx 220.4 \text{ V}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation of Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) **Step 1 — RMS of total output voltage:** $$V_{rms,total} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \dots + V_n^2}$$ **Step 2 — RMS of fundamental only:** $$V_{1,rms} = \frac{V_{ph,1,peak}}{\sqrt{2}}$$ **Step 3 — THD formula:** $$THD = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_1} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{rms,total}^2 - V_1^2}}{V_1}$$ **Step 4 — For SPWM with RL load (typical empirical result):** With $m_f = 15$ and RL filtering, the THD typically falls to **3–8%**, compared to square-wave operation where THD is approximately **31%**. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | **Parameter** | **Pure SPWM** | **THIPWM** | |---|---|---| | Reference Signal | Pure Sine | Sine + 1/6 × 3rd Harmonic | | Peak Reference | 1.0 (at $M=1$) | 0.866 (at $M=1.155$) | | Max Linear $m_a$ | 1.0 | 1.155 | | Max Fundamental Phase Voltage | $0.5 \, V_{dc}$ | $0.577 \, V_{dc}$ | | DC Bus Utilization | 86.6% | **100%** | | Switching Loss | Same | Same | | THD of Output | Slightly Higher | **Slightly Lower** | | Implementation Complexity | Simple | Moderate | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Three-Phase VSI Power Circuit Topology ```mermaid graph TB DCp[DC Bus Positive Vdc]:::pos DCn[DC Bus Negative 0V]:::neg subgraph LegA[Phase A Leg] S1[IGBT S1]:::sw S4[IGBT S4]:::sw Da1[Diode D1]:::diode Da4[Diode D4]:::diode end subgraph LegB[Phase B Leg] S3[IGBT S3]:::sw S6[IGBT S6]:::sw Db3[Diode D3]:::diode Db6[Diode D6]:::diode end subgraph LegC[Phase C Leg] S5[IGBT S5]:::sw S2[IGBT S2]:::sw Dc5[Diode D5]:::diode Dc2[Diode D2]:::diode end PhA[Phase A Output]:::out PhB[Phase B Output]:::out PhC[Phase C Output]:::out DCp --> S1 S1 --> PhA S4 --> DCn S1 -.->|D1| S4 DCp --> S3 S3 --> PhB S6 --> DCn S3 -.->|D3| S6 DCp --> S5 S5 --> PhC S2 --> DCn S5 -.->|D5| S2 classDef pos fill:#ffeb99,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef neg fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sw fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef diode fill:#ffb3ba,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef out fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram of PWM Generation & Control Strategy ```mermaid graph LR subgraph RefGen[Reference Signal Generator] Sine1[Sinusoid 50Hz 0deg]:::ref Sine2[Sinusoid 50Hz 120deg]:::ref Sine3[Sinusoid 50Hz 240deg]:::ref HarmGen[Third Harmonic Generator 150Hz]:::harmonic end Adder1[Summing Junction Phase A]:::add Adder2[Summing Junction Phase B]:::add Adder3[Summing Junction Phase C]:::add Carrier[Triangular Carrier Wave 750Hz]:::carrier CompA[Comparator Phase A]:::comp CompB[Comparator Phase B]:::comp CompC[Comparator Phase C]:::comp GateDrv[Gate Driver Circuit]:::drv Inverter[Three Phase IGBT Inverter]:::inv RLLoad[RL Load]:::load Sine1 --> Adder1 HarmGen --> Adder1 Sine2 --> Adder2 HarmGen --> Adder2 Sine3 --> Adder3 HarmGen --> Adder3 Carrier --> CompA Carrier --> CompB Carrier --> CompC Adder1 --> CompA Adder2 --> CompB Adder3 --> CompC CompA --> GateDrv CompB --> GateDrv CompC --> GateDrv GateDrv --> Inverter Inverter --> RLLoad classDef ref fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef harmonic fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef add fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef carrier fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef comp fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef drv fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef inv fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef load fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 PWM Switching Logic (Decision Flow) ```mermaid graph TD Start([Start of Switching Cycle]):::startnode Sample[Sample Reference Signal Vref]:::process Sample2[Sample Carrier Signal Vtri]:::process Compare{Vref more than Vtri?}:::decision SwitchUpper[Turn ON Upper IGBT of Leg]:::upper SwitchLower[Turn ON Lower IGBT of Leg]:::lower Apply[Apply Voltage Vdc or 0 to Phase]:::apply NextCycle[Wait for Next Carrier Cycle]:::wait End([Loop]):::endnode Start --> Sample Sample --> Sample2 Sample2 --> Compare Compare -->|YES| SwitchUpper Compare -->|NO| SwitchLower SwitchUpper --> Apply SwitchLower --> Apply Apply --> NextCycle NextCycle --> End classDef startnode fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef endnode fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef process fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef upper fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef lower fill:#ffab91,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef apply fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef wait fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Waveform Reference (Description) The actual waveforms produced are: - **Three reference sine waves** displaced by 120° (modified with 1/6 of third harmonic in THIPWM). - **One triangular carrier** wave. - **Six gate signals** (one for each IGBT). - **Three phase output voltages** ($V_{aN}$, $V_{bN}$, $V_{cN}$) — each is a bipolar PWM waveform switching between $+V_{dc}/2$ and $-V_{dc}/2$. - **Three line-to-line voltages** ($V_{ab}$, $V_{bc}$, $V_{ca}$) — these are 5-level PWM waveforms. - **Three phase currents** — due to RL load, they are smooth and approximately sinusoidal. > [!TIP] > **For the KTU Lab Record:** Plot the gate pulses, the phase voltage (PWM), the line-to-line voltage, and the load current. Show that as $m_a$ increases, the fundamental current amplitude increases, and as $m_f$ increases, the current ripple decreases. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define modulation index ($m_a$) and frequency modulation ratio ($m_f$) in SPWM. Why is $m_f$ chosen as an odd integer in three-phase inverters?** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > - **Modulation Index $m_a$:** It is the ratio of the peak amplitude of the reference (control) signal to the peak amplitude of the triangular carrier signal: $m_a = V_{ref,peak}/V_{carrier,peak}$. It controls the **fundamental output voltage magnitude** of the inverter. **(1 mark)** > - **Frequency Modulation Ratio $m_f$:** It is the ratio of the frequency of the triangular carrier to the frequency of the fundamental reference signal: $m_f = f_{carrier}/f_{reference}$. It controls the **harmonic spectrum** of the output. **(1 mark)** > - **Why odd integer?** In three-phase inverters, choosing $m_f$ as an odd multiple of 3 (i.e., 3, 9, 15, 21...) ensures that the **phase voltage harmonics are symmetric** and the **line-to-line voltage has only odd non-triplen harmonics**, eliminating even harmonics and DC components. **(1 mark)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Sample]** **What is third-harmonic injection PWM (THIPWM)? State the optimal amplitude of the third harmonic that is injected.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > - **Definition:** THIPWM is a modified SPWM technique in which a **third-harmonic component (3ω)** of the fundamental reference is added to the sinusoidal reference before comparison with the carrier. **(1.5 marks)** > - **Optimal amplitude:** The amplitude of the injected third harmonic is **1/6 (≈ 0.167) of the fundamental amplitude**. **(1 mark)** > - **Benefit:** This flattens the reference wave's peak, allowing the modulation index to be increased up to **$2/\sqrt{3} \approx 1.155$** while keeping the line-to-line voltage distortion-free. **(0.5 mark)** --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Past Paper Style]** **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **three-phase IGBT-based voltage source inverter** feeding a balanced RL load. Define the switching states and draw the phase voltage waveform for the **180° conduction mode (square-wave operation)**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the same inverter, explain **Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)** technique. Compare SPWM and square-wave operation in terms of **harmonic content, fundamental output voltage, and switching losses**. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Three-Phase IGBT VSI Operation (7 marks):** **Circuit Description:** The inverter consists of **6 IGBT switches** ($S_1$ to $S_6$) with **6 anti-parallel diodes** ($D_1$ to $D_6$), arranged as **3 legs** connected to a DC source of magnitude $V_{dc}$. Each leg has a top and bottom switch; they operate **complementarily** (if top is ON, bottom is OFF and vice versa). The output of each leg is connected to a phase of the RL load: $V_{aN}$ for Leg A, $V_{bN}$ for Leg B, $V_{cN}$ for Leg C. **(2 marks)** **Switching Sequence for 180° Conduction:** Each switch conducts for **180°** of the fundamental cycle. The conduction sequence is $S_1 \to S_3 \to S_5 \to S_4 \to S_6 \to S_2 \to S_1$, with a 60° gap between each switch activation. At any instant, **3 switches conduct** (one from each leg, all top or 2 top + 1 bottom, etc.). **(2 marks)** **Phase Voltage Waveform (180° mode):** The phase voltage is a **quasi-square wave** with magnitude $+V_{dc}/2$ for 120° and $-V_{dc}/2$ for 120° (with a 60° zero interval in between). The fundamental RMS is $V_{ph,1,rms} = 0.45 V_{dc}$. **(1.5 marks)** **Switching States (8 possible states, of which 6 are active and 2 are zero):** - Active states: $S_1 S_3 S_5$, $S_1 S_3 S_2$, $S_4 S_3 S_2$, $S_4 S_6 S_2$, $S_4 S_6 S_5$, $S_1 S_6 S_5$ - Zero states: All top ON ($S_1 S_3 S_5$), all bottom ON ($S_4 S_6 S_2$) **(1.5 marks)** --- **(b) SPWM Technique and Comparison (7 marks):** **SPWM Explanation (3.5 marks):** In SPWM, three **sinusoidal reference signals** ($V_{ra}$, $V_{rb}$, $V_{rc}$ displaced by 120°) are compared with a **common high-frequency triangular carrier** $V_{tri}$. The output of each comparator controls the switches of one leg: - If $V_{ref} > V_{tri}$: Upper switch ($S_1, S_3, S_5$) is ON - If $V_{ref} < V_{tri}$: Lower switch ($S_4, S_6, S_2$) is ON **Parameters:** - **Modulation index $m_a = V_{ref,peak}/V_{tri,peak}$** controls output voltage. - **Frequency ratio $m_f = f_{tri}/f_{ref}$** controls harmonic content (typically $m_f = 15$ to 75 for low-power applications). **Comparison Table (3.5 marks):** | **Parameter** | **180° Square Wave** | **SPWM** | |---|---|---| | Fundamental Voltage | $0.45 V_{dc}$ (fixed) | Variable: $0 \to 0.45 V_{dc}$ | | Harmonic Content | High (dominant 5th, 7th) | Lower (dominant at $m_f$) | | Dominant Harmonic Order | 5, 7, 11, 13 | Sidebands around $m_f$ | | THD of Output Voltage | ~31% | ~5–10% | | Switching Frequency | Once per cycle | High ($m_f \times 50$ Hz) | | Switching Losses | Low | High | | Filter Requirements | Large L-C filter | Smaller L filter (with RL) | | Control of Magnitude | Not possible (fixed) | Smooth via $m_a$ | --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Model Paper Style]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **maximum linear modulation index** in Third-Harmonic Injection PWM (THIPWM). Show that it can be extended to $2/\sqrt{3}$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An RL load with $R = 10 \, \Omega$ and $L = 30$ mH is connected to a three-phase VSI with $V_{dc} = 400$ V. The inverter is operated with **THIPWM at $m_a = 1.0$, $m_f = 15$, and $f_1 = 50$ Hz**. Calculate: (i) The fundamental RMS line-to-line voltage. (ii) The fundamental RMS phase voltage. (iii) The load impedance at the fundamental frequency. (iv) The fundamental RMS phase current. (v) The phase angle of the load. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Derivation of Maximum Modulation Index (7 marks):** The modified reference signal (per phase) with third harmonic injection is: $$V_{ref}(t) = M \left[ \sin(\omega t) + k \cdot \sin(3\omega t) \right]$$ For the **optimal flat-top** condition, the peak should be minimized. Differentiating and setting to zero: $$\frac{dV_{ref}}{dt} = M\omega \left[ \cos(\omega t) + 3k \cos(3\omega t) \right] = 0$$ Using the triple-angle identity $\cos(3\theta) = 4\cos^3\theta - 3\cos\theta$: $$\cos(\omega t) + 3k[4\cos^3(\omega t) - 3\cos(\omega t)] = 0$$ Let $c = \cos(\omega t)$: $$c + 12kc^3 - 9kc = 0 \implies c(1 - 9k + 12kc^2) = 0$$ Solving yields the optimum at $k = 1/6$ and $\omega t = 60°$. **Peak value at this optimum:** $$V_{ref,peak} = M \left[ \sin(60°) + \frac{1}{6}\sin(180°) \right] = M \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$ **Linear modulation requires** $V_{ref,peak} \leq 1$ (i.e., $\leq V_{carrier,peak}$): $$M \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \leq 1 \implies M \leq \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} = 1.1547$$ $$\boxed{M_{max, THIPWM} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155}$$ This is a **15.47% improvement** over SPWM. **(Full 7 marks with all steps shown)** --- **(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):** **Given:** - $R = 10 \, \Omega$, $L = 30 \, \text{mH} = 0.030 \, \text{H}$ - $V_{dc} = 400 \, \text{V}$ - $m_a = 1.0$, $m_f = 15$, $f_1 = 50 \, \text{Hz}$ **(i) Fundamental RMS line-to-line voltage:** **(1.5 marks)** $$V_{LL,1,rms} = m_a \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot V_{dc}$$ $$V_{LL,1,rms} = 1.0 \times \frac{1.732}{2.828} \times 400 = 0.6124 \times 400 = 244.95 \, \text{V}$$ $$\boxed{V_{LL,1,rms} \approx 245 \, \text{V}}$$ **(ii) Fundamental RMS phase voltage:** **(1 mark)** $$V_{ph,1,rms} = \frac{V_{LL,1,rms}}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{244.95}{1.732}$$ $$\boxed{V_{ph,1,rms} \approx 141.4 \, \text{V}}$$ **(iii) Load impedance at fundamental frequency:** **(1.5 marks)** Inductive reactance: $$X_L = 2\pi f_1 L = 2\pi \times 50 \times 0.030 = 9.4248 \, \Omega$$ Impedance: $$Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2} = \sqrt{10^2 + 9.4248^2} = \sqrt{100 + 88.83} = \sqrt{188.83}$$ $$\boxed{Z \approx 13.74 \, \Omega}$$ **(iv) Fundamental RMS phase current:** **(1.5 marks)** $$I_{ph,1,rms} = \frac{V_{ph,1,rms}}{Z} = \frac{141.4}{13.74}$$ $$\boxed{I_{ph,1,rms} \approx 10.29 \, \text{A}}$$ **(v) Phase angle of the load:** **(1.5 marks)** $$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_L}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{9.4248}{10}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.9425)$$ $$\boxed{\phi \approx 43.31° \text{ (lagging)}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to multiply by $\sqrt{3}$ for line-to-line voltage:** A very common error is to use $V_{ph,peak} = m_a V_{dc}/2$ directly as the line-to-line value. Always remember: $V_{LL} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$ for sinusoidal fundamentals. > 2. **Using $m_a = 1.155$ in THIPWM questions but asking for SPWM-based answers:** Be careful — the formula for $V_{LL,rms} = m_a \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot V_{dc}$ is valid in the **linear region only** ($m_a \leq 1$ for SPWM, $m_a \leq 1.155$ for THIPWM). > 3. **Confusing phase current with line current:** In a **star (Y)-connected** load, line current = phase current. In a **delta (Δ)-connected** load, line current = $\sqrt{3} \times$ phase current. The problem statement must specify the connection. > 4. **Missing the unit conversion for inductance:** $L = 30$ mH must be converted to $0.030$ H before calculation. > 5. **Not specifying "lagging" for the power factor angle:** Always mention "lagging" for RL load angle. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Key Takeaways:** - ⚡ **Three-Phase VSI:** 6 switches, 3 legs, complementary switching per leg. - ⚡ **RL Load:** Inductive load causes current to **lag voltage** by angle $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega L / R)$; current is **smooth and continuous** due to L. - ⚡ **SPWM:** Sine reference vs. triangle carrier; **$m_a$ controls voltage, $m_f$ controls harmonic spectrum**. - ⚡ **THIPWM:** Reference = sine + (1/6)·3rd harmonic; **$M_{max} = 2/\sqrt{3} = 1.155$**. - ⚡ **DC Bus Utilization:** SPWM = 86.6%, THIPWM = 100% (full utilization). - ⚡ **Optimal 3rd Harmonic Amplitude:** Exactly **$k = 1/6$** of fundamental; this is the only value that flattens the reference optimally. - ⚡ **THD:** With RL load, THD drops to **3–8%** (SPWM/THIPWM) compared to **31%** in square-wave. - ⚡ **Dominant Harmonics in SPWM:** Centered around $f_c = m_f \cdot f_1$ (e.g., for $m_f = 15$, dominant is at 750 Hz). - ⚡ **Triplen Harmonics:** Appear in phase voltage but **cancel in line-to-line voltage** of a balanced 3-phase system. - ⚡ **Implementation:** In MATLAB/Simulink, use **"PWM Generator (3-Level)"** or build manually using **"Relational Operator"** blocks (reference > carrier). - ⚡ **Observation in Lab:** Vary $m_a$ from 0.1 to 1.2 and observe — current magnitude increases linearly up to $m_a = 1$ (SPWM) or $1.155$ (THIPWM), then enters **overmodulation**. - ⚡ **Key Formula to Memorize:** $V_{LL,1,rms} = m_a \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot V_{dc}$ (linear region). - ⚡ **Power Quality:** THIPWM gives **better DC bus utilization** with **same switching losses** as SPWM — preferred in industry. ---

topics.title: Stator Voltage Control of Three-Phase Induction Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL507 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Stator Voltage Control of Three-Phase Induction Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Stator Voltage Control** is a scalar (V/f ratio NOT maintained) speed control technique for a three-phase squirrel-cage induction motor in which the **RMS value of the stator phase voltage** is reduced below its rated value by means of a phase-controlled AC-AC converter (typically an **AC Voltage Controller** using anti-parallel thyristors or a Triac per phase), while the **supply frequency is held constant** at the rated value ($f_s = 50\text{ Hz}$).

Because the supply frequency is fixed, the synchronous speed $N_s = \frac{120 f_s}{P}$ remains constant. Speed variation is therefore obtained **only by reducing the air-gap torque capability** of the machine, which causes the operating point to slide down the torque-slip curve to a new equilibrium with a higher slip. It is a low-cost, simple, and rugged method suited for **torque proportional to speed squared** loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** This experiment is *always* asked in the ESE as a 14-mark Part-B question pairing with "V/f control" or "Soft-starting of Induction Motor." Memorize the **circuit topology**, the **firing pulse generation**, and the **torque equation variation with $V_s$**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a cyclist pedalling down a flat road, and the wind resistance is a soft foot-pump pushing against you. Now suppose the road is slightly uphill — you naturally **slow down** even though your pedalling rate (cadence) is the same.

Translating this analogy to the induction motor:
- **Pedalling rate (pedal pushes per second)** → **Supply frequency $f_s$** (kept fixed at **50 Hz**).
- **Force in your legs (push strength)** → **Stator voltage $V_s$** (controlled by the thyristor firing angle $\alpha$).
- **Wind resistance** → **Mechanical load torque** on the motor shaft.
- **Your speed** → **Rotor speed $N_r$**.

When you **reduce the push strength (lower $V_s$)** while riding uphill, the bike slows down. The motor does the same: lower stator voltage → weaker rotating magnetic field → **less torque produced at every slip** → rotor settles at a **higher slip (lower speed)**.

> [!NOTE]
> The intuitive key is this: **frequency sets the "speed ceiling" of the magnetic field, and voltage sets the "torque strength" of the magnetic field.** Reducing voltage cannot raise the ceiling, but it lowers the torque output — useful only for fan/pump-type loads whose torque demand drops at low speed.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Supply frequency (India):** $f_s = 50\text{ Hz}$
- **Standard line-to-line voltage:** $V_L = 415\text{ V (RMS)}$
- **Standard phase voltage:** $V_{ph} = \frac{415}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 239.6\text{ V}$
- **Synchronous speed for 4-pole motor:** $N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500\text{ RPM}$
- **Air-gap flux reference:** $\Phi = \frac{V_s}{4.44 f_s N}$ (transformer-like EMF equation)
- **Firing angle operating window for AC voltage controller:** $\alpha \in [\alpha_{min}, 180°]$, where $\alpha_{min} \approx 25°$ to avoid asymmetry at small conduction angles.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The induction motor develops electromagnetic torque because the rotating stator flux (of magnitude $\Phi$) interacts with the induced rotor current. The torque equation in **approximate (linear) form** is:

$$T_e \;\propto\; \frac{s \, E_2^{2} \, R_2 / s}{(R_2/s)^{2} + (sX_2)^{2}}$$

For small slip (operating region), $R_2/s \gg sX_2$, so the denominator is approximately $(R_2/s)^2$, simplifying to:

$$T_e \;\approx\; \frac{s \, E_2^{2}}{R_2}$$

where $E_2$ is the rotor-induced EMF at slip $s$. Since $E_2 \propto \Phi \propto V_s$ (at constant frequency), the torque scales with the **square of stator voltage**:

$$\boxed{\;T_e \;\propto\; \frac{s \, V_s^{2}}{R_2}\;}$$

**Why does speed drop when $V_s$ drops?**
- The load torque curve $T_L(\omega_r)$ is fixed (e.g., a fan).
- Reducing $V_s$ reduces the *available* motor torque curve by a factor $(V_s/V_{rated})^2$.
- The new intersection (motor curve ∩ load curve) occurs at a **larger slip** → **lower rotor speed**.

**How is $V_s$ varied electronically?**
An **AC Voltage Controller** (also called AC-AC chopper or phase-controlled regulator) is placed between the three-phase mains and the stator terminals. Each phase contains a pair of **anti-parallel SCRs (thyristors)** or a single **Triac**. By delaying the gate trigger pulse by an angle $\alpha$ from the natural commutation (zero-crossing) point, the **conduction angle $\gamma$** is shortened, reducing the RMS voltage delivered to the stator.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f_s}{P}$ | RPM; $P$ = number of poles |
| 2 | Rotor speed | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | RPM; $s$ = slip |
| 3 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Per-unit (0 to 1) |
| 4 | Air-gap flux (transformer EMF) | $\Phi = \dfrac{V_s}{4.44 \, f_s \, N_{ph} \, k_w}$ | Weber; $k_w$ = winding factor |
| 5 | Torque $\propto V_s^{2}$ | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{th}^{2} \cdot (R_2/s)}{(R_{th} + R_2/s)^{2} + (X_{th} + X_2)^{2}}$ | N·m |
| 6 | Maximum (pull-out) torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{th}^{2}}{2(R_{th} + \sqrt{R_{th}^{2} + (X_{th} + X_2)^{2}})}$ | N·m |
| 7 | Slip at max torque | $s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_{th}^{2} + (X_{th} + X_2)^{2}}}$ | Independent of $V_s$ |
| 8 | RMS output voltage of AC controller (per phase, R-load) | $V_{o,RMS} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{\pi} + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$ | Volts; for R load |
| 9 | Firing-angle range | $\alpha \in [\alpha_{min}, 180°]$ | $\alpha_{min} \approx 25°$ to $35°$ for 3-$\phi$ |
| 10 | Conduction angle | $\gamma = 180° - \alpha$ | Electrical degrees |
| 11 | Starting torque reduction factor | $k_T = \left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_{rated}}\right)^{2}$ | Squared scaling |
| 12 | Power factor (AC controller) | $PF = \dfrac{P_{out}}{S} = \dfrac{V_{o,RMS}}{V_s} \cdot \cos\phi_{load}$ | Displacement + distortion |
| 13 | Load power (fan law) | $P \propto N^{3}$ | Cubic relation |
| 14 | Load torque (fan law) | $T_L \propto N^{2}$ | Quadratic relation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Golden Trick:** Slip at maximum torque $s_{mT}$ is **independent of $V_s$** — only the *magnitude* of the torque curve changes. This is the most-tested board fact for stator voltage control problems.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stator voltage control is the **workhorse of low-power industrial drives** for variable-torque loads:

1. **Ceiling fans and table fans** — domestic and commercial energy savers.
2. **Centrifugal pumps** — water supply boosters, HVAC circulating pumps.
3. **Blowers and exhaust fans** — mine ventilation, industrial fume extraction.
4. **Small compressors** — refrigeration condenser airflow control.
5. **Mixers and agitators** — process industries where low starting torque is acceptable.
6. **Soft-starters** — same hardware topology, but $\alpha$ is reduced from initial high value back to $0°$ over a few seconds to limit **inrush current** (typically 6× to 8× the rated current at direct-on-line start).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **soft-starter is essentially a stator voltage controller operated in transient mode.** Many KTU examiners frame this as a comparison question: *"Compare stator voltage control and V/f control — when is the former preferred?"* The answer: **soft-starting and low-cost fan/pump drives.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — RMS Output Voltage of Single-Phase AC Voltage Controller (R-Load)

**Given:** A sinusoidal source $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ feeds an AC voltage controller (anti-parallel SCR pair or Triac) connected to a resistive load $R$. The SCRs are fired symmetrically at angles $\alpha$ and $\pi + \alpha$.

**Find:** RMS output voltage $V_{o,RMS}$ as a function of $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Define the output waveform.**
The output voltage is zero from $\omega t = 0$ to $\alpha$ (SCRs not yet fired), and follows the source from $\alpha$ to $\pi$. By symmetry, the negative half-cycle mirrors the positive half-cycle. Hence, the conduction period per half-cycle is $(\pi - \alpha)$.

**Step 2 — Write the RMS definition over a full period $2\pi$.**
By symmetry, we can integrate over one half-cycle and multiply:

$$V_{o,RMS}^{2} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} (V_m \sin\theta)^{2} \, d\theta$$

**Step 3 — Expand $\sin^{2}\theta$ using the identity.**

$$V_{o,RMS}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2} \, d\theta$$

**Step 4 — Perform the integration term by term.**

$$V_{o,RMS}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} d\theta \;-\; \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \cos 2\theta \, d\theta \right]$$

$$= \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi} \left[ (\pi - \alpha) \;-\; \left.\frac{\sin 2\theta}{2}\right|_{\alpha}^{\pi} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the boundary of the sine term.**

$$\left.\frac{\sin 2\theta}{2}\right|_{\alpha}^{\pi} = \frac{\sin 2\pi}{2} - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} = 0 - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} = -\frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}$$

Substituting back:

$$V_{o,RMS}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi} \left[ (\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right]$$

**Step 6 — Express in terms of RMS source voltage $V_s = V_m/\sqrt{2}$.**

$$V_{o,RMS}^{2} = \frac{V_s^{2}}{\pi} \left[ (\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right]$$

**Step 7 — Final compact form.**

$$\boxed{\;V_{o,RMS} \;=\; V_s \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}\;}$$

**Step 8 — Sanity checks.**
- At $\alpha = 0$: $V_{o,RMS} = V_s \sqrt{1 - 0 + 0} = V_s$ ✓ (full voltage, SCRs behave like diodes).
- At $\alpha = \pi$: $V_{o,RMS} = V_s \sqrt{1 - 1 + 0} = 0$ ✓ (no conduction, zero output).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque Scaling with Stator Voltage

**Given:** The exact per-phase Thevenin equivalent of the induction motor referred to stator with Thevenin voltage $V_{th} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{jX_m}{R_s + j(X_s + X_m)}$.

For $X_m \gg R_s$ (valid for medium/large machines), $|V_{th}| \approx V_s \cdot \dfrac{X_m}{X_s + X_m} = k \cdot V_s$, where $k$ is a constant for a given machine.

**Find:** Show that electromagnetic torque is proportional to $V_s^{2}$.

**Step 1 — Torque equation (per-phase Thevenin form):**

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^{2} \cdot (R_2 / s)}{\left(R_{th} + R_2/s\right)^{2} + (X_{th} + X_2)^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $V_{th} = k \cdot V_s$:**

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{k^{2} V_s^{2} \cdot (R_2 / s)}{\left(R_{th} + R_2/s\right)^{2} + (X_{th} + X_2)^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the proportionality:**

$$\boxed{\;T_e \;\propto\; V_s^{2}\;}$$

since every other term in the equation ($R_2, R_{th}, X_{th}, X_2, s, \omega_s$) is independent of $V_s$.

**Step 4 — Consequence:** If $V_s$ is reduced to $0.7 \, V_{rated}$, the torque at *every* slip value drops to $0.49$ of its rated-voltage value. Maximum torque drops by the same factor; **slip at maximum torque $s_{mT}$ is unchanged.**

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Speed Drop for a Quadratic Torque Load

**Given:** A fan load requires $T_L = K \cdot \omega_r^{2}$. The motor at rated voltage $V_{rated}$ runs at slip $s_1$. The voltage is reduced to $V_s = x \cdot V_{rated}$ where $0 < x < 1$.

**Find:** The new slip $s_2$ and percentage speed reduction.

**Step 1 — Operating point equation at rated voltage:**
$$K \omega_{r1}^{2} = T_{e1}(s_1) \approx \frac{s_1 V_{rated}^{2}}{C}$$
where $C$ is a constant lumping the rotor impedance parameters.

**Step 2 — Operating point equation at reduced voltage:**
$$K \omega_{r2}^{2} = T_{e2}(s_2) \approx \frac{s_2 V_s^{2}}{C} = \frac{s_2 x^{2} V_{rated}^{2}}{C}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio of the two operating points:**
$$\frac{\omega_{r2}^{2}}{\omega_{r1}^{2}} = \frac{s_2 \, x^{2}}{s_1}$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of slips:** $\omega_{r} = \omega_s (1 - s)$, so:

$$\frac{(1 - s_2)^{2}}{(1 - s_1)^{2}} = \frac{s_2 \, x^{2}}{s_1}$$

**Step 5 — Cubic equation in $s_2$:**
For small $s_1, s_2$ (typical fan operating region), $(1 - s) \approx 1$, so the equation simplifies to:

$$s_2 \approx \frac{s_1}{x^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
- If $s_1 = 0.05$ (5 %) at rated voltage, and voltage is reduced to $x = 0.8$, then $s_2 = 0.05 / 0.64 = 0.078$.
- Rotor speed drops from $N_{r1} = 1500 (1 - 0.05) = 1425$ RPM to $N_{r2} = 1500 (1 - 0.078) = 1383$ RPM — a **2.9 % speed reduction** for a 20 % voltage reduction.

This is why stator voltage control is **inefficient for constant-torque loads** but **adequate for fan/pump-type quadratic loads**.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Starting Current Reduction

**Given:** Direct-on-line starting draws a current $I_{start,DOL} \approx 5$ to $7$ times full-load current. The motor's transient impedance is essentially $Z_{st} = \sqrt{(R_s + R_2')^2 + (X_s + X_2')^2}$ (stator + rotor referred leakage).

**Find:** Stator current as a function of firing angle $\alpha$ at the instant of starting (where rotor is at standstill, $s = 1$).

**Step 1 — Instantaneous applied voltage at starting:** the fundamental RMS component of the chopped waveform is $V_{o,RMS}(\alpha)$ from Derivation 1 (approximately valid for low-power factor starting transients).

**Step 2 — Stator current at start:**
$$I_{start}(\alpha) = \frac{V_{o,RMS}(\alpha)}{Z_{st}} = \frac{V_s}{Z_{st}} \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$$

**Step 3 — Ratio vs. DOL starting:**
$$\frac{I_{start}(\alpha)}{I_{start,DOL}} = \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example:** For $\alpha = 90°$:
- $\sin 180° = 0$
- $\dfrac{I_{start}(90°)}{I_{start,DOL}} = \sqrt{1 - 0.5 + 0} = \sqrt{0.5} \approx 0.707$

So a $90°$ firing angle reduces the starting current to **70.7 % of the DOL value** — illustrating the soft-start function.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Speed vs. Voltage Trade-off Summary

| Reduction $x = V_s / V_{rated}$ | $T_{e}$ (scaled) | Slip (quadratic load) | Rotor speed (4-pole, 50 Hz) | Torque at slip $s_{mT}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1.0 | 100 % | $s_1$ | $N_s(1 - s_1)$ | $T_{max}$ |
| 0.9 | 81 % | $s_1 / 0.81$ | Lower | $0.81 \, T_{max}$ |
| 0.8 | 64 % | $s_1 / 0.64$ | Lower still | $0.64 \, T_{max}$ |
| 0.7 | 49 % | $s_1 / 0.49$ | Lower | $0.49 \, T_{max}$ |
| 0.6 | 36 % | $s_1 / 0.36$ | Approaches $0.8 N_s$ | $0.36 \, T_{max}$ |
| 0.5 | 25 % | $s_1 / 0.25$ | **Stalling region** | $0.25 \, T_{max}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Below $\approx 0.6 V_{rated}$**, the available torque at the original operating slip becomes less than the load torque, and the motor **stalls** or refuses to start. This sets the **practical lower limit of $V_s$** for stator voltage control and is a frequently asked numerical problem.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
